Motivation: Vision-Language Models often produce plausible but ungrounded answers, especially with ambiguous visual inputs. Existing prompts to reflect are insufficiently tied to image evidence, leading to hallucinations and logic errors.

Method: MIRROR implements a closed-loop pipeline: draft → critique → region-based verification → revision, iterated until outputs are visually grounded. ReflectV provides supervision with explicit reflection triggers, region-based verification actions, and answer revisions grounded in visual evidence.

Result: Empirical results on general vision-language and reasoning benchmarks show improved correctness and reduced visual hallucinations. Training reflection as an evidence-seeking, region-aware verification process yields stronger grounding than purely textual revisions.

Conclusion: Training reflection to be evidence-seeking and region-aware enhances multimodal reasoning grounding; MIRROR generalizes across benchmarks, and ReflectV enables effective multi-turn supervision.

Abstract: In the era of Vision-Language Models (VLMs), enhancing multimodal reasoning capabilities remains a critical challenge, particularly in handling ambiguous or complex visual inputs, where initial inferences often lead to hallucinations or logic errors. Existing VLMs often produce plausible yet ungrounded answers, and even when prompted to "reflect", their corrections may remain detached from the image evidence. To address this, we propose the MIRROR framework for Multimodal Iterative Reasoning via Reflection On visual Regions. By embedding visual reflection as a core mechanism, MIRROR is formulated as a closed-loop process comprising draft, critique, region-based verification, and revision, which are repeated until the output is visually grounded. To facilitate training of this model, we construct **ReflectV**, a visual reflective dataset for multi-turn supervision that explicitly contains reflection triggers, region-based verification actions, and answer revision grounded in visual evidence. Experiments on both general vision-language benchmarks and representative vision-language reasoning benchmarks show that MIRROR improves correctness and reduces visual hallucinations, demonstrating the value of training reflection as an evidence-seeking, region-aware verification process rather than a purely textual revision step.

</details>


### [27] [Benchmarking Computational Pathology Foundation Models For Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18747)
*Lavish Ramchandani,Aashay Tinaikar,Dev Kumar Das,Rohit Garg,Tijo Thomas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Benchmarking 10 foundation models for pixel-level histopathology segmentation using attention maps as pixelwise features with XGBoost; CONCH excels, ensemble of CONCH, PathDino, and CellViT yields the best generalization (+7.95% average).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of systematic, independent evaluations of foundation models for pixel-level histopathology segmentation across tasks and cohorts.

Method: Extract attention maps from 10 foundation models (e.g., CLIP, DINO, CONCH) as pixel-wise features, then train an XGBoost classifier for segmentation without any fine-tuning. Evaluate on four histopathology datasets spanning tissue-region and cellular/nuclear segmentation. Analyze cross-model complementarity and ensemble benefits by concatenating features from multiple models.

Result: CONCH (vision-language) generally outperforms vision-only models; PathDino is a close second. Models trained on different cohorts capture complementary representations; concatenating their features improves performance. The ensemble of CONCH, PathDino, and CellViT achieves the best results, outperforming individual models by 7.95% on average across datasets.

Conclusion: Ensembling foundation-model-derived pixel features yields superior generalization for histopathology segmentation. Attention-map features with a lightweight classifier enable fast, interpretable, and model-agnostic benchmarking without fine-tuning.

Abstract: In recent years, foundation models such as CLIP, DINO,and CONCH have demonstrated remarkable domain generalization and unsupervised feature extraction capabilities across diverse imaging tasks. However, systematic and independent evaluations of these models for pixel-level semantic segmentation in histopathology remain scarce. In this study, we propose a robust benchmarking approach to asses 10 foundational models on four histopathological datasets covering both morphological tissue-region and cellular/nuclear segmentation tasks. Our method leverages attention maps of foundation models as pixel-wise features, which are then classified using a machine learning algorithm, XGBoost, enabling fast, interpretable, and model-agnostic evaluation without finetuning. We show that the vision language foundation model, CONCH performed the best across datasets when compared to vision-only foundation models, with PathDino as close second. Further analysis shows that models trained on distinct histopathology cohorts capture complementary morphological representations, and concatenating their features yields superior segmentation performance. Concatenating features from CONCH, PathDino and CellViT outperformed individual models across all the datasets by 7.95% (averaged across the datasets), suggesting that ensembles of foundation models can better generalize to diverse histopathological segmentation tasks.

</details>


### [28] [Optimizing ID Consistency in Multimodal Large Models: Facial Restoration via Alignment, Entanglement, and Disentanglement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18752)
*Yuran Dong,Hang Dai,Mang Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EditedID introduces an alignment-disentanglement-entanglement framework for robust identity-specific facial restoration in multimodal editing large models. It tackles cross-source distribution bias and cross-source feature contamination with three components: adaptive mixing of cross-source latent representations during diffusion, a hybrid solver to disentangle identity attributes from details, and an attentional gating mechanism to selectively entangle visual elements. The approach is training-free and plug-and-play, achieving state-of-the-art preservation of facial identity and edited element consistency, and sets a new benchmark for open-world single/multi-person facial restoration in real-person editing scenarios. Code is released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal editing LMs struggle with preserving facial identity during realistic portrait editing. Facial identity consistency is highly noticeable to humans, and cross-source distribution bias and cross-source feature contamination impede restoration of identity and edited elements. A robust, training-free method is needed to maintain identity while editing, enabling practical deployment in open-world, real-person scenarios.

Method: Three components: (1) Adaptive mixing strategy to align cross-source latent representations throughout the diffusion process; (2) Hybrid solver to disentangle source-specific identity attributes and details; (3) Attentional gating mechanism to selectively entangle visual elements. The framework analyzes diffusion trajectories, sampler behaviors, and attention properties to guide design.

Result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance in preserving original facial identity and edited element identity/appearance consistency (IP). The method is training-free and plug-and-play, establishing a new benchmark for practical, reliable facial restoration in open-world settings for single/multi-person editing. Code is available at the provided repository.

Conclusion: EditedID provides a robust, training-free solution for identity-specific facial restoration in multimodal editing, addressing cross-source biases and feature contamination. It enables reliable open-world editing of real persons and facilitates deployment of editing LMs in practical scenarios.

Abstract: Multimodal editing large models have demonstrated powerful editing capabilities across diverse tasks. However, a persistent and long-standing limitation is the decline in facial identity (ID) consistency during realistic portrait editing. Due to the human eye's high sensitivity to facial features, such inconsistency significantly hinders the practical deployment of these models. Current facial ID preservation methods struggle to achieve consistent restoration of both facial identity and edited element IP due to Cross-source Distribution Bias and Cross-source Feature Contamination. To address these issues, we propose EditedID, an Alignment-Disentanglement-Entanglement framework for robust identity-specific facial restoration. By systematically analyzing diffusion trajectories, sampler behaviors, and attention properties, we introduce three key components: 1) Adaptive mixing strategy that aligns cross-source latent representations throughout the diffusion process. 2) Hybrid solver that disentangles source-specific identity attributes and details. 3) Attentional gating mechanism that selectively entangles visual elements. Extensive experiments show that EditedID achieves state-of-the-art performance in preserving original facial ID and edited element IP consistency. As a training-free and plug-and-play solution, it establishes a new benchmark for practical and reliable single/multi-person facial identity restoration in open-world settings, paving the way for the deployment of multimodal editing large models in real-person editing scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/NDYBSNDY/EditedID.

</details>


### [29] [Driving with A Thousand Faces: A Benchmark for Closed-Loop Personalized End-to-End Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18757)
*Xiaoru Dong,Ruiqin Li,Xiao Han,Zhenxuan Wu,Jiamin Wang,Jian Chen,Qi Jiang,SM Yiu,Xinge Zhu,Yuexin Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Person2Drive introduces a comprehensive platform and benchmark for personalized end-to-end autonomous driving, featuring (1) an open-source data collection system for realistic, diverse, personalized datasets, (2) style-vector evaluation using MMD and KL divergence to quantify individual driving styles, and (3) a personalized E2E-AD framework with a style reward model to adapt models for safe, individualized driving; with extensive experiments supporting fine-grained analysis and reproducible evaluation; dataset/code to be released after acceptance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end autonomous driving systems typically learn a single, average driving style, ignoring individual differences. There is a need to address three gaps: lack of real-world datasets with individual-level annotations, absence of quantitative metrics for personal driving styles, and no effective algorithms to learn stylized representations from user trajectories.

Method: 1) An open-source data collection system that simulates realistic scenarios to generate scalable, diverse personalized driving datasets. 2) Style-vector based evaluation metrics using Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) and KL divergence to quantify individual driving behaviors. 3) A personalized E2E-AD framework incorporating a style reward model that enables efficient adaptation of E2E models to safe, individualized driving.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that Person2Drive enables fine-grained analysis, reproducible evaluation, and effective personalization in end-to-end autonomous driving.

Conclusion: The dataset and code will be released after acceptance, enabling the research community to analyze and develop personalized E2E-AD systems with standardized benchmarks.

Abstract: Human driving behavior is inherently diverse, yet most end-to-end autonomous driving (E2E-AD) systems learn a single average driving style, neglecting individual differences. Achieving personalized E2E-AD faces challenges across three levels: limited real-world datasets with individual-level annotations, a lack of quantitative metrics for evaluating personal driving styles, and the absence of algorithms that can learn stylized representations from users' trajectories. To address these gaps, we propose Person2Drive, a comprehensive personalized E2E-AD platform and benchmark. It includes an open-source, flexible data collection system that simulates realistic scenarios to generate scalable and diverse personalized driving datasets; style vector-based evaluation metrics with Maximum Mean Discrepancy and KL divergence to comprehensively quantify individual driving behaviors; and a personalized E2E-AD framework with a style reward model that efficiently adapts E2E models for safe and individualized driving. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Person2Drive enables fine-grained analysis, reproducible evaluation, and effective personalization in end-to-end autonomous driving. Our dataset and code will be released after acceptance.

</details>


### [30] [TAG: Thinking with Action Unit Grounding for Facial Expression Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18763)
*Haobo Lin,Tianyi Bai,Jiajun Zhang,Xuanhao Chang,Sheng Lu,Fangming Gu,Zengjie Hu,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TAG is a vision–language FER framework that grounds reasoning in facial Action Units (AUs) to improve accuracy and visual faithfulness, outperforming strong baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FER demands localized, meaningful facial cues; current VLM explanations are fluent but often ungrounded and hallucinate, hindering robustness across datasets.

Method: Train TAG with AU-grounded reasoning traces via supervised fine-tuning, then apply reinforcement learning with an AU-aware reward to align predicted regions with external AU detectors, constraining multimodal reasoning to AU-related facial regions.

Result: TAG consistently outperforms strong open-source and closed-source VLM baselines on RAF-DB, FERPlus, and AffectNet, while improving visual faithfulness; ablations show AU-grounded rewards stabilize reasoning and reduce hallucination.

Conclusion: Structured, AU-grounded intermediate representations are crucial for trustworthy multimodal reasoning in FER; code will be available for replication.

Abstract: Facial Expression Recognition (FER) is a fine-grained visual understanding task where reliable predictions require reasoning over localized and meaningful facial cues. Recent vision--language models (VLMs) enable natural language explanations for FER, but their reasoning is often ungrounded, producing fluent yet unverifiable rationales that are weakly tied to visual evidence and prone to hallucination, leading to poor robustness across different datasets. We propose TAG (Thinking with Action Unit Grounding), a vision--language framework that explicitly constrains multimodal reasoning to be supported by facial Action Units (AUs). TAG requires intermediate reasoning steps to be grounded in AU-related facial regions, yielding predictions accompanied by verifiable visual evidence. The model is trained via supervised fine-tuning on AU-grounded reasoning traces followed by reinforcement learning with an AU-aware reward that aligns predicted regions with external AU detectors. Evaluated on RAF-DB, FERPlus, and AffectNet, TAG consistently outperforms strong open-source and closed-source VLM baselines while simultaneously improving visual faithfulness. Ablation and preference studies further show that AU-grounded rewards stabilize reasoning and mitigate hallucination, demonstrating the importance of structured grounded intermediate representations for trustworthy multimodal reasoning in FER. The code will be available at https://github.com/would1920/FER_TAG .

</details>


### [31] [A high-resolution nationwide urban village mapping product for 342 Chinese cities based on foundation models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18765)
*Lubin Bai,Sheng Xiao,Ziyu Yin,Haoyu Wang,Siyang Wu,Xiuyuan Zhang,Shihong Du*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A nationwide, high-resolution UV mapping dataset for China (GeoLink-UV) using a foundation-model-driven framework across 342 cities, enabling open, validated insights for urban governance and SDG 11.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses lack of consistent nationwide UV datasets due to heterogeneity across China; enables governance, renewal, and sustainable urban development.

Method: Integrates optical remote sensing and vector data; employs foundation-model-driven mapping framework to improve generalization; includes geographically stratified accuracy assessment with independent samples from 28 cities.

Result: Dataset covers 342 cities; UVs occupy about 8% of built-up land; UVs show low-rise, high-density morphology; interregional disparities with clustering in central and south China; dataset openly available at DOI; validation confirms reliability.

Conclusion: GeoLink-UV provides an open, validated geospatial foundation for urban studies, informal settlement monitoring, and renewal planning; supports large-scale SDG 11 assessments.

Abstract: Urban Villages (UVs) represent a distinctive form of high-density informal settlement embedded within China's rapidly urbanizing cities. Accurate identification of UVs is critical for urban governance, renewal, and sustainable development. But due to the pronounced heterogeneity and diversity of UVs across China's vast territory, a consistent and reliable nationwide dataset has been lacking. In this work, we present GeoLink-UV, a high-resolution nationwide UV mapping product that clearly delineates the locations and boundaries of UVs in 342 Chinese cities. The dataset is derived from multisource geospatial data, including optical remote sensing images and geo-vector data, and is generated through a foundation model-driven mapping framework designed to address the generalization issues and improve the product quality. A geographically stratified accuracy assessment based on independent samples from 28 cities confirms the reliability and scientific credibility of the nationwide dataset across heterogeneous urban contexts. Based on this nationwide product, we reveal substantial interregional disparities in UV prevalence and spatial configuration. On average, UV areas account for 8 % of built-up land, with marked clustering in central and south China. Building-level analysis further confirms a consistent low-rise, high-density development pattern of UVs nationwide, while highlighting regionally differentiated morphological characteristics. The GeoLink-UV dataset provides an open and systematically validated geospatial foundation for urban studies, informal settlement monitoring, and evidence-based urban renewal planning, and contributes directly to large-scale assessments aligned with Sustainable Development Goal 11. The GeoLink-UV dataset introduced in this article is freely available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18688062.

</details>


### [32] [Initialization matters in few-shot adaptation of vision-language models for histopathological image classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18766)
*Pablo Meseguer,Rocío del Amor,Valery Naranjo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Zero-Shot MIL (ZS-MIL) for whole-slide image (WSI) classification by initializing the MIL classifier with class-level embeddings from a Vision-Language Model (VLM), improving zero-shot and few-shot subtyping with more robust, less variable performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MIL-based WSI classification faces sensitivity to random classifier initialization in few-shot fine-tuning; leveraging VLM text embeddings can provide informative starting weights to improve transfer learning. 

Method: Compute class-level embeddings from the VLM text encoder and use them as the MIL classifier's initial weights for bag-level prediction; evaluate robustness across multiple experiments under few-shot conditions for subtyping tasks; compare against standard initialization techniques.

Result: ZS-MIL demonstrates improved robustness and performance over traditional weight initialization methods in ETL few-shot regimes for WSI subtyping, with reduced variability.

Conclusion: Using VLM-derived text embeddings to initialize the MIL classifier offers a simple, effective zero-shot/few-shot transfer learning strategy for MIL-based WSI classification, addressing limitations of random initialization.

Abstract: Vision language models (VLM) pre-trained on datasets of histopathological image-caption pairs enabled zero-shot slide-level classification. The ability of VLM image encoders to extract discriminative features also opens the door for supervised fine-tuning for whole-slide image (WSI) classification, ideally using few labeled samples. Slide-level prediction frameworks require the incorporation of multiple instance learning (MIL) due to the gigapixel size of the WSI. Following patch-level feature extraction and aggregation, MIL frameworks rely on linear classifiers trained on top of the slide-level aggregated features. Classifier weight initialization has a large influence on Linear Probing performance in efficient transfer learning (ETL) approaches based on few-shot learning. In this work, we propose Zero-Shot Multiple-Instance Learning (ZS-MIL) to address the limitations of random classifier initialization that underperform zero-shot prediction in MIL problems. ZS-MIL uses the class-level embeddings of the VLM text encoder as the classification layer's starting point to compute each sample's bag-level probabilities. Through multiple experiments, we demonstrate the robustness of ZS-MIL compared to well-known weight initialization techniques both in terms of performance and variability in an ETL few-shot scenario for subtyping prediction.

</details>


### [33] [MaskDiME: Adaptive Masked Diffusion for Precise and Efficient Visual Counterfactual Explanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18792)
*Changlu Guo,Anders Nymark Christensen,Anders Bjorholm Dahl,Morten Rieger Hannemose*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MaskDiME is a training-free diffusion framework that yields fast, localized, semantically consistent visual counterfactual explanations, achieving 30x faster inference and strong performance across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based counterfactual methods are often computationally expensive, slow to sample, and imprecise in localizing modifications; there is a need for efficient, localized, semantically coherent explanations.

Method: Introduce MaskDiME: a diffusion-based framework with adaptive, localized sampling that focuses on decision-relevant regions to enforce spatial precision and semantic consistency; training-free.

Result: Achieves >30x faster inference than baseline; comparable or state-of-the-art across five benchmark datasets across diverse visual domains.

Conclusion: MaskDiME provides a practical, generalizable, efficient solution for visual counterfactual explanations with high fidelity and localized modifications.

Abstract: Visual counterfactual explanations aim to reveal the minimal semantic modifications that can alter a model's prediction, providing causal and interpretable insights into deep neural networks. However, existing diffusion-based counterfactual generation methods are often computationally expensive, slow to sample, and imprecise in localizing the modified regions. To address these limitations, we propose MaskDiME, a simple, fast, and effective diffusion framework that unifies semantic consistency and spatial precision through localized sampling. Our approach adaptively focuses on decision-relevant regions to achieve localized and semantically consistent counterfactual generation while preserving high image fidelity. Our training-free framework, MaskDiME, achieves over 30x faster inference than the baseline method and achieves comparable or state-of-the-art performance across five benchmark datasets spanning diverse visual domains, establishing a practical and generalizable solution for efficient counterfactual explanation.

</details>


### [34] [Rethinking Preference Alignment for Diffusion Models with Classifier-Free Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18799)
*Zhou Jiang,Yandong Wen,Zhen Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A CFG-inspired, contrastive preference guidance method that decouples positive/negative signals into two modules and uses their difference as an inference-time control to improve alignment of diffusion models without base-model retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the generalization gap in preference-based alignment for large-scale diffusion models while avoiding costly full-model finetuning.

Method: Treats preference alignment as classifier-free guidance. Introduces two separate modules trained on positive and negative data; at inference, forms a contrastive guidance vector (positive minus negative), scales it by a user-defined strength, and adds it to the base model's prediction at every step to sharpen alignment without retraining the base.

Result: Demonstrates consistent quantitative and qualitative improvements on Stable Diffusion 1.5 and Stable Diffusion XL across Pick-a-Pic v2 and HPDv3, indicating better alignment with nuanced human preferences.

Conclusion: The contrastive CFG approach provides a simple, flexible way to improve alignment without retraining the backbone model, though it relies on quality positive/negative data and incurs additional inference cost; it strengthens the alignment signal and offers user-controlled tightening of preferences.

Abstract: Aligning large-scale text-to-image diffusion models with nuanced human preferences remains challenging. While direct preference optimization (DPO) is simple and effective, large-scale finetuning often shows a generalization gap. We take inspiration from test-time guidance and cast preference alignment as classifier-free guidance (CFG): a finetuned preference model acts as an external control signal during sampling. Building on this view, we propose a simple method that improves alignment without retraining the base model. To further enhance generalization, we decouple preference learning into two modules trained on positive and negative data, respectively, and form a \emph{contrastive guidance} vector at inference by subtracting their predictions (positive minus negative), scaled by a user-chosen strength and added to the base prediction at each step. This yields a sharper and controllable alignment signal. We evaluate on Stable Diffusion 1.5 and Stable Diffusion XL with Pick-a-Pic v2 and HPDv3, showing consistent quantitative and qualitative gains.

</details>


### [35] [Learning Multi-Modal Prototypes for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18811)
*Wanqi Wang,Jingcai Guo,Yuxiang Cai,Zhi Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A dual-branch detector, LMP, fuses textual guidance with target-domain visual exemplars via a Visual Prototype Construction module to improve cross-domain few-shot object detection; it yields state-of-the-art or highly competitive mAP on six benchmarks across 1/5/10-shot settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-vocabulary detectors based on vision-language models rely on text prompts to encode domain-invariant semantics but often lack domain-specific visual cues necessary for precise localization under few-shot cross-domain conditions. There is a need to integrate target-domain visual information with open-vocabulary semantics to improve detection performance.

Method: Propose LMP with two branches. The visual-guided branch injects prototypes derived from a Visual Prototype Construction module, which aggregates class-level prototypes from support RoIs and generates hard-negative prototypes in query images via jittered boxes. The text-guided branch preserves open-vocabulary semantics. The branches are trained jointly and ensembled at inference by combining semantic abstraction with domain-adaptive details. The Visual Prototype Construction module and intra-branch components mirror the text branch to align representations.

Result: Evaluated on six cross-domain benchmark datasets under standard 1/5/10-shot settings, LMP achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive mAP.

Conclusion: Multi-modal prototypes that couple textual guidance with target-domain visual exemplars, learned in a joint, ensemble framework, effectively improve cross-domain few-shot object detection by leveraging both semantic open-vocabulary information and domain-specific visual cues.

Abstract: Cross-Domain Few-Shot Object Detection (CD-FSOD) aims to detect novel classes in unseen target domains given only a few labeled examples. While open-vocabulary detectors built on vision-language models (VLMs) transfer well, they depend almost entirely on text prompts, which encode domain-invariant semantics but miss domain-specific visual information needed for precise localization under few-shot supervision. We propose a dual-branch detector that Learns Multi-modal Prototypes, dubbed LMP, by coupling textual guidance with visual exemplars drawn from the target domain. A Visual Prototype Construction module aggregates class-level prototypes from support RoIs and dynamically generates hard-negative prototypes in query images via jittered boxes, capturing distractors and visually similar backgrounds. In the visual-guided branch, we inject these prototypes into the detection pipeline with components mirrored from the text branch as the starting point for training, while a parallel text-guided branch preserves open-vocabulary semantics. The branches are trained jointly and ensembled at inference by combining semantic abstraction with domain-adaptive details. On six cross-domain benchmark datasets and standard 1/5/10-shot settings, our method achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive mAP.

</details>


### [36] [HeRO: Hierarchical 3D Semantic Representation for Pose-aware Object Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18817)
*Chongyang Xu,Shen Cheng,Haipeng Li,Haoqiang Fan,Ziliang Feng,Shuaicheng Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based policy (HeRO) that fuses geometry-aware features (DINOv2) with global–local semantic correspondences (Stable Diffusion) into hierarchical semantic fields to enable pose-aware manipulation; achieves state-of-the-art on Place Dual Shoes (+12.3%) and an average +6.5% across six pose-aware tasks; code available on GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Purely geometric policies lack part-level semantics necessary for pose-aware manipulation. There is a need to integrate discriminative geometry-sensitive features with coherent semantic correspondences to produce robust manipulation policies.

Method: Dense semantics lifting to fuse DINOv2 geometry-sensitive features with Stable Diffusion correspondences, yielding dense, spatially consistent features. Features are partitioned into a global field and multiple local fields. A hierarchical conditioning module, built with a permutation-invariant network, conditions a diffusion denoiser on global/local fields to produce a coherent pose-aware control policy.

Result: Empirically establishes state-of-the-art performance, with a 12.3 percentage-point improvement on Place Dual Shoes and an average 6.5% gain across six pose-aware tasks.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective coupling of geometry and semantics via hierarchical semantic fields for pose-aware manipulation, enabling finer-grained control without order-sensitive bias; code provided for reproducibility.

Abstract: Imitation learning for robotic manipulation has progressed from 2D image policies to 3D representations that explicitly encode geometry. Yet purely geometric policies often lack explicit part-level semantics, which are critical for pose-aware manipulation (e.g., distinguishing a shoe's toe from heel). In this paper, we present HeRO, a diffusion-based policy that couples geometry and semantics via hierarchical semantic fields. HeRO employs dense semantics lifting to fuse discriminative, geometry-sensitive features from DINOv2 with the smooth, globally coherent correspondences from Stable Diffusion, yielding dense features that are both fine-grained and spatially consistent. These features are processed and partitioned to construct a global field and a set of local fields. A hierarchical conditioning module conditions the generative denoiser on global and local fields using permutation-invariant network architecture, thereby avoiding order-sensitive bias and producing a coherent control policy for pose-aware manipulation. In various tests, HeRO establishes a new state-of-the-art, improving success on Place Dual Shoes by 12.3% and averaging 6.5% gains across six challenging pose-aware tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/Chongyang-99/HeRO.

</details>


### [37] [Robust Self-Supervised Cross-Modal Super-Resolution against Real-World Misaligned Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18822)
*Xiaoyu Dong,Jiahuan Li,Ziteng Cui,Naoto Yokoya*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces RobSelf, a fully self-supervised, online cross-modal SR method for real-world misaligned data using a misalignment-aware translator and a content-aware reference filter; achieves state-of-the-art results and efficiency, and releases the RealMisSR dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle cross-modal super-resolution under real-world misalignment with limited paired data, requiring training-free, online optimization and no pre-alignment, while maintaining high efficiency.

Method: Two core components: (1) a misalignment-aware feature translator that reframes unsupervised cross-modal and cross-resolution alignment as a weakly-supervised translation task yielding an aligned guide feature with redundancy; (2) a content-aware reference filter that performs reference-based discriminative self-enhancement on the source guided by the translated feature to produce high-resolution, high-fidelity SR results. The approach is fully self-supervised, requires no training data or ground-truth supervision, and operates online. RealMisSR dataset introduced.

Result: RobSelf achieves state-of-the-art performance and superior efficiency across diverse cross-modal SR tasks on real-world misaligned data; the method does not require training data or pre-alignment and runs online. A real-world dataset RealMisSR is introduced to advance this research, with code available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of fully self-supervised, online cross-modal SR for real-world misalignments, delivering high-quality SR without supervision and establishing RealMisSR as a benchmark to foster further research.

Abstract: We study cross-modal super-resolution (SR) on real-world misaligned data, where only a limited number of low-resolution (LR) source and high-resolution (HR) guide image pairs with complex spatial misalignments are available. To address this challenge, we propose RobSelf--a fully self-supervised model that is optimized online, requiring no training data, ground-truth supervision, or pre-alignment. RobSelf features two key techniques: a misalignment-aware feature translator and a content-aware reference filter. The translator reformulates unsupervised cross-modal and cross-resolution alignment as a weakly-supervised, misalignment-aware translation subtask, producing an aligned guide feature with inherent redundancy. Guided by this feature, the filter performs reference-based discriminative self-enhancement on the source, enabling SR predictions with high resolution and high fidelity. Across a variety of tasks, we demonstrate that RobSelf achieves state-of-the-art performance and superior efficiency. Additionally, we introduce a real-world dataset, RealMisSR, to advance research on this topic. Dataset and code: https://github.com/palmdong/RobSelf.

</details>


### [38] [Spatial-Temporal State Propagation Autoregressive Model for 4D Object Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18830)
*Liying Yang,Jialun Liu,Jiakui Hu,Chenhao Guan,Haibin Huang,Fangqiu Yi,Chi Zhang,Yanyan Liang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces 4DSTAR, a spatial-temporal state propagation autoregressive model for 4D object generation. It uses a two-stage approach (STAR for AR token prediction with cross-timestep state propagation and a 4D VQ-VAE) to achieve spatial-temporal consistency, delivering competitive results to diffusion methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the spatial-temporal inconsistency in diffusion-based 4D object generation by leveraging information from all previous timesteps during current-step generation.

Method: 1) Spatial-Temporal State Propagation AutoRegressive Model (STAR): group tokens by timestep, propagate long-range spatial-temporal states from previous groups via a dynamic spatial-temporal container that updates effective state features to condition the next token group. 2) 4D VQ-VAE: encode the 4D structure into a discrete latent space and decode tokens into temporally coherent dynamic 3D Gaussians.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows 4DSTAR yields spatial-temporally consistent 4D objects and achieves performance competitive with diffusion-based approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed STAR-based autoregression with a 4D VQ-VAE effectively enforces temporal-spatial consistency in 4D object generation and can match diffusion-model performance.

Abstract: Generating high-quality 4D objects with spatial-temporal consistency is still formidable. Existing diffusion-based methods often struggle with spatial-temporal inconsistency, as they fail to leverage outputs from all previous timesteps to guide the generation at the current timestep. Therefore, we propose a Spatial-Temporal State Propagation AutoRegressive Model (4DSTAR), which generates 4D objects maintaining temporal-spatial consistency. 4DSTAR formulates the generation problem as the prediction of tokens that represent the 4D object. It consists of two key components: (1) The dynamic spatial-temporal state propagation autoregressive model (STAR) is proposed, which achieves spatial-temporal consistent generation. Unlike standard autoregressive models, STAR divides prediction tokens into groups based on timesteps. It models long-term dependencies by propagating spatial-temporal states from previous groups and utilizes these dependencies to guide generation at the next timestep. To this end, a spatial-temporal container is proposed, which dynamically updating the effective spatial-temporal state features from all historical groups, then updated features serve as conditional features to guide the prediction of the next token group. (2) The 4D VQ-VAE is proposed, which implicitly encodes the 4D structure into discrete space and decodes the discrete tokens predicted by STAR into temporally coherent dynamic 3D Gaussians. Experiments demonstrate that 4DSTAR generates spatial-temporal consistent 4D objects, and achieves performance competitive with diffusion models.

</details>


### [39] [IDperturb: Enhancing Variation in Synthetic Face Generation via Angular Perturbation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18831)
*Fadi Boutros,Eduarda Caldeira,Tahar Chettaoui,Naser Damer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A geometry-based identity-embedding perturbation (IDPERTURB) increases intra-class variation in synthetic face data by sampling embeddings within a constrained angular neighborhood, boosting FR generalization without modifying the diffusion model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synthetic face data from identity-conditioned diffusion models often lack intra-class diversity, which hampers training robust FR systems. Privacy concerns further incentivize synthetic data use; a simple method to enhance diversity without altering the generator is desirable.

Method: Perturb identity embeddings on the unit hypersphere within a constrained angular region and use each perturbed embedding as a conditioning vector for a pre-trained diffusion model to synthesize diverse yet identity-coherent face images; no modification to the underlying generative model is required.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows FR models trained on IDPERTURB-generated data achieve improved performance across multiple benchmarks compared with existing synthetic-data approaches.

Conclusion: A simple, model-agnostic perturbation of identity embeddings effectively expands intra-class variation and improves FR generalization without changing the diffusion model, offering a practical enhancement for synthetic data pipelines.

Abstract: Synthetic data has emerged as a practical alternative to authentic face datasets for training face recognition (FR) systems, especially as privacy and legal concerns increasingly restrict the use of real biometric data. Recent advances in identity-conditional diffusion models have enabled the generation of photorealistic and identity-consistent face images. However, many of these models suffer from limited intra-class variation, an essential property for training robust and generalizable FR models. In this work, we propose IDPERTURB, a simple yet effective geometric-driven sampling strategy to enhance diversity in synthetic face generation. IDPERTURB perturbs identity embeddings within a constrained angular region of the unit hyper-sphere, producing a diverse set of embeddings without modifying the underlying generative model. Each perturbed embedding serves as a conditioning vector for a pre-trained diffusion model, enabling the synthesis of visually varied yet identity-coherent face images suitable for training generalizable FR systems. Empirical results demonstrate that training FR on datasets generated using IDPERTURB yields improved performance across multiple FR benchmarks, compared to existing synthetic data generation approaches.

</details>


### [40] [CLAP Convolutional Lightweight Autoencoder for Plant Disease Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18833)
*Asish Bera,Subhajit Roy,Sudiptendu Banerjee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Lightweight autoencoder-based CNN (CLAP) for plant disease classification using separable convolutions with sigmoid gating; achieves competitive accuracy on several plant-disease datasets with ~5M parameters and very fast training/inference, addressing in-field computational constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Plant disease detection in real field conditions is challenged by subtle leaf variations and the need for resource-efficient models. Many CNN approaches are heavy and not suitable for on-device inference in situ.

Method: A Convolutional Lightweight Autoencoder (CLAP) using separable convolutional layers in the encoder/decoder, with a sigmoid gating mechanism to refine encoder discriminability. The decoder enhances features, and their maps are fused for a rich representation before classification. Approximately 5 million parameters.

Result: Evaluated on multiple public plant datasets (Integrated Plant Disease, Groundnut, CCMT; also cassava, tomato, maize, grapes). Reported improved or competitive accuracies, with low computational cost: ~5M parameters, training time ~20 ms, inference time ~1 ms per image.

Conclusion: CLAP delivers a favorable accuracy–computational cost trade-off for plant disease classification, making it suitable for real-time, resource-constrained in-field deployment; further work could include more extensive ablations and cross-dataset robustness.

Abstract: Convolutional neural networks have remarkably progressed the performance of distinguishing plant diseases, severity grading, and nutrition deficiency prediction using leaf images. However, these tasks become more challenging in a realistic in-situ field condition. Often, a traditional machine learning model may fail to capture and interpret discriminative characteristics of plant health, growth and diseases due to subtle variations within leaf subcategories. A few deep learning methods have used additional preprocessing stages or network modules to address the problem, whereas several other methods have utilized pre-trained backbone CNNs, most of which are computationally intensive. Therefore, to address the challenge, we propose a lightweight autoencoder using separable convolutional layers in its encoder decoder blocks. A sigmoid gating is applied for refining the prowess of the encoders feature discriminability, which is improved further by the decoder. Finally, the feature maps of the encoder decoder are combined for rich feature representation before classification. The proposed Convolutional Lightweight Autoencoder for Plant disease classification, called CLAP, has been experimented on three public plant datasets consisting of cassava, tomato, maize, groundnut, grapes, etc. for determining plant health conditions. The CLAP has attained improved or competitive accuracies on the Integrated Plant Disease, Groundnut, and CCMT datasets balancing a tradeoff between the performance, and little computational cost requiring 5 million parameters. The training time is 20 milliseconds and inference time is 1 ms per image.

</details>


### [41] [Detecting AI-Generated Forgeries via Iterative Manifold Deviation Amplification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18842)
*Jiangling Zhang,Shuxuan Gao,Bofan Liu,Siqiang Feng,Jirui Huang,Yaxiong Chen,Ziyu Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IFA-Net uses a frozen MAE realness prior with a two-stage loop (DSSN + TAPI) to localize forged regions, achieving state-of-the-art localization on diffusion-based inpainting benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need robust, pixel-level localization of realistic forgeries and generalization to unseen manipulation techniques.

Method: Two-stage closed-loop: (1) coarse localization via a Dual-Stream Segmentation Network that fuses the original image with MAE reconstruction residuals; (2) Task-Adaptive Prior Injection converts coarse prediction into prompts to guide the MAE decoder, amplifying reconstruction errors in suspect regions.

Result: Average improvements of 6.5% in IoU and 8.1% in F1-score over the second-best method; strong generalization to traditional manipulation types; validated on four diffusion-based inpainting benchmarks.

Conclusion: Reframing forgery detection as deviations from the natural image manifold with a universal realness prior from MAE yields improved localization and generalization.

Abstract: The proliferation of highly realistic AI-generated images poses critical challenges for digital forensics, demanding precise pixel-level localization of manipulated regions. Existing methods predominantly learn discriminative patterns of specific forgeries and often struggle with novel manipulations as editing techniques continue to evolve. We propose the Iterative Forgery Amplifier Network (IFA-Net), which shifts from learning "what is fake" to modeling "what is real". Grounded in the principle that all manipulations deviate from the natural image manifold, IFA-Net leverages a frozen Masked Autoencoder (MAE) pretrained on real images as a universal realness prior. Our framework operates through a two-stage closed-loop process: an initial Dual-Stream Segmentation Network (DSSN) fuses the original image with MAE reconstruction residuals for coarse localization, followed by a Task-Adaptive Prior Injection (TAPI) module that converts this coarse prediction into guiding prompts to steer the MAE decoder and amplify reconstruction failures in suspicious regions for precise refinement. Extensive experiments on four diffusion-based inpainting benchmarks show that IFA-Net achieves an average improvement of 6.5% in IoU and 8.1% in F1-score over the second-best method, while demonstrating strong generalization to traditional manipulation types.

</details>


### [42] [Echoes of Ownership: Adversarial-Guided Dual Injection for Copyright Protection in MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18845)
*Chengwei Xia,Fan Ma,Ruijie Quan,Yunqiu Xu,Kun Zhan,Yi Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a dual-injection framework to embed verifiable ownership triggers into MLLMs via a learnable trigger image that elicits ownership-related text only from fine-tuned derivatives, using (1) textual consistency with an auxiliary LLM and target text, and (2) CLIP-based semantic alignment; an adversarial auxiliary model resists triggering to improve robustness; validated across fine-tuning and domain-shift scenarios for model lineage tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With widespread MLLM deployment, provenance and IP protection are critical. The work addresses the need for robust, verifiable ownership markers in models that can be fine-tuned or domain-adapted, enabling publishers to track lineage and prove ownership.

Method: Treats the trigger image as a learnable tensor and optimizes it adversarially with dual injections: (i) enforce textual consistency between an auxiliary MLLM's output and a predefined ownership-target text, backpropagating this loss into the image; (ii) minimize the distance between CLIP features of the image and the target text at the semantic level. An additional stage trains an auxiliary model derived from the original model to resist producing the ownership text, enhancing robustness against derivatives. Evaluation spans various fine-tuning and domain-shift conditions.

Result: The dual-injection approach effectively embeds ownership triggers that can track model lineage across fine-tuning and domain shifts, while remaining inert in non-derivative models, according to extensive experiments.

Conclusion: The study offers a practical framework for copyright-trigger embedding in MLLMs to aid IP protection and provenance tracking. It highlights robustness considerations and suggests potential extensions for defense and standardization of ownership verification in large multilingual multimodal models.

Abstract: With the rapid deployment and widespread adoption of multimodal large language models (MLLMs), disputes regarding model version attribution and ownership have become increasingly frequent, raising significant concerns about intellectual property protection. In this paper, we propose a framework for generating copyright triggers for MLLMs, enabling model publishers to embed verifiable ownership information into the model. The goal is to construct trigger images that elicit ownership-related textual responses exclusively in fine-tuned derivatives of the original model, while remaining inert in other non-derivative models. Our method constructs a tracking trigger image by treating the image as a learnable tensor, performing adversarial optimization with dual-injection of ownership-relevant semantic information. The first injection is achieved by enforcing textual consistency between the output of an auxiliary MLLM and a predefined ownership-relevant target text; the consistency loss is backpropagated to inject this ownership-related information into the image. The second injection is performed at the semantic-level by minimizing the distance between the CLIP features of the image and those of the target text. Furthermore, we introduce an additional adversarial training stage involving the auxiliary model derived from the original model itself. This auxiliary model is specifically trained to resist generating ownership-relevant target text, thereby enhancing robustness in heavily fine-tuned derivative models. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our dual-injection approach in tracking model lineage under various fine-tuning and domain-shift scenarios.

</details>


### [43] [DUET-VLM: Dual stage Unified Efficient Token reduction for VLM Training and Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18846)
*Aditya Kumar Singh,Hitesh Kandala,Pratik Prabhanjan Brahma,Zicheng Liu,Emad Barsoum*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DUET-VLM introduces a dual compression framework for vision-language models: (a) compress visual encoder outputs into information-preserving tokens, and (b) apply layer-wise, text-guided token dropping in the language backbone to prune less informative tokens. End-to-end training yields substantial token reductions with minimal accuracy loss across image/video tasks, achieving state-of-the-art performance on several benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computational cost of vision-language models without sacrificing accuracy by aggressively compressing visual tokens and selectively dropping tokens in the text backbone, addressing redundancy in visual representations and inefficiencies in multimodal fusion.

Method: A two-stage, plug-and-play framework: (a) vision-only redundancy-aware compression of the vision encoder output into compact, information-preserving tokens; (b) layer-wise, salient text-guided dropping of visual tokens within the language backbone to progressively prune less informative tokens. The approach is trained end-to-end (DUET-VLM).

Result: On LLaVA-1.5-7B: maintains >99% of baseline accuracy with 67% token reduction; >97% at 89% reduction; with end-to-end training, achieves 99.7% accuracy at 67% and 97.6% at 89%. Outperforms prior SOTA visual token reduction methods across multiple benchmarks. With Video-LLaVA-7B, surpasses baseline accuracy with 53.1% token reduction and attains 97.6% at 93.4% reduction.

Conclusion: End-to-end training with DUET-VLM enables robust adaptation to reduced visual (image/video) input while preserving semantics under the same compute budget; the framework is versatile and plug-and-play, delivering compact yet semantically rich representations. Code available at the project repo.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable multimodal understanding and reasoning capabilities, yet remain computationally expensive due to dense visual tokenization. Existing efficiency approaches either merge redundant visual tokens or drop them progressively in language backbone, often trading accuracy for speed. In this work, we propose DUET-VLM, a versatile plug-and-play dual compression framework that consists of (a) vision-only redundancy aware compression of vision encoder's output into information-preserving tokens, followed by (b) layer-wise, salient text-guided dropping of visual tokens within the language backbone to progressively prune less informative tokens. This coordinated token management enables aggressive compression while retaining critical semantics. On LLaVA-1.5-7B, our approach maintains over 99% of baseline accuracy with 67% fewer tokens, and still retains >97% even at 89% reduction. With this dual-stage compression during training, it achieves 99.7% accuracy at 67% and 97.6% at 89%, surpassing prior SoTA visual token reduction methods across multiple benchmarks. When integrated into Video-LLaVA-7B, it even surpasses the baseline -- achieving >100% accuracy with a substantial 53.1% token reduction and retaining 97.6% accuracy under an extreme 93.4% setting. These results highlight end-to-end training with DUET-VLM, enabling robust adaptation to reduced visual (image/video) input without sacrificing accuracy, producing compact yet semantically rich representations within the same computational budget. Our code is available at https://github.com/AMD-AGI/DUET-VLM.

</details>


### [44] [Open-Vocabulary Domain Generalization in Urban-Scene Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18853)
*Dong Zhao,Qi Zang,Nan Pu,Wenjing Li,Nicu Sebe,Zhun Zhong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This work introduces Open-Vocabulary Domain Generalization in Semantic Segmentation (OVDG-SS) for autonomous driving, bridging domain generalization and open-set segmentation; it also proposes S2-Corr to refine text-image correlations in Vision-Language Models under domain shifts; they present a benchmark for synthetic-to-real and real-to-real generalization and show superior cross-domain performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-world semantic segmentation is required for urban driving, where both unseen domains (domain shifts) and unseen categories (open vocabulary) challenge robustness. OV-SS alone struggles under distribution shifts; there is a need to jointly address domain generalization and open-vocabulary recognition.

Method: Define the OV-DDG-SS setting (Open-Vocabulary Domain Generalization in Semantic Segmentation) and construct the first autonomous-driving benchmark covering synthetic-to-real and real-to-real generalization across unseen domains and categories. Propose S2-Corr, a state-space-driven mechanism to refine text-image correlations in pre-trained Vision-Language Models to become more robust to domain-induced distortions.

Result: Experiments on the proposed benchmark show that S2-Corr yields superior cross-domain performance and greater efficiency compared with existing OV-SS approaches.

Conclusion: Open-vocabulary domain generalization in semantic segmentation is feasible with correlation-refinement techniques like S2-Corr, enabling robust segmentation across unseen domains and categories in autonomous driving; the benchmark provides a valuable evaluation platform for this joint problem.

Abstract: Domain Generalization in Semantic Segmentation (DG-SS) aims to enable segmentation models to perform robustly in unseen environments. However, conventional DG-SS methods are restricted to a fixed set of known categories, limiting their applicability in open-world scenarios. Recent progress in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) has advanced Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation (OV-SS) by enabling models to recognize a broader range of concepts. Yet, these models remain sensitive to domain shifts and struggle to maintain robustness when deployed in unseen environments, a challenge that is particularly severe in urban-driving scenarios. To bridge this gap, we introduce Open-Vocabulary Domain Generalization in Semantic Segmentation (OVDG-SS), a new setting that jointly addresses unseen domains and unseen categories. We introduce the first benchmark for OVDG-SS in autonomous driving, addressing a previously unexplored problem and covering both synthetic-to-real and real-to-real generalization across diverse unseen domains and unseen categories. In OVDG-SS, we observe that domain shifts often distort text-image correlations in pre-trained VLMs, which hinders the performance of OV-SS models. To tackle this challenge, we propose S2-Corr, a state-space-driven text-image correlation refinement mechanism that mitigates domain-induced distortions and produces more consistent text-image correlations under distribution changes. Extensive experiments on our constructed benchmark demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior cross-domain performance and efficiency compared to existing OV-SS approaches.

</details>


### [45] [Joint Post-Training Quantization of Vision Transformers with Learned Prompt-Guided Data Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18861)
*Shile Li,Markus Karmann,Onay Urfalioglu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: End-to-end joint PTQ of Vision Transformers on ImageNet with data-free calibration via Stable Diffusion enables ultra-low-bit quantization (W4A4, W3A3; also W1.58A8 for ViT/DeiT/Swin-T), achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and enabling efficient edge deployment; one-hour single-GPU training for ViT-small.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limitations of post-training or block-wise PTQ that rely on labeled data or limited scalability. The goal is to quantize ViTs end-to-end across all layers and inter-block dependencies without data, enabling scalable, low-bit deployment.

Method: Jointly optimize quantization across all layers and inter-block connections (end-to-end PTQ). Introduce a data-free calibration strategy that synthesizes diverse samples via Stable Diffusion Turbo guided by learned multi-mode prompts; promote diversity in prompt embeddings and generated image features to match real-data calibration.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art W4A4 and W3A3 accuracies on ImageNet. Claims first PTQ results maintaining strong accuracy on ViT, DeiT, and Swin-T under extremely low-bit settings (W1.58A8). Demonstrates one-hour end-to-end quantization on ViT-small on a single GPU and potential for edge deployment.

Conclusion: Shows practical feasibility of end-to-end PTQ for ViTs with data-free calibration, delivering strong performance at ultra-low bitrates and enabling efficient edge inference; supports broader applicability to ViT-family models.

Abstract: We present a framework for end-to-end joint quantization of Vision Transformers trained on ImageNet for the purpose of image classification. Unlike prior post-training or block-wise reconstruction methods, we jointly optimize over the entire set of all layers and inter-block dependencies without any labeled data, scaling effectively with the number of samples and completing in just one hour on a single GPU for ViT-small. We achieve state-of-the-art W4A4 and W3A3 accuracies on ImageNet and, to the best of our knowledge, the first PTQ results that maintain strong accuracy on ViT, DeiT, and Swin-T models under extremely low-bit settings (W1.58A8), demonstrating the potential for efficient edge deployment. Furthermore, we introduce a data-free calibration strategy that synthesizes diverse, label-free samples using Stable Diffusion Turbo guided by learned multi-mode prompts. By encouraging diversity in both the learned prompt embeddings and the generated image features, our data-free approach achieves performance on par with real-data ImageNet calibration and surpasses simple text-prompt baselines such as "a <adjective> photo of <adjective> <cls>".

</details>


### [46] [Enhancing 3D LiDAR Segmentation by Shaping Dense and Accurate 2D Semantic Predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18869)
*Xiaoyu Dong,Tiankui Xian,Wanshui Gan,Naoto Yokoya*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-modal 2D-3D LiDAR semantic segmentation framework (MM2D3D) densifies intermediate 2D predictions via cross-modal guidance from camera images and dynamic pseudo supervision, yielding improved 3D segmentation accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Projected LiDAR maps and sparse labels induce sparsity and inaccuracies that limit 3D segmentation. Incorporating dense camera-derived semantics can regularize and densify 2D predictions, thus improving 3D results.

Method: MM2D3D uses cross-modal guided filtering to constrain intermediate 2D predictions with dense semantic relations from camera images, and dynamic cross pseudo supervision to encourage 2D predictions to emulate the dense distribution of camera-derived semantics. The approach fuses LiDAR and image modalities to generate denser, more accurate 2D predictions, which are then projected to 3D.

Result: Experiments show that the proposed techniques produce dense, accurate intermediate 2D predictions and yield higher 3D segmentation accuracy, outperforming prior methods in both 2D and 3D spaces.

Conclusion: Cross-modal guidance and dynamic pseudo supervision effectively mitigate sparsity in multi-modal 2D-3D semantic segmentation, achieving state-of-the-art performance in the tested scenarios.

Abstract: Semantic segmentation of 3D LiDAR point clouds is important in urban remote sensing for understanding real-world street environments. This task, by projecting LiDAR point clouds and 3D semantic labels as sparse maps, can be reformulated as a 2D problem. However, the intrinsic sparsity of the projected LiDAR and label maps can result in sparse and inaccurate intermediate 2D semantic predictions, which in return limits the final 3D accuracy. To address this issue, we enhance this task by shaping dense and accurate 2D predictions. Specifically, we develop a multi-modal segmentation model, MM2D3D. By leveraging camera images as auxiliary data, we introduce cross-modal guided filtering to overcome label map sparsity by constraining intermediate 2D semantic predictions with dense semantic relations derived from the camera images; and we introduce dynamic cross pseudo supervision to overcome LiDAR map sparsity by encouraging the 2D predictions to emulate the dense distribution of the semantic predictions from the camera images. Experiments show that our techniques enable our model to achieve intermediate 2D semantic predictions with dense distribution and higher accuracy, which effectively enhances the final 3D accuracy. Comparisons with previous methods demonstrate our superior performance in both 2D and 3D spaces.

</details>


### [47] [BiMotion: B-spline Motion for Text-guided Dynamic 3D Character Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18873)
*Miaowei Wang,Qingxuan Yan,Zhi Cao,Yayuan Li,Oisin Mac Aodha,Jason J. Corso,Amir Vaxman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BiMotion: a text-guided 3D motion generation framework using Laplacian-regularized B-spline motion representations to compress variable-length sequences into fixed control points, with normal-fusion and correspondence-aware/local-rigidity losses; trained on BIMO dataset; achieves expressive, high-quality, prompt-aligned motions and faster generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed-length temporally discrete representations limit expressive motion under rich textual guidance; continuous differentiable motion representations can capture semantics with fewer parameters without changing the base generative model.

Method: Represent motion with continuous B-spline curves; closed-form Laplacian-regularized B-spline solver compresses variable-length sequences into a fixed number of control points; normal-fusion strategy for input shape adherence; correspondence-aware and local-rigidity losses for motion restoration quality; train on new BIMO dataset; feed-forward BiMotion.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art in expressiveness, quality, and prompt alignment; faster generation; dataset and project page provided.

Conclusion: Continuous B-spline motion representation with Laplacian regularization and dedicated losses enables efficient, high-quality, text-guided 3D motion generation; BiMotion is a strong advance in this area.

Abstract: Text-guided dynamic 3D character generation has advanced rapidly, yet producing high-quality motion that faithfully reflects rich textual descriptions remains challenging. Existing methods tend to generate limited sub-actions or incoherent motion due to fixed-length temporal inputs and discrete frame-wise representations that fail to capture rich motion semantics. We address these limitations by representing motion with continuous differentiable B-spline curves, enabling more effective motion generation without modifying the capabilities of the underlying generative model. Specifically, our closed-form, Laplacian-regularized B-spline solver efficiently compresses variable-length motion sequences into compact representations with a fixed number of control points. Further, we introduce a normal-fusion strategy for input shape adherence along with correspondence-aware and local-rigidity losses for motion-restoration quality. To train our model, we collate BIMO, a new dataset containing diverse variable-length 3D motion sequences with rich, high-quality text annotations. Extensive evaluations show that our feed-forward framework BiMotion generates more expressive, higher-quality, and better prompt-aligned motions than existing state-of-the-art methods, while also achieving faster generation. Our project page is at: https://wangmiaowei.github.io/BiMotion.github.io/.

</details>


### [48] [Structure-Level Disentangled Diffusion for Few-Shot Chinese Font Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18874)
*Jie Li,Suorong Yang,Jian Zhao,Furao Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SLD-Font introduces structure-level disentanglement for few-shot Chinese font generation, achieving higher style fidelity with comparable content accuracy by using separate content and style channels, cross-attention with CLIP features, a pixel-space background removal module, and parameter-efficient fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods struggle with content distortion due to only feature-level disentanglement, which risks re-entangling content and style features. In few-shot font generation, robust content rendering alongside accurate style transfer is crucial, demanding a stronger separation at the structure level.

Method: Two-channel input: content templates from SimSun-style images and noisy latent features; style features extracted from target images via CLIP and integrated through cross-attention. A Background Noise Removal module operates in pixel space to clean complex stroke regions. The model is supplemented by a theoretical validation of disentanglement efficacy and a parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategy that updates only style-related modules.

Result: SLD-Font achieves significantly higher style fidelity while maintaining comparable content accuracy to state-of-the-art methods. To evaluate content quality, Grey and OCR metrics are introduced. The approach demonstrates effective structure-level disentanglement and robust style transfer in few-shot Chinese font generation.

Conclusion: By enforcing structure-level disentanglement and incorporating cross-attention-driven style integration alongside a pixel-space background cleanup, SLD-Font delivers superior style fidelity without sacrificing content accuracy, aided by efficient fine-tuning and new content-quality metrics.

Abstract: Few-shot Chinese font generation aims to synthesize new characters in a target style using only a handful of reference images. Achieving accurate content rendering and faithful style transfer requires effective disentanglement between content and style. However, existing approaches achieve only feature-level disentanglement, allowing the generator to re-entangle these features, leading to content distortion and degraded style fidelity. We propose the Structure-Level Disentangled Diffusion Model (SLD-Font), which receives content and style information from two separate channels. SimSun-style images are used as content templates and concatenated with noisy latent features as the input. Style features extracted by a CLIP model from target-style images are integrated via cross-attention. Additionally, we train a Background Noise Removal module in the pixel space to remove background noise in complex stroke regions. Based on theoretical validation of disentanglement effectiveness, we introduce a parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategy that updates only the style-related modules. This allows the model to better adapt to new styles while avoiding overfitting to the reference images' content. We further introduce the Grey and OCR metrics to evaluate the content quality of generated characters. Experimental results show that SLD-Font achieves significantly higher style fidelity while maintaining comparable content accuracy to existing state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [49] [FOCA: Frequency-Oriented Cross-Domain Forgery Detection, Localization and Explanation via Multi-Modal Large Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18880)
*Zhou Liu,Tonghua Su,Hongshi Zhang,Fuxiang Yang,Donglin Di,Yang Song,Lei Fan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FOCA is a multimodal LLM-based framework for image forgery detection and localization that fuses RGB and frequency-domain features via cross-attention to improve accuracy and interpretability; it is complemented by FSE-Set, a large diverse dataset with pixel-level masks and dual-domain annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current IFDL methods over-rely on semantic content and lack interpretability of low-level tampering traces; need for cross-domain cues and human-interpretable explanations.

Method: Employ cross-attention fusion to integrate spatial RGB features and frequency-domain cues; utilize a large language model to provide explicit cross-domain explanations; introduce FSE-Set dataset with authentic/tampered images, pixel masks, and dual-domain annotations.

Result: FOCA achieves superior detection and localization performance and enhanced interpretability across spatial and frequency domains compared to state-of-the-art methods; FSE-Set provides diverse, richly annotated data.

Conclusion: FOCA advances robust media verification by combining discriminative cross-domain features with interpretable explanations; the FSE-Set dataset supports further research and benchmarking in dual-domain forgery detection and localization.

Abstract: Advances in image tampering techniques, particularly generative models, pose significant challenges to media verification, digital forensics, and public trust. Existing image forgery detection and localization (IFDL) methods suffer from two key limitations: over-reliance on semantic content while neglecting textural cues, and limited interpretability of subtle low-level tampering traces. To address these issues, we propose FOCA, a multimodal large language model-based framework that integrates discriminative features from both the RGB spatial and frequency domains via a cross-attention fusion module. This design enables accurate forgery detection and localization while providing explicit, human-interpretable cross-domain explanations. We further introduce FSE-Set, a large-scale dataset with diverse authentic and tampered images, pixel-level masks, and dual-domain annotations. Extensive experiments show that FOCA outperforms state-of-the-art methods in detection performance and interpretability across both spatial and frequency domains.

</details>


### [50] [SceneTok: A Compressed, Diffusable Token Space for 3D Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18882)
*Mohammad Asim,Christopher Wewer,Jan Eric Lenssen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SceneTok introduces a permutation-invariant, unstructured token-based representation for 3D scenes, enabling highly compressed scene encoding, diffusable tokens decoupled from spatial grids, and fast, high-quality novel-view generation via a lightweight rectified-flow decoder.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the inefficiency and rigidity of traditional 3D representations (explicit grids, meshes, or view-aligned fields) by proposing a compact, inference-friendly token-based scene representation that permits flexible rendering and generation.

Method: A multi-view tokenizer predicts a small set of permutation-invariant scene tokens from many context views; the tokens are disentangled from a spatial grid and decoded into novel views using a lightweight rectified flow decoder.

Result: Compression is 1–3 orders of magnitude stronger than competing representations, while achieving state-of-the-art reconstruction quality; the model renders from novel trajectories (including off-trajectory paths) with uncertainty handling; scene generation is fast (~5 seconds) with a superior quality–speed trade-off compared to prior methods.

Conclusion: SceneTok presents a compact, unstructured, permutation-invariant tokenization of scenes that enables efficient, robust rendering and fast generation, challenging grid-based and field-based 3D representations.

Abstract: We present SceneTok, a novel tokenizer for encoding view sets of scenes into a compressed and diffusable set of unstructured tokens. Existing approaches for 3D scene representation and generation commonly use 3D data structures or view-aligned fields. In contrast, we introduce the first method that encodes scene information into a small set of permutation-invariant tokens that is disentangled from the spatial grid. The scene tokens are predicted by a multi-view tokenizer given many context views and rendered into novel views by employing a light-weight rectified flow decoder. We show that the compression is 1-3 orders of magnitude stronger than for other representations while still reaching state-of-the-art reconstruction quality. Further, our representation can be rendered from novel trajectories, including ones deviating from the input trajectory, and we show that the decoder gracefully handles uncertainty. Finally, the highly-compressed set of unstructured latent scene tokens enables simple and efficient scene generation in 5 seconds, achieving a much better quality-speed trade-off than previous paradigms.

</details>


### [51] [PhysConvex: Physics-Informed 3D Dynamic Convex Radiance Fields for Reconstruction and Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18886)
*Dan Wang,Xinrui Cui,Serge Belongie,Ravi Ramamoorthi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A physics-informed dynamic convex radiance field (PhysConvex) that unifies rendering and physical simulation via boundary-driven convex representations and neural skinning-based deformation, enabling accurate geometry, appearance, and material dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural rendering methods (NeRF/3DGS) excel at appearance but struggle with complex deformation and dynamics; there is a need to jointly capture geometry, appearance, and physics in dynamic scenes.

Method: Introduce a boundary-driven dynamic convex representation for deformable radiance fields; model deformation using vertex/surface dynamics; develop a reduced-order convex simulation that advects dynamic convex fields with neural skinning eigenmodes as deformation bases and time-varying reduced DOFs under Newtonian dynamics; convex dynamics provide gap-free volumetric coverage and efficiency.

Result: Empirical results show high-fidelity reconstruction of geometry, appearance, and physical properties from videos, outperforming existing methods.

Conclusion: PhysConvex offers a unified framework that couples visual rendering with physics-based simulation, delivering accurate dynamic reconstructions with a convex, topology-adaptive representation and reduced-order, physics-informed dynamics.

Abstract: Reconstructing and simulating dynamic 3D scenes with both visual realism and physical consistency remains a fundamental challenge. Existing neural representations, such as NeRFs and 3DGS, excel in appearance reconstruction but struggle to capture complex material deformation and dynamics. We propose PhysConvex, a Physics-informed 3D Dynamic Convex Radiance Field that unifies visual rendering and physical simulation. PhysConvex represents deformable radiance fields using physically grounded convex primitives governed by continuum mechanics. We introduce a boundary-driven dynamic convex representation that models deformation through vertex and surface dynamics, capturing spatially adaptive, non-uniform deformation, and evolving boundaries. To efficiently simulate complex geometries and heterogeneous materials, we further develop a reduced-order convex simulation that advects dynamic convex fields using neural skinning eigenmodes as shape- and material-aware deformation bases with time-varying reduced DOFs under Newtonian dynamics. Convex dynamics also offers compact, gap-free volumetric coverage, enhancing both geometric efficiency and simulation fidelity. Experiments demonstrate that PhysConvex achieves high-fidelity reconstruction of geometry, appearance, and physical properties from videos, outperforming existing methods.

</details>


### [52] [SafeDrive: Fine-Grained Safety Reasoning for End-to-End Driving in a Sparse World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18887)
*Jungho Kim,Jiyong Oh,Seunghoon Yu,Hongjae Shin,Donghyuk Kwak,Jun Won Choi*

Main category: cs.CV
