Conclusion: LAVIDA demonstrates the effectiveness of pseudo-anomaly generation and multimodal semantic integration for zero-shot VAD, with efficient token compression enabling practical deployment; code is released.

Abstract: The collection and detection of video anomaly data has long been a challenging problem due to its rare occurrence and spatio-temporal scarcity. Existing video anomaly detection (VAD) methods under perform in open-world scenarios. Key contributing factors include limited dataset diversity, and inadequate understanding of context-dependent anomalous semantics. To address these issues, i) we propose LAVIDA, an end-to-end zero-shot video anomaly detection framework. ii) LAVIDA employs an Anomaly Exposure Sampler that transforms segmented objects into pseudo-anomalies to enhance model adaptability to unseen anomaly categories. It further integrates a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) to bolster semantic comprehension capabilities. Additionally, iii) we design a token compression approach based on reverse attention to handle the spatio-temporal scarcity of anomalous patterns and decrease computational cost. The training process is conducted solely on pseudo anomalies without any VAD data. Evaluations across four benchmark VAD datasets demonstrate that LAVIDA achieves SOTA performance in both frame-level and pixel-level anomaly detection under the zero-shot setting. Our code is available in https://github.com/VitaminCreed/LAVIDA.

</details>


### [106] [RegionRoute: Regional Style Transfer with Diffusion Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19254)
*Bowen Chen,Jake Zuena,Alan C. Bovik,Divya Kothandaraman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes attention-guided diffusion for localized style transfer without masks; uses Focus and Cover losses to align style-token attention with object masks; adds LoRA-MoE for multi-style adaptation; introduces Regional Style Editing Score; achieves mask-free single-object stylization with improved locality and coherence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models treat style globally with no explicit spatial grounding, hindering localized stylization and causing boundary artifacts when naively applying style to specific regions.

Method: Train with attention supervision aligning style-token attention to object masks. Focus loss (KL divergence) aligns attention distributions with masks; Cover loss (binary cross-entropy) encourages dense region coverage. Employ a modular LoRA-MoE design for scalable multi-style adaptation. Evaluate using Regional Style Editing Score that combines CLIP-based regional style similarity with identity-preserving metrics (masked LPIPS, pixel-level consistency in unedited areas).

Result: Empirical results show mask-free, single-object style transfer at inference, with regionally accurate and visually coherent outputs that outperform existing diffusion-based editing approaches.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective localized diffusion editing through explicit attention grounding and scalable multi-style support; introduces a practicalRegional Style Editing Score for evaluation of regional stylization.

Abstract: Precise spatial control in diffusion-based style transfer remains challenging. This challenge arises because diffusion models treat style as a global feature and lack explicit spatial grounding of style representations, making it difficult to restrict style application to specific objects or regions. To our knowledge, existing diffusion models are unable to perform true localized style transfer, typically relying on handcrafted masks or multi-stage post-processing that introduce boundary artifacts and limit generalization. To address this, we propose an attention-supervised diffusion framework that explicitly teaches the model where to apply a given style by aligning the attention scores of style tokens with object masks during training. Two complementary objectives, a Focus loss based on KL divergence and a Cover loss using binary cross-entropy, jointly encourage accurate localization and dense coverage. A modular LoRA-MoE design further enables efficient and scalable multi-style adaptation. To evaluate localized stylization, we introduce the Regional Style Editing Score, which measures Regional Style Matching through CLIP-based similarity within the target region and Identity Preservation via masked LPIPS and pixel-level consistency on unedited areas. Experiments show that our method achieves mask-free, single-object style transfer at inference, producing regionally accurate and visually coherent results that outperform existing diffusion-based editing approaches.

</details>


### [107] [DD-CAM: Minimal Sufficient Explanations for Vision Models Using Delta Debugging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19274)
*Krishna Khadka,Yu Lei,Raghu N. Kacker,D. Richard Kuhn*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes DD-CAM, a gradient-free method to yield 1-minimal, prediction-preserving explanations by finding the smallest subset of units whose joint activation maintains the model's prediction; uses delta debugging adapted to unit interactions; yields sparse, faithful saliency maps with improved localization over CAM-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CAM-like explanations are often cluttered due to aggregating many units; need minimal, decision-preserving explanations that localize essential features; apply a gradient-free approach suitable for non-differentiable or discrete choices.

Method: Identify a 1-minimal subset of neural units whose joint activation preserves the prediction. Use delta debugging to iteratively test subsets. For models with non-interacting units, test single units; for models with interactions, test unit combinations. Generate saliency maps focusing on the minimal subset.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows DD-CAM yields more faithful explanations and higher localization accuracy than state-of-the-art CAM-based methods.

Conclusion: DD-CAM provides concise, decision-preserving explanations via a minimal subset framework; potential for broader applicability and more interpretable vision models.

Abstract: We introduce a gradient-free framework for identifying minimal, sufficient, and decision-preserving explanations in vision models by isolating the smallest subset of representational units whose joint activation preserves predictions. Unlike existing approaches that aggregate all units, often leading to cluttered saliency maps, our approach, DD-CAM, identifies a 1-minimal subset whose joint activation suffices to preserve the prediction (i.e., removing any unit from the subset alters the prediction). To efficiently isolate minimal sufficient subsets, we adapt delta debugging, a systematic reduction strategy from software debugging, and configure its search strategy based on unit interactions in the classifier head: testing individual units for models with non-interacting units and testing unit combinations for models in which unit interactions exist. We then generate minimal, prediction-preserving saliency maps that highlight only the most essential features. Our experimental evaluation demonstrates that our approach can produce more faithful explanations and achieve higher localization accuracy than the state-of-the-art CAM-based approaches.

</details>


### [108] [A Two-Stage Detection-Tracking Framework for Stable Apple Quality Inspection in Dense Conveyor-Belt Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19278)
*Keonvin Park,Aditya Pal,Jin Hong Mok*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-stage detection-tracking pipeline for stable apple quality inspection on a conveyor belt, using YOLOv8 for localization, ByteTrack for tracking, and a ResNet18 classifier on apple crops, with track- and video-level metrics to improve temporal stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial fruit inspection requires reliable temporal consistency in video streams; frame-level inference often yields prediction oscillations under dense object interactions and motion, hindering practical deployment.

Method: Stage 1: detect apples with orchard-trained YOLOv8. Stage 2: track apples over frames with ByteTrack to maintain identities. A ResNet18 classifier, fine-tuned on healthy vs defective fruit, classifies each tracked apple crop. Introduce track-level aggregation to stabilize predictions across frames. Define video-level metrics (track-level defect ratio, temporal consistency) to assess robustness under realistic processing.

Result: Compared to frame-wise inference, the approach achieves improved temporal stability; tracking reduces oscillations and enhances robustness.

Conclusion: Integrating tracking into the detection-classification pipeline is essential for practical automated fruit grading in conveyor environments; proposed metrics capture temporal robustness and could guide deployment.

Abstract: Industrial fruit inspection systems must operate reliably under dense multi-object interactions and continuous motion, yet most existing works evaluate detection or classification at the image level without ensuring temporal stability in video streams. We present a two-stage detection-tracking framework for stable multi-apple quality inspection in conveyor-belt environments. An orchard-trained YOLOv8 model performs apple localization, followed by ByteTrack multi-object tracking to maintain persistent identities. A ResNet18 defect classifier, fine-tuned on a healthy-defective fruit dataset, is applied to cropped apple regions. Track-level aggregation is introduced to enforce temporal consistency and reduce prediction oscillation across frames. We define video-level industrial metrics such as track-level defect ratio and temporal consistency to evaluate system robustness under realistic processing conditions. Results demonstrate improved stability compared to frame-wise inference, suggesting that integrating tracking is essential for practical automated fruit grading systems.

</details>


### [109] [MRI Contrast Enhancement Kinetics World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19285)
*Jindi Kong,Yuting He,Cong Xia,Rongjun Ge,Shuo Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes MRI Contrast Enhancement Kinetics World model (MRI CEKWorld) with SpatioTemporal Consistency Learning (STCL) to model MRI contrast enhancement kinetics under sparse temporal data. Introduces Latent Alignment Learning (LAL) to impose patient-specific spatial templates and Latent Difference Learning (LDL) to interpolate unobserved intervals and enforce smooth latent dynamics. Demonstrates improved realism of content and kinetics on two datasets; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MRI contrast acquisition yields low information yield due to fixed sparse acquisition sequences; training world models is hampered by limited temporal data, causing overfitting to static features and discontinuous kinetics. A continuous, physiologically plausible kinetics model is desirable to improve efficiency.

Method: Introduce MRI CEKWorld with STCL. For content fidelity under missing time data, LAL constructs a patient-specific template to constrain content alignment with spatial structures. For temporal continuity, LDL extends unobserved intervals via interpolation and enforces smooth variations in the latent space across interpolated sequences, enabling continuous kinetics without full temporal supervision.

Result: Extensive experiments on two datasets show improved realism of contents and kinetics. Code will be released at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: STCL-enabled MRI CEKWorld with LAL and LDL yields more realistic contrast content and continuous kinetics under sparse temporal sampling, offering a viable pathway toward more efficient MRI contrast acquisitions.

Abstract: Clinical MRI contrast acquisition suffers from inefficient information yield, which presents as a mismatch between the risky and costly acquisition protocol and the fixed and sparse acquisition sequence. Applying world models to simulate the contrast enhancement kinetics in the human body enables continuous contrast-free dynamics. However, the low temporal resolution in MRI acquisition restricts the training of world models, leading to a sparsely sampled dataset. Directly training a generative model to capture the kinetics leads to two limitations: (a) Due to the absence of data on missing time, the model tends to overfit to irrelevant features, leading to content distortion. (b) Due to the lack of continuous temporal supervision, the model fails to learn the continuous kinetics law over time, causing temporal discontinuities. For the first time, we propose MRI Contrast Enhancement Kinetics World model (MRI CEKWorld) with SpatioTemporal Consistency Learning (STCL). For (a), guided by the spatial law that patient-level structures remain consistent during enhancement, we propose Latent Alignment Learning (LAL) that constructs a patient-specific template to constrain contents to align with this template. For (b), guided by the temporal law that the kinetics follow a consistent smooth trend, we propose Latent Difference Learning (LDL) which extends the unobserved intervals by interpolation and constrains smooth variations in the latent space among interpolated sequences. Extensive experiments on two datasets show our MRI CEKWorld achieves better realistic contents and kinetics. Codes will be available at https://github.com/DD0922/MRI-Contrast-Enhancement-Kinetics-World-Model.

</details>


### [110] [IPv2: An Improved Image Purification Strategy for Real-World Ultra-Low-Dose Lung CT Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19314)
*Guoliang Gong,Man Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IPv2 redesigns the image purification strategy for ultra-low-dose CT denoising by adding Remove Background, Add noise, and Remove noise modules to enable denoising in background and lung parenchyma, improving evaluation with refined labels. Tested on real-world 2% dose lung CT data, it enhances background suppression and lung restoration across multiple denoising models; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The original image purification strategy effectively aligns anatomical structures to correct spatial misalignment but only suppresses noise in chest wall/bone regions and neglects the background and lung parenchyma, limiting denoising performance. A more comprehensive approach is needed to suppress noise throughout the image and specifically restore lung tissue.

Method: Redesign of the purification strategy by introducing three core modules—Remove Background, Add noise, and Remove noise—to enable denoising over background and lung tissue during training data construction and to use refined label construction for evaluation. The approach is evaluated on real-world patient lung CT data at 2% dose, across multiple mainstream denoising models, with publicly available code.

Result: IPv2 consistently improves background suppression and lung parenchyma restoration across various denoising models on the 2% dose lung CT dataset. The method demonstrates robust performance gains and provides a more reasonable evaluation protocol.

Conclusion: By addressing background and lung tissue denoising and refining evaluation labels, IPv2 enhances the original image purification strategy's denoising capability and generalizes across denoising models, offering a practical improvement for ultra-low-dose CT denoising and a transparent evaluation framework.

Abstract: The image purification strategy constructs an intermediate distribution with aligned anatomical structures, which effectively corrects the spatial misalignment between real-world ultra-low-dose CT and normal-dose CT images and significantly enhances the structural preservation ability of denoising models. However, this strategy exhibits two inherent limitations. First, it suppresses noise only in the chest wall and bone regions while leaving the image background untreated. Second, it lacks a dedicated mechanism for denoising the lung parenchyma. To address these issues, we systematically redesign the original image purification strategy and propose an improved version termed IPv2. The proposed strategy introduces three core modules, namely Remove Background, Add noise, and Remove noise. These modules endow the model with denoising capability in both background and lung tissue regions during training data construction and provide a more reasonable evaluation protocol through refined label construction at the testing stage. Extensive experiments on our previously established real-world patient lung CT dataset acquired at 2% radiation dose demonstrate that IPv2 consistently improves background suppression and lung parenchyma restoration across multiple mainstream denoising models. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/MonkeyDadLufy/Image-Purification-Strategy-v2.

</details>


### [111] [Pay Attention to CTC: Fast and Robust Pseudo-Labelling for Unified Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19316)
*Alexandros Haliassos,Rodrigo Mira,Stavros Petridis*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: USR 2.0 introduces CTC-driven teacher forcing for unified audio-visual speech recognition, enabling a single forward pass to generate both CTC and attention pseudo-labels, halving training time and improving robustness, achieving SOTA on LRS3, LRS2, and WildVSR; it mitigates exposure bias with mixed sampling and avoids costly beam search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address inefficiencies and error-proneness of autoregressive pseudo-labeling and decoupled CTC/attention supervision in current USR, aiming to reduce training cost and improve robustness under distribution shifts (long sequences, noise, unseen domains).

Method: Greedily decode CTC to generate pseudo-labels for the decoder (CTC-driven teacher forcing) in a single forward pass; use CTC and CTC-driven attention pseudo-labels of equal length to train both branches in parallel; no beam search; introduce mixed sampling to reduce exposure bias by combining CTC-derived targets with alternative sources.

Result: Training time is halved; improved robustness to out-of-distribution inputs; achieves state-of-the-art results on LRS3, LRS2, and WildVSR, surpassing USR and modality-specific self-supervised baselines.

Conclusion: USR 2.0 provides a more efficient and robust framework for unified speech recognition, leveraging CTC-driven targets to integrate CTC and attention supervision in a single pass, with mixed sampling mitigating exposure bias and achieving SOTA performance across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Unified Speech Recognition (USR) has emerged as a semi-supervised framework for training a single model for audio, visual, and audiovisual speech recognition, achieving state-of-the-art results on in-distribution benchmarks. However, its reliance on autoregressive pseudo-labelling makes training expensive, while its decoupled supervision of CTC and attention branches increases susceptibility to self-reinforcing errors, particularly under distribution shifts involving longer sequences, noise, or unseen domains. We propose CTC-driven teacher forcing, where greedily decoded CTC pseudo-labels are fed into the decoder to generate attention targets in a single forward pass. Although these can be globally incoherent, in the pseudo-labelling setting they enable efficient and effective knowledge transfer. Because CTC and CTC-driven attention pseudo-labels have the same length, the decoder can predict both simultaneously, benefiting from the robustness of CTC and the expressiveness of attention without costly beam search. We further propose mixed sampling to mitigate the exposure bias of the decoder relying solely on CTC inputs. The resulting method, USR 2.0, halves training time, improves robustness to out-of-distribution inputs, and achieves state-of-the-art results on LRS3, LRS2, and WildVSR, surpassing USR and modality-specific self-supervised baselines.

</details>


### [112] [US-JEPA: A Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture for Medical Ultrasound](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19322)
*Ashwath Radhachandran,Vedrana Ivezić,Shreeram Athreya,Ronit Anilkumar,Corey W. Arnold,William Speier*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: US-JEPA introduces a SALT objective for ultrasound, using a frozen, domain-specific teacher to provide stable latent targets. This decouples student–teacher optimization and uses masked latent prediction, achieving competitive or superior linear-probing performance on UltraBench compared with domain-specific and universal baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ultrasound imaging is noisy with speckle, making pixel-level reconstruction objectives unreliable. Existing JEPAs rely on brittle online teachers (EMA), leading to instability and inefficiency. There is a need for stable targets and robust self-supervised representations, plus a fair benchmarking of ultrasound foundation models.

Method: Propose US-JEPA with Static-teacher Asymmetric Latent Training (SALT). A frozen, domain-specific teacher provides stable latent targets for the student's masked latent prediction task. The approach decouples student–teacher optimization from the outset. Evaluation uses linear probing on Ultrasound classification tasks across organs and pathologies and compares against public ultrasound foundation models on UltraBench.

Result: US-JEPA yields performance competitive with or superior to both domain-specific and universal vision foundation model baselines under linear probing. The latent-prediction, stable-target design supports robust, efficient ultrasound representations and provides a rigorous benchmark across ultrasound models on UltraBench.

Conclusion: Masked latent prediction with a frozen domain-specific teacher is an effective and stable strategy for ultrasound self-supervised learning, making US-JEPA a strong baseline and contributing a comprehensive ultrasound foundation-model benchmark via UltraBench.

Abstract: Ultrasound (US) imaging poses unique challenges for representation learning due to its inherently noisy acquisition process. The low signal-to-noise ratio and stochastic speckle patterns hinder standard self-supervised learning methods relying on a pixel-level reconstruction objective. Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) address this drawback by predicting masked latent representations rather than raw pixels. However, standard approaches depend on hyperparameter-brittle and computationally expensive online teachers updated via exponential moving average. We propose US-JEPA, a self-supervised framework that adopts the Static-teacher Asymmetric Latent Training (SALT) objective. By using a frozen, domain-specific teacher to provide stable latent targets, US-JEPA decouples student-teacher optimization and pushes the student to expand upon the semantic priors of the teacher. In addition, we provide the first rigorous comparison of all publicly available state-of-the-art ultrasound foundation models on UltraBench, a public dataset benchmark spanning multiple organs and pathological conditions. Under linear probing for diverse classification tasks, US-JEPA achieves performance competitive with or superior to domain-specific and universal vision foundation model baselines. Our results demonstrate that masked latent prediction provides a stable and efficient path toward robust ultrasound representations.

</details>


### [113] [DefenseSplat: Enhancing the Robustness of 3D Gaussian Splatting via Frequency-Aware Filtering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19323)
*Yiran Qiao,Yiren Lu,Yunlai Zhou,Rui Yang,Linlin Hou,Yu Yin,Jing Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A frequency-aware defense for 3D Gaussian Splatting that uses wavelet-based filtering to suppress high-frequency adversarial noise, preserving low-frequency content, thereby improving robustness with minimal impact on clean data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3DGS is vulnerable to imperceptible but consistent perturbations in input views that can degrade rendering, increase training/rendering time, and even cause service denial. A defense is needed to enhance robustness without heavy reliance on clean ground-truth labels.

Method: Perform a wavelet-based frequency analysis to separate low- and high-frequency components of input views. Reconstruct training views by filtering out high-frequency noise while preserving low-frequency content, forming a frequency-aware defense. The approach does not require clean ground-truth supervision and aims to balance robustness with performance on clean data.

Result: Experiments across varying attack strengths on multiple benchmarks show substantially improved robustness of 3DGS under adversarial perturbations, with negligible degradation on clean inputs. The defense does not require access to clean ground-truth supervision.

Conclusion: Frequency-aware filtering targets the high-frequency adversarial artifacts in 3DGS, addressing a critical vulnerability and enabling more robust and secure 3D reconstructions without sacrificing clean-data performance.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for real-time and high-fidelity 3D reconstruction from posed images. However, recent studies reveal its vulnerability to adversarial corruptions in input views, where imperceptible yet consistent perturbations can drastically degrade rendering quality, increase training and rendering time, and inflate memory usage, even leading to server denial-of-service. In our work, to mitigate this issue, we begin by analyzing the distinct behaviors of adversarial perturbations in the low- and high-frequency components of input images using wavelet transforms. Based on this observation, we design a simple yet effective frequency-aware defense strategy that reconstructs training views by filtering high-frequency noise while preserving low-frequency content. This approach effectively suppresses adversarial artifacts while maintaining the authenticity of the original scene. Notably, it does not significantly impair training on clean data, achieving a desirable trade-off between robustness and performance on clean inputs. Through extensive experiments under a wide range of attack intensities on multiple benchmarks, we demonstrate that our method substantially enhances the robustness of 3DGS without access to clean ground-truth supervision. By highlighting and addressing the overlooked vulnerabilities of 3D Gaussian Splatting, our work paves the way for more robust and secure 3D reconstructions.

</details>


### [114] [RetinaVision: XAI-Driven Augmented Regulation for Precise Retinal Disease Classification using deep learning framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19324)
*Mohammad Tahmid Noor,Shayan Abrar,Jannatul Adan Mahi,Md Parvez Mia,Asaduzzaman Hridoy,Samanta Ghosh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Deep learning on OCT retinal images achieves high 8-class classification accuracy; Xception best at 95.25%, using data augmentation and interpretability, deployed in RetinaVision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early, accurate diagnosis of retinal diseases to prevent vision loss; leveraging a large OCT image dataset (C8, 24k images across eight conditions) to improve clinical decision support and evaluate model interpretability.

Method: Resize images to 224x224; compare CNNs Xception and InceptionV3; apply data augmentation (CutMix, MixUp); use GradCAM and LIME for interpretability; implement web app RetinaVision for real-world usage.

Result: Xception achieved 95.25% accuracy; InceptionV3 94.82%; the approach demonstrates strong performance and the value of interpretability for clinical deployment.

Conclusion: Deep learning is effective for OCT-based retinal disease classification; accuracy and interpretability are essential for clinical adoption; a practical workflow including a web app is feasible.

Abstract: Early and accurate classification of retinal diseases is critical to counter vision loss and for guiding clinical management of retinal diseases. In this study, we proposed a deep learning method for retinal disease classification utilizing optical coherence tomography (OCT) images from the Retinal OCT Image Classification - C8 dataset (comprising 24,000 labeled images spanning eight conditions). Images were resized to 224x224 px and tested on convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures: Xception and InceptionV3. Data augmentation techniques (CutMix, MixUp) were employed to enhance model generalization. Additionally, we applied GradCAM and LIME for interpretability evaluation. We implemented this in a real-world scenario via our web application named RetinaVision. This study found that Xception was the most accurate network (95.25%), followed closely by InceptionV3 (94.82%). These results suggest that deep learning methods allow effective OCT retinal disease classification and highlight the importance of implementing accuracy and interpretability for clinical applications.

</details>


### [115] [MultiDiffSense: Diffusion-Based Multi-Modal Visuo-Tactile Image Generation Conditioned on Object Shape and Contact Pose](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19348)
*Sirine Bhouri,Lan Wei,Jian-Qing Zheng,Dandan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A unified diffusion model (MultiDiffSense) synthesizes multi-modal tactile images for several sensors via dual conditioning on depth maps and prompts, enabling controllable, cross-modal data generation and data-efficient pose estimation, outperforming a Pix2Pix baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the data-collection bottleneck for visuo-tactile datasets and enable scalable, cross-modal synthesis across multiple tactile sensors.

Method: A single diffusion model conditioned on (1) CAD-derived, pose-aligned depth maps and (2) structured prompts encoding sensor type and 4-DoF contact pose, trained to generate images for ViTac, TacTip, and ViTacTip. Evaluated on 8 objects (5 seen, 3 novel) and unseen poses, compared to a Pix2Pix cGAN baseline. Downstream pose estimation tested with mixed synthetic/real data.

Result: Outperforms Pix2Pix baselines in SSIM: +36.3% (ViTac), +134.6% (ViTacTip), +64.7% (TacTip). 3-DoF pose estimation improves with synthetic data; mixing 50% synthetic with 50% real halves required real data while retaining competitive performance.

Conclusion: MultiDiffSense mitigates data-collection bottlenecks, enabling scalable, controllable multi-modal tactile dataset generation for robotics and cross-modal learning; effective for both improving image synthesis quality and reducing real-data needs for downstream tasks.

Abstract: Acquiring aligned visuo-tactile datasets is slow and costly, requiring specialised hardware and large-scale data collection. Synthetic generation is promising, but prior methods are typically single-modality, limiting cross-modal learning. We present MultiDiffSense, a unified diffusion model that synthesises images for multiple vision-based tactile sensors (ViTac, TacTip, ViTacTip) within a single architecture. Our approach uses dual conditioning on CAD-derived, pose-aligned depth maps and structured prompts that encode sensor type and 4-DoF contact pose, enabling controllable, physically consistent multi-modal synthesis. Evaluating on 8 objects (5 seen, 3 novel) and unseen poses, MultiDiffSense outperforms a Pix2Pix cGAN baseline in SSIM by +36.3% (ViTac), +134.6% (ViTacTip), and +64.7% (TacTip). For downstream 3-DoF pose estimation, mixing 50% synthetic with 50% real halves the required real data while maintaining competitive performance. MultiDiffSense alleviates the data-collection bottleneck in tactile sensing and enables scalable, controllable multi-modal dataset generation for robotic applications.

</details>


### [116] [UP-Fuse: Uncertainty-guided LiDAR-Camera Fusion for 3D Panoptic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19349)
*Rohit Mohan,Florian Drews,Yakov Miron,Daniele Cattaneo,Abhinav Valada*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Uncertainty-aware LiDAR-camera fusion in 2D range-view for robust 3D panoptic segmentation under camera degradation and sensor misalignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Camera sensor degradation or failure can severely impair LiDAR-camera fusion-based 3D perception; there is a need for robust fusion methods that remain reliable under adverse conditions.

Method: Project LiDAR into range-view with a LiDAR encoder; extract camera features and project into the same shared space; use an uncertainty-guided fusion module that learns uncertainty maps from representational divergence under various degradations to gate cross-modal interaction; decode fused features with a hybrid 2D-3D transformer to predict 3D panoptic segmentation. Evaluated on Panoptic nuScenes, SemanticKITTI, and Panoptic Waymo.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate robustness to severe visual corruption or misalignment, with maintained performance across datasets, indicating effective uncertainty-guided fusion and robust feature fusion.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-aware fusion in range-view provides reliable 3D panoptic segmentation in safety-critical robotic perception under camera degradation, calibration drift, or sensor failure.

Abstract: LiDAR-camera fusion enhances 3D panoptic segmentation by leveraging camera images to complement sparse LiDAR scans, but it also introduces a critical failure mode. Under adverse conditions, degradation or failure of the camera sensor can significantly compromise the reliability of the perception system. To address this problem, we introduce UP-Fuse, a novel uncertainty-aware fusion framework in the 2D range-view that remains robust under camera sensor degradation, calibration drift, and sensor failure. Raw LiDAR data is first projected into the range-view and encoded by a LiDAR encoder, while camera features are simultaneously extracted and projected into the same shared space. At its core, UP-Fuse employs an uncertainty-guided fusion module that dynamically modulates cross-modal interaction using predicted uncertainty maps. These maps are learned by quantifying representational divergence under diverse visual degradations, ensuring that only reliable visual cues influence the fused representation. The fused range-view features are decoded by a novel hybrid 2D-3D transformer that mitigates spatial ambiguities inherent to the 2D projection and directly predicts 3D panoptic segmentation masks. Extensive experiments on Panoptic nuScenes, SemanticKITTI, and our introduced Panoptic Waymo benchmark demonstrate the efficacy and robustness of UP-Fuse, which maintains strong performance even under severe visual corruption or misalignment, making it well suited for robotic perception in safety-critical settings.

</details>


### [117] [PoseCraft: Tokenized 3D Body Landmark and Camera Conditioning for Photorealistic Human Image Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19350)
*Zhilin Guo,Jing Yang,Kyle Fogarty,Jingyi Wan,Boqiao Zhang,Tianhao Wu,Weihao Xia,Chenliang Zhou,Sakar Khattar,Fangcheng Zhong,Cristina Nader Vasconcelos,Cengiz Oztireli*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion-based 3D conditioning using tokenized 3D landmarks and camera extrinsics enables photorealistic, pose-controllable human avatars; paired with a scalable data pipeline (GenHumanRF); reports superior quality versus diffusion-only methods and competitive results with volumetric rendering, with better fabric/hair fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of traditional skinning-based rigging and template-based fitting, and of canonical-template neural volumetric methods, by preserving 3D semantics under large pose/view changes and enabling scalable data generation.

Method: PoseCraft introduces discrete conditioning tokens for 3D landmarks and camera extrinsics, injected into a diffusion model via cross-attention to maintain 3D semantics; GenHumanRF renders diverse supervision from volumetric reconstructions for scalable training data.

Result: Significant perceptual quality gains over diffusion-centric baselines; competitive with state-of-the-art volumetric rendering; improved preservation of fabric and hair details.

Conclusion: 3D-token conditioned diffusion enables 3D-consistent photorealistic avatars and scalable data generation, offering advantages over both diffusion-only approaches and canonical volumetric methods.

Abstract: Digitizing humans and synthesizing photorealistic avatars with explicit 3D pose and camera controls are central to VR, telepresence, and entertainment. Existing skinning-based workflows require laborious manual rigging or template-based fittings, while neural volumetric methods rely on canonical templates and re-optimization for each unseen pose. We present PoseCraft, a diffusion framework built around tokenized 3D interface: instead of relying only on rasterized geometry as 2D control images, we encode sparse 3D landmarks and camera extrinsics as discrete conditioning tokens and inject them into diffusion via cross-attention. Our approach preserves 3D semantics by avoiding 2D re-projection ambiguity under large pose and viewpoint changes, and produces photorealistic imagery that faithfully captures identity and appearance. To train and evaluate at scale, we also implement GenHumanRF, a data generation workflow that renders diverse supervision from volumetric reconstructions. Our experiments show that PoseCraft achieves significant perceptual quality improvement over diffusion-centric methods, and attains better or comparable metrics to latest volumetric rendering SOTA while better preserving fabric and hair details.

</details>


### [118] [MentalBlackboard: Evaluating Spatial Visualization via Mathematical Transformations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19357)
*Nilay Yilmaz,Maitreya Patel,Naga Sai Abhiram Kusumba,Yixuan He,Yezhou Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MentalBlackboard benchmark assesses visual-spatial reasoning in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) using Paper Folding and Hole Punching tasks across prediction and planning. Findings show symmetry and rotation challenges; planning is weak with very low accuracy; Claude Opus 4.1 leads in planning at 10% accuracy, while the top model o3 achieves 71.6% generalization but only 25% on text-based prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically evaluate whether state-of-the-art VLMs possess spatial visualization abilities and to identify specific failure modes in symmetry, rotation handling, and multi-stage spatial reasoning.

Method: Two open-ended tasks (prediction and planning) built around Paper Folding and Hole Punching. Evaluate multiple VLMs (including Claude Opus 4.1 and o3) on prediction accuracy, planning performance, generalization (spatial-data transfer), and text-based prediction accuracy.

Result: Prediction experiments show models struggle with applying symmetric transformations despite predicting unfolding steps; rotations impair physical situational awareness. Planning reveals limited ability to analyze symmetrical relationships and perform multi-stage symmetry. Claude Opus 4.1 attains the highest planning accuracy at 10%. The top model o3 reaches 71.6% on generalization (not strictly spatial visualization but transfers spatial data) but only 25% on text-based prediction tasks.

Conclusion: Current VLMs exhibit limited spatial visualization capabilities as evidenced by targeted benchmarks. The MentalBlackboard task exposes concrete failure modes—symmetry reasoning, rotation robustness, and multi-stage symmetry processing—which call for improved spatial representations and possibly hybrid symbolic approaches to enhance cross-modal spatial reasoning.

Abstract: Spatial visualization is the mental ability to imagine, transform, and manipulate the spatial characteristics of objects and actions. This intelligence is a part of human cognition where actions and perception are connected on a mental level. To explore whether state-of-the-art Vision-Language Models (VLMs) exhibit this ability, we develop MentalBlackboard, an open-ended spatial visualization benchmark for Paper Folding and Hole Punching tests within two core tasks: prediction and planning. Our prediction experiments reveal that models struggle with applying symmetrical transformations, even when they predict the sequence of unfolding steps correctly. Also, rotations introduce a significant challenge to the physical situational awareness for models. The planning task reveals limitations of models in analyzing symmetrical relationships and in implementing the multi-stage symmetry process, with Claude Opus 4.1 achieving the highest planning score at an accuracy of 10\%. The top-performing model, o3, attains a peak performance of 71.6\% on the generalization task, which does not require spatial visualization but transfers spatial data; however, it achieves only 25\% accuracy on text-based prediction tasks.

</details>


### [119] [Referring Layer Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19358)
*Fangyi Chen,Yaojie Shen,Lu Xu,Ye Yuan,Shu Zhang,Yulei Niu,Longyin Wen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Referring Layer Decomposition (RLD), a task to decompose a single RGB image into RGBA layers conditioned on prompts; provides the RefLade dataset (1.11M triplets + 100K high-fidelity layers) and a baseline RefLayer, enabling benchmarkable, prompt-driven, object-aware editing with strong zero-shot generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for fine-grained, object-aware editing via layered representations. Holistic image editing limits compositional control; a layered decomposition approach supports explicit manipulation of individual scene elements and effects.

Method: Propose RefLayer as a prompt-conditioned decomposition model that outputs RGBA layers from a single RGB image. Build on RefLade, a large-scale dataset of image-layer-prompt triplets (1.11M) plus manually curated 100K high-fidelity layers, and employ a perceptually grounded, human-preference-aligned automatic evaluation protocol to guide training and evaluation.

Result: Demonstrates high visual fidelity and semantic alignment in layer decomposition, robust zero-shot generalization, and reliable evaluation protocols; shows effective training of the model and dataset to enable RLD as a benchmarkable task.

Conclusion: RLD provides a well-defined benchmark for object-aware image decomposition; RefLayer offers a simple yet effective baseline for prompt-conditioned layer decomposition; the dataset and evaluation framework enable future research in compositional editing and controllable generation.

Abstract: Precise, object-aware control over visual content is essential for advanced image editing and compositional generation. Yet, most existing approaches operate on entire images holistically, limiting the ability to isolate and manipulate individual scene elements. In contrast, layered representations, where scenes are explicitly separated into objects, environmental context, and visual effects, provide a more intuitive and structured framework for interpreting and editing visual content. To bridge this gap and enable both compositional understanding and controllable editing, we introduce the Referring Layer Decomposition (RLD) task, which predicts complete RGBA layers from a single RGB image, conditioned on flexible user prompts, such as spatial inputs (e.g., points, boxes, masks), natural language descriptions, or combinations thereof. At the core is the RefLade, a large-scale dataset comprising 1.11M image-layer-prompt triplets produced by our scalable data engine, along with 100K manually curated, high-fidelity layers. Coupled with a perceptually grounded, human-preference-aligned automatic evaluation protocol, RefLade establishes RLD as a well-defined and benchmarkable research task. Building on this foundation, we present RefLayer, a simple baseline designed for prompt-conditioned layer decomposition, achieving high visual fidelity and semantic alignment. Extensive experiments show our approach enables effective training, reliable evaluation, and high-quality image decomposition, while exhibiting strong zero-shot generalization capabilities.

</details>


### [120] [Detector-in-the-Loop Tracking: Active Memory Rectification for Stable Glottic Opening Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19380)
*Huayu Wang,Bahaa Alattar,Cheng-Yen Yang,Hsiang-Wei Huang,Jung Heon Kim,Linda Shapiro,Nathan White,Jenq-Neng Hwang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Closed-Loop Memory Correction (CL-MC) introduces a detector-in-the-loop framework that supervises SAM2 with confidence-guided state decisions and memory rectification to mitigate drift in glottic tracking during video laryngoscopy, achieving state-of-the-art performance on emergency intubation videos without additional training; code will be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Temporal instability in glottic opening localization arises from the lack of temporal context in single-frame detectors and memory drift in foundation-model trackers under rapid tissue deformation and occlusions common in emergency endoscopy.

Method: A detector-in-the-loop system where SAM2 is supervised via confidence-aligned state decisions. High-confidence detections trigger semantic resets that overwrite corrupted tracker memory, providing active memory rectification without training the foundation tracker. The approach emphasizes memory correction to curb drift in complex endoscopic scenes.

Result: On emergency intubation videos, CL-MC achieves state-of-the-art performance, markedly reducing drift and missing rate compared with SAM2 variants and open-loop methods.

Conclusion: Memory correction is essential for reliable clinical video tracking, and CL-MC demonstrates that a training-free memory rectification mechanism can stabilize temporally inconsistent tracking in challenging endoscopic scenarios. Code will be available at the provided repository.

Abstract: Temporal stability in glottic opening localization remains challenging due to the complementary weaknesses of single-frame detectors and foundation-model trackers: the former lacks temporal context, while the latter suffers from memory drift. Specifically, in video laryngoscopy, rapid tissue deformation, occlusions, and visual ambiguities in emergency settings require a robust, temporally aware solution that can prevent progressive tracking errors. We propose Closed-Loop Memory Correction (CL-MC), a detector-in-the-loop framework that supervises Segment Anything Model 2(SAM2) through confidence-aligned state decisions and active memory rectification. High-confidence detections trigger semantic resets that overwrite corrupted tracker memory, effectively mitigating drift accumulation with a training-free foundation tracker in complex endoscopic scenes. On emergency intubation videos, CL-MC achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly reducing drift and missing rate compared with the SAM2 variants and open loop based methods. Our results establish memory correction as a crucial component for reliable clinical video tracking. Our code will be available in https://github.com/huayuww/CL-MR.

</details>


### [121] [Adaptive Data Augmentation with Multi-armed Bandit: Sample-Efficient Embedding Calibration for Implicit Pattern Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19385)
*Minxue Tang,Yangyang Yu,Aolin Ding,Maziyar Baran Pouyan,Taha Belkhouja Yujia Bao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ADAMAB is an efficient, data-efficient embedding calibration framework for few-shot long-tail pattern recognition. It couples embedder-agnostic, lightweight calibrators with fixed embeddings and uses MAB-based adaptive data augmentation to avoid fine-tuning base models, achieving up to 40% accuracy gains with less than 5 samples per class.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-tail pattern recognition challenges stem from scarce labeled data and prohibitive computational costs of fine-tuning large foundation models. There is a need for data-efficient, model-parameter-free calibration that can leverage fixed embeddings.

Method: Train lightweight calibrators on top of fixed embedding models (embedder-agnostic) without updating base parameters. Employ adaptive data augmentation guided by a Multi-Armed Bandit mechanism with a modified upper confidence bound to select augmentation strategies. The approach aims to minimize gradient shifting and provides convergence guarantees in few-shot settings.

Result: Multi-modal experiments show superior performance of ADAMAB, with up to 40% accuracy improvement when training with fewer than 5 initial samples per class. The method reduces computational costs by avoiding fine-tuning and leverages adaptive data augmentation for data efficiency.

Conclusion: ADAMAB offers a practical and theoretically grounded solution for efficient few-shot pattern recognition. By calibrating lightweight add-on modules on fixed embeddings and leveraging MAB-guided augmentation, it achieves strong accuracy gains with limited data and reduced computational overhead.

Abstract: Recognizing implicit visual and textual patterns is essential in many real-world applications of modern AI. However, tackling long-tail pattern recognition tasks remains challenging for current pre-trained foundation models such as LLMs and VLMs. While finetuning pre-trained models can improve accuracy in recognizing implicit patterns, it is usually infeasible due to a lack of training data and high computational overhead. In this paper, we propose ADAMAB, an efficient embedding calibration framework for few-shot pattern recognition. To maximally reduce the computational costs, ADAMAB trains embedder-agnostic light-weight calibrators on top of fixed embedding models without accessing their parameters. To mitigate the need for large-scale training data, we introduce an adaptive data augmentation strategy based on the Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) mechanism. With a modified upper confidence bound algorithm, ADAMAB diminishes the gradient shifting and offers theoretically guaranteed convergence in few-shot training. Our multi-modal experiments justify the superior performance of ADAMAB, with up to 40% accuracy improvement when training with less than 5 initial data samples of each class.

</details>


### [122] [Redefining the Down-Sampling Scheme of U-Net for Precision Biomedical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19412)
*Mingjie Li,Yizheng Chen,Md Tauhidul Islam,Lei Xing*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Stair Pooling introduces a sequence of small, oriented pooling operations to slow down down-sampling in U-Net, preserving information for better long-range feature capture; yields ~3.8% Dice improvement on BIS benchmarks, using transfer entropy to select downsampling paths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: U-Nets often lose information during pooling, hindering long-range receptive fields and precise boundary recovery. A simple downsampling strategy that preserves information could improve segmentation accuracy without substantial computational cost.

Method: Apply concatenated, small/narrow pooling steps in varied orientations to moderate dimensionality reduction per pooling operation (from 1/4 to 1/2). Extendable to 3D pooling. Use transfer entropy to select optimal downsampling paths for information preservation.

Result: Average Dice score improvement of 3.8% on 2D and 3D BIS benchmarks across three datasets; quantitative evidence showing reduced information loss via transfer-entropy-guided path selection.

Conclusion: Stair Pooling is a simple, effective downsampling strategy for U-Nets that preserves spatial information during pooling, enhancing long-range information capture and segmentation accuracy in both 2D and 3D settings.

Abstract: U-Net architectures have been instrumental in advancing biomedical image segmentation (BIS) but often struggle with capturing long-range information. One reason is the conventional down-sampling techniques that prioritize computational efficiency at the expense of information retention. This paper introduces a simple but effective strategy, we call it Stair Pooling, which moderates the pace of down-sampling and reduces information loss by leveraging a sequence of concatenated small and narrow pooling operations in varied orientations. Specifically, our method modifies the reduction in dimensionality within each 2D pooling step from $\frac{1}{4}$ to $\frac{1}{2}$. This approach can also be adapted for 3D pooling to preserve even more information. Such preservation aids the U-Net in more effectively reconstructing spatial details during the up-sampling phase, thereby enhancing its ability to capture long-range information and improving segmentation accuracy. Extensive experiments on three BIS benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed Stair Pooling can increase both 2D and 3D U-Net performance by an average of 3.8\% in Dice scores. Moreover, we leverage the transfer entropy to select the optimal down-sampling paths and quantitatively show how the proposed Stair Pooling reduces the information loss.

</details>


### [123] [PA-Attack: Guiding Gray-Box Attacks on LVLM Vision Encoders with Prototypes and Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19418)
*Hefei Mei,Zirui Wang,Chang Xu,Jianyuan Guo,Minjing Dong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PA-Attack is a prototype-anchored, two-stage attentive attack for LVLMs that uses a general dissimilar prototype and attention-guided perturbations to achieve high SRR across tasks, improving cross-model transfer and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address lack of cross-task generalization in white-box attacks and inefficiency of black-box transfer; exploit the shared vision encoder as a gray-box pivot for robust, transferable adversarial perturbations.

Method: Proposed prototype-anchored guidance to direct perturbations toward a nontrivial prototype; two-stage attention enhancement: (i) use token-level attention to focus perturbations on important visual tokens; (ii) adaptively recalibrate attention weights during attack to track evolving attention.

Result: Empirically achieves average 75.1% SRR across diverse tasks and LVLM architectures; demonstrates attack effectiveness, efficiency, and task generalization; code released.

Conclusion: PA-Attack demonstrates strong, transferable adversarial attacks on LVLMs via a gray-box-centric strategy and attention-aware perturbations; suggests potential for robust evaluation and defense development across LVLM systems.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) are foundational to modern multimodal applications, yet their susceptibility to adversarial attacks remains a critical concern. Prior white-box attacks rarely generalize across tasks, and black-box methods depend on expensive transfer, which limits efficiency. The vision encoder, standardized and often shared across LVLMs, provides a stable gray-box pivot with strong cross-model transfer. Building on this premise, we introduce PA-Attack (Prototype-Anchored Attentive Attack). PA-Attack begins with a prototype-anchored guidance that provides a stable attack direction towards a general and dissimilar prototype, tackling the attribute-restricted issue and limited task generalization of vanilla attacks. Building on this, we propose a two-stage attention enhancement mechanism: (i) leverage token-level attention scores to concentrate perturbations on critical visual tokens, and (ii) adaptively recalibrate attention weights to track the evolving attention during the adversarial process. Extensive experiments across diverse downstream tasks and LVLM architectures show that PA-Attack achieves an average 75.1% score reduction rate (SRR), demonstrating strong attack effectiveness, efficiency, and task generalization in LVLMs. Code is available at https://github.com/hefeimei06/PA-Attack.

</details>


### [124] [Prefer-DAS: Learning from Local Preferences and Sparse Prompts for Domain Adaptive Segmentation of Electron Microscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19423)
*Jiabao Chen,Shan Xiong,Jialin Peng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Prefer-DAS introduces a promptable, multitask domain adaptive segmentation framework that uses sparse point prompts and local human preferences for target-domain adaptation. It supports full/partial/no prompts during training and inference, and combines self-training with prompt-guided contrastive learning. It includes Local direct Preference Optimization (LPO), sparse LPO (SLPO), and Unsupervised Preference Optimization (UPO) to align with sparse or missing feedback, enabling both weakly-supervised and unsupervised DAS with interactive, near-supervised performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UDA methods for DAS often underperform due to limited data and biased adaptation; real-world annotation is costly. There is a need for a realistic, annotation-efficient framework that can leverage sparse human preferences and interactive prompts to improve segmentation across domains.

Method: Proposes Prefer-DAS, a promptable multitask model that integrates self-training and prompt-guided contrastive learning. Unlike traditional SAM-like methods, it supports full, partial, or no point prompts in both training and inference. Introduces LPO and SLPO to align with spatially varying, sparse feedback; introduces UPO to leverage self-learned preferences when feedback is missing.

Result: Empirical evaluation on four challenging DAS tasks shows Prefer-DAS outperforms SAM-like methods and existing unsupervised/weakly-supervised DAS approaches in both automatic and interactive modes. It achieves performance near or exceeding supervised models, demonstrating strong generalizability and flexibility.

Conclusion: Prefer-DAS provides a flexible, annotation-efficient solution for domain adaptive segmentation that can operate under various levels of human supervision—ranging from full prompts to self-guided learning—while delivering competitive or superior performance compared to fully supervised baselines.

Abstract: Domain adaptive segmentation (DAS) is a promising paradigm for delineating intracellular structures from various large-scale electron microscopy (EM) without incurring extensive annotated data in each domain. However, the prevalent unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) strategies often demonstrate limited and biased performance, which hinders their practical applications. In this study, we explore sparse points and local human preferences as weak labels in the target domain, thereby presenting a more realistic yet annotation-efficient setting. Specifically, we develop Prefer-DAS, which pioneers sparse promptable learning and local preference alignment. The Prefer-DAS is a promptable multitask model that integrates self-training and prompt-guided contrastive learning. Unlike SAM-like methods, the Prefer-DAS allows for the use of full, partial, and even no point prompts during both training and inference stages and thus enables interactive segmentation. Instead of using image-level human preference alignment for segmentation, we introduce Local direct Preference Optimization (LPO) and sparse LPO (SLPO), plug-and-play solutions for alignment with spatially varying human feedback or sparse feedback. To address potential missing feedback, we also introduce Unsupervised Preference Optimization (UPO), which leverages self-learned preferences. As a result, the Prefer-DAS model can effectively perform both weakly-supervised and unsupervised DAS, depending on the availability of points and human preferences. Comprehensive experiments on four challenging DAS tasks demonstrate that our model outperforms SAM-like methods as well as unsupervised and weakly-supervised DAS methods in both automatic and interactive segmentation modes, highlighting strong generalizability and flexibility. Additionally, the performance of our model is very close to or even exceeds that of supervised models.

</details>


### [125] [Hepato-LLaVA: An Expert MLLM with Sparse Topo-Pack Attention for Hepatocellular Pathology Analysis on Whole Slide Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19424)
*Yuxuan Yang,Zhonghao Yan,Yi Zhang,Bo Yun,Muxi Diao,Guowei Zhao,Kongming Liang,Wenbin Li,Zhanyu Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hepato-LLaVA: a specialized multi-modal LLM for hepatocellular carcinoma pathology with Sparse Topo-Pack Attention to model 2D tissue topology, plus HepatoPathoVQA dataset (33K QA pairs); achieves state-of-the-art in HCC diagnosis and captioning; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current WSI-based HCC analysis suffers from fixed-resolution processing and inefficient feature aggregation, causing information loss or redundancy; lack of effective multi-scale, multi-modal reasoning; need clinically grounded datasets.

Method: Introduce Hepato-LLaVA, a Multi-modal Large Language Model designed for fine-grained hepatocellular pathology analysis. Propose Sparse Topo-Pack Attention to explicitly model 2D tissue topology and aggregate local evidence into semantic summary tokens while preserving global context. Develop HepatoPathoVQA, a clinically grounded 33K-question dataset validated by expert pathologists.

Result: Hepato-LLaVA achieves state-of-the-art performance on hepatocellular carcinoma diagnosis and captioning tasks, outperforming existing methods. HepatoPathoVQA provides a robust, expert-validated dataset to support multi-modal-HCC tasks. Code and implementation details are released at the provided URL.

Conclusion: The approach enables effective multi-scale, topology-aware reasoning for HCC pathology on gigapixel WSIs, with a large, clinically validated dataset to support future research and reproducibility.

Abstract: Hepatocellular Carcinoma diagnosis relies heavily on the interpretation of gigapixel Whole Slide Images. However, current computational approaches are constrained by fixed-resolution processing mechanisms and inefficient feature aggregation, which inevitably lead to either severe information loss or high feature redundancy. To address these challenges, we propose Hepato-LLaVA, a specialized Multi-modal Large Language Model designed for fine-grained hepatocellular pathology analysis. We introduce a novel Sparse Topo-Pack Attention mechanism that explicitly models 2D tissue topology. This mechanism effectively aggregates local diagnostic evidence into semantic summary tokens while preserving global context. Furthermore, to overcome the lack of multi-scale data, we present HepatoPathoVQA, a clinically grounded dataset comprising 33K hierarchically structured question-answer pairs validated by expert pathologists. Our experiments demonstrate that Hepato-LLaVA achieves state-of-the-art performance on HCC diagnosis and captioning tasks, significantly outperforming existing methods. Our code and implementation details are available at https://pris-cv.github.io/Hepto-LLaVA/.

</details>


### [126] [TherA: Thermal-Aware Visual-Language Prompting for Controllable RGB-to-Thermal Infrared Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19430)
*Dong-Guw Lee,Tai Hyoung Rhee,Hyunsoo Jang,Young-Sik Shin,Ukcheol Shin,Ayoung Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage RGB-to-TIR translation framework TherA combines TherA-VLM with a latent diffusion translator to produce diverse, thermally plausible TIR images from RGB inputs with user-controlled conditions; achieves state-of-the-art performance and strong zero-shot gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the data bottleneck in thermal infrared perception by generating synthetic TIR data from RGB images. RGB-based translation often relies on RGB-centric priors and ignores thermal physics, producing implausible heat distributions.

Method: Two-module approach: TherA-VLM produces a thermal-aware embedding from an RGB image and a user prompt, encoding scene, object, materials, and heat-emission context. A latent-diffusion-based translator is conditioned on this embedding to synthesize TIR images with controllable factors such as time of day, weather, and object state.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art translation performance with notable gains in zero-shot translation, up to 33% improvement averaged across evaluated metrics.

Conclusion: TherA enables diverse, thermally plausible TIR synthesis from RGB data with fine-grained controllability, offering a practical solution to augment TIR perception datasets and tasks.

Abstract: Despite the inherent advantages of thermal infrared(TIR) imaging, large-scale data collection and annotation remain a major bottleneck for TIR-based perception. A practical alternative is to synthesize pseudo TIR data via image translation; however, most RGB-to-TIR approaches heavily rely on RGB-centric priors that overlook thermal physics, yielding implausible heat distributions. In this paper, we introduce TherA, a controllable RGB-to-TIR translation framework that produces diverse and thermally plausible images at both scene and object level. TherA couples TherA-VLM with a latent-diffusion-based translator. Given a single RGB image and a user-prompted condition pair, TherA-VLM yields a thermal-aware embedding that encodes scene, object, material, and heat-emission context reflecting the input scene-condition pair. Conditioning the diffusion model on this embedding enables realistic TIR synthesis and fine-grained control across time of day, weather, and object state. Compared to other baselines, TherA achieves state-of-the-art translation performance, demonstrating improved zero-shot translation performance up to 33% increase averaged across all metrics.

</details>


### [127] [CountEx: Fine-Grained Counting via Exemplars and Exclusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19432)
*Yifeng Huang,Gia Khanh Nguyen,Minh Hoai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CountEx introduces a discriminative counting framework that supports explicit inclusion and exclusion prompts to count objects in cluttered scenes; it uses a Discriminative Query Refinement module and a new CoCount benchmark for evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prompt-based counting struggles with distractors and confusable categories in cluttered scenes due to only counting what to include; explicit exclusion is needed for robust fine-grained counting, including known and novel categories.

Method: Multimodal prompts (natural language plus optional visual exemplars) guide a Discriminative Query Refinement (DQR) module that jointly reasons over inclusion and exclusion cues: identify shared features, isolate exclusion-specific patterns, then apply selective suppression to refine the counting query.

Result: Reported substantial improvements over state-of-the-art methods for counting objects from both known and novel categories on the CoCount benchmark (1,780 videos, 10,086 frames across 97 category pairs); data and code released.

Conclusion: CountEx enables precise counting in cluttered scenes by allowing explicit inclusion and exclusion through multimodal prompts, with DQR as the core mechanism; CoCount provides a systematic benchmark to evaluate fine-grained counting methods and supports future research in this area.

Abstract: This paper presents CountEx, a discriminative visual counting framework designed to address a key limitation of existing prompt-based methods: the inability to explicitly exclude visually similar distractors. While current approaches allow users to specify what to count via inclusion prompts, they often struggle in cluttered scenes with confusable object categories, leading to ambiguity and overcounting. CountEx enables users to express both inclusion and exclusion intent, specifying what to count and what to ignore, through multimodal prompts including natural language descriptions and optional visual exemplars. At the core of CountEx is a novel Discriminative Query Refinement module, which jointly reasons over inclusion and exclusion cues by first identifying shared visual features, then isolating exclusion-specific patterns, and finally applying selective suppression to refine the counting query. To support systematic evaluation of fine-grained counting methods, we introduce CoCount, a benchmark comprising 1,780 videos and 10,086 annotated frames across 97 category pairs. Experiments show that CountEx achieves substantial improvements over state-of-the-art methods for counting objects from both known and novel categories. The data and code are available at https://github.com/bbvisual/CountEx.

</details>


### [128] [FinSight-Net:A Physics-Aware Decoupled Network with Frequency-Domain Compensation for Underwater Fish Detection in Smart Aquaculture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19437)
*Jinsong Yang,Zeyuan Hu,Yichen Li,Hong Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FinSight-Net is a physics-aware, lightweight underwater fish detector with MS-DDSP and EPA-FPN, achieving 92.8% mAP on UW-BlurredFish and 4.8% improvement over YOLOv11s, with 29% fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Underwater imaging suffers from wavelength-dependent absorption and turbidity-induced scattering that degrade contrast and blur details; existing detectors rely on heavier models with limited physics integration.

Method: Introduces Multi-Scale Decoupled Dual-Stream Processing (MS-DDSP) with heterogeneous convolutional branches to recover frequency-specific information and suppress backscattering; channel-weighted, scale-aware pathways; Efficient Path Aggregation FPN (EPA-FPN) with long-range skip connections and pruned fusion routes to restore high-frequency details in deep layers for non-rigid fish under blur/turbidity.

Result: On UW-BlurredFish, achieves 92.8% mAP, surpassing YOLOv11s by 4.8% and reducing parameters by 29%; validated on DeepFish and AquaFishSet with strong overall performance (state-of-the-art).

Conclusion: FiniteSight-Net demonstrates a practical, physics-aware approach enabling real-time, robust underwater fish detection in challenging aquaculture environments; the architecture balances accuracy and efficiency.

Abstract: Underwater fish detection (UFD) is a core capability for smart aquaculture and marine ecological monitoring. While recent detectors improve accuracy by stacking feature extractors or introducing heavy attention modules, they often incur substantial computational overhead and, more importantly, neglect the physics that fundamentally limits UFD: wavelength-dependent absorption and turbidity-induced scattering significantly degrade contrast, blur fine structures, and introduce backscattering noise, leading to unreliable localization and recognition. To address these challenges, we propose FinSight-Net, an efficient and physics-aware detection framework tailored for complex aquaculture environments. FinSight-Net introduces a Multi-Scale Decoupled Dual-Stream Processing (MS-DDSP) bottleneck that explicitly targets frequency-specific information loss via heterogeneous convolutional branches, suppressing backscattering artifacts while compensating distorted biological cues through scale-aware and channel-weighted pathways. We further design an Efficient Path Aggregation FPN (EPA-FPN) as a detail-filling mechanism: it restores high-frequency spatial information typically attenuated in deep layers by establishing long-range skip connections and pruning redundant fusion routes, enabling robust detection of non-rigid fish targets under severe blur and turbidity. Extensive experiments on DeepFish, AquaFishSet, and our challenging UW-BlurredFish benchmark demonstrate that FinSight-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance. In particular, on UW-BlurredFish, FinSight-Net reaches 92.8% mAP, outperforming YOLOv11s by 4.8% while reducing parameters by 29.0%, providing a strong and lightweight solution for real-time automated monitoring in smart aquaculture.

</details>


### [129] [UrbanAlign: Post-hoc Semantic Calibration for VLM-Human Preference Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19442)
*Yecheng Zhang,Rong Zhao,Zhizhou Sha,Yong Li,Lei Wang,Ce Hou,Wen Ji,Hao Huang,Yunshan Wan,Jian Yu,Junhao Xia,Yuru Zhang,Chunlei Shi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free post-hoc concept-bottleneck pipeline (UrbanAlign) aligns VLM outputs with human preferences without model training through concept mining, multi-agent scoring, and geometric calibration; achieves 72.2% accuracy (κ=0.45) on Place Pulse 2.0 across six categories, outperforming supervised baselines; zero model weight modification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To align VLM outputs with human ratings in subjective perception tasks without labeled data or expensive fine-tuning; address calibrator gap in VLMs by external calibration.

Method: Three-stage pipeline: concept mining to extract interpretable dimensions from few annotations; Observer-Debater-Judge chain to derive robust continuous concept scores from frozen VLM; locally-weighted ridge regression on a hybrid visual-semantic manifold to calibrate scores against human ratings; end-to-end dimension optimization loop.

Result: On Place Pulse 2.0 across six categories, 72.2% accuracy (κ=0.45); outperforms best supervised baseline by +15.1 percentage points and uncalibrated VLM scoring by +16.3 pp; yields full dimension-level interpretability with zero model-weight modification.

Conclusion: A training-free, interpretable, externally-calibrated approach that leverages VLMs as concept extractors while compensating calibration externally for subjective tasks.

Abstract: Aligning vision-language model (VLM) outputs with human preferences in domain-specific tasks typically requires fine-tuning or reinforcement learning, both of which demand labelled data and GPU compute. We show that for subjective perception tasks, this alignment can be achieved without any model training: VLMs are already strong concept extractors but poor decision calibrators, and the gap can be closed externally. We propose a training-free post-hoc concept-bottleneck pipeline consisting of three tightly coupled stages: concept mining, multi-agent structured scoring, and geometric calibration, unified by an end-to-end dimension optimization loop. Interpretable evaluation dimensions are mined from a handful of human annotations; an Observer-Debater-Judge chain extracts robust continuous concept scores from a frozen VLM; and locally-weighted ridge regression on a hybrid visual-semantic manifold calibrates these scores against human ratings. Applied to urban perception as UrbanAlign, the framework achieves 72.2% accuracy ($κ=0.45$) on Place Pulse 2.0 across six categories, outperforming the best supervised baseline by +15.1 pp and uncalibrated VLM scoring by +16.3 pp, with full dimension-level interpretability and zero model-weight modification.

</details>


### [130] [Decoupling Vision and Language: Codebook Anchored Visual Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19449)
*Jason Wu,Tianchen Zhao,Chang Liu,Jiarui Cai,Zheng Zhang,Zhuowei Li,Aaditya Singh,Xiang Xu,Mani Srivastava,Jonathan Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CRAFT is a discrete codebook-based fine-tuning method that decouples the vision encoder from the language model by anchoring visual features to a fixed codebook, enabling domain adaptation for LVLMs without altering the LM. It yields strong gains on domain tasks while preserving linguistic abilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision encoders in LVLMs underperform on domain-specific tasks, and existing adaptation methods modify continuous interfaces, tying encoder and LM together and causing re-alignment whenever the encoder changes. A decoupled, discrete representation could provide robust domain adaptation.

Method: CRAFT introduces a discrete codebook and fine-tunes the encoder to map visual representations to codebook tokens. This anchors features in a stable token space, decoupling the encoder from the LM. The same codebook can be shared across LVLMs with different architectures, enabling cross-model compatibility.

Result: Average improvement of 13.51% across 10 domain-specific benchmarks (e.g., VQARAD, PlantVillage). It preserves the LLM’s linguistic capabilities and outperforms methods that operate on continuous tokens.

Conclusion: A decoupled, codebook-regulated fine-tuning approach provides robust domain adaptation for LVLMs by anchoring visual representations to a stable discrete space, enabling compatibility across language models and reducing the need for repeated re-alignment with encoder changes.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) use their vision encoders to translate images into representations for downstream reasoning, but the encoders often underperform in domain-specific visual tasks such as medical image diagnosis or fine-grained classification, where representation errors can cascade through the language model, leading to incorrect responses. Existing adaptation methods modify the continuous feature interface between encoder and language model through projector tuning or other parameter-efficient updates, which still couples the two components and requires re-alignment whenever the encoder changes. We introduce CRAFT (Codebook RegulAted Fine-Tuning), a lightweight method that fine-tunes the encoder using a discrete codebook that anchors visual representations to a stable token space, achieving domain adaptation without modifying other parts of the model. This decoupled design allows the adapted encoder to seamlessly boost the performance of LVLMs with different language architectures, as long as they share the same codebook. Empirically, CRAFT achieves an average gain of 13.51% across 10 domain-specific benchmarks such as VQARAD and PlantVillage, while preserving the LLM's linguistic capabilities and outperforming peer methods that operate on continuous tokens.

</details>


### [131] [HD-TTA: Hypothesis-Driven Test-Time Adaptation for Safer Brain Tumor Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19454)
*Kartik Jhawar,Lipo Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hypothesis-Driven Test-Time Adaptation (HD-TTA) reframes TTA as a dynamic decision process with competing geometric hypotheses (compaction vs inflation) and a representation-guided selector plus a Gatekeeper to ensure safety; validated on cross-domain brain tumor segmentation showing improved safety metrics with comparable Dice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In safety-critical medical segmentation, standard TTA is a blind optimization that can either spill tumor masks into healthy tissue or degrade correct predictions. There is a need for selective, safe adaptation that preserves accuracy while reducing harmful errors.

Method: HD-TTA generates two intuitive geometric hypotheses during adaptation: compaction (trim artifacts/noise) and inflation (inflate under-segmented tumor). A representation-guided selector autonomously chooses the safest outcome based on texture consistency; a pre-screening Gatekeeper skips adaptation on confident cases to prevent negative transfer. Evaluated on cross-domain brain tumor segmentation by adapting a source adult BraTS model to pediatric and meningioma targets; compared against state-of-the-art baselines.

Result: HD-TTA achieved safety-oriented gains: lowering HD95 by ~6.4 mm and increasing Precision by >4%, while maintaining Dice scores comparable to baselines, indicating improved safety-adaptation trade-off in cross-domain medical segmentation.
