### [159] [HDR Reconstruction Boosting with Training-Free and Exposure-Consistent Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19706)
*Yo-Tin Lin,Su-Kai Chen,Hou-Ning Hu,Yen-Yu Lin,Yu-Lun Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free diffusion-based inpainting method enhances single-LDR-to-HDR reconstruction by filling over-exposed regions, improving perceptual quality and metrics without retraining, and integrating with existing HDR pipelines via an iterative luminance-coherence compensation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Over-exposed regions in LDR-to-HDR reconstruction cause information loss; existing HDR methods struggle in these areas. A training-free, plug-in enhancement is sought to recover plausible content while preserving multi-exposure consistency.

Method: A diffusion-based inpainting pipeline that combines text-guided diffusion models with SDEdit refinement to generate plausible content in over-exposed regions. An iterative compensation mechanism enforces luminance coherence across multiple exposures and allows seamless integration with existing HDR reconstruction techniques without requiring training.

Result: Demonstrates significant improvements in perceptual quality and quantitative metrics on standard HDR datasets and in-the-wild captures. The approach recovers natural details in challenging scenes while preserving the advantages of existing HDR pipelines.

Conclusion: A practical, training-free enhancement for HDR reconstruction that addresses incomplete information in over-exposed regions and remains compatible with current HDR pipelines, yielding better perceptual and quantitative performance.

Abstract: Single LDR to HDR reconstruction remains challenging for over-exposed regions where traditional methods often fail due to complete information loss. We present a training-free approach that enhances existing indirect and direct HDR reconstruction methods through diffusion-based inpainting. Our method combines text-guided diffusion models with SDEdit refinement to generate plausible content in over-exposed areas while maintaining consistency across multi-exposure LDR images. Unlike previous approaches requiring extensive training, our method seamlessly integrates with existing HDR reconstruction techniques through an iterative compensation mechanism that ensures luminance coherence across multiple exposures. We demonstrate significant improvements in both perceptual quality and quantitative metrics on standard HDR datasets and in-the-wild captures. Results show that our method effectively recovers natural details in challenging scenarios while preserving the advantages of existing HDR reconstruction pipelines. Project page: https://github.com/EusdenLin/HDR-Reconstruction-Boosting

</details>


### [160] [ChimeraLoRA: Multi-Head LoRA-Guided Synthetic Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19708)
*Hoyoung Kim,Minwoo Jang,Jabin Koo,Sangdoo Yun,Jungseul Ok*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hybrid diffusion fine-tuning: separate class-shared LoRA for priors and per-image LoRAs for detail, with semantic bounding-box boosting and Dirichlet-based mixing, yielding diverse, detail-rich augmented data and better few-shot accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data scarcity in specialized domains (privacy-constrained medical, fine-grained tasks) leads to biased models. Existing single-image or single-class LoRA strategies trade off detail vs. diversity; a hybrid approach aims to capture both aspects while preserving class semantics.

Method: 1) Train a class-shared LoRA A to encode class priors across shots. 2) Train per-image LoRAs B on individual images. 3) Apply semantic boosting by preserving class bounding boxes during training to align A with coherent class semantics. 4) Generate images by composing A with a Dirichlet-mixture of B's to balance class priors and image-specific details.

Result: The synthesized images are both diverse and detail-rich and closely align with the few-shot real distribution across datasets, leading to robust gains in downstream classification accuracy.

Conclusion: Separating adapters into a shared class-prior LoRA and per-image LoRAs, augmented with semantic bounding-box guidance and Dirichlet-based mixing, effectively balances diversity and fidelity in diffusion-based data augmentation for few-shot learning.

Abstract: Beyond general recognition tasks, specialized domains including privacy-constrained medical applications and fine-grained settings often encounter data scarcity, especially for tail classes. To obtain less biased and more reliable models under such scarcity, practitioners leverage diffusion models to supplement underrepresented regions of real data. Specifically, recent studies fine-tune pretrained diffusion models with LoRA on few-shot real sets to synthesize additional images. While an image-wise LoRA trained on a single image captures fine-grained details yet offers limited diversity, a class-wise LoRA trained over all shots produces diverse images as it encodes class priors yet tends to overlook fine details. To combine both benefits, we separate the adapter into a class-shared LoRA~$A$ for class priors and per-image LoRAs~$\mathcal{B}$ for image-specific characteristics. To expose coherent class semantics in the shared LoRA~$A$, we propose a semantic boosting by preserving class bounding boxes during training. For generation, we compose $A$ with a mixture of $\mathcal{B}$ using coefficients drawn from a Dirichlet distribution. Across diverse datasets, our synthesized images are both diverse and detail-rich while closely aligning with the few-shot real distribution, yielding robust gains in downstream classification accuracy.

</details>


### [161] [Pixels Don't Lie (But Your Detector Might): Bootstrapping MLLM-as-a-Judge for Trustworthy Deepfake Detection and Reasoning Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19715)
*Kartik Kuckreja,Parul Gupta,Muhammad Haris Khan,Abhinav Dhall*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DeepfakeJudge provides scalable reasoning supervision and evaluation for deepfake detection by integrating an out-of-distribution benchmark, a human-annotated visual reasoning subset, and specialized evaluators. It uses a bootstrapped generator–evaluator loop to produce structured reasoning supervision, enabling pointwise and pairwise evaluation. It achieves high accuracy (96.2%) on a meta-evaluation benchmark, strong human-alignment, and 70% user preference for its reasonings; code and data are open-sourced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current deepfake detectors often output explanations that are not grounded in visual evidence, leading to unreliable reasoning. Existing evaluations focus on classification accuracy and ignore reasoning fidelity. There is a need for scalable supervision and evaluation of visual-reasoning in deepfake detection.

Method: Integrates an out-of-distribution benchmark with recent generative/editing forgeries, a human-annotated subset with visual reasoning labels, and a suite of evaluation models that assess reasoning rationales without requiring explicit ground-truth reasoning. A bootstrapped generator–evaluator process scales human feedback into structured reasoning supervision and supports both pointwise and pairwise evaluation.

Result: On the proposed meta-evaluation benchmark, the reasoning-bootstrapped model achieves 96.2% accuracy, outperforming 30× larger baselines. The reasoning judge shows very high correlation with human ratings and 98.9% pairwise agreement on the human-annotated subset. A user study reports 70% preference for the framework’s reasonings regarding faithfulness, groundedness, and usefulness. All datasets, models, and code are open-sourced.

Conclusion: Reasoning fidelity is a quantifiable dimension for deepfake detection, and scalable supervision enables interpretable, faithful, and grounded deepfake reasoning. The work provides datasets, models, and code to support future research in this area.

Abstract: Deepfake detection models often generate natural-language explanations, yet their reasoning is frequently ungrounded in visual evidence, limiting reliability. Existing evaluations measure classification accuracy but overlook reasoning fidelity. We propose DeepfakeJudge, a framework for scalable reasoning supervision and evaluation, that integrates an out-of-distribution benchmark containing recent generative and editing forgeries, a human-annotated subset with visual reasoning labels, and a suite of evaluation models, that specialize in evaluating reasoning rationales without the need for explicit ground truth reasoning rationales. The Judge is optimized through a bootstrapped generator-evaluator process that scales human feedback into structured reasoning supervision and supports both pointwise and pairwise evaluation. On the proposed meta-evaluation benchmark, our reasoning-bootstrapped model achieves an accuracy of 96.2\%, outperforming \texttt{30x} larger baselines. The reasoning judge attains very high correlation with human ratings and 98.9\% percent pairwise agreement on the human-annotated meta-evaluation subset. These results establish reasoning fidelity as a quantifiable dimension of deepfake detection and demonstrate scalable supervision for interpretable deepfake reasoning. Our user study shows that participants preferred the reasonings generated by our framework 70\% of the time, in terms of faithfulness, groundedness, and usefulness, compared to those produced by other models and datasets. All of our datasets, models, and codebase are \href{https://github.com/KjAeRsTuIsK/DeepfakeJudge}{open-sourced}.

</details>


### [162] [Generative 6D Pose Estimation via Conditional Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19719)
*Amir Hamza,Davide Boscaini,Weihang Li,Benjamin Busam,Fabio Poiesi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Flose, a conditional flow matching approach for 6D pose estimation that denoises in R^3 using local features and semantic appearance cues, with RANSAC to handle outliers; achieves +4.5 Average Recall on five BOP datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limitations of direct SE(3) regression (prone to symmetry failure) and local-feature matching (fails without distinctive features) in instance-level 6D pose estimation.

Method: Formulates 6D pose estimation as conditional flow matching in R^3. Flose performs a denoising process conditioned on local and appearance-based semantic features, addressing object symmetries. Incorporates RANSAC-based registration to robustly handle outliers.

Result: Evaluated on five BOP datasets; Flose achieves an average improvement of 4.5 in Average Recall over prior methods.

Conclusion: Conditioning flow-based denoising on semantic features improves robustness to symmetries in 6D pose estimation; combining with RANSAC yields strong, outlier-resistant pose estimates.

Abstract: Existing methods for instance-level 6D pose estimation typically rely on neural networks that either directly regress the pose in $\mathrm{SE}(3)$ or estimate it indirectly via local feature matching. The former struggle with object symmetries, while the latter fail in the absence of distinctive local features. To overcome these limitations, we propose a novel formulation of 6D pose estimation as a conditional flow matching problem in $\mathbb{R}^3$. We introduce Flose, a generative method that infers object poses via a denoising process conditioned on local features. While prior approaches based on conditional flow matching perform denoising solely based on geometric guidance, Flose integrates appearance-based semantic features to mitigate ambiguities caused by object symmetries. We further incorporate RANSAC-based registration to handle outliers. We validate Flose on five datasets from the established BOP benchmark. Flose outperforms prior methods with an average improvement of +4.5 Average Recall. Project Website : https://tev-fbk.github.io/Flose/

</details>


### [163] [Towards Personalized Multi-Modal MRI Synthesis across Heterogeneous Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19723)
*Yue Zhang,Zhizheng Zhuo,Siyao Xu,Shan Lv,Zhaoxi Liu,Jun Qiu,Qiuli Wang,Yaou Liu,S. Kevin Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PMM-Synth: a cross-dataset, personalized MRI synthesis framework that generalizes across heterogeneous datasets using dataset-conditioned feature modulation, modality-consistent batch scheduling, and selective supervision, improving reconstruction quality and downstream diagnostic tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unified MRI synthesis models often lack generalization across datasets with different modality coverage, diseases, and intensity distributions; robust cross-dataset synthesis is needed for real-world deployment.

Method: Multi-dataset joint training with three innovations: (1) Personalized Feature Modulation that adapts features based on dataset identifier, (2) Modality-Consistent Batch Scheduler to stabilize training under inconsistent modality conditions, (3) Selective supervision loss to learn when some ground-truth modalities are missing.

Result: PMM-Synth outperforms state-of-the-art methods on four clinical datasets for one-to-one and many-to-one synthesis, achieving higher PSNR and SSIM; qualitative results show better preservation of anatomical and pathological details; downstream tasks (tumor segmentation, radiological reporting) indicate potential for reliable diagnosis under modality-missing scenarios.

Conclusion: PMM-Synth demonstrates strong cross-dataset generalization for versatile MRI synthesis and holds promise for real-world deployment where modality coverage is incomplete.

Abstract: Synthesizing missing modalities in multi-modal magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is vital for ensuring diagnostic completeness, particularly when full acquisitions are infeasible due to time constraints, motion artifacts, and patient tolerance. Recent unified synthesis models have enabled flexible synthesis tasks by accommodating various input-output configurations. However, their training and evaluation are typically restricted to a single dataset, limiting their generalizability across diverse clinical datasets and impeding practical deployment. To address this limitation, we propose PMM-Synth, a personalized MRI synthesis framework that not only supports various synthesis tasks but also generalizes effectively across heterogeneous datasets. PMM-Synth is jointly trained on multiple multi-modal MRI datasets that differ in modality coverage, disease types, and intensity distributions. It achieves cross-dataset generalization through three core innovations: a Personalized Feature Modulation module that dynamically adapts feature representations based on dataset identifier to mitigate the impact of distributional shifts; a Modality-Consistent Batch Scheduler that facilitates stable and efficient batch training under inconsistent modality conditions; and a selective supervision loss to ensure effective learning when ground truth modalities are partially missing. Evaluated on four clinical multi-modal MRI datasets, PMM-Synth consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both one-to-one and many-to-one synthesis tasks, achieving superior PSNR and SSIM scores. Qualitative results further demonstrate improved preservation of anatomical structures and pathological details. Additionally, downstream tumor segmentation and radiological reporting studies suggest that PMM-Synth holds potential for supporting reliable diagnosis under real-world modality-missing scenarios.

</details>


### [164] [VGGT-MPR: VGGT-Enhanced Multimodal Place Recognition in Autonomous Driving Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19735)
*Jingyi Xu,Zhangshuo Qi,Zhongmiao Yan,Xuyu Gao,Qianyun Jiao,Songpengcheng Xia,Xieyuanli Chen,Ling Pei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VGGT-MPR introduces a unified Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer to perform both global multimodal place retrieval and a training-free re-ranking for robust localization. It uses depth-aware supervision and LiDAR densification to create strong global descriptors and employs mask-guided keypoint extraction with confidence-aware scoring for re-ranking, achieving state-of-the-art results on autonomous driving benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal place recognition methods rely on hand-crafted fusion strategies and heavy backbones, leading to costly retraining and limited robustness to appearance, viewpoint, and occlusion changes. A unified geometric transformer could provide better cross-modal alignment and efficiency.

Method: Adopt VGGT as a unified geometric engine for global retrieval and re-ranking. Global retrieval uses depth-aware supervision and point-map densification via predicted depth maps to produce discriminative global descriptors. Re-ranking is training-free, leveraging VGGT's cross-view keypoint tracking with mask-guided keypoint extraction and confidence-aware correspondence scoring.

Result: Extensive experiments on large-scale autonomous driving benchmarks and self-collected data show state-of-the-art performance with strong robustness to environmental changes, viewpoint shifts, and occlusions.

Conclusion: A unified geometric transformer (VGGT) effectively bundles global retrieval and re-ranking in multimodal place recognition, reducing retraining costs and improving robustness; results suggest good generalization and potential for broad deployment, with code and data to be released.

Abstract: In autonomous driving, robust place recognition is critical for global localization and loop closure detection. While inter-modality fusion of camera and LiDAR data in multimodal place recognition (MPR) has shown promise in overcoming the limitations of unimodal counterparts, existing MPR methods basically attend to hand-crafted fusion strategies and heavily parameterized backbones that require costly retraining. To address this, we propose VGGT-MPR, a multimodal place recognition framework that adopts the Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer (VGGT) as a unified geometric engine for both global retrieval and re-ranking. In the global retrieval stage, VGGT extracts geometrically-rich visual embeddings through prior depth-aware and point map supervision, and densifies sparse LiDAR point clouds with predicted depth maps to improve structural representation. This enhances the discriminative ability of fused multimodal features and produces global descriptors for fast retrieval. Beyond global retrieval, we design a training-free re-ranking mechanism that exploits VGGT's cross-view keypoint-tracking capability. By combining mask-guided keypoint extraction with confidence-aware correspondence scoring, our proposed re-ranking mechanism effectively refines retrieval results without additional parameter optimization. Extensive experiments on large-scale autonomous driving benchmarks and our self-collected data demonstrate that VGGT-MPR achieves state-of-the-art performance, exhibiting strong robustness to severe environmental changes, viewpoint shifts, and occlusions. Our code and data will be made publicly available.

</details>


### [165] [InfScene-SR: Spatially Continuous Inference for Arbitrary-Size Image Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19736)
*Shoukun Sun,Zhe Wang,Xiang Que,Jiyin Zhang,Xiaogang Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: InfScene-SR enables spatially continuous diffusion-based super-resolution for large, arbitrary scenes by a guided, variance-corrected fusion mechanism that avoids retraining, demonstrated on remote sensing data with high perceptual quality and no boundary seams.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address memory and boundary issues when applying patch-based diffusion SR to arbitrarily large images; standard methods (e.g., SR3) are limited by fixed patch sizes and produce seams, hindering scalability and consistency for large scenes.

Method: Adapt the iterative refinement of diffusion models with a novel guided and variance-corrected fusion mechanism to seamlessly fuse overlapping patches into a coherent large-scale HR image; no retraining of the diffusion model is required, enabling scalable deployment on arbitrarily large inputs.

Result: Empirically validated on remote sensing datasets, InfScene-SR achieves high perceptual quality in reconstructed details and effectively eliminates boundary artifacts, benefiting downstream tasks such as semantic segmentation.

Conclusion: InfScene-SR enables spatially continuous SR for large, arbitrary scenes without retraining, addressing memory and seam issues inherent to patch-based diffusion SR and improving applicability to remote sensing and similar domains.

Abstract: Image Super-Resolution (SR) aims to recover high-resolution (HR) details from low-resolution (LR) inputs, a task where Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs) have recently shown superior performance compared to Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) based approaches. However, standard diffusion-based SR models, such as SR3, are typically trained on fixed-size patches and struggle to scale to arbitrary-sized images due to memory constraints. Applying these models via independent patch processing leads to visible seams and inconsistent textures across boundaries. In this paper, we propose InfScene-SR, a framework enabling spatially continuous super-resolution for large, arbitrary scenes. We adapt the iterative refinement process of diffusion models with a novel guided and variance-corrected fusion mechanism, allowing for the seamless generation of large-scale high-resolution imagery without retraining. We validate our approach on remote sensing datasets, demonstrating that InfScene-SR not only reconstructs fine details with high perceptual quality but also eliminates boundary artifacts, benefiting downstream tasks such as semantic segmentation.

</details>


### [166] [RAP: Fast Feedforward Rendering-Free Attribute-Guided Primitive Importance Score Prediction for Efficient 3D Gaussian Splatting Processing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19753)
*Kaifa Yang,Qi Yang,Yiling Xu,Zhu Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A rendering-free, feedforward method (RAP) predicts per-primitive importance in 3D Gaussian Splatting to enable efficient pruning, compression, and transmission without view-dependent rendering; it generalizes to unseen scenes after training on a small set of scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting generates many primitives with varying significance, and rendering-based importance estimation is costly, view-dependent, and scales poorly. A fast, generalizable predictor is needed to prune redundancy and support efficient transmission.

Method: RAP uses intrinsic Gaussian attributes and local neighborhood statistics to feed a compact MLP that outputs per-primitive importance scores. Training uses rendering loss, pruning-aware loss, and significance distribution regularization. Inference is rendering-free, enabling plug-and-play integration after training on a small dataset; the model generalizes to unseen scenes.

Result: A fast, rendering-free attribute-guided predictor for primitive importance in 3DGS; generalizes to unseen scenes and can be integrated into reconstruction, compression, and transmission pipelines. Code is publicly available.

Conclusion: RAP provides a scalable, plug-and-play alternative to rendering-based importance estimation for 3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling efficient pruning, compression, and transmission.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a leading technology for high-quality 3D scene reconstruction. However, the iterative refinement and densification process leads to the generation of a large number of primitives, each contributing to the reconstruction to a substantially different extent. Estimating primitive importance is thus crucial, both for removing redundancy during reconstruction and for enabling efficient compression and transmission. Existing methods typically rely on rendering-based analyses, where each primitive is evaluated through its contribution across multiple camera viewpoints. However, such methods are sensitive to the number and selection of views, rely on specialized differentiable rasterizers, and have long calculation times that grow linearly with view count, making them difficult to integrate as plug-and-play modules and limiting scalability and generalization. To address these issues, we propose RAP, a fast feedforward rendering-free attribute-guided method for efficient importance score prediction in 3DGS. RAP infers primitive significance directly from intrinsic Gaussian attributes and local neighborhood statistics, avoiding rendering-based or visibility-dependent computations. A compact MLP predicts per-primitive importance scores using rendering loss, pruning-aware loss, and significance distribution regularization. After training on a small set of scenes, RAP generalizes effectively to unseen data and can be seamlessly integrated into reconstruction, compression, and transmission pipelines. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/yyyykf/RAP.

</details>


### [167] [Multimodal Dataset Distillation Made Simple by Prototype-Guided Data Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19756)
*Junhyeok Choi,Sangwoo Mo,Minwoo Chae*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a learning-free multimodal dataset distillation framework that uses CLIP embeddings and an unCLIP decoder to synthesize images from prototypes, achieving strong cross-architecture generalization and outperforming optimization-based distillation and subset selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale image-text datasets are costly to train on; existing multimodal distillation methods still require full datasets and joint optimization, limiting efficiency and cross-architecture generalization.

Method: Extract aligned image-text embeddings with CLIP, derive prototypes from these embeddings, and use an unCLIP decoder to synthesize images, creating a distilled multimodal dataset without large-scale training or joint optimization.

Result: Extensive experiments show the proposed method consistently outperforms optimization-based distillation and subset selection, achieving state-of-the-art cross-architecture generalization.

Conclusion: A learning-free, CLIP-based prototype synthesis approach yields scalable, architecture-agnostic multimodal dataset distillation with strong cross-architecture generalization.

Abstract: Recent advances in multimodal learning have achieved remarkable success across diverse vision-language tasks. However, such progress heavily relies on large-scale image-text datasets, making training costly and inefficient. Prior efforts in dataset filtering and pruning attempt to mitigate this issue, but still require relatively large subsets to maintain performance and fail under very small subsets. Dataset distillation offers a promising alternative, yet existing multimodal dataset distillation methods require full-dataset training and joint optimization of image pixels and text features, making them architecture-dependent and limiting cross-architecture generalization. To overcome this, we propose a learning-free dataset distillation framework that eliminates the need for large-scale training and optimization while enhancing generalization across architectures. Our method uses CLIP to extract aligned image-text embeddings, obtains prototypes, and employs an unCLIP decoder to synthesize images, enabling efficient and scalable multimodal dataset distillation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms optimization-based dataset distillation and subset selection methods, achieving state-of-the-art cross-architecture generalization.

</details>


### [168] [Training Deep Stereo Matching Networks on Tree Branch Imagery: A Benchmark Study for Real-Time UAV Forestry Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19763)
*Yida Lin,Bing Xue,Mengjie Zhang,Sam Schofield,Richard Green*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: First comprehensive evaluation of ten deep stereo networks on real tree-branch imagery using DEFOM-based disparity targets; BANet-3D best quality, RAFT-Stereo best scene understanding; AnyNet fastest at 1080P on Jetson Orin; 720P vs 1080P tradeoffs outlined.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate real-time depth from stereo is essential for autonomous drone pruning. Disparity errors translate to depth errors at working distances. Vegetation-specific disparity generation and a broad comparison of deep stereo architectures on real-tree data are needed to guide deployment and hardware choices.

Method: Train ten deep stereo networks on the Canterbury Tree Branches dataset (5,313 stereo pairs) with DEFOM-generated disparity maps as training targets. Networks cover step-by-step refinement, 3D convolution, edge-aware attention, and lightweight designs. Evaluate with perceptual metrics (SSIM, LPIPS, ViTScore) and feature matching (SIFT/ORB). Scene-level understanding via ViTScore. Test on NVIDIA Jetson Orin (16 GB) mounted on a drone; measure FPS at 1080P. Compare 720P vs 1080P processing times.

Result: BANet-3D achieves best overall quality (SSIM 0.883, LPIPS 0.157). RAFT-Stereo scores highest on scene-level understanding (ViTScore 0.799). AnyNet reaches 6.99 FPS at 1080P (near-real-time); BANet-2D offers best quality-speed balance at 1.21 FPS. 720P vs 1080P processing times guide resolution choices for forestry drones.

Conclusion: First study to train/test ten deep stereo networks on real tree branches; provides guidance on depth-estimation pipeline design for forestry drones, highlighting tradeoffs between perceptual/structural accuracy and real-time performance, and suggesting directions for optimization and resolution strategies.

Abstract: Autonomous drone-based tree pruning needs accurate, real-time depth estimation from stereo cameras. Depth is computed from disparity maps using $Z = f B/d$, so even small disparity errors cause noticeable depth mistakes at working distances. Building on our earlier work that identified DEFOM-Stereo as the best reference disparity generator for vegetation scenes, we present the first study to train and test ten deep stereo matching networks on real tree branch images. We use the Canterbury Tree Branches dataset -- 5,313 stereo pairs from a ZED Mini camera at 1080P and 720P -- with DEFOM-generated disparity maps as training targets. The ten methods cover step-by-step refinement, 3D convolution, edge-aware attention, and lightweight designs. Using perceptual metrics (SSIM, LPIPS, ViTScore) and structural metrics (SIFT/ORB feature matching), we find that BANet-3D produces the best overall quality (SSIM = 0.883, LPIPS = 0.157), while RAFT-Stereo scores highest on scene-level understanding (ViTScore = 0.799). Testing on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Super (16 GB, independently powered) mounted on our drone shows that AnyNet reaches 6.99 FPS at 1080P -- the only near-real-time option -- while BANet-2D gives the best quality-speed balance at 1.21 FPS. We also compare 720P and 1080P processing times to guide resolution choices for forestry drone systems.

</details>


### [169] [One2Scene: Geometric Consistent Explorable 3D Scene Generation from a Single Image](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19766)
*Pengfei Wang,Liyi Chen,Zhiyuan Ma,Yanjun Guo,Guowen Zhang,Lei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A novel One2Scene framework enables explorable 3D scenes from a single image by sequentially generating anchor panoramas, lifting to a 3D scaffold via Gaussian Splatting, reframing with multi-view stereo across sparse anchors, and using a scaffold-guided novel-view generator to render photorealistic views; achieves stable large-camera exploration and outperforms recent methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ill-posed problem: recovering 3D geometry and appearance from a single image leads to severe geometric distortions and implausible views as the viewpoint moves away from the input; need for a stable, explorable 3D reconstruction from minimal input.

Method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) panorama generator to create anchor views from the input image; (2) lift anchors to a 3D geometric scaffold using a generalizable, feed-forward Gaussian Splatting network; (3) project panorama into multiple sparse anchor views and formulate reconstruction as multi-view stereo with a bidirectional feature fusion module to enforce cross-view consistency; final stage uses the scaffold as prior for a novel-view generator to render photorealistic, geometrically accurate views at arbitrary camera poses.

Result: Extensive experiments show substantial improvements over state-of-the-art in panorama depth estimation, feed-forward 360° reconstruction, and explorable 3D scene generation; robust under large camera motions; code and models will be released.

Conclusion: One2Scene provides a practical, scalable framework that converts a single image into an explorable, geometry-consistent 3D scene by integrating 3D priors, explicit geometry scaffolds, and view synthesis conditioned on a 3D-consistent scaffold, outperforming relevant baselines.

Abstract: Generating explorable 3D scenes from a single image is a highly challenging problem in 3D vision. Existing methods struggle to support free exploration, often producing severe geometric distortions and noisy artifacts when the viewpoint moves far from the original perspective. We introduce \textbf{One2Scene}, an effective framework that decomposes this ill-posed problem into three tractable sub-tasks to enable immersive explorable scene generation. We first use a panorama generator to produce anchor views from a single input image as initialization. Then, we lift these 2D anchors into an explicit 3D geometric scaffold via a generalizable, feed-forward Gaussian Splatting network. Instead of treating the panorama as a single image for reconstruction, we project it into multiple sparse anchor views and reformulate the reconstruction task as multi-view stereo matching, which allows us to leverage robust geometric priors learned from large-scale multi-view datasets. A bidirectional feature fusion module is used to enforce cross-view consistency, yielding an efficient and geometrically reliable scaffold. Finally, the scaffold serves as a strong prior for a novel view generator to produce photorealistic and geometrically accurate views at arbitrary cameras. By explicitly conditioning on a 3D-consistent scaffold to perform reconstruction, One2Scene works stably under large camera motions, supporting immersive scene exploration. Extensive experiments show that One2Scene substantially outperforms state-of-the-art methods in panorama depth estimation, feed-forward 360° reconstruction, and explorable 3D scene generation. Code and models will be released.

</details>


### [170] [Efficient endometrial carcinoma screening via cross-modal synthesis and gradient distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19822)
*Dongjing Shan,Yamei Luo,Jiqing Xuan,Lu Huang,Jin Li,Mengchu Yang,Zeyu Chen,Fajin Lv,Yong Tang,Chunxiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-stage deep learning for endometrial cancer screening via ultrasound: structure-guided cross-modal MRI-to-US synthesis to augment scarce data, plus gradient-distilled lightweight classifier for efficient, accurate detection, achieving high sensitivity/specificity on a large multicenter cohort with low computational cost, outperforming expert sonographers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Endometrial carcinoma detection with transvaginal ultrasound is hampered by poor tissue contrast, operator dependence, and limited positive samples. There is a need for data-efficient, computationally light, generalizable AI that can operate in resource-limited primary care settings.

Method: A structure-guided cross-modal generation network synthesizes diverse, high-fidelity ultrasound images from unpaired MRI data while preserving critical anatomical junctions. A lightweight screening network uses gradient distillation to transfer discriminative knowledge from a high-capacity teacher model, guiding sparse attention to task-relevant regions. The workflow combines cross-modal synthetic augmentation with a student model for efficient inference.

Result: On a multicenter cohort of 7,951 participants, the approach achieved 99.5% sensitivity, 97.2% specificity, and AUC 0.987, with a computational cost of 0.289 GFLOPs, substantially outperforming average expert sonographer accuracy.

Conclusion: Cross-modal synthetic data augmentation paired with a knowledge-driven, efficient inference strategy can democratize expert-level, real-time EC screening in resource-constrained primary care settings.

Abstract: Early detection of myometrial invasion is critical for the staging and life-saving management of endometrial carcinoma (EC), a prevalent global malignancy. Transvaginal ultrasound serves as the primary, accessible screening modality in resource-constrained primary care settings; however, its diagnostic reliability is severely hindered by low tissue contrast, high operator dependence, and a pronounced scarcity of positive pathological samples. Existing artificial intelligence solutions struggle to overcome this severe class imbalance and the subtle imaging features of invasion, particularly under the strict computational limits of primary care clinics. Here we present an automated, highly efficient two-stage deep learning framework that resolves both data and computational bottlenecks in EC screening. To mitigate pathological data scarcity, we develop a structure-guided cross-modal generation network that synthesizes diverse, high-fidelity ultrasound images from unpaired magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) data, strictly preserving clinically essential anatomical junctions. Furthermore, we introduce a lightweight screening network utilizing gradient distillation, which transfers discriminative knowledge from a high-capacity teacher model to dynamically guide sparse attention towards task-critical regions. Evaluated on a large, multicenter cohort of 7,951 participants, our model achieves a sensitivity of 99.5\%, a specificity of 97.2\%, and an area under the curve of 0.987 at a minimal computational cost (0.289 GFLOPs), substantially outperforming the average diagnostic accuracy of expert sonographers. Our approach demonstrates that combining cross-modal synthetic augmentation with knowledge-driven efficient modeling can democratize expert-level, real-time cancer screening for resource-constrained primary care settings.

</details>


### [171] [TraceVision: Trajectory-Aware Vision-Language Model for Human-Like Spatial Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19768)
*Fan Yang,Shurong Zheng,Hongyin Zhao,Yufei Zhan,Xin Li,Yousong Zhu,Chaoyang Zhao Ming Tang,Jinqiao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TraceVision introduces a trajectory-aware vision-language model with a TVP module for joint spatial reasoning over trajectories and regions, including keypoint-based geometric simplification, a three-stage training pipeline, trajectory-guided segmentation and video understanding, plus a RILN dataset; it achieves state-of-the-art results on trajectory-guided tasks and enhances interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LVLMs currently emphasize global image understanding and struggle to align descriptions with specific regions or human-like attention trajectories, hindering explainability and fine-grained spatial reasoning.

Method: Propose Trajectory-aware Visual Perception (TVP) for bidirectional fusion of visual features and trajectory information; use geometric simplification to extract semantic keypoints from trajectories; implement a three-stage training pipeline where trajectories guide description generation and region localization; extend to trajectory-guided segmentation and video scene understanding, enabling cross-frame tracking and temporal attention analysis; construct the RILN dataset for reasoning-based localized narratives.

Result: The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on trajectory-guided captioning, text-guided trajectory prediction, understanding, and segmentation across tasks; demonstrates improved cross-frame tracking and interpretable spatial interaction.

Conclusion: TraceVision establishes a foundation for intuitive spatial interaction and interpretable visual understanding, by integrating trajectory-aware perception, localization, and generation within a unified end-to-end framework.

Abstract: Recent Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) demonstrate remarkable capabilities in image understanding and natural language generation. However, current approaches focus predominantly on global image understanding, struggling to simulate human visual attention trajectories and explain associations between descriptions and specific regions. We propose TraceVision, a unified vision-language model integrating trajectory-aware spatial understanding in an end-to-end framework. TraceVision employs a Trajectory-aware Visual Perception (TVP) module for bidirectional fusion of visual features and trajectory information. We design geometric simplification to extract semantic keypoints from raw trajectories and propose a three-stage training pipeline where trajectories guide description generation and region localization. We extend TraceVision to trajectory-guided segmentation and video scene understanding, enabling cross-frame tracking and temporal attention analysis. We construct the Reasoning-based Interactive Localized Narratives (RILN) dataset to enhance logical reasoning and interpretability. Extensive experiments on trajectory-guided captioning, text-guided trajectory prediction, understanding, and segmentation demonstrate that TraceVision achieves state-of-the-art performance, establishing a foundation for intuitive spatial interaction and interpretable visual understanding.

</details>


### [172] [GOAL: Geometrically Optimal Alignment for Continual Generalized Category Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19872)
*Jizhou Han,Chenhao Ding,SongLin Dong,Yuhang He,Shaokun Wang,Qiang Wang,Yihong Gong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GOAL introduces a fixed Equiangular Tight Frame (ETF) classifier for continual generalized category discovery, stabilizing learning and reducing forgetting compared with prior methods such as Happy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In continual GCD, updating classifier weights leads to forgetting of known classes and misalignment of features; a fixed geometric classifier like ETF can preserve class relationships and provide a stable backbone for integrating new classes.

Method: Use a fixed ETF classifier to enforce consistent angular geometry in the classifier layer. Perform supervised alignment for labeled samples and confidence-guided alignment for novel samples, avoiding updates to the classifier weights while integrating new classes incrementally.

Result: On four benchmarks, GOAL outperforms the prior method Happy, reducing forgetting by 16.1% and boosting novel class discovery by 3.2%.

Conclusion: GOAL provides a robust solution for long-horizon continual discovery by maintaining stable feature geometry and preventing degradation of old knowledge while assimilating new classes.

Abstract: Continual Generalized Category Discovery (C-GCD) requires identifying novel classes from unlabeled data while retaining knowledge of known classes over time. Existing methods typically update classifier weights dynamically, resulting in forgetting and inconsistent feature alignment. We propose GOAL, a unified framework that introduces a fixed Equiangular Tight Frame (ETF) classifier to impose a consistent geometric structure throughout learning. GOAL conducts supervised alignment for labeled samples and confidence-guided alignment for novel samples, enabling stable integration of new classes without disrupting old ones. Experiments on four benchmarks show that GOAL outperforms the prior method Happy, reducing forgetting by 16.1% and boosting novel class discovery by 3.2%, establishing a strong solution for long-horizon continual discovery.

</details>


### [173] [Make Some Noise: Unsupervised Remote Sensing Change Detection Using Latent Space Perturbations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19881)
*Blaž Rolih,Matic Fučka,Filip Wolf,Luka Čehovin Zajc*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MaSoN is an unsupervised change detection method that synthesizes diverse latent-space changes during training, guided by target data statistics, enabling robust, modality-flexible detection and achieving state-of-the-art results across five benchmarks (avg F1 +14.1pp).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current UCD methods rely on training-free foundation models or pixel-space synthetic changes built on handcrafted rules, which limit generalization to unseen, complex or rare change types. A data-driven, latent-space approach can better capture domain-specific variations.

Method: MaSoN (Make Some Noise) implements end-to-end UCD by generating changes directly in the latent feature space during training. It dynamically estimates changes from feature statistics of target data, producing diverse changes aligned with the target domain and easily extending to new modalities (e.g., SAR).

Result: Delivers state-of-the-art performance on five benchmarks, with an average F1 score improvement of 14.1 percentage points over prior methods.

Conclusion: By learning data-driven, latent-space changes, MaSoN improves generalization to diverse and unseen change types and modalities, reducing dependence on handcrafted rules and external datasets.

Abstract: Unsupervised change detection (UCD) in remote sensing aims to localise semantic changes between two images of the same region without relying on labelled data during training. Most recent approaches rely either on frozen foundation models in a training-free manner or on training with synthetic changes generated in pixel space. Both strategies inherently rely on predefined assumptions about change types, typically introduced through handcrafted rules, external datasets, or auxiliary generative models. Due to these assumptions, such methods fail to generalise beyond a few change types, limiting their real-world usage, especially in rare or complex scenarios. To address this, we propose MaSoN (Make Some Noise), an end-to-end UCD framework that synthesises diverse changes directly in the latent feature space during training. It generates changes that are dynamically estimated using feature statistics of target data, enabling diverse yet data-driven variation aligned with the target domain. It also easily extends to new modalities, such as SAR. MaSoN generalises strongly across diverse change types and achieves state-of-the-art performance on five benchmarks, improving the average F1 score by 14.1 percentage points. Project page: https://blaz-r.github.io/mason_ucd

</details>


### [174] [Open-vocabulary 3D scene perception in industrial environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19823)
*Keno Moenck,Adrian Philip Florea,Julian Koch,Thorsten Schüppstuhl*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free open-vocabulary 3D perception pipeline that uses merged superpoints for mask proposals, avoiding pre-trained class-agnostic segmentation, and validates with IndustrialCLIP in a 3D industrial scene showing qualitative success in segmenting industrial objects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-vocabulary 3D perception aims to recognize arbitrary concepts, but existing 2D VLFM-based methods rely on class-agnostic 3D segmentation models trained on non-industrial data, which generalize poorly to industrial objects; a domain-specific, training-free alternative is needed.

Method: Generate masks by merging pre-computed superpoints using semantic features; do not rely on external instance proposals; apply IndustrialCLIP (domain-adapted VLFM) for open-vocabulary querying; evaluate on representative 3D industrial workshop scene.

Result: Qualitative demonstration of successful segmentation of industrial objects; no quantitative metrics provided in abstract.

Conclusion: Shows feasibility of training-free, open-vocabulary 3D perception in industrial settings and that industrial-domain VLFM (IndustrialCLIP) can support open-vocabulary segmentation in 3D when combined with superpoint-based masks.

Abstract: Autonomous vision applications in production, intralogistics, or manufacturing environments require perception capabilities beyond a small, fixed set of classes. Recent open-vocabulary methods, leveraging 2D Vision-Language Foundation Models (VLFMs), target this task but often rely on class-agnostic segmentation models pre-trained on non-industrial datasets (e.g., household scenes). In this work, we first demonstrate that such models fail to generalize, performing poorly on common industrial objects. Therefore, we propose a training-free, open-vocabulary 3D perception pipeline that overcomes this limitation. Instead of using a pre-trained model to generate instance proposals, our method simply generates masks by merging pre-computed superpoints based on their semantic features. Following, we evaluate the domain-adapted VLFM "IndustrialCLIP" on a representative 3D industrial workshop scene for open-vocabulary querying. Our qualitative results demonstrate successful segmentation of industrial objects.

</details>


### [175] [TextShield-R1: Reinforced Reasoning for Tampered Text Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19828)
*Chenfan Qu,Yiwu Zhong,Jian Liu,Xuekang Zhu,Bohan Yu,Lianwen Jin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TextShield-R1 is a reinforcement-learning based multimodal LLM for tampered text detection and reasoning, featuring a forensic continual pre-training curriculum, group relative policy optimization during fine-tuning, OCR rectification for localization, and the Text Forensics Reasoning (TFR) benchmark; it achieves state-of-the-art interpretable tampered-text detection and robust cross-language/domain evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tampered images are increasingly prevalent and pose security risks. While multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) can analyze forgeries, they struggle to detect micro-level artifacts and precisely localize tampered text, and rely on expensive annotations for reasoning about forgeries.

Method: Forensic Continual Pre-training: an easy-to-hard curriculum leveraging large-scale cheap data from natural image forensic tasks and OCR tasks. Fine-tuning with Group Relative Policy Optimization using novel reward functions to reduce annotation needs and improve reasoning. Inference-time OCR Rectification to refine localization by exploiting the MLLM's OCR capabilities. Proposed Text Forensics Reasoning (TFR) benchmark with 45k+ real and tampered images across 16 languages, 10 tampering techniques, and diverse domains, including rich reasoning-style annotations.

Result: Extensive experiments show that TextShield-R1 significantly advances the state of the art in interpretable tampered-text detection.

Conclusion: TextShield-R1 introduces the first RL-based MLLM solution for tampered text detection and reasoning, supported by the comprehensive TFR benchmark; it demonstrates strong performance, reduced annotation dependency, and robust cross-style, cross-method, and cross-language evaluation.

Abstract: The growing prevalence of tampered images poses serious security threats, highlighting the urgent need for reliable detection methods. Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) demonstrate strong potential in analyzing tampered images and generating interpretations. However, they still struggle with identifying micro-level artifacts, exhibit low accuracy in localizing tampered text regions, and heavily rely on expensive annotations for forgery interpretation. To this end, we introduce TextShield-R1, the first reinforcement learning based MLLM solution for tampered text detection and reasoning. Specifically, our approach introduces Forensic Continual Pre-training, an easy-to-hard curriculum that well prepares the MLLM for tampered text detection by harnessing the large-scale cheap data from natural image forensic and OCR tasks. During fine-tuning, we perform Group Relative Policy Optimization with novel reward functions to reduce annotation dependency and improve reasoning capabilities. At inference time, we enhance localization accuracy via OCR Rectification, a method that leverages the MLLM's strong text recognition abilities to refine its predictions. Furthermore, to support rigorous evaluation, we introduce the Text Forensics Reasoning (TFR) benchmark, comprising over 45k real and tampered images across 16 languages, 10 tampering techniques, and diverse domains. Rich reasoning-style annotations are included, allowing for comprehensive assessment. Our TFR benchmark simultaneously addresses seven major limitations of existing benchmarks and enables robust evaluation under cross-style, cross-method, and cross-language conditions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TextShield-R1 significantly advances the state of the art in interpretable tampered text detection.

</details>


### [176] [M3S-Net: Multimodal Feature Fusion Network Based on Multi-scale Data for Ultra-short-term PV Power Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19832)
*Penghui Niu,Taotao Cai,Suqi Zhang,Junhua Gu,Ping Zhang,Qiqi Liu,Jianxin Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: M3S-Net fuses multi-scale visual and meteorological data for ultra-short-term PV forecasting using partial convolutions for cloud boundaries, FFT-based time-frequency analysis, and a cross-modal Mamba module with dynamic C-matrix swapping; achieves 6.2% MAE improvement on 10-minute forecasts on a fine-grained PV dataset; dataset and code to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address inherent variability of solar irradiance due to cloud advection; limitations of existing multimodal methods that rely on shallow fusion and binary cloud masks.

Method: Proposes multi-scale partial channel selection network with partial convolutions to isolate optically thin cloud boundaries; multi-scale sequence-to-image network using FFT-based time-frequency representation; cross-modal Mamba interaction module with dynamic C-matrix swapping to couple visual and temporal streams with linear complexity.

Result: MAE reduction of 6.2% for 10-minute forecasts vs state-of-the-art baselines on a new fine-grained PV power dataset; dataset and code will be available at the given GitHub.

Conclusion: M3S-Net demonstrates effective fine-grained feature extraction and cross-modal coupling for ultra-short-term PV forecasting, with scalable complexity and impact on forecast accuracy.

Abstract: The inherent intermittency and high-frequency variability of solar irradiance, particularly during rapid cloud advection, present significant stability challenges to high-penetration photovoltaic grids. Although multimodal forecasting has emerged as a viable mitigation strategy, existing architectures predominantly rely on shallow feature concatenation and binary cloud segmentation, thereby failing to capture the fine-grained optical features of clouds and the complex spatiotemporal coupling between visual and meteorological modalities. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes M3S-Net, a novel multimodal feature fusion network based on multi-scale data for ultra-short-term PV power forecasting. First, a multi-scale partial channel selection network leverages partial convolutions to explicitly isolate the boundary features of optically thin clouds, effectively transcending the precision limitations of coarse-grained binary masking. Second, a multi-scale sequence to image analysis network employs Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)-based time-frequency representation to disentangle the complex periodicity of meteorological data across varying time horizons. Crucially, the model incorporates a cross-modal Mamba interaction module featuring a novel dynamic C-matrix swapping mechanism. By exchanging state-space parameters between visual and temporal streams, this design conditions the state evolution of one modality on the context of the other, enabling deep structural coupling with linear computational complexity, thus overcoming the limitations of shallow concatenation. Experimental validation on the newly constructed fine-grained PV power dataset demonstrates that M3S-Net achieves a mean absolute error reduction of 6.2% in 10-minute forecasts compared to state-of-the-art baselines. The dataset and source code will be available at https://github.com/she1110/FGPD.

</details>


### [177] [When Pretty Isn't Useful: Investigating Why Modern Text-to-Image Models Fail as Reliable Training Data Generators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19946)
*Krzysztof Adamkiewicz,Brian Moser,Stanislav Frolov,Tobias Christian Nauen,Federico Raue,Andreas Dengel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: New text-to-image models with higher visual fidelity do not improve, and can worsen, classifier performance when used to generate training data. Newer models collapse toward a narrow, aesthetics-driven distribution, reducing data diversity and label-image alignment, challenging the idea that realism implies data realism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate whether synthetic data from state-of-the-art T2I diffusion models can substitute real training data for image classification.

Method: Generate large-scale synthetic datasets using state-of-the-art T2I models released 2022–2025, train standard classifiers solely on synthetic data, and evaluate on real test data.

Result: Classification accuracy declines as newer T2I models are used to generate training data; synthetic data exhibit reduced diversity and poor label-image alignment due to an aesthetic-centric distribution; progress in generative realism does not imply progress in data realism.

Conclusion: Modern T2I models are not reliable standalone training data generators; there is a need to rethink data-generation strategies and incorporate mechanisms to preserve diversity and alignment with real-world labels.

Abstract: Recent text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models produce visually stunning images and demonstrate excellent prompt following. But do they perform well as synthetic vision data generators? In this work, we revisit the promise of synthetic data as a scalable substitute for real training sets and uncover a surprising performance regression. We generate large-scale synthetic datasets using state-of-the-art T2I models released between 2022 and 2025, train standard classifiers solely on this synthetic data, and evaluate them on real test data. Despite observable advances in visual fidelity and prompt adherence, classification accuracy on real test data consistently declines with newer T2I models as training data generators. Our analysis reveals a hidden trend: These models collapse to a narrow, aesthetic-centric distribution that undermines diversity and label-image alignment. Overall, our findings challenge a growing assumption in vision research, namely that progress in generative realism implies progress in data realism. We thus highlight an urgent need to rethink the capabilities of modern T2I models as reliable training data generators.

</details>


### [178] [DerMAE: Improving skin lesion classification through conditioned latent diffusion and MAE distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19848)
*Francisco Filho,Kelvin Cunha,Fábio Papais,Emanoel dos Santos,Rodrigo Mota,Thales Bezerra,Erico Medeiros,Paulo Borba,Tsang Ing Ren*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Class-conditioned diffusion-generated synthetic dermatology images, combined with MAE self-supervised pretraining on synthetic data and knowledge distillation to a smaller ViT, address class imbalance in skin lesion datasets and enable efficient on-device inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Severe class imbalance in skin lesion datasets yields biased decision boundaries; need robust domain-specific features and practical deployment on mobile devices.

Method: 1) Generate class-conditioned diffusion model images to balance malignant/benign classes. 2) Pretrain large ViT with MAE using synthetic data to learn robust features. 3) Distill knowledge to a smaller ViT student for mobile deployment.

Result: MAE pretraining on synthetic data plus distillation improves classification performance and enables efficient on-device inference.

Conclusion: Synthetic data with MAE pretraining and distillation is effective for robust, domain-relevant feature learning and practical clinical deployment on mobile devices.

Abstract: Skin lesion classification datasets often suffer from severe class imbalance, with malignant cases significantly underrepresented, leading to biased decision boundaries during deep learning training. We address this challenge using class-conditioned diffusion models to generate synthetic dermatological images, followed by self-supervised MAE pretraining to enable huge ViT models to learn robust, domain-relevant features. To support deployment in practical clinical settings, where lightweight models are required, we apply knowledge distillation to transfer these representations to a smaller ViT student suitable for mobile devices. Our results show that MAE pretraining on synthetic data, combined with distillation, improves classification performance while enabling efficient on-device inference for practical clinical use.

</details>


### [179] [Contrastive meta-domain adaptation for robust skin lesion classification across clinical and acquisition conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19857)
*Rodrigo Mota,Kelvin Cunha,Emanoel dos Santos,Fábio Papais,Francisco Filho,Thales Bezerra,Erico Medeiros,Paulo Borba,Tsang Ing Ren*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Transfers visual representations from large dermoscopic datasets to clinical skin images using visual meta-domains, yielding improved robustness and reduced domain gap in skin lesion classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning performance degrades when subjected to acquisition variability and domain-specific visual characteristics; clinical deployment requires models robust to domain shifts between dermoscopic (highly controlled) and clinical images.

Method: An adaptation strategy grounded in visual meta-domains that transfers visual representations from large dermoscopic datasets into clinical image domains to improve generalization.

Result: Across multiple dermatology datasets, the approach yields consistent gains in classification performance and reduces the gap between dermoscopic and clinical images, indicating improved generalization robustness.

Conclusion: Domain-aware training that leverages visual meta-domains is important for deployable skin lesion classification systems and helps bridge cross-domain disparities between dermoscopic and clinical data.

Abstract: Deep learning models for dermatological image analysis remain sensitive to acquisition variability and domain-specific visual characteristics, leading to performance degradation when deployed in clinical settings. We investigate how visual artifacts and domain shifts affect deep learning-based skin lesion classification. We propose an adaptation strategy, grounded in the idea of visual meta-domains, that transfers visual representations from larger dermoscopic datasets into clinical image domains, thereby improving generalization robustness. Experiments across multiple dermatology datasets show consistent gains in classification performance and reduced gaps between dermoscopic and clinical images. These results emphasize the importance of domain-aware training for deployable systems.

</details>


### [180] [Brewing Stronger Features: Dual-Teacher Distillation for Multispectral Earth Observation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19863)
*Filip Wolf,Blaž Rolih,Luka Čehovin Zajc*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Dual-teacher contrastive distillation for multispectral EO data that aligns multispectral and optical foundation models, enabling cross-modal knowledge transfer and achieving state-of-the-art results across optical and multispectral benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EO data exhibit diverse sensors and modalities; a single universal model is unlikely to perform optimally across all data. Efficient cross-modal transfer between specialized EOFMs is needed to leverage complementary information from multispectral and optical streams.

Method: Train a student EOFM under a dual-teacher contrastive distillation setup: a multispectral teacher guides the student with respect to multispectral semantics, while an optical VFM teacher provides cross-modal supervision to align representations with optical foundation models. The approach follows a contrastive self-distillation paradigm akin to modern VFMs, fostering coherent cross-modal, cross-sensor representations.

Result: Empirical evaluation on diverse optical and multispectral benchmarks shows the student adapts to multispectral data without compromising optical-only performance, achieving state-of-the-art in semantic segmentation, change detection, and classification (average gains: 3.64 percentage points in segmentation, 1.2 in change detection, 1.31 in classification). Demonstrates scalability and efficiency of contrastive distillation for heterogeneous EO sources.

Conclusion: Contrastive distillation with dual teachers is a principled and efficient path for scalable representation learning across heterogeneous EO data, enabling coexistence and transfer among specialized EOFMs while preserving performance across modalities.

Abstract: Foundation models are transforming Earth Observation (EO), yet the diversity of EO sensors and modalities makes a single universal model unrealistic. Multiple specialized EO foundation models (EOFMs) will likely coexist, making efficient knowledge transfer across modalities essential. Most existing EO pretraining relies on masked image modeling, which emphasizes local reconstruction but provides limited control over global semantic structure. To address this, we propose a dual-teacher contrastive distillation framework for multispectral imagery that aligns the student's pretraining objective with the contrastive self-distillation paradigm of modern optical vision foundation models (VFMs). Our approach combines a multispectral teacher with an optical VFM teacher, enabling coherent cross-modal representation learning. Experiments across diverse optical and multispectral benchmarks show that our model adapts to multispectral data without compromising performance on optical-only inputs, achieving state-of-the-art results in both settings, with an average improvement of 3.64 percentage points in semantic segmentation, 1.2 in change detection, and 1.31 in classification tasks. This demonstrates that contrastive distillation provides a principled and efficient approach to scalable representation learning across heterogeneous EO data sources. Project page: \textcolor{magenta}{https://wolfilip.github.io/DEO/}.

</details>


### [181] [ApET: Approximation-Error Guided Token Compression for Efficient VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19870)
*Qiankun Ma,Ziyao Zhang,Haofei Wang,Jie Chen,Zhen Song,Hairong Zheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An attention-free token compression method (ApET) that reconstructs visual tokens via a small basis set and uses reconstruction error to drop uninformative tokens, achieving high performance retention with substantial token reduction and compatibility with FlashAttention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-Language Models suffer from redundant visual tokens causing high compute; prior attention-based pruning introduces bias and conflicts with efficient kernels like FlashAttention; a non-attention method is needed for practical acceleration.

Method: ApET constructs a linear approximation of original visual tokens using a compact basis set. The approximation error guides pruning by removing the least informative tokens. The design is attention-free and can integrate with efficient attention kernels (e.g., FlashAttention).

Result: Empirically, ApET preserves 95.2% of image-understanding performance and 100.4% of video-understanding performance while reducing token budgets by 88.9% (images) and 87.5% (video). Provides compatibility with FlashAttention to enable further acceleration.

Conclusion: ApET demonstrates that information-theoretic, attention-free token compression can achieve substantial speedups with minimal performance loss, facilitating practical VLM deployment; code is available at the authors’ repository.

Abstract: Recent Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated remarkable multimodal understanding capabilities, yet the redundant visual tokens incur prohibitive computational overhead and degrade inference efficiency. Prior studies typically relies on [CLS] attention or text-vision cross-attention to identify and discard redundant visual tokens. Despite promising results, such solutions are prone to introduce positional bias and, more critically, are incompatible with efficient attention kernels such as FlashAttention, limiting their practical deployment for VLM acceleration. In this paper, we step away from attention dependencies and revisit visual token compression from an information-theoretic perspective, aiming to maximally preserve visual information without any attention involvement. We present ApET, an Approximation-Error guided Token compression framework. ApET first reconstructs the original visual tokens with a small set of basis tokens via linear approximation, then leverages the approximation error to identify and drop the least informative tokens. Extensive experiments across multiple VLMs and benchmarks demonstrate that ApET retains 95.2% of the original performance on image-understanding tasks and even attains 100.4% on video-understanding tasks, while compressing the token budgets by 88.9% and 87.5%, respectively. Thanks to its attention-free design, ApET seamlessly integrates with FlashAttention, enabling further inference acceleration and making VLM deployment more practical. Code is available at https://github.com/MaQianKun0/ApET.

</details>


### [182] [Descriptor: Dataset of Parasitoid Wasps and Associated Hymenoptera (DAPWH)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20028)
*Joao Manoel Herrera Pinheiro,Gabriela Do Nascimento Herrera,Luciana Bueno Dos Reis Fernandes,Alvaro Doria Dos Santos,Ricardo V. Godoy,Eduardo A. B. Almeida,Helena Carolina Onody,Marcelo Andrade Da Costa Vieira,Angelica Maria Penteado-Dias,Marcelo Becker*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A curated image dataset for Ichneumonoidea (Ichneumonidae and Braconidae) and related families to enable automated identification via computer vision, with 3,556 high‑resolution images and a COCO‑formatted subset (1,739 images) featuring multi‑class bounding boxes for the full body, wing venation, and scale bars.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Taxonomic difficulty and extreme diversity of Ichneumonoidea hinder traditional identification and biodiversity monitoring; robust digital resources are needed to develop automated identification systems and support agricultural management.

Method: Compile a curated set of high‑resolution images from Neotropical Ichneumonidae and Braconidae, include supplementary families to boost model robustness, and provide 1,739 COCO‑formatted annotations with multi‑class bounding boxes for body, wing venation, and scale bars.

Result: The dataset comprises 3,556 images; 1,739 are annotated in COCO format with bounding boxes for full body, wing venation, and scale bars, establishing a foundation for developing computer vision models to identify these taxa.

Conclusion: This resource addresses the scarcity of robust digital taxonomic data for hyper‑diverse parasitoid wasps and is expected to catalyze the development of automated identification systems and improved taxonomic workflows.

Abstract: Accurate taxonomic identification is the cornerstone of biodiversity monitoring and agricultural management, particularly for the hyper-diverse superfamily Ichneumonoidea. Comprising the families Ichneumonidae and Braconidae, these parasitoid wasps are ecologically critical for regulating insect populations, yet they remain one of the most taxonomically challenging groups due to their cryptic morphology and vast number of undescribed species. To address the scarcity of robust digital resources for these key groups, we present a curated image dataset designed to advance automated identification systems. The dataset contains 3,556 high-resolution images, primarily focused on Neotropical Ichneumonidae and Braconidae, while also including supplementary families such as Andrenidae, Apidae, Bethylidae, Chrysididae, Colletidae, Halictidae, Megachilidae, Pompilidae, and Vespidae to improve model robustness. Crucially, a subset of 1,739 images is annotated in COCO format, featuring multi-class bounding boxes for the full insect body, wing venation, and scale bars. This resource provides a foundation for developing computer vision models capable of identifying these families.

</details>


### [183] [BigMaQ: A Big Macaque Motion and Animation Dataset Bridging Image and 3D Pose Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19874)
*Lucas Martini,Alexander Lappe,Anna Bognár,Rufin Vogels,Martin A. Giese*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces BigMaQ, a large-scale 3D pose-and-shape macaque dataset with subject-specific textured avatars, plus BigMaQ500 for action recognition; shows pose descriptors improve recognition (mAP); code/data publicly available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between 3D pose-shape representations and animal action recognition for non-human primates, addressing limitations of keypoint-based or surface-only tracking.

Method: Collect and annotate 750+ scenes of interacting rhesus macaques; create subject-specific textured templates adapted to individuals; derive BigMaQ500 linking surface pose vectors to single frames; compare image/video encoders with/without pose descriptors; evaluate using mean average precision (mAP).

Result: Pose-informed features yield substantial improvements in mAP; first dataset to integrate dynamic 3D pose-shape with animal action recognition; provides resources to study appearance, posture, and social interaction; code and data released publicly.

Conclusion: BigMaQ advances the study of primate behavior and vision by enabling richer, 3D-aware representations for action understanding; sets groundwork for future multi-modal and cross-species analyses.

Abstract: The recognition of dynamic and social behavior in animals is fundamental for advancing ethology, ecology, medicine and neuroscience. Recent progress in deep learning has enabled automated behavior recognition from video, yet an accurate reconstruction of the three-dimensional (3D) pose and shape has not been integrated into this process. Especially for non-human primates, mesh-based tracking efforts lag behind those for other species, leaving pose descriptions restricted to sparse keypoints that are unable to fully capture the richness of action dynamics. To address this gap, we introduce the $\textbf{Big Ma}$ca$\textbf{Q}$ue 3D Motion and Animation Dataset ($\texttt{BigMaQ}$), a large-scale dataset comprising more than 750 scenes of interacting rhesus macaques with detailed 3D pose descriptions. Extending previous surface-based animal tracking methods, we construct subject-specific textured avatars by adapting a high-quality macaque template mesh to individual monkeys. This allows us to provide pose descriptions that are more accurate than previous state-of-the-art surface-based animal tracking methods. From the original dataset, we derive BigMaQ500, an action recognition benchmark that links surface-based pose vectors to single frames across multiple individual monkeys. By pairing features extracted from established image and video encoders with and without our pose descriptors, we demonstrate substantial improvements in mean average precision (mAP) when pose information is included. With these contributions, $\texttt{BigMaQ}$ establishes the first dataset that both integrates dynamic 3D pose-shape representations into the learning task of animal action recognition and provides a rich resource to advance the study of visual appearance, posture, and social interaction in non-human primates. The code and data are publicly available at https://martinivis.github.io/BigMaQ/ .

</details>


### [184] [SEAL-pose: Enhancing 3D Human Pose Estimation via a Learned Loss for Structural Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20051)
*Yeonsung Kim,Junggeun Do,Seunguk Do,Sangmin Kim,Jaesik Park,Jay-Yoon Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SEAL-pose introduces a learnable loss-net that assesses structural plausibility in a joint graph for 3D HPE, yielding improved per-joint accuracy and pose plausibility over backbones and explicit priors, across datasets and in the wild.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional supervised losses treat joints independently; hand-crafted priors are non-differentiable and require manual design; need differentiable, data-driven approach to capture complex dependencies.

Method: A loss-net, operating on a joint graph, is learned to evaluate structural plausibility; used as training objective to supervise a pose-net; data-driven learning of dependencies; tested with eight backbones across three 3D HPE benchmarks; cross-dataset and in-the-wild evaluation.

Result: Consistently reduces per-joint errors and improves pose plausibility across backbones; outperforms models with explicit structural constraints; shows relationship between loss-net and structural consistency; demonstrates cross-dataset and in-the-wild performance.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of data-driven, differentiable loss for enforcing structure in 3D HPE; SEAL-pose offers an end-to-end alternative to hand-crafted priors; generalizes across backbones and datasets; fosters learning of complex joint dependencies.

Abstract: 3D human pose estimation (HPE) is characterized by intricate local and global dependencies among joints. Conventional supervised losses are limited in capturing these correlations because they treat each joint independently. Previous studies have attempted to promote structural consistency through manually designed priors or rule-based constraints; however, these approaches typically require manual specification and are often non-differentiable, limiting their use as end-to-end training objectives. We propose SEAL-pose, a data-driven framework in which a learnable loss-net trains a pose-net by evaluating structural plausibility. Rather than relying on hand-crafted priors, our joint-graph-based design enables the loss-net to learn complex structural dependencies directly from data. Extensive experiments on three 3D HPE benchmarks with eight backbones show that SEAL-pose reduces per-joint errors and improves pose plausibility compared with the corresponding backbones across all settings. Beyond improving each backbone, SEAL-pose also outperforms models with explicit structural constraints, despite not enforcing any such constraints.
