Finally, we analyze the relationship between the loss-net and structural consistency, and evaluate SEAL-pose in cross-dataset and in-the-wild settings.

</details>


### [185] [Monocular Mesh Recovery and Body Measurement of Female Saanen Goats](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19896)
*Bo Jin,Shichao Zhao,Jin Lyu,Bin Zhang,Tao Yu,Liang An,Yebin Liu,Meili Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A dataset and a parametric 3D model for female Saanen goats are developed to enable accurate single-view 3D reconstruction and automated measurement of six body dimensions, enabling precision livestock farming.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable precise measurement of body size related to lactation performance in Saanen goats and address the lack of authentic goat-specific 3D data for reconstruction.

Method: Create FemaleSaanenGoat dataset with synchronized eight-view RGBD videos of 55 goats; apply multi-view DynamicFusion to fuse into 3D scans; build SaanenGoat parametric model with 41 joints and improved udder; register to scans; construct a shape space from 48 goats; enable single-view RGBD reconstruction and automatic measurement of six dimensions.

Result: Achieves high-precision 3D reconstruction and body measurements; superior accuracy compared to baselines; demonstrates potential for large-scale 3D vision in precision livestock farming.

Conclusion: Introduces a goat-specific 3D dataset and parametric model that enables accurate body measurements from RGBD data, supporting improved assessment of milk production potential and broader precision farming applications.

Abstract: The lactation performance of Saanen dairy goats, renowned for their high milk yield, is intrinsically linked to their body size, making accurate 3D body measurement essential for assessing milk production potential, yet existing reconstruction methods lack goat-specific authentic 3D data. To address this limitation, we establish the FemaleSaanenGoat dataset containing synchronized eight-view RGBD videos of 55 female Saanen goats (6-18 months). Using multi-view DynamicFusion, we fuse noisy, non-rigid point cloud sequences into high-fidelity 3D scans, overcoming challenges from irregular surfaces and rapid movement. Based on these scans, we develop SaanenGoat, a parametric 3D shape model specifically designed for female Saanen goats. This model features a refined template with 41 skeletal joints and enhanced udder representation, registered with our scan data. A comprehensive shape space constructed from 48 goats enables precise representation of diverse individual variations. With the help of SaanenGoat model, we get high-precision 3D reconstruction from single-view RGBD input, and achieve automated measurement of six critical body dimensions: body length, height, chest width, chest girth, hip width, and hip height. Experimental results demonstrate the superior accuracy of our method in both 3D reconstruction and body measurement, presenting a novel paradigm for large-scale 3D vision applications in precision livestock farming.

</details>


### [186] [ExpPortrait: Expressive Portrait Generation via Personalized Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19900)
*Junyi Wang,Yudong Guo,Boyang Guo,Shengming Yang,Juyong Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a high-fidelity personalized head representation and expression transfer for diffusion-based portrait video generation, enabling better disentanglement of identity and expression; uses this as a conditional signal to train a DiT-based generator, achieving improved identity preservation, expression accuracy, and temporal stability over prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current 2D landmarks and parametric models offer limited disentanglement and fail to capture personalized details, hindering expressive, coherent portrait video generation.

Method: Introduce a high-fidelity personalized head representation that models static global geometry and dynamic expression details; add an expression transfer module for personalized transfer of pose and expression across identities; train a diffusion-transformer (DiT) generator using this head model as conditioning.

Result: Extensive experiments on self- and cross-reenactment demonstrate superior identity preservation, expression accuracy, and temporal stability, with better capture of fine-grained motion details compared to previous models.

Conclusion: The proposed head representation and expression transfer enable more expressive, identity-consistent, and temporally stable portrait video synthesis, advancing controllable video reenactment with diffusion-based architectures.

Abstract: While diffusion models have shown great potential in portrait generation, generating expressive, coherent, and controllable cinematic portrait videos remains a significant challenge. Existing intermediate signals for portrait generation, such as 2D landmarks and parametric models, have limited disentanglement capabilities and cannot express personalized details due to their sparse or low-rank representation. Therefore, existing methods based on these models struggle to accurately preserve subject identity and expressions, hindering the generation of highly expressive portrait videos. To overcome these limitations, we propose a high-fidelity personalized head representation that more effectively disentangles expression and identity. This representation captures both static, subject-specific global geometry and dynamic, expression-related details. Furthermore, we introduce an expression transfer module to achieve personalized transfer of head pose and expression details between different identities. We use this sophisticated and highly expressive head model as a conditional signal to train a diffusion transformer (DiT)-based generator to synthesize richly detailed portrait videos. Extensive experiments on self- and cross-reenactment tasks demonstrate that our method outperforms previous models in terms of identity preservation, expression accuracy, and temporal stability, particularly in capturing fine-grained details of complex motion.

</details>


### [187] [HeatPrompt: Zero-Shot Vision-Language Modeling of Urban Heat Demand from Satellite Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20066)
*Kundan Thota,Xuanhao Mu,Thorsten Schlachter,Veit Hagenmeyer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HeatPrompt uses zero-shot vision-language models to extract semantic building attributes from satellite imagery and basic GIS to predict annual heat demand; an MLP trained on the generated captions yields substantial improvements (R^2 uplift ~93.7%, MAE ↓~30% vs baseline) and supports heat planning in data-scarce regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate heat-demand maps are essential for decarbonization but unavailable building-level data in many municipalities. A lightweight, scalable approach leveraging existing imagery and GIS can enable wide-area planning without detailed inventories.

Method: Utilize pretrained large vision-language models with a domain-specific prompt to generate captions from RGB satellite imagery. Extract visual attributes (e.g., roof age, density) that correlate with thermal load; train an MLP regressor on these captions, optionally integrating basic GIS and building-level features, to estimate annual heat demand.

Result: Reported R^2 uplift of 93.7% and MAE reduction of 30% relative to a baseline model; qualitative analysis indicates high-impact tokens align with high-demand zones, suggesting actionable insights for heat planning in data-scarce regions.

Conclusion: HeatPrompt demonstrates a lightweight, zero-shot vision-language approach to heat-demand estimation that can operate in data-scarce settings, enabling scalable planning support by translating semantic image captions into thermal-load predictions.

Abstract: Accurate heat-demand maps play a crucial role in decarbonizing space heating, yet most municipalities lack detailed building-level data needed to calculate them. We introduce HeatPrompt, a zero-shot vision-language energy modeling framework that estimates annual heat demand using semantic features extracted from satellite images, basic Geographic Information System (GIS), and building-level features. We feed pretrained Large Vision Language Models (VLMs) with a domain-specific prompt to act as an energy planner and extract the visual attributes such as roof age, building density, etc, from the RGB satellite image that correspond to the thermal load. A Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) regressor trained on these captions shows an $R^2$ uplift of 93.7% and shrinks the mean absolute error (MAE) by 30% compared to the baseline model. Qualitative analysis shows that high-impact tokens align with high-demand zones, offering lightweight support for heat planning in data-scarce regions.

</details>


### [188] [Gradient based Severity Labeling for Biomarker Classification in OCT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19907)
*Kiran Kokilepersaud,Mohit Prabhushankar,Ghassan AlRegib,Stephanie Trejo Corona,Charles Wykoff*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Severity-guided supervised contrastive learning for unlabeled OCT images improves diabetic retinopathy biomarker classification by up to 6% over self-supervised baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In medical imaging, standard augmentations can distort small biomarker regions. Aligning representations by disease severity preserves relevant structures and improves downstream biomarker tasks.

Method: Generate disease severity labels for unlabeled OCT scans using gradient responses from an anomaly detection algorithm; use these labels to train a supervised contrastive learning framework for biomarker classification.

Result: Biomarker classification accuracy improved by up to 6% over self-supervised baselines for key DR indicators.

Conclusion: Severity-aware contrastive learning effectively guides representation learning toward disease progression, enhancing downstream biomarker performance in medical imaging.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel selection strategy for contrastive learning for medical images. On natural images, contrastive learning uses augmentations to select positive and negative pairs for the contrastive loss. However, in the medical domain, arbitrary augmentations have the potential to distort small localized regions that contain the biomarkers we are interested in detecting. A more intuitive approach is to select samples with similar disease severity characteristics, since these samples are more likely to have similar structures related to the progression of a disease. To enable this, we introduce a method that generates disease severity labels for unlabeled OCT scans on the basis of gradient responses from an anomaly detection algorithm. These labels are used to train a supervised contrastive learning setup to improve biomarker classification accuracy by as much as 6% above self-supervised baselines for key indicators of Diabetic Retinopathy.

</details>


### [189] [StructXLIP: Enhancing Vision-language Models with Multimodal Structural Cues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20089)
*Zanxi Ruan,Qiuyu Kong,Songqun Gao,Yiming Wang,Marco Cristani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A fine-tuning paradigm, StructXLIP, leverages edge-based visual structure to improve vision-language alignment and cross-modal retrieval by applying three structure-centric losses and an auxiliary mutual-information objective on multimodal structure representations; it achieves state-of-the-art results and is plug-and-play with available code and models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge-based cues capture essential visual structure that is transferable across modalities. Traditional CLIP optimizes only visual–textual mutual information without explicit structural supervision, which can hinder alignment for long, detail-rich captions. By focusing on structure, StructXLIP aims to produce more robust, semantically stable multimodal representations and better generalization, especially for detailed descriptions.

Method: 1) Extract edge maps (e.g., Canny) from images as structural proxies. 2) Filter captions to emphasize structure, creating structure-centric text. 3) Fine-tune with three structure-centric losses: (i) align edge maps with structural text; (ii) match local edge regions to textual chunks; (iii) connect edge maps to color images to prevent representation drift. Theoretically, in addition to maximizing mutual information between visual and textual embeddings (as in CLIP), StructXLIP maximizes mutual information between multimodal structural representations, guiding optimization toward more robust minima.

Result: Outperforms current competitors on cross-modal retrieval in general and specialized domains. Demonstrates that structural supervision can boost vision–language alignment and serves as a general plug-and-play boosting recipe for future methods. Code and pretrained models are publicly available.

Conclusion: StructXLIP shows that emphasizing structural cues through edge-based representations and structure-centric textual filtering yields more robust vision–language alignment and better cross-modal retrieval, and can be readily integrated into existing frameworks to boost performance.

Abstract: Edge-based representations are fundamental cues for visual understanding, a principle rooted in early vision research and still central today. We extend this principle to vision-language alignment, showing that isolating and aligning structural cues across modalities can greatly benefit fine-tuning on long, detail-rich captions, with a specific focus on improving cross-modal retrieval. We introduce StructXLIP, a fine-tuning alignment paradigm that extracts edge maps (e.g., Canny), treating them as proxies for the visual structure of an image, and filters the corresponding captions to emphasize structural cues, making them "structure-centric". Fine-tuning augments the standard alignment loss with three structure-centric losses: (i) aligning edge maps with structural text, (ii) matching local edge regions to textual chunks, and (iii) connecting edge maps to color images to prevent representation drift. From a theoretical standpoint, while standard CLIP maximizes the mutual information between visual and textual embeddings, StructXLIP additionally maximizes the mutual information between multimodal structural representations. This auxiliary optimization is intrinsically harder, guiding the model toward more robust and semantically stable minima, enhancing vision-language alignment. Beyond outperforming current competitors on cross-modal retrieval in both general and specialized domains, our method serves as a general boosting recipe that can be integrated into future approaches in a plug-and-play manner. Code and pretrained models are publicly available at: https://github.com/intelligolabs/StructXLIP.

</details>


### [190] [Multi-Modal Representation Learning via Semi-Supervised Rate Reduction for Generalized Category Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19910)
*Wei He,Xianghan Meng,Zhiyuan Huang,Xianbiao Qi,Rong Xiao,Chun-Guang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A novel multi-modal framework for Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) named SSR^2-GCD, using Semi-Supervised Rate Reduction to shape cross- and intra-modality representations, and integrating Vision-Language Model prompts to boost cross-modal knowledge transfer; shows strong performance on generic and fine-grained benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GCD requires identifying known and unknown categories with partial labels for known categories. Existing approaches emphasize inter-modality alignment but neglect intra-modality structural relationships, which can hamper the formation of meaningful representation spaces and open-set discrimination. There is a need to enforce intra-modality alignment to produce desirable distribution structures and improve generalization, while leveraging cross-modal transfer through prompts.

Method: Introduce SSR^2-GCD, a multi-modal representation learning framework for GCD based on Semi-Supervised Rate Reduction to encourage desired intra-modality relationships and cross-modality alignment. Use rate reduction objective to control the structure of representations. Incorporate prompt candidates from Vision-Language Models to exploit inter-modal alignment and boost knowledge transfer across modalities.

Result: Extensive experiments on generic and fine-grained benchmark datasets show superior performance of SSR^2-GCD compared to baselines, validating its effectiveness in learning structured cross-modality representations for GCD.

Conclusion: SSR^2-GCD provides a principled approach to GCD by combining semi-supervised rate reduction with cross-modal learning and prompt-based transfer from VLMs, yielding improved performance on diverse GCD benchmarks and highlighting the importance of intra-modality structure in representation learning.

Abstract: Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) aims to identify both known and unknown categories, with only partial labels given for the known categories, posing a challenging open-set recognition problem. State-of-the-art approaches for GCD task are usually built on multi-modality representation learning, which is heavily dependent upon inter-modality alignment. However, few of them cast a proper intra-modality alignment to generate a desired underlying structure of representation distributions. In this paper, we propose a novel and effective multi-modal representation learning framework for GCD via Semi-Supervised Rate Reduction, called SSR$^2$-GCD, to learn cross-modality representations with desired structural properties based on emphasizing to properly align intra-modality relationships. Moreover, to boost knowledge transfer, we integrate prompt candidates by leveraging the inter-modal alignment offered by Vision Language Models. We conduct extensive experiments on generic and fine-grained benchmark datasets demonstrating superior performance of our approach.

</details>


### [191] [Transcending the Annotation Bottleneck: AI-Powered Discovery in Biology and Medicine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20100)
*Soumick Chatterjee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unsupervised and self-supervised learning (SSL) can leverage the intrinsic structure of unlabeled biomedical data to enable phenotype discovery, morpho-genetic linkage, and anomaly detection at biobank scale, often rivaling supervised methods while reducing dependency on expert annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The major bottleneck in biomedical AI is the reliance on expert-labeled data. Biobank-scale datasets contain vast unlabeled information that SSL can exploit to learn meaningful representations without costly annotations.

Method: A literature synthesis of seminal and recent SSL approaches applied to biomedicine across modalities (MRI, histology, genomics) and tasks (phenotype discovery, genotype–phenotype mapping, pathology detection), emphasizing learning frameworks that operate without labels and their evaluation against supervised baselines.

Result: SSL frameworks can derive heritable cardiac traits, predict spatial gene expression in histology, and detect pathologies with performance on par with or surpassing supervised methods, enabling discovery without labeled data and reducing annotation burden.

Conclusion: Learning without labels unlocks the potential of biobank-scale data, enabling discovery of novel phenotypes and genotype–phenotype links while diminishing reliance on expert annotations; continued development and integration of SSL approaches are warranted.

Abstract: The dependence on expert annotation has long constituted the primary rate-limiting step in the application of artificial intelligence to biomedicine. While supervised learning drove the initial wave of clinical algorithms, a paradigm shift towards unsupervised and self-supervised learning (SSL) is currently unlocking the latent potential of biobank-scale datasets. By learning directly from the intrinsic structure of data - whether pixels in a magnetic resonance image (MRI), voxels in a volumetric scan, or tokens in a genomic sequence - these methods facilitate the discovery of novel phenotypes, the linkage of morphology to genetics, and the detection of anomalies without human bias. This article synthesises seminal and recent advances in "learning without labels," highlighting how unsupervised frameworks can derive heritable cardiac traits, predict spatial gene expression in histology, and detect pathologies with performance that rivals or exceeds supervised counterparts.

</details>


### [192] [Augmented Radiance Field: A General Framework for Enhanced Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19916)
*Yixin Yang,Bojian Wu,Yang Zhou,Hui Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Augments 3D Gaussian Splatting with an enhanced Gaussian kernel that models view-dependent specular effects and an error-driven compensation strategy, improving rendering quality and parameter efficiency over NeRF baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3DGS relies on spherical harmonics for color encoding, which constrains separation of diffuse and specular components and struggles with complex reflections; a mechanism to accurately model specularities while maintaining real-time rendering is needed.

Method: 1) Initialize with 2D Gaussians; 2) adaptively insert and optimize enhanced Gaussian kernels to encode specular effects via view-dependent opacity; 3) apply an error-driven compensation strategy to improve existing 3DGS scenes; 4) produce an augmented radiance field.

Result: Experiments show the method surpasses state-of-the-art NeRF methods in rendering performance and achieves greater parameter efficiency.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling specular effects and using an error-driven compensation strategy extend 3DGS, yielding improved rendering quality and efficiency; a project page is provided.

Abstract: Due to the real-time rendering performance, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as the leading method for radiance field reconstruction. However, its reliance on spherical harmonics for color encoding inherently limits its ability to separate diffuse and specular components, making it challenging to accurately represent complex reflections. To address this, we propose a novel enhanced Gaussian kernel that explicitly models specular effects through view-dependent opacity. Meanwhile, we introduce an error-driven compensation strategy to improve rendering quality in existing 3DGS scenes. Our method begins with 2D Gaussian initialization and then adaptively inserts and optimizes enhanced Gaussian kernels, ultimately producing an augmented radiance field. Experiments demonstrate that our method not only surpasses state-of-the-art NeRF methods in rendering performance but also achieves greater parameter efficiency. Project page at: https://xiaoxinyyx.github.io/augs.

</details>


### [193] [Learning Positive-Incentive Point Sampling in Neural Implicit Fields for Object Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19937)
*Yifei Shi,Boyan Wan,Xin Xu,Kai Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes an SO(3)-equivariant implicit network with a positive-incentive point sampling (PIPS) strategy to improve 3D pose estimation from neural implicit fields, especially in unobserved camera regions, occlusion, and noisy/unseen shapes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Canonical coordinates for unobserved camera-space regions suffer from high uncertainty due to limited observational signals; dense full-space sampling is inefficient and can hinder learning.

Method: Introduce an SO(3)-equivariant convolutional implicit network to produce point-level attributes at arbitrary query locations; integrate PIPS to adaptively select informative sampling points based on input, boosting accuracy and training efficiency.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art on three pose estimation datasets, with notable gains in challenging scenarios: unseen poses, high occlusion, novel geometry, and severe noise.

Conclusion: The combination of symmetry-aware implicit representations and data-driven, adaptive sampling yields more accurate, robust, and efficient pose estimation from neural implicit fields, reducing reliance on exhaustive sampling.

Abstract: Learning neural implicit fields of 3D shapes is a rapidly emerging field that enables shape representation at arbitrary resolutions. Due to the flexibility, neural implicit fields have succeeded in many research areas, including shape reconstruction, novel view image synthesis, and more recently, object pose estimation. Neural implicit fields enable learning dense correspondences between the camera space and the object's canonical space-including unobserved regions in camera space-significantly boosting object pose estimation performance in challenging scenarios like highly occluded objects and novel shapes. Despite progress, predicting canonical coordinates for unobserved camera-space regions remains challenging due to the lack of direct observational signals. This necessitates heavy reliance on the model's generalization ability, resulting in high uncertainty. Consequently, densely sampling points across the entire camera space may yield inaccurate estimations that hinder the learning process and compromise performance. To alleviate this problem, we propose a method combining an SO(3)-equivariant convolutional implicit network and a positive-incentive point sampling (PIPS) strategy. The SO(3)-equivariant convolutional implicit network estimates point-level attributes with SO(3)-equivariance at arbitrary query locations, demonstrating superior performance compared to most existing baselines. The PIPS strategy dynamically determines sampling locations based on the input, thereby boosting the network's accuracy and training efficiency. Our method outperforms the state-of-the-art on three pose estimation datasets. Notably, it demonstrates significant improvements in challenging scenarios, such as objects captured with unseen pose, high occlusion, novel geometry, and severe noise.

</details>


### [194] [Benchmarking Unlearning for Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20114)
*Kairan Zhao,Iurie Luca,Peter Triantafillou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: First benchmarking study of machine unlearning (MU) for Vision Transformers (VTs) across ViT and Swin-T, with datasets, MU algorithms, and single-shot/continual protocols; focuses on memorization-based MU proxies and provides unified forget/accuracy metrics to enable reproducible comparisons; establishes baseline performance for VT MU research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in MU benchmarking for Vision Transformers, which have different memorization characteristics from CNNs, and to understand how existing MU algorithms perform when applied to VTs.

Method: Evaluate multiple MU algorithms that rely on training-data memorization proxies across two VT families (ViT and Swin-T) and varying capacities, using datasets of different scales/complexities, and both single-shot and continual unlearning protocols; use unified metrics for forget quality and accuracy on unseen and retained data.

Result: Provide a reproducible benchmarking baseline for MU in Vision Transformers, quantify how VTs memorize training data relative to CNNs, and assess how different memorization proxies affect MU performance; identify the relative performance of current MU methods in VT contexts and establish baseline expectations.

Conclusion: This work enables fair and comprehensive comparison of MU algorithms on Vision Transformers, and highlights VT-specific considerations for memorization-based unlearning, offering a reference point for future improvements and fair benchmarking.

Abstract: Research in machine unlearning (MU) has gained strong momentum: MU is now widely regarded as a critical capability for building safe and fair AI. In parallel, research into transformer architectures for computer vision tasks has been highly successful: Increasingly, Vision Transformers (VTs) emerge as strong alternatives to CNNs. Yet, MU research for vision tasks has largely centered on CNNs, not VTs. While benchmarking MU efforts have addressed LLMs, diffusion models, and CNNs, none exist for VTs. This work is the first to attempt this, benchmarking MU algorithm performance in different VT families (ViT and Swin-T) and at different capacities. The work employs (i) different datasets, selected to assess the impacts of dataset scale and complexity; (ii) different MU algorithms, selected to represent fundamentally different approaches for MU; and (iii) both single-shot and continual unlearning protocols. Additionally, it focuses on benchmarking MU algorithms that leverage training data memorization, since leveraging memorization has been recently discovered to significantly improve the performance of previously SOTA algorithms. En route, the work characterizes how VTs memorize training data relative to CNNs, and assesses the impact of different memorization proxies on performance. The benchmark uses unified evaluation metrics that capture two complementary notions of forget quality along with accuracy on unseen (test) data and on retained data. Overall, this work offers a benchmarking basis, enabling reproducible, fair, and comprehensive comparisons of existing (and future) MU algorithms on VTs. And, for the first time, it sheds light on how well existing algorithms work in VT settings, establishing a promising reference performance baseline.

</details>


### [195] [RL-RIG: A Generative Spatial Reasoner via Intrinsic Reflection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19974)
*Tianyu Wang,Zhiyuan Ma,Qian Wang,Xinyi Zhang,Xinwei Long,Bowen Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RL-RIG introduces a reflection-based reinforcement learning framework for image generation to enhance spatial reasoning and structural integrity, achieving up to 11% gains over state-of-the-art on spatial accuracy metrics (Scene Graph IoU) using a VLM-based judge on LAION-SG.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current diffusion-based image generators struggle with fine-grained spatial relationships and structural coherence; improving spatial reasoning is essential for controllable image synthesis from prompts.

Method: A Generate-Reflect-Edit paradigm with four components: Diffuser, Checker, Actor, and Inverse Diffuser. The model uses RL to optimize reflection-based reasoning. Reflection-GRPO trains the VLM Actor for edit prompts and the Image Editor to improve image quality under prompts.

Result: The approach outperforms existing open-source models by up to 11% in controllable and precise spatial reasoning on the LAION-SG dataset, as measured by Scene Graph IoU and a VLM-as-a-Judge evaluation.

Conclusion: RL-RIG effectively enhances spatial accuracy and structural integrity in image generation through a reflection-based RL framework, augmented by targeted training for reasoning trajectories and evaluation via VLM-based judgments.

Abstract: Recent advancements in image generation have achieved impressive results in producing high-quality images. However, existing image generation models still generally struggle with a spatial reasoning dilemma, lacking the ability to accurately capture fine-grained spatial relationships from the prompt and correctly generate scenes with structural integrity. To mitigate this dilemma, we propose RL-RIG, a Reinforcement Learning framework for Reflection-based Image Generation. Our architecture comprises four primary components: Diffuser, Checker, Actor, and Inverse Diffuser, following a Generate-Reflect-Edit paradigm to spark the Chain of Thought reasoning ability in image generation for addressing the dilemma. To equip the model with better intuition over generation trajectories, we further develop Reflection-GRPO to train the VLM Actor for edit prompts and the Image Editor for better image quality under a given prompt, respectively. Unlike traditional approaches that solely produce visually stunning yet structurally unreasonable content, our evaluation metrics prioritize spatial accuracy, utilizing Scene Graph IoU and employing a VLM-as-a-Judge strategy to assess the spatial consistency of generated images on LAION-SG dataset. Experimental results show that RL-RIG outperforms existing state-of-the-art open-source models by up to 11% in terms of controllable and precise spatial reasoning in image generation.

</details>


### [196] [RADE-Net: Robust Attention Network for Radar-Only Object Detection in Adverse Weather](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19994)
*Christof Leitgeb,Thomas Puchleitner,Max Peter Ronecker,Daniel Watzenig*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A paper proposing RADE-Net for 3D projection of 4D RADE radar tensors, enabling dense feature extraction with high efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Radar has robustness in adverse weather but full 4D RADE tensors are large; sparse radar representations lead to information loss. Dense, fast processing is needed for practical perception. 

Method: Introduce a 3D projection of fast-Fourier-transformed RADE (Range-Azimuth-Doppler-Elevation) tensors. Develop RADE-Net, a lightweight backbone with spatial and channel attention to exploit low- and high-level radar cues. Use decoupled detection heads to predict center-points in Range-Azimuth and regress rotated 3D boxes in Cartesian space.

Result: Data per frame reduced by 91.9% compared to full tensor. Achieves 16.7% improvement over their baseline and 6.5% over current radar-only models on K-Radar. Outperforms several LiDAR approaches in adverse weather. Code released.

Conclusion: The approach delivers faster training/inference and lower model complexity while improving radar-based perception under adverse weather, highlighting the efficacy of 3D RADE tensor projections for dense radar features.

Abstract: Automotive perception systems are obligated to meet high requirements. While optical sensors such as Camera and Lidar struggle in adverse weather conditions, Radar provides a more robust perception performance, effectively penetrating fog, rain, and snow. Since full Radar tensors have large data sizes and very few datasets provide them, most Radar-based approaches work with sparse point clouds or 2D projections, which can result in information loss. Additionally, deep learning methods show potential to extract richer and more dense features from low level Radar data and therefore significantly increase the perception performance. Therefore, we propose a 3D projection method for fast-Fourier-transformed 4D Range-Azimuth-Doppler-Elevation (RADE) tensors. Our method preserves rich Doppler and Elevation features while reducing the required data size for a single frame by 91.9% compared to a full tensor, thus achieving higher training and inference speed as well as lower model complexity. We introduce RADE-Net, a lightweight model tailored to 3D projections of the RADE tensor. The backbone enables exploitation of low-level and high-level cues of Radar tensors with spatial and channel-attention. The decoupled detection heads predict object center-points directly in the Range-Azimuth domain and regress rotated 3D bounding boxes from rich feature maps in the cartesian scene. We evaluate the model on scenes with multiple different road users and under various weather conditions on the large-scale K-Radar dataset and achieve a 16.7% improvement compared to their baseline, as well as 6.5% improvement over current Radar-only models. Additionally, we outperform several Lidar approaches in scenarios with adverse weather conditions. The code is available under https://github.com/chr-is-tof/RADE-Net.

</details>


### [197] [Token-UNet: A New Case for Transformers Integration in Efficient and Interpretable 3D UNets for Brain Imaging Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20008)
*Louis Fabrice Tshimanga,Andrea Zanola,Federico Del Pup,Manfredo Atzori*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Token-UNet is a resource-efficient 3D segmentation architecture that integrates TokenLearner/TokenFuser with a UNet backbone, reducing memory, compute, and parameters while achieving slightly higher Dice accuracy than SwinUNETR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers enable global interactions in 3D medical imaging but face heavy computational costs due to quadratic attention and large input resolutions; there is a need for efficient architectures that run on common hardware.

Method: Maintain a convolutional UNet encoder and replace standard heavy Transformers with TokenLearner-based tokenization of 3D feature maps, using a fixed number of tokens derived from local/global structures (TokenFuser/TokenLearner) to encode task-relevant information and produce interpretable attention maps.

Result: Heaviest model requires 33% memory, 10% inference time, and 35% of SwinUNETR’s parameters; Dice score improves from 86.75%±0.19% (SwinUNETR) to 87.21%±0.35% (Token-UNet).

Conclusion: Token-UNet enables efficient 3D medical image segmentation on constrained hardware, facilitating easier training, fine-tuning, and transfer learning, while delivering competitive or improved accuracy and interpretable attention

Abstract: We present Token-UNet, adopting the TokenLearner and TokenFuser modules to encase Transformers into UNets.
  While Transformers have enabled global interactions among input elements in medical imaging, current computational challenges hinder their deployment on common hardware. Models like (Swin)UNETR adapt the UNet architecture by incorporating (Swin)Transformer encoders, which process tokens that each represent small subvolumes ($8^3$ voxels) of the input.
  The Transformer attention mechanism scales quadratically with the number of tokens, which is tied to the cubic scaling of 3D input resolution.
  This work reconsiders the role of convolution and attention, introducing Token-UNets, a family of 3D segmentation models that can operate in constrained computational environments and time frames.
  To mitigate computational demands, our approach maintains the convolutional encoder of UNet-like models, and applies TokenLearner to 3D feature maps. This module pools a preset number of tokens from local and global structures.
  Our results show this tokenization effectively encodes task-relevant information, yielding naturally interpretable attention maps. The memory footprint, computation times at inference, and parameter counts of our heaviest model are reduced to 33\%, 10\%, and 35\% of the SwinUNETR values, with better average performance (86.75\% $\pm 0.19\%$ Dice score for SwinUNETR vs our 87.21\% $\pm 0.35\%$).
  This work opens the way to more efficient trainings in contexts with limited computational resources, such as 3D medical imaging. Easing model optimization, fine-tuning, and transfer-learning in limited hardware settings can accelerate and diversify the development of approaches, for the benefit of the research community.

</details>


### [198] [Closing the gap in multimodal medical representation alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20046)
*Eleonora Grassucci,Giordano Cicchetti,Danilo Comminiello*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Authors identify a modality gap in CLIP-style multimodal learning—including medical alignment—and propose a modality-agnostic framework to close the gap, yielding better cross-modal alignment and downstream performance (retrieval and captioning).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CLIP-like contrastive losses can produce sparse, fragmented latent spaces due to modality gaps. This misalignment persists in complex domains like medicine, potentially hindering semantic alignment between modalities (e.g., radiology images and clinical text).

Method: Introduce a modality-agnostic alignment framework that enforces semantic consistency across modalities, designed to work regardless of source modality, thus reducing the modality gap between radiology images and clinical text.

Result: Improved alignment between radiology images and clinical text, leading to enhanced cross-modal retrieval and image captioning.

Conclusion: The modality gap exists in medical alignment and can be mitigated with a modality-agnostic approach, yielding better semantic alignment across modalities and improved downstream tasks.

Abstract: In multimodal learning, CLIP has emerged as the de-facto approach for mapping different modalities into a shared latent space by bringing semantically similar representations closer while pushing apart dissimilar ones. However, CLIP-based contrastive losses exhibit unintended behaviors that negatively impact true semantic alignment, leading to sparse and fragmented latent spaces. This phenomenon, known as the modality gap, has been partially mitigated for standard text and image pairs but remains unknown and unresolved in more complex multimodal settings, such as the medical domain. In this work, we study this phenomenon in the latter case, revealing that the modality gap is present also in medical alignment, and we propose a modality-agnostic framework that closes this gap, ensuring that semantically related representations are more aligned, regardless of their source modality. Our method enhances alignment between radiology images and clinical text, improving cross-modal retrieval and image captioning.

</details>


### [199] [Decoupling Defense Strategies for Robust Image Watermarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20053)
*Jiahui Chen,Zehang Deng,Zeyu Zhang,Chaoyang Li,Lianchen Jia,Lifeng Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AdvMark: a two-stage decoupled fine-tuning framework for deep-learning image watermarking that improves robustness to distortion, regeneration, and adversarial attacks while preserving image quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep-learning watermarking is vulnerable to adversarial and regeneration attacks; joint encoder-decoder defenses that include a noise layer often hurt clean accuracy and struggle to be robust against multiple advanced attacks. A decoupled training strategy is needed to preserve quality while enhancing robustness.

Method: Stage 1: adversarial training that fine-tunes the encoder (conditional decoder updates) to push the image into a non-attackable region, preserving clean accuracy. Stage 2: direct image optimization with a constrained loss that balances deviation from the cover and previous encoded images, plus a quality-aware early-stop; theoretical guarantees accompany the loss formulation to maintain robustness gained in Stage 1.

Result: Empirical results show AdvMark achieves higher robustness with improved image quality, reporting up to 29% distortion-attack accuracy gain, 33% regeneration-attack gain, and 46% adversarial-attack gain compared to baselines.

Conclusion: A two-stage, decoupled optimization framework effectively enhances robustness of deep-learning watermarking while maintaining clean accuracy and visual quality, outperforming traditional joint-training defenses.

Abstract: Deep learning-based image watermarking, while robust against conventional distortions, remains vulnerable to advanced adversarial and regeneration attacks. Conventional countermeasures, which jointly optimize the encoder and decoder via a noise layer, face 2 inevitable challenges: (1) decrease of clean accuracy due to decoder adversarial training and (2) limited robustness due to simultaneous training of all three advanced attacks. To overcome these issues, we propose AdvMark, a novel two-stage fine-tuning framework that decouples the defense strategies. In stage 1, we address adversarial vulnerability via a tailored adversarial training paradigm that primarily fine-tunes the encoder while only conditionally updating the decoder. This approach learns to move the image into a non-attackable region, rather than modifying the decision boundary, thus preserving clean accuracy. In stage 2, we tackle distortion and regeneration attacks via direct image optimization. To preserve the adversarial robustness gained in stage 1, we formulate a principled, constrained image loss with theoretical guarantees, which balances the deviation from cover and previous encoded images. We also propose a quality-aware early-stop to further guarantee the lower bound of visual quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate AdvMark outperforms with the highest image quality and comprehensive robustness, i.e. up to 29\%, 33\% and 46\% accuracy improvement for distortion, regeneration and adversarial attacks, respectively.

</details>


### [200] [The Invisible Gorilla Effect in Out-of-distribution Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20068)
*Harry Anthony,Ziyun Liang,Hermione Warr,Konstantinos Kamnitsas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Identifies an unseen bias in OOD detection: near-OOD performance improves when artefacts resemble the ROI (Invisible Gorilla Effect), with many methods weakening when artefacts differ. Thorough evaluation across datasets and counterfactuals shows robustness gaps; code and annotations released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OOD detection is critical for reliable deployment, but performance bias by artefact characteristics is underexplored. Understanding this bias is essential to design detectors that are robust across artefact types and distributions.

Method: Annotate artefacts by colour in 11,355 images from three public datasets (e.g., ISIC) and generate colour-swapped counterfactuals to rule out dataset bias. Evaluate 40 OOD methods across 7 benchmarks.

Result: Hard-to-detect (near-OOD) artefacts yield better detection when artefacts share visual similarity with the ROI (e.g., colour). For example, Mahalanobis Score detects red ink (similar) 31.5% higher AUROC than black ink (dissimilar). Across evaluations, many methods suffer significant performance drops when artefacts differ from the ROI.

Conclusion: Reveals a previously unreported failure mode in OOD detection—the Invisible Gorilla Effect—and underscores the need for detectors robust to artefact-ROI colour relationships. Provides datasets and code for reproducibility.

Abstract: Deep Neural Networks achieve high performance in vision tasks by learning features from regions of interest (ROI) within images, but their performance degrades when deployed on out-of-distribution (OOD) data that differs from training data. This challenge has led to OOD detection methods that aim to identify and reject unreliable predictions. Although prior work shows that OOD detection performance varies by artefact type, the underlying causes remain underexplored. To this end, we identify a previously unreported bias in OOD detection: for hard-to-detect artefacts (near-OOD), detection performance typically improves when the artefact shares visual similarity (e.g. colour) with the model's ROI and drops when it does not - a phenomenon we term the Invisible Gorilla Effect. For example, in a skin lesion classifier with red lesion ROI, we show the method Mahalanobis Score achieves a 31.5% higher AUROC when detecting OOD red ink (similar to ROI) compared to black ink (dissimilar) annotations. We annotated artefacts by colour in 11,355 images from three public datasets (e.g. ISIC) and generated colour-swapped counterfactuals to rule out dataset bias. We then evaluated 40 OOD methods across 7 benchmarks and found significant performance drops for most methods when artefacts differed from the ROI. Our findings highlight an overlooked failure mode in OOD detection and provide guidance for more robust detectors. Code and annotations are available at: https://github.com/HarryAnthony/Invisible_Gorilla_Effect.

</details>


### [201] [SemanticNVS: Improving Semantic Scene Understanding in Generative Novel View Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20079)
*Xinya Chen,Christopher Wewer,Jiahao Xie,Xinting Hu,Jan Eric Lenssen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based NVS model (SemanticNVS) conditions on pre-trained semantic features to improve long-range novel view synthesis, using two strategies (warped features and alternating understanding/generation) and achieving notable FID gains over SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current NVS methods struggle with long-range camera motion due to weak conditioning on scene semantics, leading to semantically implausible or distorted images. Stronger semantic conditioning is needed to preserve realism across viewpoints.

Method: Introduce pre-trained semantic feature extractors into a camera-conditioned multi-view diffusion framework. Investigate two strategies: (1) warp semantic features to align with target views; (2) alternating scheme of understanding and generation at each denoising step, enhancing conditioning throughout generation.

Result: Experiments on multiple datasets show clear qualitative and quantitative improvements, with FID improvements in the range of 4.69% to 15.26% over state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Incorporating strong semantic conditioning via pre-trained extractors and the proposed strategies yields more accurate and consistent NVS across distant viewpoints, addressing long-range degradation.

Abstract: We present SemanticNVS, a camera-conditioned multi-view diffusion model for novel view synthesis (NVS), which improves generation quality and consistency by integrating pre-trained semantic feature extractors. Existing NVS methods perform well for views near the input view, however, they tend to generate semantically implausible and distorted images under long-range camera motion, revealing severe degradation. We speculate that this degradation is due to current models failing to fully understand their conditioning or intermediate generated scene content. Here, we propose to integrate pre-trained semantic feature extractors to incorporate stronger scene semantics as conditioning to achieve high-quality generation even at distant viewpoints. We investigate two different strategies, (1) warped semantic features and (2) an alternating scheme of understanding and generation at each denoising step. Experimental results on multiple datasets demonstrate the clear qualitative and quantitative (4.69%-15.26% in FID) improvement over state-of-the-art alternatives.

</details>


### [202] [Do Large Language Models Understand Data Visualization Principles?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20084)
*Martin Sinnona,Valentin Bonas,Viviana Siless,Emmanuel Iarussi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Systematic evaluation of LLMs and VLMs as principle checkers for data visualization, using ASP-ground truth. A dataset of ~2,000 Vega-Lite specs with explicit principle violations and >300 real charts was created to assess checking and fixing tasks. Models show promise as validators/editors but lag behind symbolic solvers on nuanced perception; frontier models fix violations more effectively than they detect them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether large language and vision-language models can reason about and enforce data-visualization principles directly, without translating principles into formal symbolic rules.

Method: Compile natural-language visualization principles into a controlled dataset; generate ~2,000 Vega-Lite specifications annotated with violations and >300 real-world charts; use Answer Set Programming (ASP) as ground truth; evaluate LLMs and VLMs on detection (checking) and correction (fixing) of principle violations.

Result: Frontier LLMs/VLMs show potential as flexible validators and editors for visualization design, but there remains a performance gap compared to symbolic solvers for nuanced perceptual aspects; an asymmetry is observed: frontier models tend to be more effective at correcting violations than at reliably detecting them.

Conclusion: LLMs and VLMs can serve as adaptable principle validators and editors in data visualization, but require integration with or guidance from symbolic methods to match the rigor of formal constraint solvers for subtle perceptual criteria.

Abstract: Data visualization principles, derived from decades of research in design and perception, ensure proper visual communication. While prior work has shown that large language models (LLMs) can generate charts or flag misleading figures, it remains unclear whether they and their vision-language counterparts (VLMs) can reason about and enforce visualization principles directly. Constraint based systems encode these principles as logical rules for precise automated checks, but translating them into formal specifications demands expert knowledge. This motivates leveraging LLMs and VLMs as principle checkers that can reason about visual design directly, bypassing the need for symbolic rule specification. In this paper, we present the first systematic evaluation of both LLMs and VLMs on their ability to reason about visualization principles, using hard verification ground truth derived from Answer Set Programming (ASP). We compiled a set of visualization principles expressed as natural-language statements and generated a controlled dataset of approximately 2,000 Vega-Lite specifications annotated with explicit principle violations, complemented by over 300 real-world Vega-Lite charts. We evaluated both checking and fixing tasks, assessing how well models detect principle violations and correct flawed chart specifications. Our work highlights both the promise of large (vision-)language models as flexible validators and editors of visualization designs and the persistent gap with symbolic solvers on more nuanced aspects of visual perception. They also reveal an interesting asymmetry: frontier models tend to be more effective at correcting violations than at detecting them reliably.

</details>


### [203] [Do Large Language Models Understand Data Visualization Rules?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20137)
*Martin Sinnona,Valentin Bonas,Emmanuel Iarussi,Viviana Siless*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LLMs can function as flexible, language-based validators for visualization rules derived from Draco, achieving high prompt-adherence and good detection on common violations, but limited on subtle perceptual rules and when processing ASP-based formulations; translating constraints to natural language boosts small-model performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether LLMs can reason about and enforce visualization rules using hard ground truth from ASP, bridging symbolic constraint checking with language models.

Method: Translate Draco constraints subset to natural language; create a controlled dataset of 2000 Vega-Lite specs annotated for rule violations; evaluate various LLMs on violation detection accuracy and prompt adherence; compare to symbolic solvers; test effect of translation on smaller models.

Result: Frontier models exhibit near-perfect prompt adherence (100% to 98%), detect common violations with F1 up to 0.82; performance declines for subtle perceptual rules (F1 < 0.15); outputs from ASP formulations are challenging; translating constraints improves performance by up to 150% for smaller models.

Conclusion: LLMs can serve as flexible, language-driven validators for visualization rules, but current capabilities lag behind symbolic solvers; natural-language translation of constraints can amplify small-model performance, suggesting a hybrid approach as promising.

Abstract: Data visualization rules-derived from decades of research in design and perception-ensure trustworthy chart communication. While prior work has shown that large language models (LLMs) can generate charts or flag misleading figures, it remains unclear whether they can reason about and enforce visualization rules directly. Constraint-based systems such as Draco encode these rules as logical constraints for precise automated checks, but maintaining symbolic encodings requires expert effort, motivating the use of LLMs as flexible rule validators. In this paper, we present the first systematic evaluation of LLMs against visualization rules using hard-verification ground truth derived from Answer Set Programming (ASP). We translated a subset of Draco's constraints into natural-language statements and generated a controlled dataset of 2,000 Vega-Lite specifications annotated with explicit rule violations. LLMs were evaluated on both accuracy in detecting violations and prompt adherence, which measures whether outputs follow the required structured format. Results show that frontier models achieve high adherence (Gemma 3 4B / 27B: 100%, GPT-oss 20B: 98%) and reliably detect common violations (F1 up to 0.82),yet performance drops for subtler perceptual rules (F1 < 0.15 for some categories) and for outputs generated from technical ASP formulations.Translating constraints into natural language improved performance by up to 150% for smaller models. These findings demonstrate the potential of LLMs as flexible, language-driven validators while highlighting their current limitations compared to symbolic solvers.

</details>


### [204] [Flow3r: Factored Flow Prediction for Scalable Visual Geometry Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20157)
*Zhongxiao Cong,Qitao Zhao,Minsik Jeon,Shubham Tulsiani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Flow3r uses dense 2D flow as supervision for scalable monocular video training to learn 3D/4D reconstruction, via a factorized flow model that uses geometry latents from one image and pose latents from the other, achieving state-of-the-art across static/dynamic scenes with unlabeled data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense geometry and pose supervision are expensive and scarce, especially for dynamic real-world scenes. Unlabeled monocular video offers a scalable supervision signal, but requires effective use in learning geometry and motion.

Method: Integrate a factored flow predictor into existing visual-geometry architectures. The flow between two frames is predicted using geometry latents from one frame and pose latents from the other. Train on ~800k unlabeled videos to learn scene geometry and camera motion without labels.

Result: Factored flow outperforms alternative designs. Performance scales with more unlabeled data. Achieves state-of-the-art results on eight benchmarks spanning static and dynamic scenes, with the largest gains on in-the-wild dynamic videos where labeled data is scarce.

Conclusion: Factorizing flow into geometry and pose components provides an effective supervision signal for scalable 3D/4D reconstruction from unlabeled monocular videos, extending well to dynamic scenes and enabling strong performance gains with large unlabeled data.

Abstract: Current feed-forward 3D/4D reconstruction systems rely on dense geometry and pose supervision -- expensive to obtain at scale and particularly scarce for dynamic real-world scenes. We present Flow3r, a framework that augments visual geometry learning with dense 2D correspondences (`flow') as supervision, enabling scalable training from unlabeled monocular videos. Our key insight is that the flow prediction module should be factored: predicting flow between two images using geometry latents from one and pose latents from the other. This factorization directly guides the learning of both scene geometry and camera motion, and naturally extends to dynamic scenes. In controlled experiments, we show that factored flow prediction outperforms alternative designs and that performance scales consistently with unlabeled data. Integrating factored flow into existing visual geometry architectures and training with ${\sim}800$K unlabeled videos, Flow3r achieves state-of-the-art results across eight benchmarks spanning static and dynamic scenes, with its largest gains on in-the-wild dynamic videos where labeled data is most scarce.

</details>


### [205] [tttLRM: Test-Time Training for Long Context and Autoregressive 3D Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20160)
*Chen Wang,Hao Tan,Wang Yifan,Zhiqin Chen,Yuheng Liu,Kalyan Sunkavalli,Sai Bi,Lingjie Liu,Yiwei Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: tttLRM introduces a Test-Time Training layer to enable long-context, autoregressive 3D reconstruction with linear cost, by folding multiple image observations into fast weights to form an implicit latent representation decodable into explicit formats (e.g., Gaussian splats); it includes an online streaming variant and demonstrates transfer from novel view synthesis pretraining to improved 3D reconstruction, achieving state-of-the-art results for feedforward 3D Gaussian reconstruction on objects and scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for scalable, long-context 3D reconstruction with efficient computation. Leverage test-time adaptation to accumulate information from multiple views and produce explicit 3D representations, bridging implicit latent representations with explicit formats.

Method: Propose tttLRM with a Test-Time Training (TTT) layer that compresses multiple image observations into the layer's fast weights, yielding an implicit 3D latent representation that can be decoded into explicit formats like Gaussian splats. Include an online learning variant for progressive reconstruction from streaming observations. Pretraining on novel view synthesis tasks facilitates transfer to explicit 3D modeling.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art in feedforward 3D Gaussian reconstruction for both objects and scenes, with improved reconstruction quality and faster convergence. The online/ streaming variant enables progressive refinement from streaming observations.

Conclusion: tttLRM provides a scalable, efficient framework for long-context 3D reconstruction by integrating observations via a TTT layer, enabling implicit-to-explicit decoding (e.g., Gaussian splats) and benefiting from NVS pretraining; suitable for high-quality, fast-converging 3D Gaussian reconstructions.

Abstract: We propose tttLRM, a novel large 3D reconstruction model that leverages a Test-Time Training (TTT) layer to enable long-context, autoregressive 3D reconstruction with linear computational complexity, further scaling the model's capability. Our framework efficiently compresses multiple image observations into the fast weights of the TTT layer, forming an implicit 3D representation in the latent space that can be decoded into various explicit formats, such as Gaussian Splats (GS) for downstream applications. The online learning variant of our model supports progressive 3D reconstruction and refinement from streaming observations. We demonstrate that pretraining on novel view synthesis tasks effectively transfers to explicit 3D modeling, resulting in improved reconstruction quality and faster convergence. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves superior performance in feedforward 3D Gaussian reconstruction compared to state-of-the-art approaches on both objects and scenes.

</details>


### [206] [Mobile-O: Unified Multimodal Understanding and Generation on Mobile Device](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20161)
*Abdelrahman Shaker,Ahmed Heakl,Jaseel Muhammad,Ritesh Thawkar,Omkar Thawakar,Senmao Li,Hisham Cholakkal,Ian Reid,Eric P. Xing,Salman Khan,Fahad Shahbaz Khan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Mobile-O introduces a compact vision-language-diffusion model for real-time, on-device unified multimodal understanding and generation, using a Mobile Conditioning Projector (MCP) to fuse vision-language features with a diffusion generator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing unified multimodal models are data-hungry and computationally heavy, hindering edge deployment; goal is to enable on-device capabilities without cloud reliance.

Method: Proposes MCP that uses depthwise-separable convolutions and layerwise alignment to achieve efficient cross-modal conditioning; diffusion generator; post-training in a quadruplet format (generation prompt, image, question, answer); trained on a few million samples; on-device optimizations; evaluation on mobile hardware.

Result: GenEval score 74%; outperforms Show-O by 5% and JanusFlow by 11%; 6x and 11x speedups relative to those models; visual understanding improvements of 15.3% and 5.1% across seven benchmarks; ~3 seconds per 512x512 image on iPhone; claims first practical real-time on-device unified multimodal framework; resources available.

Conclusion: Mobile-O lowers barrier to real-time on-device unified multimodal intelligence; supports on-device inference with no cloud dependency; open-source resources include code, models, datasets, app; aims to spur future research.

Abstract: Unified multimodal models can both understand and generate visual content within a single architecture. Existing models, however, remain data-hungry and too heavy for deployment on edge devices. We present Mobile-O, a compact vision-language-diffusion model that brings unified multimodal intelligence to a mobile device. Its core module, the Mobile Conditioning Projector (MCP), fuses vision-language features with a diffusion generator using depthwise-separable convolutions and layerwise alignment. This design enables efficient cross-modal conditioning with minimal computational cost. Trained on only a few million samples and post-trained in a novel quadruplet format (generation prompt, image, question, answer), Mobile-O jointly enhances both visual understanding and generation capabilities. Despite its efficiency, Mobile-O attains competitive or superior performance compared to other unified models, achieving 74% on GenEval and outperforming Show-O and JanusFlow by 5% and 11%, while running 6x and 11x faster, respectively. For visual understanding, Mobile-O surpasses them by 15.3% and 5.1% averaged across seven benchmarks. Running in only ~3s per 512x512 image on an iPhone, Mobile-O establishes the first practical framework for real-time unified multimodal understanding and generation on edge devices. We hope Mobile-O will ease future research in real-time unified multimodal intelligence running entirely on-device with no cloud dependency. Our code, models, datasets, and mobile application are publicly available at https://amshaker.github.io/Mobile-O/

</details>


<div id='cs.CG'></div>

# cs.CG [[Back]](#toc)

### [207] [Dynamic 3D Convex Hulls Revisited and Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18653)
*Haitao Wang*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: Refines Chan’s dynamic 3D convex-hull framework to reduce deletions to O(log^3 n log log n) amortized while maintaining O(log^2 n) amortized insertions; query time increases to O(log^3 n / log log n). Extends to dynamic lower envelopes of general 3D surfaces and, via duality, to 2D nearest-neighbor search with general distances; overall improvements are deletion-dominated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve the efficiency of dynamic maintenance of convex hulls in 3D under insertions and deletions and extend the framework to nearest-neighbor problems under general distances.

Method: Modify Chan’s framework using refined data-structural components (e.g., randomized vertical shallow cuttings) and dynamic lower envelopes for 3D surfaces, preserving insertion bounds while reducing deletion costs; analysis extends to 2D dual problems via lower envelopes and vertical ray-sh shooting.

Result: Achieves O(log^2 n) amortized insertions, O(log^3 n log log n) amortized deletions, and O(log^3 n / log log n) worst-case queries; the approach extends to dynamic lower envelopes of general 3D surfaces and enables nearest-neighbor searching under general distance functions via duality.

Conclusion: When deletions dominate overall complexity, the scheme yields a roughly logarithmic improvement over prior results; a trade-off emerges: lower deletion cost at the expense of higher query cost, while preserving the ability to handle 2D NN and general distance functions.

Abstract: Chan [JACM, 2010] gave a data structure for maintaining the convex hull of a dynamic set of 3D points under insertions and deletions, supporting extreme-point queries. Subsequent refinements by Kaplan, Mulzer, Roditty, Seiferth, and Sharir [DCG, 2020] and Chan [DCG, 2020] preserved the framework while improving bounds. The current best result achieves $O(\log^2 n)$ amortized insertion time, $O(\log^4 n)$ amortized deletion time, and $O(\log^2 n)$ worst-case query time. These techniques also yield dynamic 2D Euclidean nearest neighbor searching via duality, where the problem becomes maintaining the lower envelope of 3D planes for vertical ray-shooting queries. Using randomized vertical shallow cuttings, Kaplan et al. [DCG, 2020] and Liu [SICOMP, 2022] extended the framework to dynamic lower envelopes of general 3D surfaces, obtaining the same asymptotic bounds and enabling nearest neighbor searching under general distance functions. We revisit Chan's framework and present a modified structure that reduces the deletion time to $O(\log^3 n \log\log n)$, while retaining $O(\log^2 n)$ amortized insertion time, at the cost of increasing the query time to $O(\log^3 n / \log\log n)$. When the overall complexity is dominated by deletions, this yields an improvement of roughly a logarithmic factor over previous results.

</details>


### [208] [On Voronoi diagrams in the Funk Conical Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18980)
*Aditya Acharya,Auguste Henry Gezalyan,David M. Mount,Danesh Sivakumar*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: The paper studies Voronoi diagrams under forward and reverse Funk metrics in polygonal and elliptical cones. Key results: (i) bisectors are rays from the apex; (ii) d‑dimensional forward/reverse Funk Voronoi diagrams are equivalent to additively-weighted Voronoi diagrams on (d−1)-D cross sections; (iii) algorithms: O(n^{ceil((d−1)/2)+1}) via Apollonius transformation for elliptical cones; O(mn log n) for 3D polygonal cones (m facets) via reduction to abstract Voronoi diagrams; complete 3-site circumcenter characterization in 3D cones. This is among the first algorithmic studies of Funk metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Funk metrics are fundamental distance functions on convex bodies and underpin Hilbert and Thompson metrics; understanding Voronoi structures in these metrics advances computational geometry in non-Euclidean settings and broadens algorithmic toolbox for convex geometry problems.

Method: (1) Prove that Voronoi  bisectors in Funk metrics are unions of rays emanating from the cone apex; (2) show equivalence to additively-weighted Voronoi diagrams on bounded cross sections in one dimension lower; (3) derive algorithms by transforming to/from Apollonius diagrams for elliptical cones; (4) for 3D polygonal cones with m facets, reduce to abstract Voronoi diagrams; (5) characterize 3-site circumcenters in 3D cones.

Result: Provided geometric structure of Funk-Voronoi diagrams, established equivalence to AW-Voronoi on cross sections, and delivered two concrete algorithms with stated complexities; also provided a complete circumcenter criterion for three sites in 3D cones.

Conclusion: This work launches the algorithmic study of Funk metric Voronoi diagrams, providing foundational results and efficient construction methods, and opening avenues for extended dimensionality and other cone types.

Abstract: The forward and reverse Funk weak metrics are fundamental distance functions on convex bodies that serve as the building blocks for the Hilbert and Thompson metrics. In this paper we study Voronoi diagrams under the forward and reverse Funk metrics in polygonal and elliptical cones. We establish several key geometric properties: (1) bisectors consist of a set of rays emanating from the apex of the cone, and (2) Voronoi diagrams in the $d$-dimensional forward (or reverse) Funk metrics are equivalent to additively-weighted Voronoi diagrams in the $(d-1)$-dimensional forward (or reverse) Funk metrics on bounded cross sections of the cone. Leveraging this, we provide an $O\big(n^{\ceil{\frac{d-1}{2}}+1}\big)$ time algorithm for creating these diagrams in $d$-dimensional elliptical cones using a transformation to and from Apollonius diagrams, and an $O(mn\log(n))$ time algorithm for 3-dimensional polygonal cones with $m$ facets via a reduction to abstract Voronoi diagrams. We also provide a complete characterization of when three sites have a circumcenter in 3-dimensional cones. This is one of the first algorithmic studies of the Funk metrics.

</details>


### [209] [Structured Bitmap-to-Mesh Triangulation for Geometry-Aware Discretization of Image-Derived Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19474)
*Wei Feng,Haiyong Zheng*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: Template-driven boundary-aware retriangulation on image-derived domains enabling stable PDE discretization with deterministic, parallelizable triangulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Constrained Delaunay triangulation (CDT) can trigger global connectivity updates and lacks deterministic, parallel-friendly behavior near complex boundaries; a stable boundary-respecting mesh is needed for reliable PDE discretization on image-derived domains.

Method: Classify all local boundary-intersection configurations up to discrete equivalence and triangle symmetries, yielding a finite symbolic lookup table that maps each case to a conflict-free retriangulation template. Retrainngulate only triangles intersected by the boundary, preserve the base mesh, and enable synchronization-free parallel execution.

Result: Mesh is closed, has bounded angles, and is compatible with cotangent-based discretizations and standard finite element methods. Experiments show fewer sliver elements, more regular triangles, and improved geometric fidelity near complex boundaries across elliptic and parabolic PDEs, signal interpolation, and structural metrics.

Conclusion: The framework provides deterministic, scalable, and real-time-capable triangulation for image-derived domains, improving mesh quality and fidelity compared with CDT, and is well suited for real-time geometric analysis and physically based simulation.

Abstract: We propose a template-driven triangulation framework that embeds raster- or segmentation-derived boundaries into a regular triangular grid for stable PDE discretization on image-derived domains. Unlike constrained Delaunay triangulation (CDT), which may trigger global connectivity updates, our method retriangulates only triangles intersected by the boundary, preserves the base mesh, and supports synchronization-free parallel execution. To ensure determinism and scalability, we classify all local boundary-intersection configurations up to discrete equivalence and triangle symmetries, yielding a finite symbolic lookup table that maps each case to a conflict-free retriangulation template. We prove that the resulting mesh is closed, has bounded angles, and is compatible with cotangent-based discretizations and standard finite element methods. Experiments on elliptic and parabolic PDEs, signal interpolation, and structural metrics show fewer sliver elements, more regular triangles, and improved geometric fidelity near complex boundaries. The framework is well suited for real-time geometric analysis and physically based simulation over image-derived domains.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [210] [Revisiting the Seasonal Trend Decomposition for Enhanced Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18465)
*Sanjeev Panta,Xu Yuan,Li Chen,Nian-Feng Tzeng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decomposition-based dual-MLP approach improves multivariate time series forecasting with ~10% MSE reduction and linear complexity, validated on benchmarks and USGS data; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve forecast accuracy and efficiency by leveraging component-wise handling of trend and seasonality, addressing normalization effectiveness and computational costs in current state-of-the-art models.

Method: Decompose the time series into trend and seasonal components. Apply reversible instance normalization (RIN) to the trend component, while the seasonal component uses backbone models directly without normalization. Combine these into a dual-MLP architecture to enhance predictive performance with greater efficiency. Evaluate on four baselines and a USGS hydrological dataset; provide open-source code.

Result: Approximately 10% average reduction in MSE across four state-of-the-art baselines on benchmark datasets; consistent improvements with linear time complexity; significant performance gains on the USGS hydrological dataset, indicating real-world effectiveness.

Conclusion: A decomposition-guided dual-MLP framework yields robust improvements in multivariate time series forecasting while maintaining computational efficiency, supported by benchmark and real-world data; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Time series forecasting presents significant challenges in real-world applications across various domains. Building upon the decomposition of the time series, we enhance the architecture of machine learning models for better multivariate time series forecasting. To achieve this, we focus on the trend and seasonal components individually and investigate solutions to predict them with less errors. Recognizing that reversible instance normalization is effective only for the trend component, we take a different approach with the seasonal component by directly applying backbone models without any normalization or scaling procedures. Through these strategies, we successfully reduce error values of the existing state-of-the-art models and finally introduce dual-MLP models as more computationally efficient solutions. Furthermore, our approach consistently yields positive results with around 10% MSE average reduction across four state-of-the-art baselines on the benchmark datasets. We also evaluate our approach on a hydrological dataset extracted from the United States Geological Survey (USGS) river stations, where our models achieve significant improvements while maintaining linear time complexity, demonstrating real-world effectiveness. The source code is available at https://github.com/Sanjeev97/Time-Series-Decomposition

</details>


### [211] [Physiologically Informed Deep Learning: A Multi-Scale Framework for Next-Generation PBPK Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18472)
*Shunqi Liu,Han Qiu,Tong Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified SciML framework for PBPK: Transformers, Constrained Diffusion Models, Neural Allometry to improve physiological plausibility and speed; lowers physiological violation rates and enables faster simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address PBPK adoption barriers—high computational cost, parameter identifiability in complex biology, and interspecies extrapolation uncertainty—by integrating mechanistic PBPK with data-driven Scientific ML.

Method: 1) Foundation PBPK Transformers reframing PK forecasting as sequence modeling; 2) Physiologically Constrained Diffusion Models (PCDM) using physics-informed losses to generate biologically compliant virtual populations; 3) Neural Allometry combining Graph Neural Networks with Neural ODEs to learn continuous cross-species scaling laws.

Result: Synthetic-data experiments show physiological-violation rates drop from 2.00% to 0.50%, with faster simulations anticipated due to the proposed architectures.
