Conclusion: The framework provides a path toward faster, more plausible PBPK simulations with mechanistic interpretability retained, by unifying mechanistic modeling and data-driven learning.

Abstract: Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modeling is a cornerstone of model-informed drug development (MIDD), providing a mechanistic framework to predict drug absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (ADME). Despite its utility, adoption is hindered by high computational costs for large-scale simulations, difficulty in parameter identification for complex biological systems, and uncertainty in interspecies extrapolation. In this work, we propose a unified Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) framework that bridges mechanistic rigor and data-driven flexibility. We introduce three contributions: (1) Foundation PBPK Transformers, which treat pharmacokinetic forecasting as a sequence modeling task; (2) Physiologically Constrained Diffusion Models (PCDM), a generative approach that uses a physics-informed loss to synthesize biologically compliant virtual patient populations; and (3) Neural Allometry, a hybrid architecture combining Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with Neural ODEs to learn continuous cross-species scaling laws. Experiments on synthetic datasets show that the framework reduces physiological violation rates from 2.00% to 0.50% under constraints while offering a path to faster simulation.

</details>


### [212] [Decentralized Attention Fails Centralized Signals: Rethinking Transformers for Medical Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18473)
*Guoqi Yu,Juncheng Wang,Chen Yang,Jing Qin,Angelica I. Aviles-Rivero,Shujun Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoTAR introduces a centralized, MLP-based core token aggregation module to replace decentralized attention in Transformer-based models for medical time series (MedTS). It aligns better with the centralized nature of MedTS, reduces computational complexity from quadratic to linear, and achieves state-of-the-art-like performance with substantial efficiency gains across five benchmarks (up to 12.13% APAVA improvement; ~33% memory, ~20% inference time).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MedTS data exhibit strong temporal dependencies within channels and dependencies across channels. Traditional Transformer attention is decentralized and may fail to capture global synchronization and unified waveform patterns inherent to MedTS. A centralized mechanism could better model channel dependencies while improving efficiency.

Method: Propose CoTAR (Core Token Aggregation-Redistribution), a centralized MLP-based module that replaces standard attention. It uses a global core token as a proxy to interact with other tokens, enabling centralized aggregation and redistribution of information. This design enforces centralized interaction among tokens and reduces complexity from quadratic to linear (likely via token-to-core-to-token scheme).

Result: Empirical validation on five benchmarks shows improved effectiveness and efficiency. Specifically, on the APAVA dataset, CoTAR yields up to 12.13% performance improvement. Computational benefits include ~33% memory usage and ~20% faster inference compared to prior state-of-the-art methods. Code and training scripts are publicly available.

Conclusion: CoTAR effectively aligns the model’s interaction pattern with the centralized nature of MedTS, achieving better performance with substantial efficiency gains. It presents a viable alternative to self-attention for MedTS and potentially other centralized-pattern data, while raising questions about scalability, ablations, and generalization to broader domains.

Abstract: Accurate analysis of medical time series (MedTS) data, such as electroencephalography (EEG) and electrocardiography (ECG), plays a pivotal role in healthcare applications, including the diagnosis of brain and heart diseases. MedTS data typically exhibit two critical patterns: temporal dependencies within individual channels and channel dependencies across multiple channels. While recent advances in deep learning have leveraged Transformer-based models to effectively capture temporal dependencies, they often struggle with modeling channel dependencies. This limitation stems from a structural mismatch: MedTS signals are inherently centralized, whereas the Transformer's attention mechanism is decentralized, making it less effective at capturing global synchronization and unified waveform patterns. To address this mismatch, we propose CoTAR (Core Token Aggregation-Redistribution), a centralized MLP-based module designed to replace decentralized attention. Instead of allowing all tokens to interact directly, as in standard attention, CoTAR introduces a global core token that serves as a proxy to facilitate inter-token interactions, thereby enforcing a centralized aggregation and redistribution strategy. This design not only better aligns with the centralized nature of MedTS signals but also reduces computational complexity from quadratic to linear. Experiments on five benchmarks validate the superiority of our method in both effectiveness and efficiency, achieving up to a 12.13% improvement on the APAVA dataset, while using only 33% of the memory and 20% of the inference time compared to the previous state of the art. Code and all training scripts are available at https://github.com/Levi-Ackman/TeCh.

</details>


### [213] [Support Vector Data Description for Radar Target Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18486)
*Jean Pinsolle,Yadang Alexis Rouzoumka,Chengfang Ren,Chistèle Morisseau,Jean-Philippe Ovarlez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SVDD-based CFAR detectors for radar in heavy-tailed clutter, enabling robust one-class target detection without explicit noise covariance estimation; two novel SVDD-based detectors evaluated on simulated data, including a Deep SVDD variant.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome degradation of Gaussian-based adaptive detectors in heavy-tailed clutter (CES, CGD) and mixed thermal noise, by avoiding direct covariance estimation and employing one-class learning for robust target detection.

Method: Develop two SVDD-based detection algorithms and their deep extension (Deep SVDD), adapting them as CFAR detectors for radar target detection; evaluate on simulated radar data under non-Gaussian clutter.

Result: Demonstrated effectiveness of the proposed SVDD-based detectors on simulated data, showing robustness to non-Gaussian clutter and potential advantages over traditional covariance-based detectors.

Conclusion: SVDD-based detection provides a promising, covariance-estimation-free approach to CFAR radar detection in non-Gaussian environments, with Deep SVDD offering a learned representation that can further enhance performance; results on simulations support viability and motivate further testing on real data.

Abstract: Classical radar detection techniques rely on adaptive detectors that estimate the noise covariance matrix from target-free secondary data. While effective in Gaussian environments, these methods degrade in the presence of clutter, which is better modeled by heavy-tailed distributions such as the Complex Elliptically Symmetric (CES) and Compound-Gaussian (CGD) families. Robust covariance estimators like M-estimators or Tyler's estimator address this issue, but still struggle when thermal noise combines with clutter. To overcome these challenges, we investigate the use of Support Vector Data Description (SVDD) and its deep extension, Deep SVDD, for target detection. These one-class learning methods avoid direct noise covariance estimation and are adapted here as CFAR detectors. We propose two novel SVDD-based detection algorithms and demonstrate their effectiveness on simulated radar data.

</details>


### [214] [Learning to Remember: End-to-End Training of Memory Agents for Long-Context Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18493)
*Kehao Zhang,Shangtong Gui,Sheng Yang,Wei Chen,Yang Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UMA is an end-to-end RL framework that unifies memory management and QA, using a dual memory system (core summary + Memory Bank with CRUD) to enable proactive, long-horizon reasoning; evaluated with Ledger-QA, showing strong improvements over long-context and RAG baselines with competitive retrieval performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context LLMs and RAG systems defer state tracking, contradiction resolution, and evidence aggregation to query time, making them brittle for ultra-long streams with frequent updates. There is a need for learned, end-to-end memory management that can actively track and consolidate information over time.

Method: Introduce Unified Memory Agent (UMA), an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that unifies memory operations and question answering in a single policy. Uses a dual memory representation: a compact core summary for global context and a Memory Bank that supports explicit CRUD (create, update, delete, reorganize) over key-value entries. Enables proactive consolidation during streaming. Also introduces Ledger-QA, a diagnostic benchmark for continuous state tracking where answers are latent values derived from accumulated updates rather than local span retrieval.

Result: UMA substantially outperforms long-context and RAG baselines on dynamic reasoning and learning tasks across 13 datasets in Ledger-QA, Test-Time Learning, and Accurate Retrieval, while remaining competitive on standard retrieval benchmarks.

Conclusion: Learned, end-to-end memory management is important for long-horizon reasoning in streaming contexts; UMA demonstrates the value of proactive memory consolidation through a unified policy and dual-memory architecture.

Abstract: Long-context LLMs and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems process information passively, deferring state tracking, contradiction resolution, and evidence aggregation to query time, which becomes brittle under ultra long streams with frequent updates. We propose the Unified Memory Agent (UMA), an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that unifies memory operations and question answering within a single policy. UMA maintains a dual memory representation: a compact core summary for global context and a structured Memory Bank that supports explicit CRUD (create, update, delete, reorganize) over key value entries, enabling proactive consolidation during streaming. To evaluate long-horizon memory behavior, we introduce Ledger-QA, a diagnostic benchmark for continuous state tracking where answers are latent values derived from accumulated updates rather than lo cal span retrieval. Across 13 datasets spanning Ledger-QA, Test-Time Learning, and Accurate Retrieval, UMA substantially outperforms long-context and RAG baselines on dynamic reasoning and learning tasks while remaining competitive on standard retrieval benchmarks, underscoring the importance of learned, end-to-end memory management.

</details>


### [215] [Weak-Form Evolutionary Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks for Solving Partial Differential Equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18515)
*Bongseok Kim,Jiahao Zhang,Guang Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A weak-form evolutionary Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) for PDEs that improves stability and scalability by decoupling linear-system size from data via weak formulation; enforces Dirichlet/periodic BCs with boundary-constrained KANs and includes Neumann BCs in the weak form.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Strong-form evolutionary methods yield ill-conditioned systems and scale poorly with sample size; a scalable, accurate PDE predictor is needed for scientific ML and engineering.

Method: Introduce weak-form evolutionary KAN; formulate residuals weakly to form a linear system whose size is independent of training samples; construct boundary-constrained KANs to satisfy Dirichlet/periodic BCs; incorporate Neumann BCs through derivative terms in the weak form.

Result: Conceptual framework; claims improved stability and scalability; boundary-condition enforcement; potential relevance to engineering applications; no explicit numerical results provided in abstract.

Conclusion: Weak-form evolutionary KAN offers a stable, scalable framework for PDE prediction in scientific ML and may impact future engineering use-cases.

Abstract: Partial differential equations (PDEs) form a central component of scientific computing. Among recent advances in deep learning, evolutionary neural networks have been developed to successively capture the temporal dynamics of time-dependent PDEs via parameter evolution. The parameter updates are obtained by solving a linear system derived from the governing equation residuals at each time step. However, strong-form evolutionary approaches can yield ill-conditioned linear systems due to pointwise residual discretization, and their computational cost scales unfavorably with the number of training samples. To address these limitations, we propose a weak-form evolutionary Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) for the scalable and accurate prediction of PDE solutions. We decouple the linear system size from the number of training samples through the weak formulation, leading to improved scalability compared to strong-form approaches. We also rigorously enforce boundary conditions by constructing the trial space with boundary-constrained KANs to satisfy Dirichlet and periodic conditions, and by incorporating derivative boundary conditions directly into the weak formulation for Neumann conditions. In conclusion, the proposed weak-form evolutionary KAN framework provides a stable and scalable approach for PDEs and contributes to scientific machine learning with potential relevance to future engineering applications.

</details>


### [216] [Measuring the Prevalence of Policy Violating Content with ML Assisted Sampling and LLM Labeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18518)
*Attila Dobi,Aravindh Manickavasagam,Benjamin Thompson,Xiaohan Yang,Faisal Farooq*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A design-based system to estimate policy-violating content prevalence from user impressions using daily probability sampling, ML-assisted weightings, and multimodal LLM labeling, delivering unbiased, design-consistent prevalence estimates with detailed drilldowns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurately measuring real-user exposure to policy violations is hard; violations are rare and labeling expensive; need frequent, platform-representative estimates to inform safety teams.

Method: Daily probability samples drawn from impression stream; ML-assisted weights concentrate labeling on high-exposure/high-risk items while preserving unbiasedness; labels are generated by a multimodal LLM guided by policy prompts and gold-set validation; post-stratified estimation yields prevalence with CIs; one global daily sample enables pivoting by surface, geography, age, etc.

Result: Produces design-consistent prevalence estimates with confidence intervals and dashboards; variance estimators, label-quality monitoring, configurable workflow.

Conclusion: A scalable, flexible framework for measuring content-policy prevalence across multiple surfaces and segments from a single sample, enabling multi-dimensional analysis and timely safety insights.

Abstract: Content safety teams need metrics that reflect what users actually experience, not only what is reported. We study prevalence: the fraction of user views (impressions) that went to content violating a given policy on a given day. Accurate prevalence measurement is challenging because violations are often rare and human labeling is costly, making frequent, platform-representative studies slow. We present a design-based measurement system that (i) draws daily probability samples from the impression stream using ML-assisted weights to concentrate label budget on high-exposure and high-risk content while preserving unbiasedness, (ii) labels sampled items with a multimodal LLM governed by policy prompts and gold-set validation, and (iii) produces design-consistent prevalence estimates with confidence intervals and dashboard drilldowns. A key design goal is one global sample with many pivots: the same daily sample supports prevalence by surface, viewer geography, content age, and other segments through post-stratified estimation. We describe the statistical estimators, variance and confidence interval construction, label-quality monitoring, and an engineering workflow that makes the system configurable across policies.

</details>


### [217] [Wide Open Gazes: Quantifying Visual Exploratory Behavior in Soccer with Pose Enhanced Positional Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18519)
*Joris Bekkers*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel continuous probabilistic vision layer quantifies players' visual perception from pose-enhanced tracking, integrating with pitch control to predict end-of-action value; claims position-independence, annotation-free, and open-source tools, demonstrated on 32 Copa America games.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcomes limitations of traditional visual exploratory actions (VEAs) like velocity thresholds, positional bias, binary measures, annotation burden, and incompatibility with pitch control in soccer analytics.

Method: Develops probabilistic field-of-view and occlusion models using head/shoulder rotations from pose-enhanced tracking to create speed-dependent vision maps in a 2D plane. Integrates these maps with pitch control/value surfaces to analyze the awaiting phase and subsequent on-ball phase, focusing on visual metrics (e.g., percentage of defended area observed). Tested on 32 synchronized games from 2024 Copa America; provides continuous, annotation-free measurements and open-source tooling.

Result: Aggregated visual metrics correlate with and are predictive of controlled pitch value gained at the end of dribbling actions.

Conclusion: The approach is position-agnostic, eliminates manual annotation, and integrates into existing analytics frameworks; open-source tools facilitate adoption and broader validation across datasets.

Abstract: Traditional approaches to measuring visual exploratory behavior in soccer rely on counting visual exploratory actions (VEAs) based on rapid head movements exceeding 125°/s, but this method suffer from player position bias (i.e., a focus on central midfielders), annotation challenges, binary measurement constraints (i.e., a player is scanning, or not), lack the power to predict relevant short-term in-game future success, and are incompatible with fundamental soccer analytics models such as pitch control. This research introduces a novel formulaic continuous stochastic vision layer to quantify players' visual perception from pose-enhanced spatiotemporal tracking. Our probabilistic field-of-view and occlusion models incorporate head and shoulder rotation angles to create speed-dependent vision maps for individual players in a two-dimensional top-down plane. We combine these vision maps with pitch control and pitch value surfaces to analyze the awaiting phase (when a player is awaiting the ball to arrive after a pass for a teammate) and their subsequent on-ball phase. We demonstrate that aggregated visual metrics - such as the percentage of defended area observed while awaiting a pass - are predictive of controlled pitch value gained at the end of dribbling actions using 32 games of synchronized pose-enhanced tracking data and on-ball event data from the 2024 Copa America. This methodology works regardless of player position, eliminates manual annotation requirements, and provides continuous measurements that seamlessly integrate into existing soccer analytics frameworks. To further support the integration with existing soccer analytics frameworks we open-source the tools required to make these calculations.

</details>


### [218] [AdaptStress: Online Adaptive Learning for Interpretable and Personalized Stress Prediction Using Multivariate and Sparse Physiological Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18521)
*Xueyi Wang,Claudine J. C. Lamoth,Elisabeth Wilhelm*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A personalized, explainable time-series model using smartwatch data to forecast stress across multiple horizons, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines with sleep as the dominant predictor and strong personalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address continuous stress monitoring using consumer wearables to enable scalable, personalized mental health interventions with interpretable models.

Method: Multivariate time-series forecasting leveraging HRV, activity, and sleep metrics to predict stress. Horizon settings: history window 3/5/7/9 days; forecast window 1/3/5/7 days. Evaluated on 16 participants over 10–15 weeks, comparing against Informer, TimesNet, PatchTST and CNN/LSTM baselines. Optimal setting: 5-day input, 1-day prediction. Metrics: MSE 0.053, MAE 0.190, RMSE 0.226. Explainability analysis shows sleep as the dominant predictor (importance ~1.1; consistency 0.9–1.0) with high inter-participant variability in activity; model captures individual-specific patterns.

Result: The proposed model achieves MSE 0.053, MAE 0.190, RMSE 0.226 in the optimal setting and outperforms the baselines (TimesNet, PatchTST, CNN-LSTM, LSTM, CNN) under all conditions, with improvements of 36.9%, 25.5%, and 21.5% over the best-performing baseline across evaluated horizons.

Conclusion: Consumer wearables, combined with adaptive, interpretable deep learning, can deliver accurate, personalized, and explainable stress assessment suitable for real-world, continuous mental health monitoring.

Abstract: Continuous stress forecasting could potentially contribute to lifestyle interventions. This paper presents a novel, explainable, and individualized approach for stress prediction using physiological data from consumer-grade smartwatches. We develop a time series forecasting model that leverages multivariate features, including heart rate variability, activity patterns, and sleep metrics, to predict stress levels across 16 temporal horizons (History window: 3, 5, 7, 9 days; forecasting window: 1, 3, 5, 7 days). Our evaluation involves 16 participants monitored for 10-15 weeks. We evaluate our approach across 16 participants, comparing against state-of-the-art time series models (Informer, TimesNet, PatchTST) and traditional baselines (CNN, LSTM, CNN-LSTM) across multiple temporal horizons. Our model achieved performance with an MSE of 0.053, MAE of 0.190, and RMSE of 0.226 in optimal settings (5-day input, 1-day prediction). A comparison with the baseline models shows that our model outperforms TimesNet, PatchTST, CNN-LSTM, LSTM, and CNN under all conditions, representing improvements of 36.9%, 25.5%, and 21.5% over the best baseline. According to the explanability analysis, sleep metrics are the most dominant and consistent stress predictors (importance: 1.1, consistency: 0.9-1.0), while activity features exhibit high inter-participant variability (0.1-0.2). Most notably, the model captures individual-specific patterns where identical features can have opposing effects across users, validating its personalization capabilities. These findings establish that consumer wearables, combined with adaptive and interpretable deep learning, can deliver relevant stress assessment adapted to individual physiological responses, providing a foundation for scalable, continuous, explainable mental health monitoring in real-world settings.

</details>


### [219] [The Geometry of Multi-Task Grokking: Transverse Instability, Superposition, and Weight Decay Phase Structure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18523)
*Yongzhong Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Grokking in multi-task modular arithmetic shows five robust phenomena across dual/tri-task transformers, revealing a compression-driven, low-dimensional optimization manifold and fragility to perturbations and pruning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how grokking generalization transitions extend from single-task to multi-task settings, and how weight decay shapes the dynamics and geometry of learning.

Method: Train shared-trunk Transformers on modular arithmetic tasks (mod-add, mod-mul, mod-sq) in dual-task (mod-add + mod-mul) and tri-task configurations; sweep weight decay; analyze optimization trajectories, low-dimensional manifolds, commutator defects, and robustness to pruning and SVD truncation; compare across seeds.

Result: Five consistent phenomena emerge: (1) staggered grokking order (multiplication, then squaring, then addition); (2) universal integrability (trajectories confined to a low-dimensional execution manifold; commutator defects orthogonal to this manifold precede generalization); (3) weight decay phase structure (grokking timescales, curvature depth, reconstruction threshold, and defects covary with decay; distinct dynamical regimes; sharp no-decay failure); (4) holographic incompressibility (final solutions occupy 4–8 principal trajectory directions yet use full-rank weights; minimal perturbations destroy performance; SVD truncation, magnitude pruning, and uniform scaling fail to preserve); (5) transverse fragility and redundancy (removing <10% of orthogonal gradient components eliminates grokking; dual-task models show partial recovery under extreme deletion, indicating redundant center manifolds from overparameterization).

Conclusion: Multi-task grokking constructs a compact superposition subspace in parameter space; weight decay acts as compression pressure; excess parameters supply geometric redundancy in optimization pathways, suggesting a dynamical picture where grokking arises from constrained, low-dimensional dynamics under regulated pruning and task interactions.

Abstract: Grokking -- the abrupt transition from memorization to generalization long after near-zero training loss -- has been studied mainly in single-task settings. We extend geometric analysis to multi-task modular arithmetic, training shared-trunk Transformers on dual-task (mod-add + mod-mul) and tri-task (mod-add + mod-mul + mod-sq) objectives across a systematic weight decay sweep. Five consistent phenomena emerge. (1) Staggered grokking order: multiplication generalizes first, followed by squaring, then addition, with consistent delays across seeds. (2) Universal integrability: optimization trajectories remain confined to an empirically invariant low-dimensional execution manifold; commutator defects orthogonal to this manifold reliably precede generalization. (3) Weight decay phase structure: grokking timescale, curvature depth, reconstruction threshold, and defect lead covary systematically with weight decay, revealing distinct dynamical regimes and a sharp no-decay failure mode. (4) Holographic incompressibility: final solutions occupy only 4--8 principal trajectory directions yet are distributed across full-rank weights and destroyed by minimal perturbations; SVD truncation, magnitude pruning, and uniform scaling all fail to preserve performance. (5) Transverse fragility and redundancy: removing less than 10% of orthogonal gradient components eliminates grokking, yet dual-task models exhibit partial recovery under extreme deletion, suggesting redundant center manifolds enabled by overparameterization. Together, these results support a dynamical picture in which multi-task grokking constructs a compact superposition subspace in parameter space, with weight decay acting as compression pressure and excess parameters supplying geometric redundancy in optimization pathways.

</details>


### [220] [Audio-Visual Continual Test-Time Adaptation without Forgetting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18528)
*Sarthak Kumar Maharana,Akshay Mehra,Bhavya Ramakrishna,Yunhui Guo,Guan-Ming Su*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes AV-CTTA: an unsupervised, source-data-free test-time adaptation framework for audio-visual models that maintains a buffer of fusion-layer parameters and dynamically retrieves the best fusion parameters for each test batch, enabling robust continual adaptation across non-stationary domains while mitigating catastrophic forgetting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address catastrophic forgetting and performance degradation in audio-visual continual test-time adaptation (CTTA) when domains shift at test time and no source data is available; identify that adapting the fusion layer yields cross-domain transfer benefits.

Method: Maintain a buffer of fusion-layer parameters. For each test batch, select and retrieve the most suitable fusion-layer parameters from the buffer and integrate them into the model to adapt to the current distribution; update the buffer with the newly used parameters for future use. Operates without access to source data and handles both unimodal and bimodal corruptions.

Result: Extensive experiments on benchmarks with unimodal and bimodal corruptions show AV-CTTA significantly outperforms existing methods and reduces catastrophic forgetting.

Conclusion: Adaptive fusion-layer parameter retrieval is a transferable and effective strategy for AV CTTA under data-free test-time constraints, enabling robust cross-domain adaptation without retaining all source data.

Abstract: Audio-visual continual test-time adaptation involves continually adapting a source audio-visual model at test-time, to unlabeled non-stationary domains, where either or both modalities can be distributionally shifted, which hampers online cross-modal learning and eventually leads to poor accuracy. While previous works have tackled this problem, we find that SOTA methods suffer from catastrophic forgetting, where the model's performance drops well below the source model due to continual parameter updates at test-time. In this work, we first show that adapting only the modality fusion layer to a target domain not only improves performance on that domain but can also enhance performance on subsequent domains. Based on this strong cross-task transferability of the fusion layer's parameters, we propose a method, $\texttt{AV-CTTA}$, that improves test-time performance of the models without access to any source data. Our approach works by using a selective parameter retrieval mechanism that dynamically retrieves the best fusion layer parameters from a buffer using only a small batch of test data. These parameters are then integrated into the model, adapted to the current test distribution, and saved back for future use. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets involving unimodal and bimodal corruptions show our proposed $\texttt{AV-CTTA}$ significantly outperforms existing methods while minimizing catastrophic forgetting.

</details>


### [221] [Deep Reinforcement Learning for Optimizing Energy Consumption in Smart Grid Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18531)
*Abeer Alsheikhi,Amirfarhad Farhadi,Azadeh Zamanifar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PINN-based surrogates accelerate RL for Optimal Power Flow in smart grids, outperforming other data-driven surrogates by enabling strong policies without true-simulator samples and cutting training time by about 50%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL-based OPF suffers from severe sample inefficiency due to expensive simulators; there is a need for physics-informed surrogates that can replicate system dynamics while drastically reducing data requirements.

Method: Construct a Physics-Informed Neural Network surrogate of the power-flow simulator, integrate it into the RL training loop to accelerate policy learning, and compare against benchmark data-driven surrogates. Evaluate convergence speed and policy performance with/without access to real simulator samples.

Result: The PINN surrogate enables a strong RL policy even without samples from the true simulator and reduces RL training time by ~50% compared to learning with no surrogate. The generated performance scores closely match those obtained from the original simulator.

Conclusion: Physics-informed surrogates (PINNs) can effectively replace costly smart grid simulators in RL-based OPF, achieving faster convergence while preserving policy quality and enabling simulators to generate comparable performance scores.

Abstract: The energy management problem in the context of smart grids is inherently complex due to the interdependencies among diverse system components. Although Reinforcement Learning (RL) has been proposed for solving Optimal Power Flow (OPF) problems, the requirement for iterative interaction with an environment often necessitates computationally expensive simulators, leading to significant sample inefficiency. In this study, these challenges are addressed through the use of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), which can replace conventional and costly smart grid simulators. The RL policy learning process is enhanced so that convergence can be achieved in a fraction of the time required by the original environment. The PINN-based surrogate is compared with other benchmark data-driven surrogate models. By incorporating knowledge of the underlying physical laws, the results show that the PINN surrogate is the only approach considered in this context that can obtain a strong RL policy even without access to samples from the true simulator. The results demonstrate that using PINN surrogates can accelerate training by 50% compared to RL training without a surrogate. This approach enables the rapid generation of performance scores similar to those produced by the original simulator.

</details>


### [222] [Sub-City Real Estate Price Index Forecasting at Weekly Horizons Using Satellite Radar and News Sentiment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18572)
*Baris Arat,Hasan Fehmi Ates,Emre Sefer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sub-city weekly price indices can be forecasted with multimodal data; long-horizon forecasts (26-34 weeks) show substantial MAE reductions (35%) when combining unit-level transaction history with satellite SAR backscatter and news sentiment, with nonparametric models outperforming deep learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: City-level, low-frequency real estate indicators fail for neighborhood monitoring and long-horizon planning. Forecasting sub-city indices using a fusion of market data, remote sensing, and text sentiment can yield timely, actionable insights.

Method: Using 350,000+ Dubai Land Department transactions (2015-2025) to construct weekly indices for 19 sub-city regions, forecasting 2–34 weeks ahead. Features: regional transaction history, Sentinel-1 SAR backscatter, news sentiment (lexical tone + semantic embeddings), and macroeconomic context. Models include multimodal nonparametric learners and deep architectures; evaluating MAE across horizons.

Result: Horizon-dependent gains: for short horizons (≤10 weeks) price history suffices; for horizons >14 weeks SAR and sentiment become important. At 26–34 weeks, the full multimodal model reduces MAE from 4.48 to 2.93 (≈35% reduction), with significant regional improvements. Nonparametric learners outperform deep models in this regime.

Conclusion: The study establishes benchmarks for weekly sub-city index forecasting and demonstrates that remote sensing and news sentiment materially improve predictability at strategically relevant horizons.

Abstract: Reliable real estate price indicators are typically published at city level and low frequency, limiting their use for neighborhood-scale monitoring and long-horizon planning. We study whether sub-city price indices can be forecasted at weekly frequency by combining physical development signals from satellite radar with market narratives from news text. Using over 350,000 transactions from Dubai Land Department (2015-2025), we construct weekly price indices for 19 sub-city regions and evaluate forecasts from 2 to 34 weeks ahead. Our framework fuses regional transaction history with Sentinel-1 SAR backscatter, news sentiment combining lexical tone and semantic embeddings, and macroeconomic context. Results are strongly horizon dependent: at horizons up to 10 weeks, price history alone matches multimodal configurations, but beyond 14 weeks sentiment and SAR become critical. At long horizons (26-34 weeks), the full multimodal model reduces mean absolute error from 4.48 to 2.93 (35% reduction), with gains statistically significant across regions. Nonparametric learners consistently outperform deep architectures in this data regime. These findings establish benchmarks for weekly sub-city index forecasting and demonstrate that remote sensing and news sentiment materially improve predictability at strategically relevant horizons.

</details>


### [223] [Learning Beyond Optimization: Stress-Gated Dynamical Regime Regulation in Autonomous Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18581)
*Sheng Ran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A self-regulating, stress-driven, two-timescale network learns without explicit objectives by triggering structural changes during internally detected dysfunction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable autonomous long-horizon learning where external objectives are ill-defined, by permitting the system to self-assess internal dynamics and regulate plasticity.

Method: Two-timescale architecture separating fast state evolution from slow structural adaptation, coupled via an internal stress variable that accumulates evidence of persistent dysfunction; structural modifications occur as state-dependent events; demonstrated on a minimal toy model.

Result: Demonstrates temporally segmented, self-organized learning episodes without external goals; internal regulation yields autonomous learning behavior.

Conclusion: Supports a plausible route toward autonomous, self-assessing learning systems capable of internally reorganizing structure; further work needed for scalability and broader evaluation.

Abstract: Despite their apparent diversity, modern machine learning methods can be reduced to a remarkably simple core principle: learning is achieved by continuously optimizing parameters to minimize or maximize a scalar objective function. This paradigm has been extraordinarily successful for well-defined tasks where goals are fixed and evaluation criteria are explicit. However, if artificial systems are to move toward true autonomy-operating over long horizons and across evolving contexts-objectives may become ill-defined, shifting, or entirely absent. In such settings, a fundamental question emerges: in the absence of an explicit objective function, how can a system determine whether its ongoing internal dynamics are productive or pathological? And how should it regulate structural change without external supervision? In this work, we propose a dynamical framework for learning without an explicit objective. Instead of minimizing external error signals, the system evaluates the intrinsic health of its own internal dynamics and regulates structural plasticity accordingly. We introduce a two-timescale architecture that separates fast state evolution from slow structural adaptation, coupled through an internally generated stress variable that accumulates evidence of persistent dynamical dysfunction. Structural modification is then triggered not continuously, but as a state-dependent event. Through a minimal toy model, we demonstrate that this stress-regulated mechanism produces temporally segmented, self-organized learning episodes without reliance on externally defined goals. Our results suggest a possible route toward autonomous learning systems capable of self-assessment and internally regulated structural reorganization.

</details>


### [224] [GIST: Targeted Data Selection for Instruction Tuning via Coupled Optimization Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18584)
*Guanghui Min,Tianhao Huang,Ke Wan,Chen Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GIST replaces axis-aligned scaling with subspace alignment for data selection in parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT). It derives a task-specific gradient subspace from validation gradients via SVD, projects training gradients into this subspace, and scores/selects examples by alignment with target directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In PEFT settings like LoRA, the optimization geometry exhibits strong cross-parameter coupling and non-trivial off-diagonal interactions, making diagonal (axis-aligned) statistics (e.g., Adam states) a poor proxy for update geometry. A low-dimensional, task-relevant subspace can capture essential updates.

Method: Compute validation gradients, perform spectral filtering (SVD) to extract a task-specific subspace, project training gradients into this coupled subspace, and score each training example by its alignment with target directions for selection.

Result: GIST matches or outperforms state-of-the-art baselines while using only 0.29% of the storage and 25% of the computational time under the same selection budget.

Conclusion: Subspace-aligned gradient approaches can capture cross-parameter coupling in PEFT and enable scalable, effective data selection that maintains or improves accuracy with substantial efficiency gains.

Abstract: Targeted data selection has emerged as a crucial paradigm for efficient instruction tuning, aiming to identify a small yet influential subset of training examples for a specific target task. In practice, influence is often measured through the effect of an example on parameter updates. To make selection scalable, many approaches leverage optimizer statistics (e.g., Adam states) as an axis-aligned surrogate for update geometry (i.e., diagonal precondition), implicitly treating parameters as coordinate-wise independent. We show that this assumption breaks down in parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods such as LoRA. In this setting, the induced optimization geometry exhibits strong cross-parameter coupling with non-trivial off-diagonal interactions, while the task-relevant update directions are confined to a low-dimensional subspace. Motivated by this mismatch, we propose GIST (Gradient Isometric Subspace Transformation), a simple yet principled alternative that replaces axis-aligned scaling with robust subspace alignment. GIST recovers a task-specific subspace from validation gradients via spectral filtering (SVD), projects training gradients into this coupled subspace, and scores examples by their alignment with target directions.Extensive experiments have demonstrated that GIST matches or outperforms the state-of-the-art baseline with only 0.29% of the storage and 25% of the computational time under the same selection budget.

</details>


### [225] [Ensemble Prediction of Task Affinity for Efficient Multi-Task Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18591)
*Afiya Ayman,Ayan Mukhopadhyay,Aron Laszka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ETAP combines a gradient-based affinity score with non-linear predictors to predict multi-task learning gains for task groups, enabling scalable and accurate task grouping with limited ground-truth data; it outperforms baselines on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reducing the combinatorial search for task groupings in multi-task learning by accurately predicting gains, while keeping the method scalable and data-efficient.

Method: 1) Gradient-based affinity score: measure similarity of parameter updates when training on pairs (or groups) of tasks; linear estimator that extends to groups. 2) Non-linear predictors: learn transformations to refine affinity estimates and capture complex task relationships; trained on a limited set of task groups for which ground-truth MTL gains are obtained.

Result: Empirical evaluation on benchmark datasets shows that ETAP improves MTL gain prediction and enables more effective task grouping, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines across diverse domains.

Conclusion: ETAP provides a scalable, effective framework for predicting MTL gains and guiding task grouping with limited labeled data, with strong empirical performance across benchmarks.

Abstract: A fundamental problem in multi-task learning (MTL) is identifying groups of tasks that should be learned together. Since training MTL models for all possible combinations of tasks is prohibitively expensive for large task sets, a crucial component of efficient and effective task grouping is predicting whether a group of tasks would benefit from learning together, measured as per-task performance gain over single-task learning. In this paper, we propose ETAP (Ensemble Task Affinity Predictor), a scalable framework that integrates principled and data-driven estimators to predict MTL performance gains. First, we consider the gradient-based updates of shared parameters in an MTL model to measure the affinity between a pair of tasks as the similarity between the parameter updates based on these tasks. This linear estimator, which we call affinity score, naturally extends to estimating affinity within a group of tasks. Second, to refine these estimates, we train predictors that apply non-linear transformations and correct residual errors, capturing complex and non-linear task relationships. We train these predictors on a limited number of task groups for which we obtain ground-truth gain values via multi-task learning for each group. We demonstrate on benchmark datasets that ETAP improves MTL gain prediction and enables more effective task grouping, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines across diverse application domains.

</details>


### [226] [MapTab: Can MLLMs Master Constrained Route Planning?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18600)
*Ziqiao Shang,Lingyue Ge,Yang Chen,Shi-Yu Tian,Zhenyu Huang,Wenbo Fu,Yu-Feng Li,Lan-Zhe Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MapTab introduces a multimodal benchmark to evaluate constrained reasoning in MLLMs using route planning tasks that fuse map visuals with structured tabular data, across two real-world scenarios (Metromap and Travelmap). It scales to 328 images, 196,800 route queries, and 3,936 QA items, focusing on Time, Price, Comfort, and Reliability; results show current MLLMs struggle with constrained multimodal reasoning, especially with limited visual perception.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks inadequately assess constrained, real-world multimodal reasoning in MLLMs; a robust, scalable testbed is needed to push toward reliable multimodal grounding and reasoning under practical constraints relevant to route planning and decision making.

Method: Construct MapTab with two datasets: Metromap (metro networks in 160 cities across 52 countries) and Travelmap (168 tourist attractions across 19 countries). The benchmark integrates visual cues from map images with structured attributes (Time, Price, Comfort, Reliability) and comprises 328 images, 196,800 route planning queries, and 3,936 QA queries. Evaluations span 15 representative MLLMs to assess constrained reasoning and cross-modal collaboration.

Result: Models exhibit substantial difficulty in constrained multimodal reasoning; when visual perception is limited, multimodal models can underperform unimodal baselines, highlighting inefficiencies in cross-modal collaboration under practical constraints.

Conclusion: MapTab offers a realistic and challenging benchmark to systematically evaluate MLLMs on constrained multimodal reasoning, highlighting current gaps and guiding future model development toward better grounding and reasoning in multimodal, constraint-heavy tasks.

Abstract: Systematic evaluation of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) is crucial for advancing Artificial General Intelligence (AGI). However, existing benchmarks remain insufficient for rigorously assessing their constrained reasoning capabilities. To bridge this gap, we introduce MapTab, a multimodal benchmark specifically designed to evaluate constrained reasoning in MLLMs via route planning tasks. MapTab requires MLLMs to perceive and ground visual cues from map images alongside route attributes (e.g., Time, Price) from structured tabular data. The benchmark encompasses two scenarios: Metromap, covering metro networks in 160 cities across 52 countries, and Travelmap, depicting 168 representative tourist attractions from 19 countries. In total, MapTab comprises 328 images, 196,800 route planning queries, and 3,936 QA queries, all incorporating 4 key constraints: Time, Price, Comfort, and Reliability. Extensive evaluations across 15 representative MLLMs reveal that current models face substantial challenges in constrained multimodal reasoning. Notably, under conditions of limited visual perception, multimodal collaboration often underperforms compared to unimodal approaches. We believe MapTab provides a challenging and realistic testbed to advance the systematic evaluation of MLLMs.

</details>


### [227] [Diagnosing LLM Reranker Behavior Under Fixed Evidence Pools](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18613)
*Baris Arat,Emre Sefer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a diagnostic to isolate reranking from retrieval quality by using fixed eight-document evidence pools (Multi-News clusters) and identical inputs, with BM25 and MMR as interpretable baselines. Evaluates 345 clusters to study how LLMs' ranking behavior differs in redundancy and lexical coverage, independent of retriever variance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard reranking evaluations conflate ranking policy with upstream retrieval quality, making it hard to attribute performance differences to ranking alone.

Method: Create fixed eight-document evidence pools per cluster from Multi-News; feed identical inputs to all rankers; use BM25 and MMR as lexical matching and diversity baselines; compare LLMs and baselines across 345 clusters to analyze redundancy and lexical coverage.

Result: Redundancy patterns depend on the model: one LLM diversifies at larger budgets while another increases redundancy; LLMs underperform lexical coverage at small budgets. Rankings diverge substantially from both baselines, indicating ranking policies, not retrieval, drive differences.

Conclusion: Eliminating retrieval variance enables attribution of ranking-policy effects; the diagnostic is model-agnostic and applicable to any ranker (open source or proprietary APIs).

Abstract: Standard reranking evaluations study how a reranker orders candidates returned by an upstream retriever. This setup couples ranking behavior with retrieval quality, so differences in output cannot be attributed to the ranking policy alone. We introduce a controlled diagnostic that isolates reranking by using Multi-News clusters as fixed evidence pools. We limit each pool to exactly eight documents and pass identical inputs to all rankers. Within this setup, BM25 and MMR serve as interpretable reference points for lexical matching and diversity optimization. Across 345 clusters, we find that redundancy patterns vary by model: one LLM implicitly diversifies at larger selection budgets, while another increases redundancy. In contrast, LLMs underperform on lexical coverage at small selection budgets. As a result, LLM rankings diverge substantially from both baselines rather than consistently approximating either strategy. By eliminating retrieval variance, we can attribute these differences directly to the ranking policy. This diagnostic is model-agnostic and applicable to any ranker, including open source systems and proprietary APIs.

</details>


### [228] [Non-Interfering Weight Fields: Treating Model Parameters as a Continuously Extensible Function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18628)
*Sarim Chaudhry*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NIWF uses a learned weight-field function over a continuous capability space to generate weights on demand, committing regions to lock in capabilities and prevent forgetting, enabling versioned neural capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Catastrophic forgetting in fixed-weight transformers lacks structural guarantees; existing methods protect weights but do not remove interference between old and new tasks.

Method: Replace fixed weights with a hypernetwork-style weight-field that maps capability coordinates to weight configurations; after training on a task, snapshot field outputs at anchor points to create a committed region that is protected during future training; evaluate on sequential instruction following and code generation tasks with Mistral-7B.

Result: Demonstrates zero forgetting on committed tasks while maintaining competitive perplexity on new tasks.

Conclusion: Introduces software-like versioning for neural networks, enabling committing, extending, composing, and rolling back capabilities without retraining.

Abstract: Large language models store all learned knowledge in a single, fixed weight vector. Teaching a model new capabilities requires modifying those same weights, inevitably degrading previously acquired knowledge. This fundamental limitation, known as catastrophic forgetting, has resisted principled solutions for decades. Existing approaches treat weights as immutable artifacts that must be protected through techniques like regularization heuristics, replay buffers, or isolated adapter modules. The problem is none of these provide a structural guarantee against forgetting. In this work, we propose Non-Interfering Weight Fields (NIWF), a framework that replaces the fixed weight paradigm with a learned function that generates weight configurations on demand from a continuous capability coordinate space. After training on a task, we commit the occupied coordinate region by snapshotting the fields outputs on anchor points to enforce a functional lock during all future training. We validate NIWF on sequential instructionfollowing and code generation tasks using Mistral-7B, demonstrating zero forgetting on committed tasks with competitive perplexity on new tasks. The framework introduces the notion of software-like versioning for neural network intelligence, where capabilities can be committed, extended, composed, and rolled back without retraining.

</details>


### [229] [Online decoding of rat self-paced locomotion speed from EEG using recurrent neural networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18637)
*Alejandro de Miguel,Nelson Totah,Uri Maoz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Non-invasive, cortex-wide EEG can continuously and accurately decode self-paced locomotion speed in rats using a 32-channel skull EEG and an RNN-based asynchronous BCI, achieving high within-subject decoding (R=0.88, R^2=0.78) with generalization across sessions but not across animals; cortical dynamics convey current, past, and future speed information up to ~1s.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in decoding self-paced locomotion non-invasively. Demonstrate cortex-wide EEG can recover action dynamics, enabling non-invasive BCIs for rehabilitation, prosthetics, and neuroscience of locomotion.

Method: 32-electrode skull EEG (0.01–45 Hz) from head-fixed rats on a non-motorized treadmill. An asynchronous BCI with recurrent neural networks maps ongoing EEG to instantaneous treadmill speed. Dataset >133 hours. Pre-training on a single session enables decoding on other sessions of the same rat. Analysis of electrode contributions and frequency content.

Result: Decoding performance: r=0.88 (R^2=0.78) for speed. Visual cortex electrodes and low-frequency (<8 Hz) oscillations drive performance. Within-rat generalization across sessions after single-session pre-training; poor cross-animal transfer. Cortical states encode current, and predict future and past speed up to 1000 ms.

Conclusion: Self-paced locomotion speed can be accurately and continuously decoded from non-invasive EEG, enabling high-performing BCI approaches and offering insights into distributed neural representations of action dynamics.

Abstract: $\textit{Objective.}$ Accurate neural decoding of locomotion holds promise for advancing rehabilitation, prosthetic control, and understanding neural correlates of action. Recent studies have demonstrated decoding of locomotion kinematics across species on motorized treadmills. However, efforts to decode locomotion speed in more natural contexts$-$where pace is self-selected rather than externally imposed$-$are scarce, generally achieve only modest accuracy, and require intracranial implants. Here, we aim to decode self-paced locomotion speed non-invasively and continuously using cortex-wide EEG recordings from rats. $\textit{Approach.}$ We introduce an asynchronous brain$-$computer interface (BCI) that processes a stream of 32-electrode skull-surface EEG (0.01$-$45 Hz) to decode instantaneous speed from a non-motorized treadmill during self-paced locomotion in head-fixed rats. Using recurrent neural networks and a dataset of over 133 h of recordings, we trained decoders to map ongoing EEG activity to treadmill speed. $\textit{Main results.}$ Our decoding achieves a correlation of 0.88 ($R^2$ = 0.78) for speed, primarily driven by visual cortex electrodes and low-frequency ($< 8$ Hz) oscillations. Moreover, pre-training on a single session permitted decoding on other sessions from the same rat, suggesting uniform neural signatures that generalize across sessions but fail to transfer across animals. Finally, we found that cortical states not only carry information about current speed, but also about future and past dynamics, extending up to 1000 ms. $\textit{Significance.}$ These findings demonstrate that self-paced locomotion speed can be decoded accurately and continuously from non-invasive, cortex-wide EEG. Our approach provides a framework for developing high-performing, non-invasive BCI systems and contributes to understanding distributed neural representations of action dynamics.

</details>


### [230] [Learning Invariant Visual Representations for Planning with Joint-Embedding Predictive World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18639)
*Leonardo F. Toso,Davit Shadunts,Yunyang Lu,Nihal Sharma,Donglin Zhan,Nam H. Nguyen,James Anderson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bisimulation-regularized predictive world model yields robust latent dynamics with smaller latent space, agnostic to encoder, improving DINO-WM-like models against slow features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address robustness drop of latent predictive architectures like DINO-WM caused by slow features (background changes, distractors) that are irrelevant to the task; enforce control-relevant state equivalence.

Method: Introduce a bisimulation encoder added to the predictive objective to map states with similar transition dynamics to nearby latent representations, filtering out slow features; encoder-agnostic and compatible with pretrained visual encoders including DINOv2, SimDINOv2, and iBOT.

Result: On a simple navigation task with test-time background changes/distractors, the approach consistently improves robustness to slow features, yields latent representations up to 10x smaller than DINO-WM, and remains robust across different encoders (DINOv2, SimDINOv2, iBOT).

Conclusion: Bisimulation-regularized predictive world models achieve more robust, compact latent representations that generalize across encoders, mitigating sensitivity to slow features.

Abstract: World models learned from high-dimensional visual observations allow agents to make decisions and plan directly in latent space, avoiding pixel-level reconstruction. However, recent latent predictive architectures (JEPAs), including the DINO world model (DINO-WM), display a degradation in test time robustness due to their sensitivity to "slow features". These include visual variations such as background changes and distractors that are irrelevant to the task being solved. We address this limitation by augmenting the predictive objective with a bisimulation encoder that enforces control-relevant state equivalence, mapping states with similar transition dynamics to nearby latent states while limiting contributions from slow features. We evaluate our model on a simple navigation task under different test-time background changes and visual distractors. Across all benchmarks, our model consistently improves robustness to slow features while operating in a reduced latent space, up to 10x smaller than that of DINO-WM. Moreover, our model is agnostic to the choice of pretrained visual encoder and maintains robustness when paired with DINOv2, SimDINOv2, and iBOT features.

</details>


### [231] [Adaptive Time Series Reasoning via Segment Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18645)
*Shvat Messica,Jiawen Zhang,Kevin Li,Theodoros Tsiligkaridis,Marinka Zitnik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ARTIST reframes time-series reasoning as a sequential decision problem with adaptive temporal segment selection. A controller selects informative segments while a reasoner performs segment-conditioned reasoning, trained via reinforcement learning and hierarchical policy optimization, yielding improved accuracy over baselines and strong gains on rare-event and multi-segment tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current time-series reasoning approaches often encode the entire sequence into a fixed representation, which can waste computation and miss task-relevant information. There is a need for models that selectively inspect only task-relevant temporal segments.

Method: Propose ARTIST with a controller-reasoner architecture that interleaves reasoning with adaptive segment selection. The controller is trained via reinforcement learning to pick informative segments; the reasoner uses segment-conditioned reasoning traces and outputs final answers. A hierarchical policy optimization method is used for post-training, plus supervised fine-tuning.

Result: On six benchmarks, ARTIST achieves an average absolute improvement of 6.46 percentage points over the strongest baseline. Gains are especially large for rare event localization and multi-segment reasoning. Supervised fine-tuning helps; reinforcement learning provides additional gains by optimizing task-adaptive segment selection.

Conclusion: Selective data use via adaptive segment acquisition enables effective time-series reasoning, outperforming static full-sequence encodings and underscoring the value of interactive, task-driven information gathering during inference.

Abstract: Time series reasoning tasks often start with a natural language question and require targeted analysis of a time series. Evidence may span the full series or appear in a few short intervals, so the model must decide what to inspect. Most existing approaches encode the entire time series into a fixed representation before inference, regardless of whether or not the entire sequence is relevant. We introduce ARTIST, which formulates time-series reasoning as a sequential decision problem. ARTIST interleaves reasoning with adaptive temporal segment selection. It adopts a controller-reasoner architecture and uses reinforcement learning to train the controller role to select informative segments and the reasoner role to generate segment-conditioned reasoning traces and final answers. During inference, the model actively acquires task-relevant information instead of relying on a static summary of the full sequence. We use a novel hierarchical policy optimization approach for post-training that allows the model to excel in both segment selection and question-answering behavior. We evaluate ARTIST on six time-series reasoning benchmarks and compare it with large language models, vision-language models, and prior time-series reasoning systems. ARTIST improves average accuracy by 6.46 absolute percentage points over the strongest baseline. The largest gains appear on rare event localization and multi-segment reasoning tasks. Supervised fine-tuning improves performance, and reinforcement learning provides additional gains by optimizing question-adaptive segment selection. These results show that selective data use drives effective time-series reasoning.

</details>


### [232] [Information-Guided Noise Allocation for Efficient Diffusion Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18647)
*Gabriel Raya,Bac Nguyen,Georgios Batzolis,Yuhta Takida,Dejan Stancevic,Naoki Murata,Chieh-Hsin Lai,Yuki Mitsufuji,Luca Ambrogioni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: InfoNoise introduces a data-adaptive, information-theoretic noise schedule for diffusion-model training, enabling better efficiency and cross-domain transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual noise schedules waste compute and hinder transfer; need data-dependent, principled scheduling.

Method: Define conditional entropy rate of the forward process; estimate entropy-reduction rates from existing denoising losses; derive a noise sampling distribution; implement InfoNoise.

Result: Matches or exceeds tuned schedules on natural images; 1.4x speedup on CIFAR-10; on discrete datasets, superior quality in up to 3x fewer steps.

Conclusion: Noise scheduling becomes data-adaptive, reducing per-dataset design effort as diffusion models scale across domains.

Abstract: Training diffusion models typically relies on manually tuned noise schedules, which can waste computation on weakly informative noise regions and limit transfer across datasets, resolutions, and representations. We revisit noise schedule allocation through an information-theoretic lens and propose the conditional entropy rate of the forward process as a theoretically grounded, data-dependent diagnostic for identifying suboptimal noise-level allocation in existing schedules. Based on these insight, we introduce InfoNoise, a principled data-adaptive training noise schedule that replaces heuristic schedule design with an information-guided noise sampling distribution derived from entropy-reduction rates estimated from denoising losses already computed during training. Across natural-image benchmarks, InfoNoise matches or surpasses tuned EDM-style schedules, in some cases with a substantial training speedup (about $1.4\times$ on CIFAR-10). On discrete datasets, where standard image-tuned schedules exhibit significant mismatch, it reaches superior quality in up to $3\times$ fewer training steps. Overall, InfoNoise makes noise scheduling data-adaptive, reducing the need for per-dataset schedule design as diffusion models expand across domains.

</details>


### [233] [Global Low-Rank, Local Full-Rank: The Holographic Encoding of Learned Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18649)
*Yongzhong Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Grokking arises from globally low-dimensional learning directions in a high-dimensional parameter space; trajectory PCA captures most performance, while static decompositions fail; proposess the holographic encoding principle.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain how a low-dimensional learning dynamic can yield high-dimensional, robust solutions (grokking) and reconcile low-rank trajectories with full-rank parameters; test across multi-task modular arithmetic using Transformer architectures.

Method: Train shared-trunk Transformers with separate heads for addition, multiplication, and a quadratic operation modulo 97 across three sizes (315K–2.2M params) and five weight-decay settings. Compare three reconstruction methods: per-matrix SVD, joint cross-matrix SVD, and trajectory PCA. Analyze grokking trajectories, rank properties of weight matrices, and reconstruction accuracy.

Result: Grokking trajectories lie in a 2–6 dimensional global subspace while individual weight matrices remain effectively full-rank. Reconstructing final performance from 3–5 trajectory PCs recovers >95% of final accuracy; per-matrix SVD and joint SVD fail at sub-full rank. Static decompositions capture spectral energy but destroy task-relevant structure.

Conclusion: Learned algorithms are encoded through dynamically coordinated updates across all matrices, not localized low-rank components. This leads to the holographic encoding principle: solutions are globally low-rank in the learning-direction space but locally full-rank in parameter space, with implications for compression, interpretability, and understanding how neural networks encode computation.

Abstract: Grokking -- the abrupt transition from memorization to generalization after extended training -- has been linked to the emergence of low-dimensional structure in learning dynamics. Yet neural network parameters inhabit extremely high-dimensional spaces. How can a low-dimensional learning process produce solutions that resist low-dimensional compression?
  We investigate this question in multi-task modular arithmetic, training shared-trunk Transformers with separate heads for addition, multiplication, and a quadratic operation modulo 97. Across three model scales (315K--2.2M parameters) and five weight decay settings, we compare three reconstruction methods: per-matrix SVD, joint cross-matrix SVD, and trajectory PCA.
  Across all conditions, grokking trajectories are confined to a 2--6 dimensional global subspace, while individual weight matrices remain effectively full-rank. Reconstruction from 3--5 trajectory PCs recovers over 95\% of final accuracy, whereas both per-matrix and joint SVD fail at sub-full rank. Even when static decompositions capture most spectral energy, they destroy task-relevant structure.
  These results show that learned algorithms are encoded through dynamically coordinated updates spanning all matrices, rather than localized low-rank components. We term this the holographic encoding principle: grokked solutions are globally low-rank in the space of learning directions but locally full-rank in parameter space, with implications for compression, interpretability, and understanding how neural networks encode computation.

</details>


### [234] [Communication-Efficient Personalized Adaptation via Federated-Local Model Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18658)
*Yinan Zou,Md Kamran Chowdhury Shisher,Christopher G. Brinton,Vishrant Tripathi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Potara presents a principled federated personalization framework by merging a general federated model with a local personalized model via linear mode connectivity. It derives closed-form optimal mixing weights by minimizing a variance trace upper bound on the expected task loss, ensuring the merged model outperforms either component. Empirically, it achieves better personalization with reduced communication for vision and language tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Task-level heterogeneity in federated learning makes personalization challenging. Existing approaches rely on heuristic mixing and expensive model merging, leading to inefficiency and weak theoretical guarantees. There is a need for a principled, communication-efficient method that guarantees improved performance by combining general and personalized knowledge.

Method: Construct a federated model (general knowledge) and a local model (personalized knowledge). Use linear mode connectivity to analyze the expected task loss as a variance trace; derive a closed-form solution for optimal mixing weights that minimizes the upper bound on the merged model's loss. Merge the models with these weights to create a personalized client model. Evaluate on vision and language benchmarks, focusing on performance-computation-communication trade-offs.

Result: Potara consistently improves personalization across vision and language benchmarks while reducing communication, achieving a favorable performance-communication trade-off compared to baseline personalization approaches.

Conclusion: A principled, efficient approach to federated personalization: by optimally merging a general federated model with a local personalized model under linear mode connectivity, Potara provides theoretical guarantees (variance bound) and practical gains (better personalization with lower communication) over existing heuristic methods.

Abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods, such as LoRA, offer a practical way to adapt large vision and language models to client tasks. However, this becomes particularly challenging under task-level heterogeneity in federated deployments. In this regime, personalization requires balancing general knowledge with personalized knowledge, yet existing approaches largely rely on heuristic mixing rules and lack theoretical justification. Moreover, prior model merging approaches are also computation and communication intensive, making the process inefficient in federated settings. In this work, we propose Potara, a principled framework for federated personalization that constructs a personalized model for each client by merging two complementary models: (i) a federated model capturing general knowledge, and (ii) a local model capturing personalized knowledge. Through the construct of linear mode connectivity, we show that the expected task loss admits a variance trace upper bound, whose minimization yields closed-form optimal mixing weights that guarantee a tighter bound for the merged model than for either the federated or local model alone. Experiments on vision and language benchmarks show that Potara consistently improves personalization while reducing communication, leading to a strong performance-communication trade-off.

</details>


### [235] [Large Causal Models for Temporal Causal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18662)
*Nikolaos Kougioulis,Nikolaos Gkorgkolis,MingXue Wang,Bora Caglayan,Dario Simionato,Andrea Tonon,Ioannis Tsamardinos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Large Causal Models (LCMs): a foundation-model approach for temporal causal discovery trained on diverse synthetic and realistic data, scalable to many variables and deep architectures, with competitive/ superior accuracy and fast single-pass inference, especially out-of-distribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcomes dataset-specific causal discovery limitations: small variable counts, synthetic-data reliance, poor generalization across datasets; aims to enable a single pretrained model for temporal causality.

Method: Introduce LCM framework combining diverse synthetic data generators with realistic time-series datasets; pretrain a neural architecture to handle high-variable and deep models; emphasize efficiency with single-pass inference.

Result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic, semi-synthetic, and realistic benchmarks shows LCMs scale to higher variable counts and deeper architectures, maintaining strong performance and often superior accuracy relative to classical and neural baselines; strong out-of-distribution performance; fast inference.

Conclusion: LCMs embody a foundation-model paradigm for temporal causal discovery, offering scalable, generalizable performance and providing code and weights publicly.

Abstract: Causal discovery for both cross-sectional and temporal data has traditionally followed a dataset-specific paradigm, where a new model is fitted for each individual dataset. Such an approach limits the potential of multi-dataset pretraining. The concept of large causal models (LCMs) envisions a class of pre-trained neural architectures specifically designed for temporal causal discovery. Prior approaches are constrained to small variable counts, degrade with larger inputs, and rely heavily on synthetic data, limiting generalization. We propose a principled framework for LCMs, combining diverse synthetic generators with realistic time-series datasets, allowing learning at scale. Extensive experiments on synthetic, semi-synthetic and realistic benchmarks show that LCMs scale effectively to higher variable counts and deeper architectures while maintaining strong performance. Trained models achieve competitive or superior accuracy compared to classical and neural baselines, particularly in out-of-distribution settings, while enabling fast, single-pass inference. Results demonstrate LCMs as a promising foundation-model paradigm for temporal causal discovery. Experiments and model weights are available at https://github.com/kougioulis/LCM-paper/.

</details>


### [236] [Robustness of Deep ReLU Networks to Misclassification of High-Dimensional Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18674)
*Věra Kůrková*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical analysis of local robustness of parameterized neural networks to small random input perturbations, deriving lower bounds on robustness for deep ReLU networks in terms of input dimensionality and total unit count.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address reliability of neural network classifications under random perturbations; quantify how input size and network capacity affect robustness.

Method: Theoretically model additive perturbations at a given input, analyze probability of misclassification for ReLU networks, derive lower bounds on local robustness as a function of input dimension and total number of units.

Result: Established explicit lower bounds on local robustness expressed as a function of input dimension and total unit count, providing guaranteed minimum robustness under small random perturbations.

Conclusion: Shows that local robustness can be bounded in terms of architecture and input size, informing design choices to improve robustness by managing dimensionality and network capacity.

Abstract: We present a theoretical study of the robustness of parameterized networks to random input perturbations. Specifically, we analyze local robustness at a given network input by quantifying the probability that a small additive random perturbation of the input leads to misclassification. For deep networks with rectified linear units, we derive lower bounds on local robustness in terms of the input dimensionality and the total number of network units.

</details>


### [237] [Transformers for dynamical systems learn transfer operators in-context](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18679)
*Anthony Bao,Jeffrey Lai,William Gilpin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Small transformer learns in-context forecasting for a dynamical system and transfers to unseen systems; reveals early double-descent tradeoffs and an attention-based transfer-operator mechanism using delay embedding and invariant sets to exploit global attractors for short-term forecasts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how foundation models generalize to unseen physical dynamics and the mechanism of in-context learning in dynamical systems; to explain zero-shot forecasting without retraining.

Method: Train a two-layer, single-head transformer on one dynamical system; test forecasting on another system without retraining; analyze training dynamics; examine attention-based transfer-operator strategy via delay embedding and invariant-set forecasting.

Result: Identify an early tradeoff between in-distribution and out-of-distribution performance (secondary double descent); show attention models perform transfer-operator forecasting by lifting signals with delay embedding and predicting long-lived invariant sets; demonstrate mechanism enabling zero-shot forecasting.

Conclusion: Attention-based models can forecast unseen physical systems without retraining by leveraging transfer-operator structure and global attractor information; clarifies how large pretrained models leverage attractor dynamics for short-term forecasts.

Abstract: Large-scale foundation models for scientific machine learning adapt to physical settings unseen during training, such as zero-shot transfer between turbulent scales. This phenomenon, in-context learning, challenges conventional understanding of learning and adaptation in physical systems.
