Here, we study in-context learning of dynamical systems in a minimal setting: we train a small two-layer, single-head transformer to forecast one dynamical system, and then evaluate its ability to forecast a different dynamical system without retraining. We discover an early tradeoff in training between in-distribution and out-of-distribution performance, which manifests as a secondary double descent phenomenon. We discover that attention-based models apply a transfer-operator forecasting strategy in-context. They (1) lift low-dimensional time series using delay embedding, to detect the system's higher-dimensional dynamical manifold, and (2) identify and forecast long-lived invariant sets that characterize the global flow on this manifold. Our results clarify the mechanism enabling large pretrained models to forecast unseen physical systems at test without retraining, and they illustrate the unique ability of attention-based models to leverage global attractor information in service of short-term forecasts.

</details>


### [238] [In-Context Planning with Latent Temporal Abstractions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18694)
*Baiting Luo,Yunuo Zhang,Nathaniel S. Keplinger,Samir Gupta,Abhishek Dubey,Ayan Mukhopadhyay*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: I-TAP unifies offline RL with in-context planning in a discrete latent temporal-abstraction space, enabling efficient token-space MCTS for robust planning under partial observability and regime shifts, achieving competitive results on MuJoCo and Adroit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Planning at primitive time scales leads to prohibitive branching and long horizons; partial observability and regime shifts invalidate stationary dynamics. There is a need for offline-to-online planning that can adapt in-context using offline data without online gradient updates.

Method: Train an observation-conditioned residual-quantization VAE to compress observation-macro-action segments into a coarse-to-fine stack of discrete tokens; train a temporal Transformer to autoregressively predict token stacks from a short history. The sequence model serves as a context-conditioned prior over abstract actions and as a latent dynamics model. At test time, perform Monte Carlo Tree Search directly in token space using short histories for implicit adaptation, then decode selected token stacks into executable actions.

Result: Across deterministic MuJoCo, stochastic MuJoCo with per-episode latent dynamics regimes, and high-dimensional Adroit manipulation, including partially observable variants, I-TAP matches or outperforms strong model-free and model-based offline baselines.

Conclusion: I-TAP demonstrates efficient and robust in-context planning under stochastic dynamics and partial observability by combining offline trajectory learning with token-space planning and in-context adaptation.

Abstract: Planning-based reinforcement learning for continuous control is bottlenecked by two practical issues: planning at primitive time scales leads to prohibitive branching and long horizons, while real environments are frequently partially observable and exhibit regime shifts that invalidate stationary, fully observed dynamics assumptions. We introduce I-TAP (In-Context Latent Temporal-Abstraction Planner), an offline RL framework that unifies in-context adaptation with online planning in a learned discrete temporal-abstraction space. From offline trajectories, I-TAP learns an observation-conditioned residual-quantization VAE that compresses each observation-macro-action segment into a coarse-to-fine stack of discrete residual tokens, and a temporal Transformer that autoregressively predicts these token stacks from a short recent history. The resulting sequence model acts simultaneously as a context-conditioned prior over abstract actions and a latent dynamics model. At test time, I-TAP performs Monte Carlo Tree Search directly in token space, using short histories for implicit adaptation without gradient update, and decodes selected token stacks into executable actions. Across deterministic MuJoCo, stochastic MuJoCo with per-episode latent dynamics regimes, and high-dimensional Adroit manipulation, including partially observable variants, I-TAP consistently matches or outperforms strong model-free and model-based offline baselines, demonstrating efficient and robust in-context planning under stochastic dynamics and partial observability.

</details>


### [239] [Insertion Based Sequence Generation with Learnable Order Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18695)
*Dhruvesh Patel,Benjamin Rozonoyer,Gaurav Pandey,Tahira Naseem,Ramón Fernandez Astudillo,Andrew McCallum*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Insertion-based variable-length sequence generation via trainable order dynamics in discrete flow matching improves training tractability and generation quality, especially for de novo molecule design; shows trade-offs between flexibility, stability, and effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Insertion models have a much larger action space than autoregressive models, making learning difficult. Introducing trainable order dynamics aims to control this space, enabling tractable joint training and improved generation quality, including higher validity in molecules.

Method: Integrate trainable order dynamics into the target rates of discrete flow matching. Jointly train the order dynamics and the generator without numerical simulation. Use a variable-length masked diffusion model that inserts and fills mask tokens. Empirically explore parameterizations on graph traversal tasks where a locally optimal insertion order is known, and compare with uniform order dynamics on de novo small molecule generation.

Result: Joint training of order dynamics and generator is tractable without numerical simulation. Identified trade-offs among flexibility, training stability, and generation quality. Learned order dynamics increases the number of valid molecules and improves quality compared to uniform order dynamics in de novo molecule generation.

Conclusion: Learned order dynamics can substantially enhance insertion-based generative models, improving validity and quality of outputs. The parameterization of the order dynamics significantly impacts performance, suggesting domain-specific tuning. The approach is promising for domains requiring variable-length sequence generation with insertions.

Abstract: In many domains generating variable length sequences through insertions provides greater flexibility over autoregressive models. However, the action space of insertion models is much larger than that of autoregressive models (ARMs) making the learning challenging. To address this, we incorporate trainable order dynamics into the target rates for discrete flow matching, and show that with suitable choices of parameterizations, joint training of the target order dynamics and the generator is tractable without the need for numerical simulation. As the generative insertion model, we use a variable length masked diffusion model, which generates by inserting and filling mask tokens. On graph traversal tasks for which a locally optimal insertion order is known, we explore the choices of parameterization empirically and demonstrate the trade-offs between flexibility, training stability and generation quality. On de novo small molecule generation, we find that the learned order dynamics leads to an increase in the number of valid molecules generated and improved quality, when compared to uniform order dynamics.

</details>


### [240] [Phase-Consistent Magnetic Spectral Learning for Multi-View Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18728)
*Mingdong Lu,Zhikui Chen,Meng Liu,Shubin Ma,Liang Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Phase-Consistent Magnetic Spectral Learning for unsupervised multi-view clustering, introducing a phase term to capture cross-view directional agreement, combining with a nonnegative magnitude backbone to form a complex-valued magnetic affinity, and using a Hermitian magnetic Laplacian to derive a stable shared spectral signal guiding representation learning; uses anchor-based high-order consensus and refinement for scalability; achieves consistent gains on public benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MVC methods rely on magnitude-only affinities or early pseudo targets, which are unstable when views induce relations with comparable strengths but contradictory directional tendencies, distorting spectral geometry and harming clustering.

Method: Model cross-view directional agreement as a phase term; combine with magnitude to yield complex-valued magnetic affinity; extract shared spectral signal via Hermitian magnetic Laplacian; use as structured self-supervision for unsupervised multi-view representation learning and clustering; construct compact shared structure with anchor-based high-order consensus; apply lightweight refinement to suppress noisy/inconsistent relations.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple public multi-view benchmarks show consistent improvement over strong baselines.

Conclusion: Phase-consistent magnetic spectral learning provides a robust shared spectral signal for MVC and improves clustering performance; scalability is achieved via anchor-based structure and refinement, with demonstrated effectiveness across datasets.

Abstract: Unsupervised multi-view clustering (MVC) aims to partition data into meaningful groups by leveraging complementary information from multiple views without labels, yet a central challenge is to obtain a reliable shared structural signal to guide representation learning and cross-view alignment under view discrepancy and noise. Existing approaches often rely on magnitude-only affinities or early pseudo targets, which can be unstable when different views induce relations with comparable strengths but contradictory directional tendencies, thereby distorting the global spectral geometry and degrading clustering. In this paper, we propose \emph{Phase-Consistent Magnetic Spectral Learning} for MVC: we explicitly model cross-view directional agreement as a phase term and combine it with a nonnegative magnitude backbone to form a complex-valued magnetic affinity, extract a stable shared spectral signal via a Hermitian magnetic Laplacian, and use it as structured self-supervision to guide unsupervised multi-view representation learning and clustering. To obtain robust inputs for spectral extraction at scale, we construct a compact shared structure with anchor-based high-order consensus modeling and apply a lightweight refinement to suppress noisy or inconsistent relations. Extensive experiments on multiple public multi-view benchmarks demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms strong baselines.

</details>


### [241] [Prior Aware Memorization: An Efficient Metric for Distinguishing Memorization from Generalization in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18733)
*Trishita Tiwari,Ari Trachtenberg,G. Edward Suh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Prior-Aware Memorization, a training-free criterion to distinguish genuine memorization from statistically common sequences in LLMs, reducing false positives in leakage assessments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current memorization measures conflate memorized data with patterns that are statistically common in training data; Counterfactual Memorization is accurate but computationally expensive, hindering scalable leakage analysis.

Method: Proposes a lightweight metric that tests whether a candidate suffix is specifically linked to its training prefix or likely to appear across unrelated prompts due to statistical priors; validates on LLaMA and OPT training corpora using long sequences and named entities, plus SATML dataset.

Result: Finds that 55–90% of sequences previously labeled as memorized are statistically common rather than unique memorization; about 40% of SATML sequences show common-pattern behavior despite appearing only once in training.

Conclusion: Low frequency alone is insufficient evidence of memorization; accounting for model priors yields more accurate leakage assessments; the method is training-free and scalable, aiding privacy, security, and copyright risk evaluation.

Abstract: Training data leakage from Large Language Models (LLMs) raises serious concerns related to privacy, security, and copyright compliance. A central challenge in assessing this risk is distinguishing genuine memorization of training data from the generation of statistically common sequences. Existing approaches to measuring memorization often conflate these phenomena, labeling outputs as memorized even when they arise from generalization over common patterns. Counterfactual Memorization provides a principled solution by comparing models trained with and without a target sequence, but its reliance on retraining multiple baseline models makes it computationally expensive and impractical at scale.
  This work introduces Prior-Aware Memorization, a theoretically grounded, lightweight and training-free criterion for identifying genuine memorization in LLMs. The key idea is to evaluate whether a candidate suffix is strongly associated with its specific training prefix or whether it appears with high probability across many unrelated prompts due to statistical commonality.
  We evaluate this metric on text from the training corpora of two pre-trained models, LLaMA and OPT, using both long sequences (to simulate copyright risks) and named entities (to simulate PII leakage). Our results show that between 55% and 90% of sequences previously labeled as memorized are in fact statistically common. Similar findings hold for the SATML training data extraction challenge dataset, where roughly 40% of sequences exhibit common-pattern behavior despite appearing only once in the training data. These results demonstrate that low frequency alone is insufficient evidence of memorization and highlight the importance of accounting for model priors when assessing leakage.

</details>


### [242] [When World Models Dream Wrong: Physical-Conditioned Adversarial Attacks against World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18739)
*Zhixiang Guo,Siyuan Liang,Andras Balogh,Noah Lunberry,Rong-Cheng Tu,Mark Jelasity,Dacheng Tao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a white-box attack on generative world models used for driving videos, PhysCond-WMA, by perturbing physical-condition channels to induce semantic distortions while maintaining perceptual fidelity; a two-stage optimization achieves temporally coherent semantic shifts with measurable downstream risks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Expose and quantify security vulnerabilities in generative world models for autonomous driving, and motivate security-oriented safeguards and defenses.

Method: Two-stage attack: (1) quality-preserving guidance constrains reverse-diffusion loss below a calibrated threshold; (2) momentum-guided denoising accumulates target-aligned gradients along the denoising trajectory to produce stable, temporally coherent semantic shifts. Attacks target physical-condition channels (e.g., HDMap embeddings, 3D-box features) in a white-box setting.

Result: Effectiveness demonstrated: FID increases ~9% on average; FVD increases ~3.9% on average. Targeted attack success rate (ASR) ~0.55. Downstream effects: 3D detection performance decreases by ~4%; open-loop planning performance worsens by ~20%.

Conclusion: First characterization of security vulnerabilities in generative world models for driving video synthesis; highlights need for security checkers and robust defenses against physical-condition channel perturbations.

Abstract: Generative world models (WMs) are increasingly used to synthesize controllable, sensor-conditioned driving videos, yet their reliance on physical priors exposes novel attack surfaces. In this paper, we present Physical-Conditioned World Model Attack (PhysCond-WMA), the first white-box world model attack that perturbs physical-condition channels, such as HDMap embeddings and 3D-box features, to induce semantic, logic, or decision-level distortion while preserving perceptual fidelity. PhysCond-WMA is optimized in two stages: (1) a quality-preserving guidance stage that constrains reverse-diffusion loss below a calibrated threshold, and (2) a momentum-guided denoising stage that accumulates target-aligned gradients along the denoising trajectory for stable, temporally coherent semantic shifts. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that our approach remains effective while increasing FID by about 9% on average and FVD by about 3.9% on average. Under the targeted attack setting, the attack success rate (ASR) reaches 0.55. Downstream studies further show tangible risk, which using attacked videos for training decreases 3D detection performance by about 4%, and worsens open-loop planning performance by about 20%. These findings has for the first time revealed and quantified security vulnerabilities in generative world models, driving more comprehensive security checkers.

</details>


### [243] [HONEST-CAV: Hierarchical Optimization of Network Signals and Trajectories for Connected and Automated Vehicles with Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18740)
*Ziyan Zhang,Changxin Wan,Peng Hao,Kanok Boriboonsomsin,Matthew J. Barth,Yongkang Liu,Seyhan Ucar,Guoyuan Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hierarchical, decentralized MARL-based traffic signal control framework combined with SPaT-informed ML trajectory planning enhances network efficiency and energy performance in mixed HV/CAV networks, with notable gains at higher CAV penetration and electrification; MARL TSC outperforms Webster baseline, and MLTPA further improves energy and idling metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inefficiencies in traditional traffic signal control and the lack of integrated eco-driving strategies in mixed traffic, and to evaluate how CAV penetration and powertrain types affect mobility and energy outcomes.

Method: A two-level framework: (1) decentralized MARL using Value Decomposition Network (VDN) to manage cycle-based traffic signal control (TSC) at intersections; (2) SPaT prediction integrated with a Machine Learning-based Trajectory Planning Algorithm (MLTPA) to guide CAVs in Eco-Approach and Departure (EAD) maneuvers. Evaluated on a 4×4 real-world network with varying CAV proportions and powertrain types, comparing against Webster method baseline.

Result: The MARL-based TSC method outperforms the Webster baseline in speed, fuel consumption, and idling time. Adding MLTPA yields further energy and idling reductions for HONEST-CAV. At 60% CAV, average speed increases by 7.67%, fuel consumption decreases by 10.23%, and idling time decreases by 45.83%, with discussions on how CAV share and powertrain type affect performance.

Conclusion: The proposed hierarchical framework improves mobility and energy efficiency in mixed traffic, with larger gains as automation and electrification increase; results highlight the value of integrating MARL-based TSC with SPaT-informed eco-driving and suggest implications for optimal CAV penetration and powertrain planning.

Abstract: This study presents a hierarchical, network-level traffic flow control framework for mixed traffic consisting of Human-driven Vehicles (HVs), Connected and Automated Vehicles (CAVs). The framework jointly optimizes vehicle-level eco-driving behaviors and intersection-level traffic signal control to enhance overall network efficiency and decrease energy consumption. A decentralized Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) approach by Value Decomposition Network (VDN) manages cycle-based traffic signal control (TSC) at intersections, while an innovative Signal Phase and Timing (SPaT) prediction method integrates a Machine Learning-based Trajectory Planning Algorithm (MLTPA) to guide CAVs in executing Eco-Approach and Departure (EAD) maneuvers. The framework is evaluated across varying CAV proportions and powertrain types to assess its effects on mobility and energy performance. Experimental results conducted in a 4*4 real-world network demonstrate that the MARL-based TSC method outperforms the baseline model (i.e., Webster method) in speed, fuel consumption, and idling time. In addition, with MLTPA, HONEST-CAV benefits the traffic system further in energy consumption and idling time. With a 60% CAV proportion, vehicle average speed, fuel consumption, and idling time can be improved/saved by 7.67%, 10.23%, and 45.83% compared with the baseline. Furthermore, discussions on CAV proportions and powertrain types are conducted to quantify the performance of the proposed method with the impact of automation and electrification.

</details>


### [244] [RadioGen3D: 3D Radio Map Generation via Adversarial Learning on Large-Scale Synthetic Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18744)
*Junshen Chen,Angzi Xu,Zezhong Zhang,Shiyao Zhang,Junting Chen,Shuguang Cui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RadioGen3D introduces a 3D radio map estimation framework using synthetic data (Radio3DMix) derived from a parametric model, and a cGAN-based 3D U-Net for accurate, fast 3D RME with good transferability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DL-based RME mostly handles 2D near-ground scenarios and fails to capture 3D propagation and polarization due to scarce 3D data and training challenges; a scalable 3D data generation and robust model are needed for 6G and low-altitude networks.

Method: Develop a parametric target model that fuses 2D ray-tracing with 3D channel fading; extract realistic coefficient combinations from minimal real measurements to create a large synthetic dataset (Radio3DMix). Train a conditional GAN to synthesize 3D RME outputs, yielding a 3D U-Net that handles diverse input features.

Result: The proposed RadioGen3D framework outperforms baselines in both estimation accuracy and speed; fine-tuning experiments indicate strong generalization via knowledge transfer.

Conclusion: RadioGen3D provides an effective and scalable solution for 3D radio map estimation, addressing 3D propagation and polarization challenges and enabling robust transferability for practical deployment in 6G and low-altitude networks.

Abstract: Radio maps are essential for efficient radio resource management in future 6G and low-altitude networks. While deep learning (DL) techniques have emerged as an efficient alternative to conventional ray-tracing for radio map estimation (RME), most existing DL approaches are confined to 2D near-ground scenarios. They often fail to capture essential 3D signal propagation characteristics and antenna polarization effects, primarily due to the scarcity of 3D data and training challenges. To address these limitations, we present the RadioGen3D framework. First, we propose an efficient data synthesis method to generate high-quality 3D radio map data. By establishing a parametric target model that captures 2D ray-tracing and 3D channel fading characteristics, we derive realistic coefficient combinations from minimal real measurements, enabling the construction of a large-scale synthetic dataset, Radio3DMix. Utilizing this dataset, we propose a 3D model training scheme based on a conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN), yielding a 3D U-Net capable of accurate RME under diverse input feature combinations. Experimental results demonstrate that RadioGen3D surpasses all baselines in both estimation accuracy and speed. Furthermore, fine-tuning experiments verify its strong generalization capability via successful knowledge transfer.

</details>


### [245] [GLaDiGAtor: Language-Model-Augmented Multi-Relation Graph Learning for Predicting Disease-Gene Associations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18769)
*Osman Onur Kuzucu,Tunca Doğan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel encoder-decoder GNN, GLaDiGAtor, predicts disease-gene associations using a heterogeneous biological graph enriched with contextual embeddings, achieving superior accuracy and generalization over 14 baselines; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual curation of disease-gene associations is labor-intensive and not scalable. There is a need to leverage graph-based learning and language-model-derived features to integrate heterogeneous biomedical data for improved prediction and discovery of candidate disease genes.

Method: Build a heterogeneous graph with gene-gene, disease-disease, and gene-disease edges from curated databases. Use an encoder-decoder GNN (graph learning-based) with node features enriched by ProtT5 (protein sequences) and BioBERT (disease text). Train and evaluate against 14 existing methods; perform literature-supported case studies. Publicly release code and processed datasets.

Result: GLaDiGAtor achieves superior predictive accuracy and generalization compared to 14 baselines. Case studies validate biological relevance of high-confidence novel predictions. Code and datasets are publicly available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the efficacy of graph convolutional networks in modelling complex biomedical relationships and holds potential to accelerate disease-gene discovery and drug development by revealing new gene-disease links.

Abstract: Understanding disease-gene associations is essential for unravelling disease mechanisms and advancing diagnostics and therapeutics. Traditional approaches based on manual curation and literature review are labour-intensive and not scalable, prompting the use of machine learning on large biomedical data. In particular, graph neural networks (GNNs) have shown promise for modelling complex biological relationships. To address limitations in existing models, we propose GLaDiGAtor (Graph Learning-bAsed DIsease-Gene AssociaTiOn pRediction), a novel GNN framework with an encoder-decoder architecture for disease-gene association prediction. GLaDiGAtor constructs a heterogeneous biological graph integrating gene-gene, disease-disease, and gene-disease interactions from curated databases, and enriches each node with contextual features from well-known language models (ProtT5 for protein sequences and BioBERT for disease text). In evaluations, our model achieves superior predictive accuracy and generalisation, outperforming 14 existing methods. Literature-supported case studies confirm the biological relevance of high-confidence novel predictions, highlighting GLaDiGAtor's potential to discover candidate disease genes. These results underscore the power of graph convolutional networks in biomedical informatics and may ultimately facilitate drug discovery by revealing new gene-disease links. The source code and processed datasets are publicly available at https://github.com/HUBioDataLab/GLaDiGAtor.

</details>


### [246] [CaliCausalRank: Calibrated Multi-Objective Ad Ranking with Robust Counterfactual Utility Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18786)
*Xikai Yang,Sebastian Sun,Yilin Li,Yue Xing,Ming Wang,Yang Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified framework CaliCausalRank for production rankers that jointly calibrates scores, enforces multi-objective constraints, and uses robust counterfactual estimation, improving AUC, calibration, and utility across traffic segments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Score scale inconsistency across traffic segments hinders threshold transferability; position bias in click data causes offline-online metric gaps; a single approach addressing calibration, constraints, and unbiased evaluation is needed for reliable multi-objective learning in ranking systems.

Method: Integrate score calibration as a training objective (not post-hoc), apply Lagrangian relaxation to satisfy multi-objective constraints, and use variance-reduced counterfactual estimators for robust offline evaluation.

Result: On Criteo and Avazu, CaliCausalRank achieves 1.1% relative AUC improvement, 31.6% calibration error reduction, and 3.2% utility gain over the best baseline (PairRank), with stable performance across traffic segments.

Conclusion: CaliCausalRank effectively unifies calibration, constraint-based optimization, and bias-robust evaluation to deliver improved ranking performance and consistent cross-segment behavior in production-like settings.

Abstract: Ad ranking systems must simultaneously optimize multiple objectives including click-through rate (CTR), conversion rate (CVR), revenue, and user experience metrics. However, production systems face critical challenges: score scale inconsistency across traffic segments undermines threshold transferability, and position bias in click logs causes offline-online metric discrepancies. We propose CaliCausalRank, a unified framework that integrates training-time scale calibration, constraint-based multi-objective optimization, and robust counterfactual utility estimation. Our approach treats score calibration as a first-class training objective rather than post-hoc processing, employs Lagrangian relaxation for constraint satisfaction, and utilizes variance-reduced counterfactual estimators for reliable offline evaluation. Experiments on the Criteo and Avazu datasets demonstrate that CaliCausalRank achieves 1.1% relative AUC improvement, 31.6% calibration error reduction, and 3.2% utility gain compared to the best baseline (PairRank) while maintaining consistent performance across different traffic segments.

</details>


### [247] [From Few-Shot to Zero-Shot: Towards Generalist Graph Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18793)
*Yixin Liu,Shiyuan Li,Yu Zheng,Qingfeng Chen,Chengqi Zhang,Philip S. Yu,Shirui Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ARC is a few-shot generalist graph anomaly detection (GAD) framework that uses a feature alignment module, a residual GNN encoder, and a cross-attentive in-context learning module to detect anomalies across unseen datasets with minimal labeled normal data. ARC_zero extends this to zero-shot GAD by a pseudo-context mechanism for fully label-free inference. Evaluated on 17 real-world graph datasets, ARC and ARC_zero show strong generalization and efficiency in few-shot and zero-shot settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The conventional single-dataset, single-model paradigm for GAD incurs high data/compute costs, limited transferability to new datasets, and privacy barriers. A unified, generalist GAD model capable of handling unseen datasets with limited or no labeled data would improve efficiency, scalability, and privacy compliance.

Method: ARC comprises three core modules: (1) a feature Alignment module that harmonizes features across diverse datasets; (2) a Residual GNN encoder to produce dataset-agnostic anomaly representations; (3) a cross-attentive in-Context learning module that scores anomalies using few-shot normal samples at inference. ARC_zero builds on ARC by introducing a pseudo-context mechanism that selects representative pseudo-normal nodes to enable fully label-free (zero-shot) inference on unseen data.

Result: Extensive experiments on 17 real-world graph datasets demonstrate that ARC and ARC_zero achieve effective anomaly detection, strong cross-dataset generalization, and efficiency under few-shot and zero-shot conditions.

Conclusion: ARC and ARC_zero present a viable generalist GAD paradigm, enabling anomaly detection across unseen datasets with limited or no labeled data, while maintaining efficiency. The approach offers practical benefits for privacy-sensitive and resource-constrained scenarios and lays groundwork for robust cross-domain anomaly detection.

Abstract: Graph anomaly detection (GAD) is critical for identifying abnormal nodes in graph-structured data from diverse domains, including cybersecurity and social networks. The existing GAD methods often focus on the learning paradigms of "one-model-for-one-dataset", requiring dataset-specific training for each dataset to achieve optimal performance. However, this paradigm suffers from significant limitations, such as high computational and data costs, limited generalization and transferability to new datasets, and challenges in privacy-sensitive scenarios where access to full datasets or sufficient labels is restricted. To address these limitations, we propose a novel generalist GAD paradigm that aims to develop a unified model capable of detecting anomalies on multiple unseen datasets without extensive retraining/fine-tuning or dataset-specific customization. To this end, we propose ARC, a few-shot generalist GAD method that leverages in-context learning and requires only a few labeled normal samples at inference time. Specifically, ARC consists of three core modules: a feature Alignment module to unify and align features across datasets, a Residual GNN encoder to capture dataset-agnostic anomaly representations, and a cross-attentive in-Context learning module to score anomalies using few-shot normal context. Building on ARC, we further introduce ARC_zero for the zero-shot generalist GAD setting, which selects representative pseudo-normal nodes via a pseudo-context mechanism and thus enables fully label-free inference on unseen datasets. Extensive experiments on 17 real-world graph datasets demonstrate that both ARC and ARC_zero effectively detect anomalies, exhibit strong generalization ability, and perform efficiently under few-shot and zero-shot settings.

</details>


### [248] [Vectorized Bayesian Inference for Latent Dirichlet-Tree Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18795)
*Zheng Wang,Nizar Bouguila*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LDTA generalizes LDA by replacing the Dirichlet prior with a Dirichlet-Tree prior, enabling rich topic correlations and hierarchies while preserving LDA's generative structure. It introduces universal mean-field variational inference and Expectation Propagation for all Dirichlet-Tree distributions, with fully vectorized, GPU-accelerated implementations, expanding modeling capacity without sacrificing scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LDA's Dirichlet prior cannot capture complex correlations or hierarchical relationships among topics. A more expressive prior over topic proportions is desired, motivating a Dirichlet-Tree (DT) prior to allow tree-structured dependencies while keeping the LDA-like generative model.

Method: Replace the Dirichlet prior with a Dirichlet-Tree distribution within the LDA framework. Develop universal mean-field variational inference and Expectation Propagation algorithms tailored to DT priors, derive vectorized updates, and implement fully vectorized, GPU-accelerated inference.

Result: The proposed LDTA framework yields tractable, scalable inference under DT priors via universal mean-field variational inference and EP. The inference methods are vectorized, enabling GPU acceleration. This expands LDA's modeling capacity while preserving scalability.

Conclusion: LDTA broadens the expressiveness of topic models by enabling tree-structured priors over topic proportions without sacrificing the computational efficiency and scalability characteristic of LDA, thus enabling richer thematic discovery.

Abstract: Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) is a foundational model for discovering latent thematic structure in discrete data, but its Dirichlet prior cannot represent the rich correlations and hierarchical relationships often present among topics. We introduce the framework of Latent Dirichlet-Tree Allocation (LDTA), a generalization of LDA that replaces the Dirichlet prior with an arbitrary Dirichlet-Tree (DT) distribution. LDTA preserves LDA's generative structure but enables expressive, tree-structured priors over topic proportions. To perform inference, we develop universal mean-field variational inference and Expectation Propagation, providing tractable updates for all DT. We reveal the vectorized nature of the two inference methods through theoretical development, and perform fully vectorized, GPU-accelerated implementations. The resulting framework substantially expands the modeling capacity of LDA while maintaining scalability and computational efficiency.

</details>


### [249] [SGNO: Spectral Generator Neural Operators for Stable Long Horizon PDE Rollouts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18801)
*Jiayi Li,Zhaonan Wang,Flora D. Salim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SGNO is a spectral, residual neural operator for PDE surrogates that stabilizes long rollouts by constraining linear growth in Fourier space and gating nonlinear updates, plus spectral truncation. It achieves improved long-horizon accuracy and stability on APEBench 1D/2D/3D PDEs, with ablations confirming the roles of the constraints, gating, and filtering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive neural operators struggle with instability in long-horizon rollouts due to cumulative one-step errors and feedback of high-frequency components. A principled mechanism to stabilize both linear and nonlinear dynamics in the spectral domain is needed.

Method: A residual time-stepper (SGNO) using exponential time differencing in Fourier space for the linear part with a learned diagonal generator whose real part is constrained to be nonpositive; a gated nonlinear forcing term with channel mixing within each Fourier mode; optional spectral truncation and smooth masking on the forcing pathway to suppress high-frequency feedback; theoretical one-step amplification bound and finite-horizon rollout bound; conditions ensuring non-growth of latent L2 norm across steps.

Result: Empirical evaluation on APEBench across 1D/2D/3D PDE families shows SGNO achieves lower long-horizon error and longer stable rollout lengths than strong neural operator baselines. Ablations highlight the importance of the generator constraint, gating, and spectral filtering.

Conclusion: SGNO provides a stable, accurate PDE surrogate with provable rollout bounds and practical performance gains; code available at the provided GitHub URL.

Abstract: Neural operators provide fast PDE surrogates and often generalize across parameters and resolutions. However, in the short train long test setting, autoregressive rollouts can become unstable. This typically happens for two reasons: one step errors accumulate over time, and high frequency components feed back and grow.
  We introduce the Spectral Generator Neural Operator (SGNO), a residual time stepper that targets both effects. For the linear part, SGNO uses an exponential time differencing update in Fourier space with a learned diagonal generator. We constrain the real part of this generator to be nonpositive, so iterating the step does not amplify the linear dynamics. For nonlinear dynamics, SGNO adds a gated forcing term with channel mixing within each Fourier mode, which keeps the nonlinear update controlled. To further limit high frequency feedback, SGNO applies spectral truncation and an optional smooth mask on the forcing pathway.
  We derive a one step amplification bound and a finite horizon rollout error bound. The bound separates generator approximation error from nonlinear mismatch and gives sufficient conditions under which the latent $L^2$ norm does not grow across rollout steps. On APEBench spanning 1D, 2D, and 3D PDE families, SGNO achieves lower long horizon error and longer stable rollout lengths than strong neural operator baselines. Ablations confirm the roles of the generator constraint, gating, and filtering.The code is available at https://github.com/lijy32123-cloud/SGNO.

</details>


### [250] [Bayesian Lottery Ticket Hypothesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18825)
*Nicholas Kuhn,Arvid Weyrauch,Lars Heyen,Achim Streit,Markus Götz,Charlotte Debus*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bayesian Lottery Tickets exist: sparse Bayesian subnetworks can reach same or better accuracy as full BNNs across model sizes, with degradation at very high sparsity. Pruning should prioritize magnitude, then standard deviation. Mask structure and initialization influence outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: BNNs enable uncertainty quantification but are computationally expensive. Identifying sparse Bayesian subnetworks could reduce compute for training and inference while preserving uncertainty estimates, and provide insights into how Bayesian optimization interacts with pruning.

Method: Translate Lottery Ticket Hypothesis experiments to a Bayesian setting using standard computer vision models. Characterize Bayesian lottery tickets and explore a transplantation method linking BNNs with deterministic Lottery Tickets. Evaluate pruning criteria (magnitude vs posterior std) and assess dependence on mask structure and weight initialization across model sizes.

Result: The Lottery Ticket Hypothesis holds in Bayesian neural networks: winning tickets achieving matching or surpassing accuracy exist independent of model size, though degradation occurs at very high sparsities. Pruning should be driven primarily by weight magnitude, secondarily by standard deviation. Models rely on mask structure and weight initialization to varying degrees.

Conclusion: Bayesian lottery tickets exist and can inform sparse training strategies for BNNs; understanding the roles of mask structure and initialization is key to leveraging sparse Bayesian subnetworks for efficient uncertainty quantification.

Abstract: Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) are a useful tool for uncertainty quantification, but require substantially more computational resources than conventional neural networks. For non-Bayesian networks, the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis (LTH) posits the existence of sparse subnetworks that can train to the same or even surpassing accuracy as the original dense network. Such sparse networks can lower the demand for computational resources at inference, and during training. The existence of the LTH and corresponding sparse subnetworks in BNNs could motivate the development of sparse training algorithms and provide valuable insights into the underlying training process. Towards this end, we translate the LTH experiments to a Bayesian setting using common computer vision models. We investigate the defining characteristics of Bayesian lottery tickets, and extend our study towards a transplantation method connecting BNNs with deterministic Lottery Tickets. We generally find that the LTH holds in BNNs, and winning tickets of matching and surpassing accuracy are present independent of model size, with degradation at very high sparsities. However, the pruning strategy should rely primarily on magnitude, secondly on standard deviation. Furthermore, our results demonstrate that models rely on mask structure and weight initialization to varying degrees.

</details>


### [251] [L2G-Net: Local to Global Spectral Graph Neural Networks via Cauchy Factorizations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18837)
*Samuel Fernández-Menduiña,Eduardo Pavez,Antonio Ortega*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces L2G-Net, a local-to-global spectral GNN that factorizes the graph Fourier transform into subgraph operators connected by Cauchy matrices, enabling quadratic complexity, fewer learnable parameters, and strong performance on long-range dependence benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spectral GNNs promise global information but suffer from costly eigendecomposition and poor vertex-domain locality. There is a need for scalable methods that preserve global structure while remaining efficient and expressively powerful.

Method: Factorize the GFT into operators acting on subgraphs; fuse these via a sequence of structured Cauchy matrices. Process spectral representations of subgraphs and combine them without performing full eigendecomposition. Complexity scales quadratically with the number of nodes, modulated by subgraph interface size. The approach yields a Local-to-Global (L2G) architecture.

Result: Empirically, L2G-Net surpasses existing spectral methods on benchmarks that stress non-local dependencies and is competitive with state-of-the-art GNNs, all while using orders of magnitude fewer learnable parameters.

Conclusion: The paper presents a scalable, parameter-efficient spectral GNN that reconstructs global information through local spectral processing and structured fusion, enabling effective modeling of long-range dependencies without full eigendecomposition.

Abstract: Despite their theoretical advantages, spectral methods based on the graph Fourier transform (GFT) are seldom used in graph neural networks (GNNs) due to the cost of computing the eigenbasis and the lack of vertex-domain locality in spectral representations. As a result, most GNNs rely on local approximations such as polynomial Laplacian filters or message passing, which limit their ability to model long-range dependencies. In this paper, we introduce a novel factorization of the GFT into operators acting on subgraphs, which are then combined via a sequence of Cauchy matrices. We use this factorization to propose a new class of spectral GNNs, which we term L2G-Net (Local-to-Global Net). Unlike existing spectral methods, which are either fully global (when they use the GFT) or local (when they use polynomial filters), L2G-Net operates by processing the spectral representations of subgraphs and then combining them via structured matrices. Our algorithm avoids full eigendecompositions, exploiting graph topology to construct the factorization with quadratic complexity in the number of nodes, scaled by the subgraph interface size. Experiments on benchmarks stressing non-local dependencies show that L2G-Net outperforms existing spectral techniques and is competitive with the state-of-the-art with orders of magnitude fewer learnable parameters.

</details>


### [252] [Exact Attention Sensitivity and the Geometry of Transformer Stability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18849)
*Seyed Morteza Emadi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A stability theory for transformer training explaining pre-LN, DeepNorm, and warmup from exact softmax Jacobian norms and a block geometry, with depth-independent Lipschitz bounds; validated on large models; core claim: stability arises from architecture rather than attention dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why transformers are brittle to train and why certain normalization and scaling choices work, from first principles.

Method: Derive exact operator norm of the softmax Jacobian; introduce block-infinity / RMS geometry; prove Lipschitz bounds independent of sequence length; analyze pre-LN vs post-LN with depth; derive N^{-1/4} scaling for DeepNorm from attention's projection matrices; empirical validation on 774M-parameter models.

Result: Found that θ(p) ≈ 1 persists during training; attention does not sharpen to increase sensitivity; stability is governed by architectural gradient flow; pre-LN preserves identity gradient paths; post-LN accumulates LayerNorm Jacobians with depth; explain why warmup is necessary.

Conclusion: Transformers stability should be reasoned about via architectural design, not learned attention patterns; implications for training and model design.

Abstract: Despite powering modern AI, transformers remain mysteriously brittle to train. We develop a stability theory that explains why pre-LayerNorm works, why DeepNorm uses $N^{-1/4}$ scaling, and why warmup is necessary, all from first principles. Our framework has two pillars: (1) We derive the \emph{exact} operator norm of the softmax Jacobian, $\|J_{softmax}(u/τ)\|_{\infty\to 1} = θ(p)/τ$, where the balanced-mass factor $θ(p)\in[0,1]$ quantifies attention sensitivity. (2) We introduce a block-$\infty$/RMS geometry aligned with tokenwise computation, yielding Lipschitz bounds independent of sequence length. Using this framework, we prove that pre-LN preserves identity gradient paths while post-LN compounds LayerNorm Jacobians exponentially with depth, and we show that DeepNorm's $N^{-1/4}$ emerges from the quartic structure of attention's four projection matrices. We validate our theory on 774M-parameter models and find that, contrary to the intuition that attention sharpens during training to reduce sensitivity, $θ(p) \approx 1$ persists throughout. Transformer stability arises entirely from architectural gradient flow, not from attention dynamics. This finding changes how we reason about training: the architecture itself must handle sensitivity, not learned attention patterns.

</details>


### [253] [Rank-Aware Spectral Bounds on Attention Logits for Stable Low-Precision Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18851)
*Seyed Morteza Emadi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Rank-aware concentration bounds tighten attention-logit overflow risk in FP8 training by exploiting low-rank structure of QK^T; geometry-aware scaling via spectral-norm scales per layer enables overflow-free training with minimal accuracy loss across large transformers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overflow risk in low-precision transformer training arises from large attention logits. When the interaction matrix M = W^Q W^{K^T} has low rank r, the tail of max_{i,j}|S_{ij}| concentrates much more sharply than rank-agnostic bounds would predict, enabling principled scaling without extensive monitoring of activations.

Method: Derive a rank-aware concentration inequality: P(max_{i,j}|S_{ij}|>d^{?}) decays as exp(-d^{2} α^{2}/(γ r)) for rank r. Apply to FP8 training by computing per-layer scales from the spectral norm ||W^Q W^{K^T}||_2 via implicit power iteration; propose grouped query attention to avoid key expansion; ensure compatibility with fused attention kernels.

Result: For transformer attention with r = d_h, concentration bound is 8–28× tighter than rank-agnostic bounds. Geometry-aware scaling eliminates overflows in transient FP8 scenarios (where delayed scaling fails) from GPT-2 XL to Llama-2-70B, while preserving comparable downstream MMLU accuracy.

Conclusion: Rank-aware analysis provides principled overflow guarantees in FP8 transformer training and yields scalable, kernel-compatible scaling strategies that leverage low-rank structure without affecting model performance.

Abstract: Attention scores in transformers are bilinear forms $S_{ij} = x_i^\top M x_j / \sqrt{d_h}$ whose maximum magnitude governs overflow risk in low-precision training. We derive a \emph{rank-aware concentration inequality}: when the interaction matrix $M = W^Q W^{K\top}$ has rank $r \ll d$, tail probabilities for $\max_{i,j}|S_{ij}|$ decay as $\exp(-d^{2}α^{2}/(γr))$ rather than $\exp(-dα^{2})$, where $γ> 1$ is a typicality parameter. For transformer attention where $r = d_h$, this yields $8$--$28\times$ tighter concentration than rank-agnostic bounds in modern architectures. We apply this result to FP8 training, deriving \emph{geometry-aware scale factors} that provide principled overflow guarantees without observing activations. The method computes per-layer scales from the spectral norm $\|W^Q W^{K\top}\|_2$ via implicit power iteration, includes a grouped query attention formulation that avoids key expansion, and remains compatible with fused attention kernels. Across GPT-2 XL to Llama-2-70B, geometry-aware scaling eliminates overflows in transient scenarios where delayed scaling fails, while achieving comparable downstream MMLU accuracy.

</details>


### [254] [Issues with Measuring Task Complexity via Random Policies in Robotic Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18856)
*Reabetswe M. Nkhumise,Mohamed S. Talamali,Aditya Gilra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RWG-based metrics for task complexity (PIC/POIC) in non-tabular RL yield counterintuitive results and contradict established RL intuition; empirical evaluation across robotic tasks under dense/sparse rewards shows these metrics may be unreliable, underscoring the need for better complexity measures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-tabular RL lacks robust task-complexity metrics. RWG-based approaches like PIC and POIC depend on random policy performance, but their validity in real-world continuous control tasks is uncertain. Evaluating these metrics against known difficulty benchmarks can reveal their reliability and guide metric development.

Method: Empirical study across progressively difficult robotic manipulation tasks with both dense and sparse rewards. Compute RWG-derived metrics (PIC, POIC) and compare their task difficulty ordering to established RL intuition and known relative complexities.

Result: PIC ranks a two-link arm as easier than a single-link arm, contrary to RL control expectations. POIC indicates sparse-reward tasks are easier than dense-reward ones, also counterintuitive. Both results contradict typical RL understanding, suggesting RWG-based metrics may fail to capture true task complexity in non-tabular RL.

Conclusion: RWG-based metrics (PIC/POIC) can be misleading in non-tabular RL. The study motivates moving beyond RWG toward more reliable complexity measures and provides a task framework as a starting point for developing such metrics.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has enabled major advances in fields such as robotics and natural language processing. A key challenge in RL is measuring task complexity, which is essential for creating meaningful benchmarks and designing effective curricula. While there are numerous well-established metrics for assessing task complexity in tabular settings, relatively few exist in non-tabular domains. These include (i) Statistical analysis of the performance of random policies via Random Weight Guessing (RWG), and (ii) information-theoretic metrics Policy Information Capacity (PIC) and Policy-Optimal Information Capacity (POIC), which are reliant on RWG. In this paper, we evaluate these methods using progressively difficult robotic manipulation setups, with known relative complexity, with both dense and sparse reward formulations. Our empirical results reveal that measuring complexity is still nuanced. Specifically, under the same reward formulation, PIC suggests that a two-link robotic arm setup is easier than a single-link setup - which contradicts the robotic control and empirical RL perspective whereby the two-link setup is inherently more complex. Likewise, for the same setup, POIC estimates that tasks with sparse rewards are easier than those with dense rewards. Thus, we show that both PIC and POIC contradict typical understanding and empirical results from RL. These findings highlight the need to move beyond RWG-based metrics towards better metrics that can more reliably capture task complexity in non-tabular RL with our task framework as a starting point.

</details>


### [255] [VariBASed: Variational Bayes-Adaptive Sequential Monte-Carlo Planning for Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18857)
*Joery A. de Vries,Jinke He,Yaniv Oren,Pascal R. van der Vaart,Mathijs M. de Weerdt,Matthijs T. J. Spaan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VariBASeD: a variational Bayes-adaptive RL framework combining variational belief learning, sequential Monte Carlo planning, and meta-RL to scale Bayes-optimal RL on a single GPU, yielding better sample- and runtime-efficiency than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bayes-optimal agents require belief-state maintenance and planning, which are intractable; existing deep RL scales are still costly; need efficient, scalable methods for learning and planning in Bayes-adaptive MDPs.

Method: A variational framework that integrates variational belief learning (approximating posterior over MDP dynamics/uncertainties), sequential Monte Carlo planning (particle-based planning with resampling), and meta-reinforcement learning (fast adaptation to changing beliefs) within a Bayes-adaptive MDP setting; implemented to run on a single GPU.

Result: Empirical results show VariBASeD scales favorably with larger planning budgets; improves sample efficiency and runtime efficiency compared to prior Bayes-RL methods.

Conclusion: Presents VariBASeD as a scalable approach for Bayes-adaptive RL, potentially enabling more data-efficient and computation-efficient planning; suggests avenues for further scaling and comparisons with broader baselines.

Abstract: Optimally trading-off exploration and exploitation is the holy grail of reinforcement learning as it promises maximal data-efficiency for solving any task. Bayes-optimal agents achieve this, but obtaining the belief-state and performing planning are both typically intractable. Although deep learning methods can greatly help in scaling this computation, existing methods are still costly to train. To accelerate this, this paper proposes a variational framework for learning and planning in Bayes-adaptive Markov decision processes that coalesces variational belief learning, sequential Monte-Carlo planning, and meta-reinforcement learning. In a single-GPU setup, our new method VariBASeD exhibits favorable scaling to larger planning budgets, improving sample- and runtime-efficiency over prior methods.

</details>


### [256] [Hyperbolic Busemann Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18858)
*Ziheng Chen,Bernhard Schölkopf,Nicu Sebe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes lifting two neural network components (MLR and FC) into hyperbolic space via Busemann functions, yielding BMLR and BFC with a unified interpretation; demonstrates improved effectiveness and efficiency across diverse tasks; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hyperbolic geometry naturally models hierarchical and tree-structured data due to exponential volume growth; to exploit this, intrinsic, efficient hyperbolic neural network components are needed. A unified, compact hyperbolic formulation for MLR and FC can enable better representation and computation, with a Euclidean limit for compatibility.

Method: Introduce Busemann-function-based hyperbolic layers: BMLR for multinomial logistic regression and BFC for fully connected layers (and activation analogs). Provide a unified mathematical interpretation centered on point-to-horosphere distances in hyperbolic space. Highlight compact parameterization, batch-efficient computation, and a Euclidean limit. Compare with prior hyperbolic layers in terms of complexity and expressiveness.

Result: Empirical evaluations across image classification, genome sequence learning, node classification, and link prediction show improvements in effectiveness and efficiency over prior hyperbolic layers. The work provides code at the stated GitHub repository.

Conclusion: BMLR and BFC offer compact, interpretable, and efficient hyperbolic neural network components with a unified framework, achieving strong empirical gains across diverse tasks and enabling practical hyperbolic deep learning with a Euclidean fallback.

Abstract: Hyperbolic spaces provide a natural geometry for representing hierarchical and tree-structured data due to their exponential volume growth. To leverage these benefits, neural networks require intrinsic and efficient components that operate directly in hyperbolic space. In this work, we lift two core components of neural networks, Multinomial Logistic Regression (MLR) and Fully Connected (FC) layers, into hyperbolic space via Busemann functions, resulting in Busemann MLR (BMLR) and Busemann FC (BFC) layers with a unified mathematical interpretation. BMLR provides compact parameters, a point-to-horosphere distance interpretation, batch-efficient computation, and a Euclidean limit, while BFC generalizes FC and activation layers with comparable complexity. Experiments on image classification, genome sequence learning, node classification, and link prediction demonstrate improvements in effectiveness and efficiency over prior hyperbolic layers. The code is available at https://github.com/GitZH-Chen/HBNN.

</details>


### [257] [Boosting for Vector-Valued Prediction and Conditional Density Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18866)
*Jian Qian,Shu Ge*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Defines (α,β)-boostability for aggregation in structured prediction and shows geometric-median aggregation yields stability across divergences. Proposes GeoMedBoost, unifying boosting algorithms (MedBoost, AdaBoost, SAMME) under a geometric framework for vector-valued/conditional density prediction; analyzes boostability under common divergences and KL via Hellinger, with dimension-dependent vs dimension-free regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend theoretical understanding of boosting beyond scalar losses in structured prediction, by studying aggregation of vector-valued and density predictions under general divergences and identifying stability conditions that amplify weak guarantees into strong ones.

Method: Introduce (α,β)-boostability as a stability property. Prove that geometric-median aggregation achieves (α,β)-boostability for a broad class of divergences. Characterize boostability under l1, l2, total variation, and Hellinger divergences; discuss KL divergence indirectly via Hellinger. Propose GeoMedBoost using exponential reweighting and geometric-median aggregation; analyze performance under a weak learner condition. Show connections to MedBoost, AdaBoost, and SAMME.

Result: Foundational stability framework for boosting in structured prediction. Geometric median aggregation provides (α,β)-boostability with geometry-dependent tradeoffs; boostability under common divergences reveals dimension-dependent vs dimension-free regimes; KL is not directly boostable, but can be controlled via Hellinger. GeoMedBoost achieves exponential decay in empirical divergence exceedance and unifies classic boosting algorithms under a single geometric approach.

Conclusion: A unified geometric boosting framework for structured prediction with broad applicability across divergences. The framework clarifies how aggregation can amplify weak guarantees, recovers classical boosting algorithms, and offers a principled way to analyze vector-valued and density-valued predictions through (α,β)-boostability.

Abstract: Despite the widespread use of boosting in structured prediction, a general theoretical understanding of aggregation beyond scalar losses remains incomplete. We study vector-valued and conditional density prediction under general divergences and identify stability conditions under which aggregation amplifies weak guarantees into strong ones.
  We formalize this stability property as \emph{$(α,β)$-boostability}. We show that geometric median aggregation achieves $(α,β)$-boostability for a broad class of divergences, with tradeoffs that depend on the underlying geometry. For vector-valued prediction and conditional density estimation, we characterize boostability under common divergences ($\ell_1$, $\ell_2$, $\TV$, and $\Hel$) with geometric median, revealing a sharp distinction between dimension-dependent and dimension-free regimes. We further show that while KL divergence is not directly boostable via geometric median aggregation, it can be handled indirectly through boostability under Hellinger distance.
  Building on these structural results, we propose a generic boosting framework \textsc{GeoMedBoost} based on exponential reweighting and geometric-median aggregation. Under a weak learner condition and $(α,β)$-boostability, we obtain exponential decay of the empirical divergence exceedance error. Our framework recovers classical algorithms such as \textsc{MedBoost}, \textsc{AdaBoost}, and \textsc{SAMME} as special cases, and provides a unified geometric view of boosting for structured prediction.

</details>


### [258] [HEHRGNN: A Unified Embedding Model for Knowledge Graphs with Hyperedges and Hyper-Relational Edges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18897)
*Rajesh Rajagopalamenon,Unnikrishnan Cheramangalath*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes HEHRGNN, a unified GNN-based model for n-ary knowledge graphs that combines hyperedges and hyper-relational edges through a unified fact representation and a novel message-passing encoder; shows improved inductive link prediction performance across real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world knowledge graphs contain complex, non-binary facts that cannot be captured by simple graphs. Existing KG embedding/GNN approaches treat hyperedges and hyper-relational edges separately, limiting their ability to unify representations and leverage shared structural information.

Method: Introduce HEHR unified fact representation (for n-ary facts) and HEHRGNN encoder with a novel message-passing scheme that jointly propagates information through both hyperedges and hyper-relational edges in a unified framework to learn node/entity, relation, and higher-arity representations.

Result: Empirical evaluation on link prediction tasks shows that HEHRGNN achieves effective unified embeddings with inductive prediction capability, outperforming baseline models tailored to hyperedges or hyper-relational data across multiple real-world datasets.

Conclusion: HEHRGNN provides a unified embedding framework for n-ary KGs with both hyperedges and hyper-relational edges, demonstrating improved link prediction performance and inductive capabilities, suggesting potential applicability to other KG tasks and more complex relational structures.

Abstract: Knowledge Graph(KG) has gained traction as a machine-readable organization of real-world knowledge for analytics using artificial intelligence systems. Graph Neural Network(GNN), is proven to be an effective KG embedding technique that enables various downstream tasks like link prediction, node classification, and graph classification. The focus of research in both KG embedding and GNNs has been mostly oriented towards simple graphs with binary relations. However, real-world knowledge bases have a significant share of complex and n-ary facts that cannot be represented by binary edges. More specifically, real-world knowledge bases are often a mix of two types of n-ary facts - (i) that require hyperedges and (ii) that require hyper-relational edges. Though there are research efforts catering to these n-ary fact types, they are pursued independently for each type. We propose $H$yper$E$dge $H$yper-$R$elational edge $GNN$(HEHRGNN), a unified embedding model for n-ary relational KGs with both hyperedges and hyper-relational edges. The two main components of the model are i)HEHR unified fact representation format, and ii)HEHRGNN encoder, a GNN-based encoder with a novel message propagation model capable of capturing complex graph structures comprising both hyperedges and hyper-relational edges. The experimental results of HEHRGNN on link prediction tasks show its effectiveness as a unified embedding model, with inductive prediction capability, for link prediction across real-world datasets having different types of n-ary facts. The model also shows improved link prediction performance over baseline models for hyperedge and hyper-relational datasets.

</details>


### [259] [PCA-VAE: Differentiable Subspace Quantization without Codebook Collapse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18904)
*Hao Lu,Onur C. Koyun,Yongxin Guo,Zhengjie Zhu,Abbas Alili,Metin Nafi Gurcan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Replace vector quantization with a differentiable online PCA bottleneck (Oja's rule) in a VAE, achieving competitive reconstructions with far fewer latent bits and interpretable latent axes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Eliminate non-differentiable quantizers, straight-through hacks, and collapse risks by using a principled, differentiable dimensionality-reduction bottleneck.

Method: Train an online PCA bottleneck within a VAE using Oja's rule to learn an orthogonal, variance-ordered latent basis; no codebooks, commitment losses, or lookup noise; end-to-end differentiable.

Result: PCA-VAE surpasses VQ-GAN and SimVQ on CelebAHQ in reconstruction quality while using 10–100× fewer latent bits; latent dimensions become interpretable (pose, lighting, gender cues) without adversarial or disentanglement objectives.

Conclusion: PCA is a viable replacement for VQ in generative modeling: mathematically grounded, stable, bit-efficient, and semantically structured, opening new directions beyond vector quantization.

Abstract: Vector-quantized autoencoders deliver high-fidelity latents but suffer inherent flaws: the quantizer is non-differentiable, requires straight-through hacks, and is prone to collapse. We address these issues at the root by replacing VQ with a simple, principled, and fully differentiable alternative: an online PCA bottleneck trained via Oja's rule. The resulting model, PCA-VAE, learns an orthogonal, variance-ordered latent basis without codebooks, commitment losses, or lookup noise. Despite its simplicity, PCA-VAE exceeds VQ-GAN and SimVQ in reconstruction quality on CelebAHQ while using 10-100x fewer latent bits. It also produces naturally interpretable dimensions (e.g., pose, lighting, gender cues) without adversarial regularization or disentanglement objectives. These results suggest that PCA is a viable replacement for VQ: mathematically grounded, stable, bit-efficient, and semantically structured, offering a new direction for generative models beyond vector quantization.

</details>


### [260] [TRUE: A Trustworthy Unified Explanation Framework for Large Language Model Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18905)
*Yujiao Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRUE provides multi-level, verifiable explanations for LLM reasoning by integrating executable verification, feasible-region DAGs, and causal failure mode analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current explanation methods lack trustworthy structural insight, are typically single-instance, and fail to reveal reasoning stability or systematic failures across inputs and classes.

Method: Three-part framework: 1) executable reasoning verification via blind execution of reasoning traces treated as process specifications; 2) local structure via feasible-region DAGs built from structure-consistent perturbations to characterize reasoning stability and the executable input region; 3) class-level causal failure mode analysis using Shapley values to identify recurring failure patterns and quantify their influence.

Result: Extensive experiments across multiple reasoning benchmarks show the framework delivers multi-level explanations: executable reasoning structures for individual instances, feasible-region representations for neighboring inputs, and interpretable, quantitatively valued failure modes at the class level.

Conclusion: TRUE offers a unified, principled paradigm to improve interpretability and reliability of LLM reasoning by providing verifiable, multi-level explanations and insights into stability and failure mechanisms.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in complex reasoning tasks, yet their decision-making processes remain difficult to interpret. Existing explanation methods often lack trustworthy structural insight and are limited to single-instance analysis, failing to reveal reasoning stability and systematic failure mechanisms. To address these limitations, we propose the Trustworthy Unified Explanation Framework (TRUE), which integrates executable reasoning verification, feasible-region directed acyclic graph (DAG) modeling, and causal failure mode analysis. At the instance level, we redefine reasoning traces as executable process specifications and introduce blind execution verification to assess operational validity. At the local structural level, we construct feasible-region DAGs via structure-consistent perturbations, enabling explicit characterization of reasoning stability and the executable region in the local input space. At the class level, we introduce a causal failure mode analysis method that identifies recurring structural failure patterns and quantifies their causal influence using Shapley values. Extensive experiments across multiple reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed framework provides multi-level, verifiable explanations, including executable reasoning structures for individual instances, feasible-region representations for neighboring inputs, and interpretable failure modes with quantified importance at the class level. These results establish a unified and principled paradigm for improving the interpretability and reliability of LLM reasoning systems.

</details>


### [261] [DeepInterestGR: Mining Deep Multi-Interest Using Multi-Modal LLMs for Generative Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18907)
*Yangchen Zeng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DeepInterestGR introduces three innovations to overcome the Shallow Interest problem in generative recommender systems: Multi-LLM Interest Mining (MLIM), Reward-Labeled Deep Interest (RLDI), and Interest-Enhanced Item Discretization (IEID). It uses a two-stage training pipeline (supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement learning with an interest-aware reward) and demonstrates improved HR@K and NDCG@K on three Amazon Review benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current generative recommender methods rely on shallow surface signals (titles/descriptions) to represent items, failing to capture latent, semantically rich user interests. This Shallow Interest limitation hampers personalization depth and interpretability; a richer, multimodal, semantically grounded interest representation is needed.

Method: (1) Multi-LLM Interest Mining (MLIM): utilize multiple frontier LLMs and multi-modal variants with Chain-of-Thought prompting to extract deep textual and visual interests. (2) Reward-Labeled Deep Interest (RLDI): train a lightweight binary classifier to assign reward labels to mined interests for supervision in reinforcement learning. (3) Interest-Enhanced Item Discretization (IEID): encode deep interests into semantic embeddings and quantize into SID tokens via RQ-VAE. Training: (a) supervised fine-tuning aligns the generative model with deep interest signals and collaborative filtering patterns, (b) reinforcement learning with GRPO optimized by an Interest-Aware Reward.

Result: Empirical evaluation on three Amazon Review benchmarks shows DeepInterestGR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in HR@K and NDCG@K.

Conclusion: The combination of MLIM, RLDI, and IEID within a two-stage training pipeline effectively leverages deep, multimodal interest signals to improve personalization and interpretability in autoregressive SID-based recommender systems; demonstrated through strong ranking metrics on benchmark data.

Abstract: Recent generative recommendation frameworks have demonstrated remarkable scaling potential by reformulating item prediction as autoregressive Semantic ID (SID) generation. However, existing methods primarily rely on shallow behavioral signals, encoding items solely through surface-level textual features such as titles and descriptions. This reliance results in a critical Shallow Interest problem: the model fails to capture the latent, semantically rich interests underlying user interactions, limiting both personalization depth and recommendation interpretability. DeepInterestGR introduces three key innovations: (1) Multi-LLM Interest Mining (MLIM): We leverage multiple frontier LLMs along with their multi-modal variants to extract deep textual and visual interest representations through Chain-of-Thought prompting. (2) Reward-Labeled Deep Interest (RLDI): We employ a lightweight binary classifier to assign reward labels to mined interests, enabling effective supervision signals for reinforcement learning. (3) Interest-Enhanced Item Discretization (IEID): The curated deep interests are encoded into semantic embeddings and quantized into SID tokens via RQ-VAE. We adopt a two-stage training pipeline: supervised fine-tuning aligns the generative model with deep interest signals and collaborative filtering patterns, followed by reinforcement learning with GRPO optimized by our Interest-Aware Reward. Experiments on three Amazon Review benchmarks demonstrate that DeepInterestGR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across HR@K and NDCG@K metrics.

</details>


### [262] [SLDP: Semi-Local Differential Privacy for Density-Adaptive Analytics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18910)
*Alexey Kroshnin,Alexandra Suvorikova*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Semi-Local Differential Privacy (SLDP) to enable density-adaptive domain discretization under LDP without extra privacy budget for refinement, via an interactive protocol that defines privacy regions per user.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome privacy-cost growth in iterative refinement under LDP when building high-resolution, density-adaptive discretizations for privacy-preserving analytics.

Method: Proposes SLDP that assigns a privacy region to each user based on local data density and defines adjacency by movement within the region. An interactive $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-SLDP protocol, with an honest-but-curious server on a public channel, estimates these regions privately. Crucially, privacy cost is decoupled from refinement iterations, enabling high-resolution grids without extra privacy budget. Empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets.

Result: Demonstrates effective estimation of privacy regions and improved utility for estimation tasks compared to baseline approaches, across synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: SLDP decouples refinement costs from privacy budget, enabling high-resolution, density-adaptive discretization under LDP. The framework relies on an interactive server-mediated protocol and assumes an honest-but-curious server and public channel, with empirical results supporting its utility and scalability.

Abstract: Density-adaptive domain discretization is essential for high-utility privacy-preserving analytics but remains challenging under Local Differential Privacy (LDP) due to the privacy-budget costs associated with iterative refinement. We propose a novel framework, Semi-Local Differential Privacy (SLDP), that assigns a privacy region to each user based on local density and defines adjacency by the potential movement of a point within its privacy region. We present an interactive $(\varepsilon, δ)$-SLDP protocol, orchestrated by an honest-but-curious server over a public channel, to estimate these regions privately. Crucially, our framework decouples the privacy cost from the number of refinement iterations, allowing for high-resolution grids without additional privacy budget cost. We experimentally demonstrate the framework's effectiveness on estimation tasks across synthetic and real-world datasets.

</details>


### [263] [From Human-Level AI Tales to AI Leveling Human Scales](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18911)
*Peter Romero,Fernando Martínez-Plumed,Zachary R. Tyler,Matthieu Téhénan,Sipeng Chen,Álvaro David Gómez Antón,Luning Sun,Manuel Cebrian,Lexin Zhou,Yael Moros Daval,Daniel Romero-Alvarado,Félix Martí Pérez,Kevin Wei,José Hernández-Orallo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework for calibrating AI capability benchmarks to a world-population anchored, logarithmic scale, using public human benchmarks and LLM-derived demographic extrapolation to estimate a common base; enables recalibration and standardization across capabilities with group-slice and post-stratification validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
