Motivation: Standard “human-level” claims are often misleading when benchmarks are incommensurate or human baselines are narrow. A world-population anchored scale provides a common frame for meaningful cross-model, cross-capability comparisons.

Method: Develop multi-level, logarithmic capability scales (base B) calibrated per capability using public human data (PISA, TIMSS, ICAR, UKBioBank, ReliabilityBench). Estimate base B by extrapolating between two demographic profiles with LLMs. Evaluate calibration quality via group slicing and post-stratification.

Result: Feasibility demonstrated: scales can be recalibrated to reflect whole-world population; B can be estimated through demographic extrapolation with LLMs; evaluation using group slicing and post-stratification supports the validity of the mappings across capabilities (reasoning, comprehension, knowledge, volume).

Conclusion: The world-population anchored calibration framework offers a principled, scalable method to standardize AI capability reporting, reducing misinterpretation of “human-level” claims and enabling fairer cross-model comparisons; further expansion of data sources and refinement of extrapolation methods could strengthen estimates.

Abstract: Comparing AI models to "human level" is often misleading when benchmark scores are incommensurate or human baselines are drawn from a narrow population. To address this, we propose a framework that calibrates items against the 'world population' and report performance on a common, human-anchored scale. Concretely, we build on a set of multi-level scales for different capabilities where each level should represent a probability of success of the whole world population on a logarithmic scale with a base $B$. We calibrate each scale for each capability (reasoning, comprehension, knowledge, volume, etc.) by compiling publicly released human test data spanning education and reasoning benchmarks (PISA, TIMSS, ICAR, UKBioBank, and ReliabilityBench). The base $B$ is estimated by extrapolating between samples with two demographic profiles using LLMs, with the hypothesis that they condense rich information about human populations. We evaluate the quality of different mappings using group slicing and post-stratification. The new techniques allow for the recalibration and standardization of scales relative to the whole-world population.

</details>


### [264] [LoMime: Query-Efficient Membership Inference using Model Extraction in Label-Only Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18934)
*Abdullah Caglar Oksuz,Anisa Halimi,Erman Ayday*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A practical label-only MIA using a surrogate extracted via targeted query strategies, moving most of the work to a one-time model extraction phase to reduce ongoing query costs while maintaining high accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MIAs rely on assumptions (public data, shadow models, detailed confidences, distribution knowledge) and fail under defenses like confidence masking; label-only MIAs often require many queries per sample. A black-box, label-only attack with lower query burden is needed.

Method: Query the target model M with active sampling, perturbation-based selection, and synthetic data to construct a functionally similar surrogate S. Perform membership inference on S, transferring the attack to M with a one-time extraction phase, thus eliminating repeated queries.

Result: The approach matches state-of-the-art label-only MIAs in effectiveness while dramatically reducing query costs. On Purchase, Location, and Texas Hospital benchmarks, roughly 1% of training samples suffice to extract S and reach MIA accuracy within ±1% of M. The work also evaluates standard defenses against label-only MIAs.

Conclusion: Query overhead is shifted to an initial extraction phase under strict black-box constraints, yielding a practical and robust MIA with competitive performance and resilience to defenses.

Abstract: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) threaten the privacy of machine learning models by revealing whether a specific data point was used during training. Existing MIAs often rely on impractical assumptions such as access to public datasets, shadow models, confidence scores, or training data distribution knowledge and making them vulnerable to defenses like confidence masking and adversarial regularization. Label-only MIAs, even under strict constraints suffer from high query requirements per sample. We propose a cost-effective label-only MIA framework based on transferability and model extraction. By querying the target model M using active sampling, perturbation-based selection, and synthetic data, we extract a functionally similar surrogate S on which membership inference is performed. This shifts query overhead to a one-time extraction phase, eliminating repeated queries to M . Operating under strict black-box constraints, our method matches the performance of state-of-the-art label-only MIAs while significantly reducing query costs. On benchmarks including Purchase, Location, and Texas Hospital, we show that a query budget equivalent to testing $\approx1\%$ of training samples suffices to extract S and achieve membership inference accuracy within $\pm1\%$ of M . We also evaluate the effectiveness of standard defenses proposed for label-only MIAs against our attack.

</details>


### [265] [Exponential Convergence of (Stochastic) Gradient Descent for Separable Logistic Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18946)
*Sacchit Kale,Piyushi Manupriya,Pierre Marion,Francis bach,Anant Raj*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Exponential convergence for both GD and SGD is achievable with growing step sizes in separable logistic regression under a margin condition, without relying on edge-of-stability instability; GD is non-adaptively increasing and SGD uses a lightweight adaptive step-size rule, yielding horizon-free, exponential acceleration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address whether acceleration requires instability and edge-of-stability phenomena. The work challenges the view that large steps exploit instability by showing stable-regime acceleration via structured step-size growth and provides practical, horizon-free algorithms with strong convergence guarantees.

Method: GD: introduce a simple, non-adaptive increasing step-size schedule and prove exponential convergence for separable logistic regression under a margin condition, staying within a stable regime; algorithm is anytime and does not need horizon or target accuracy. SGD: introduce a lightweight adaptive step-size rule that avoids line search and specialized procedures, proving exponential convergence. Both analyses show that step-size growth alone can induce acceleration without instability.

Result: Proves exponential convergence guarantees for both GD and SGD under the proposed step-size schemes in the separable logistic regression setting with a margin condition; the GD method is non-adaptive and stable, while the SGD method uses a practical adaptive rule and achieves exponential rates without line searches.

Conclusion: Carefully structured step-size growth suffices for exponential acceleration in both GD and SGD, and instability at the edge is not necessary; the results replace reliance on edge-of-stability with horizon-free, practical step-size schedules.

Abstract: Gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent are central to modern machine learning, yet their behavior under large step sizes remains theoretically unclear. Recent work suggests that acceleration often arises near the edge of stability, where optimization trajectories become unstable and difficult to analyze. Existing results for separable logistic regression achieve faster convergence by explicitly leveraging such unstable regimes through constant or adaptive large step sizes. In this paper, we show that instability is not inherent to acceleration. We prove that gradient descent with a simple, non-adaptive increasing step-size schedule achieves exponential convergence for separable logistic regression under a margin condition, while remaining entirely within a stable optimization regime. The resulting method is anytime and does not require prior knowledge of the optimization horizon or target accuracy. We also establish exponential convergence of stochastic gradient descent using a lightweight adaptive step-size rule that avoids line search and specialized procedures, improving upon existing polynomial-rate guarantees. Together, our results demonstrate that carefully structured step-size growth alone suffices to obtain exponential acceleration for both gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent.

</details>


### [266] [Toward Manifest Relationality in Transformers via Symmetry Reduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18948)
*J. François,L. Ravera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Presents a symmetry-reduction framework for Transformers by reformulating representations and attention in invariant relational terms to remove redundancy and analyze optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers exhibit coordinate-dependent redundancies and continuous symmetries in model and head spaces; a symmetry-reduction, invariant-relational approach aims to reduce parameters and provide geometric insight into optimization.

Method: Reformulate representations, attention, and optimization dynamics using invariant relational quantities; design architectures that operate on relational structures thereby eliminating redundant degrees of freedom by construction.

Result: A theoretical framework and architecture class that operates on relational structures, yielding a principled geometric perspective on parameter reduction and optimization analysis (no empirical results reported in the abstract).

Conclusion: Symmetry reduction via invariant relational formulations complements explicit symmetry breaking, offering a foundational framework for parameter efficiency and optimization understanding in Transformers.

Abstract: Transformer models contain substantial internal redundancy arising from coordinate-dependent representations and continuous symmetries, in model space and in head space, respectively. While recent approaches address this by explicitly breaking symmetry, we propose a complementary framework based on symmetry reduction. We reformulate representations, attention mechanisms, and optimization dynamics in terms of invariant relational quantities, eliminating redundant degrees of freedom by construction. This perspective yields architectures that operate directly on relational structures, providing a principled geometric framework for reducing parameter redundancy and analyzing optimization.

</details>


### [267] [Incremental Transformer Neural Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18955)
*Philip Mortimer,Cristiana Diaconu,Tommy Rochussen,Bruno Mlodozeniec,Richard E. Turner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: incTNP introduces incremental updates for Transformer Neural Processes using causal masking, KV caching, and autoregressive training to achieve linear-time updates with comparable predictive performance to standard TNPs, enabling efficient streaming inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable cheap, incremental updates for sequential data streams in Transformer Neural Processes without sacrificing accuracy or consistency.

Method: Propose incTNP with causal masking, key-value caching for fast updates, and a data-efficient autoregressive training regime. Train and evaluate on synthetic and real-world tasks (e.g., tabular regression, temperature prediction), comparing against non-causal TNPs. Assess both predictive performance and update efficiency, plus the consistency of updates via a metric related to implicit Bayesianness.

Result: incTNP matches or surpasses standard non-causal TNPs in predictive performance while reducing update cost from quadratic to linear; achieves orders-of-magnitude speedups for sequential inference. Also preserves an implicitly Bayesian update rule similar to non-causal TNPs.

Conclusion: IncTNP provides computationally efficient streaming inference with maintained consistency, enabling practical real-time sequential modelling with Transformer Neural Processes.

Abstract: Neural Processes (NPs), and specifically Transformer Neural Processes (TNPs), have demonstrated remarkable performance across tasks ranging from spatiotemporal forecasting to tabular data modelling. However, many of these applications are inherently sequential, involving continuous data streams such as real-time sensor readings or database updates. In such settings, models should support cheap, incremental updates rather than recomputing internal representations from scratch for every new observation -- a capability existing TNP variants lack. Drawing inspiration from Large Language Models, we introduce the Incremental TNP (incTNP). By leveraging causal masking, Key-Value (KV) caching, and a data-efficient autoregressive training strategy, incTNP matches the predictive performance of standard TNPs while reducing the computational cost of updates from quadratic to linear time complexity. We empirically evaluate our model on a range of synthetic and real-world tasks, including tabular regression and temperature prediction. Our results show that, surprisingly, incTNP delivers performance comparable to -- or better than -- non-causal TNPs while unlocking orders-of-magnitude speedups for sequential inference. Finally, we assess the consistency of the model's updates -- by adapting a metric of ``implicit Bayesianness", we show that incTNP retains a prediction rule as implicitly Bayesian as standard non-causal TNPs, demonstrating that incTNP achieves the computational benefits of causal masking without sacrificing the consistency required for streaming inference.

</details>


### [268] [Conditionally Site-Independent Neural Evolution of Antibody Sequences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18982)
*Stephen Zhewen Lu,Aakarsh Vermani,Kohei Sanno,Jiarui Lu,Frederick A Matsen,Milind Jagota,Yun S. Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoSiNE combines a continuous-time Markov chain with a deep neural network to model antibody sequence evolution, capturing epistasis; it yields a first-order approximation to the sequential mutation process with quadratic error in branch length, improves zero-shot variant effect prediction, and, with Guided Gillespie, enables inference-time affinity optimization toward specific antigens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between marginal sequence models and explicit evolutionary dynamics in antibody maturation by capturing epistatic interactions and context-dependent somatic hypermutation.

Method: CoSiNE: a CTMC parameterized by a deep neural network. The authors prove a first-order approximation to the intractable sequential mutation process with an error bound quadratic in branch length. They also introduce Guided Gillespie, a classifier-guided sampling scheme for inference-time optimization of binding affinity toward chosen antigens.

Result: CoSiNE outperforms state-of-the-art language models in zero-shot variant effect prediction by disentangling selection from context-dependent hypermutation. Guided Gillespie enables efficient affinity optimization toward specific antigens.

Conclusion: The framework provides a principled, expressive model of antibody evolution that combines tractable probabilistic dynamics with deep learning, enabling accurate variant effect prediction and targeted affinity design.

Abstract: Common deep learning approaches for antibody engineering focus on modeling the marginal distribution of sequences. By treating sequences as independent samples, however, these methods overlook affinity maturation as a rich and largely untapped source of information about the evolutionary process by which antibodies explore the underlying fitness landscape. In contrast, classical phylogenetic models explicitly represent evolutionary dynamics but lack the expressivity to capture complex epistatic interactions. We bridge this gap with CoSiNE, a continuous-time Markov chain parameterized by a deep neural network. Mathematically, we prove that CoSiNE provides a first-order approximation to the intractable sequential point mutation process, capturing epistatic effects with an error bound that is quadratic in branch length. Empirically, CoSiNE outperforms state-of-the-art language models in zero-shot variant effect prediction by explicitly disentangling selection from context-dependent somatic hypermutation. Finally, we introduce Guided Gillespie, a classifier-guided sampling scheme that steers CoSiNE at inference time, enabling efficient optimization of antibody binding affinity toward specific antigens.

</details>


### [269] [Why ReLU? A Bit-Model Dichotomy for Deep Network Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19017)
*Ilan Doron-Arad,Elchanan Mossel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Finite-precision ERM is #P-hard for deep networks with polynomial activations (degree ≥2); ReLU/piecewise-linear activations enable NP-complete/tractable training under bit constraints, highlighting precision as a fundamental determinant of learnability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge theoretical ERM complexity with realistic, finite-precision hardware; understand how numeric representation impacts learnability and optimization in neural networks.

Method: Complexity-theoretic reductions under a bit-level model (ERM_bit): prove #P-hardness of training for deep networks with polynomial activations (degree ≥2) and rational coefficients; show hardness for sign of a gradient and for determining a bit in the gradient; contrast with ReLU, where ERM_bit ⊆ NP (NP-complete) and backpropagation runs in polynomial time.

Result: A dichotomy: polynomial-activation networks (degree≥2) render ERM_bit #P-hard; certain gradient-related problems are hard (sign of a partial derivative, specific gradient bit). In contrast, piecewise-linear activations like ReLU yield tractable results: ERM_bit is in NP (indeed NP-complete) and backpropagation remains polynomial-time.

Conclusion: Finite-precision constraints fundamentally affect learnability; activation function choice determines computational tractability in ERM under bit-level models, offering a theoretical lens on phenomena like exploding/vanishing gradients and guiding design near hardware limits.

Abstract: Theoretical analyses of Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) are standardly framed within the Real-RAM model of computation. In this setting, training even simple neural networks is known to be $\exists \mathbb{R}$-complete -- a complexity class believed to be harder than NP, that characterizes the difficulty of solving systems of polynomial inequalities over the real numbers. However, this algebraic framework diverges from the reality of digital computation with finite-precision hardware. In this work, we analyze the theoretical complexity of ERM under a realistic bit-level model ($\mathsf{ERM}_{\text{bit}}$), where network parameters and inputs are constrained to be rational numbers with polynomially bounded bit-lengths. Under this model, we reveal a sharp dichotomy in tractability governed by the network's activation function. We prove that for deep networks with {\em any} polynomial activations with rational coefficients and degree at least $2$, the bit-complexity of training is severe: deciding $\mathsf{ERM}_{\text{bit}}$ is $\#P$-Hard, hence believed to be strictly harder than NP-complete problems. Furthermore, we show that determining the sign of a single partial derivative of the empirical loss function is intractable (unlikely in BPP), and deciding a specific bit in the gradient is $\#P$-Hard. This provides a complexity-theoretic perspective for the phenomenon of exploding and vanishing gradients. In contrast, we show that for piecewise-linear activations such as ReLU, the precision requirements remain manageable: $\mathsf{ERM}_{\text{bit}}$ is contained within NP (specifically NP-complete), and standard backpropagation runs in polynomial time. Our results demonstrate that finite-precision constraints are not merely implementation details but fundamental determinants of learnability.

</details>


### [270] [Learning to Detect Language Model Training Data via Active Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19020)
*Junjie Oscar Yin,John X. Morris,Vitaly Shmatikov,Sewon Min,Hannaneh Hajishirzi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Active Data Reconstruction Attacks (ADRA) use on-policy RL to finetune a policy that actively reconstructs training data, improving membership inference performance over passive MIAs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional MIAs are passive and rely on fixed weights; training data may be more reconstructible than non-members, suggesting detectable differences that can be exploited through active data reconstruction.

Method: Introduce ADRA and ADRA+ that finetune a policy initialized from the target model using on-policy RL to induce data reconstruction; design reconstruction metrics and contrastive rewards; evaluate with a pool of candidate data (BookMIA, AIME).

Result: ADRA/ADRA+ outperform existing MIAs in detecting pre-training, post-training, and distillation data, with an average improvement of 10.7 percentage points over the previous runner-up; ADRA+ improves over Min-K%++ by 18.8% on BookMIA (pre-training) and 7.6% on AIME (post-training).

Conclusion: Active, RL-driven reconstruction attacks significantly enhance membership inference capabilities, validating the hypothesis that training data is more reconstructible and can be exploited to detect data membership across various training scenarios.

Abstract: Detecting LLM training data is generally framed as a membership inference attack (MIA) problem. However, conventional MIAs operate passively on fixed model weights, using log-likelihoods or text generations. In this work, we introduce \textbf{Active Data Reconstruction Attack} (ADRA), a family of MIA that actively induces a model to reconstruct a given text through training. We hypothesize that training data are \textit{more reconstructible} than non-members, and the difference in their reconstructibility can be exploited for membership inference. Motivated by findings that reinforcement learning (RL) sharpens behaviors already encoded in weights, we leverage on-policy RL to actively elicit data reconstruction by finetuning a policy initialized from the target model. To effectively use RL for MIA, we design reconstruction metrics and contrastive rewards. The resulting algorithms, \textsc{ADRA} and its adaptive variant \textsc{ADRA+}, improve both reconstruction and detection given a pool of candidate data. Experiments show that our methods consistently outperform existing MIAs in detecting pre-training, post-training, and distillation data, with an average improvement of 10.7\% over the previous runner-up. In particular, \MethodPlus~improves over Min-K\%++ by 18.8\% on BookMIA for pre-training detection and by 7.6\% on AIME for post-training detection.

</details>


### [271] [Pushing the Limits of Inverse Lithography with Generative Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19027)
*Haoyu Yang,Haoxing Ren*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A probabilistic, conditional mask-synthesis framework for inverse lithography that proposes multiple candidate masks and refines them via ILT-guided policy optimization, yielding higher-quality masks with faster throughput than purely numerical ILT or deterministic GenAI baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ILT's highly non-convex objectives cause optimization to stall in poor local minima. Deterministic generative warm-starts commonly mimic suboptimal data and offer limited guidance for escaping traps during refinement.

Method: Pretrain a conditional generator with WGAN plus a reconstruction loss to model the mask distribution. Fine-tune with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) using an ILT-guided imitation loss. At inference, sample several masks, perform fast batched ILT refinement, evaluate lithography metrics (e.g., EPE, process window), and select the best candidate.

Result: On LithoBench: reduces EPE violations under a 3 nm tolerance and roughly doubles throughput versus a strong numerical ILT baseline, while improving final mask quality. On ICCAD13: >20% EPE improvement with 3× speedup over the SOTA numerical ILT solver.

Conclusion: Learning to propose ILT-friendly initializations mitigates non-convexity and delivers gains beyond traditional solvers or deterministic GenAI approaches.

Abstract: Inverse lithography (ILT) is critical for modern semiconductor manufacturing but suffers from highly non-convex objectives that often trap optimization in poor local minima. Generative AI has been explored to warm-start ILT, yet most approaches train deterministic image-to-image translators to mimic sub-optimal datasets, providing limited guidance for escaping non-convex traps during refinement. We reformulate mask synthesis as conditional sampling: a generator learns a distribution over masks conditioned on the design and proposes multiple candidates. The generator is first pretrained with WGAN plus a reconstruction loss, then fine-tuned using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with an ILT-guided imitation loss. At inference, we sample a small batch of masks, run fast batched ILT refinement, evaluate lithography metrics (e.g., EPE, process window), and select the best candidate. On \texttt{LithoBench} dataset, the proposed hybrid framework reduces EPE violations under a 3\,nm tolerance and roughly doubles throughput versus a strong numerical ILT baseline, while improving final mask quality. We also present over 20\% EPE improvement on \texttt{ICCAD13} contest cases with 3$\times$ speedup over the SOTA numerical ILT solver. By learning to propose ILT-friendly initializations, our approach mitigates non-convexity and advances beyond what traditional solvers or GenAI can achieve.

</details>


### [272] [A Markovian View of Iterative-Feedback Loops in Image Generative Models: Neural Resonance and Model Collapse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19033)
*Vibhas Kumar Vats,David J. Crandall,Samuel Goree*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes 'neural resonance': a low-dimensional invariant latent structure that emerges under iterative AI feedback in training data, modeled as a Markov process; identifies ergodicity and directional contraction as necessary conditions for collapse; validates across diffusion models, CycleGAN, and audio feedback; offers an eight-pattern taxonomy and practical diagnostics to detect and mitigate collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand the mechanisms driving long-term degeneration when training data contains AI-generated samples, which can cause feedback loops and model collapse; provide a unified framework and actionable diagnostics.

Method: Frame iterative feedback as a Markov chain on latent representations; show that ergodicity of the feedback process and directional contraction of latent space lead to a resonant, low-dimensional invariant structure. Empirically test via diffusion models on MNIST and ImageNet, CycleGAN, and an audio-feedback experiment; analyze local/global manifold geometry and classify collapse into eight patterns.

Result: Demonstrates the emergence of a low-dimensional invariant latent structure (neural resonance) under feedback; maps evolution of geometry across models and datasets; provides an eight-pattern taxonomy of collapse behaviors and practical diagnostics for identifying and mitigating collapse.

Conclusion: Neural resonance offers a unified explanation for long-term degenerate behavior in generative models due to feedback; enables diagnostics and, potentially, mitigation strategies to prevent collapse.

Abstract: AI training datasets will inevitably contain AI-generated examples, leading to ``feedback'' in which the output of one model impacts the training of another. It is known that such iterative feedback can lead to model collapse, yet the mechanisms underlying this degeneration remain poorly understood. Here we show that a broad class of feedback processes converges to a low-dimensional invariant structure in latent space, a phenomenon we call neural resonance. By modeling iterative feedback as a Markov Chain, we show that two conditions are needed for this resonance to occur: ergodicity of the feedback process and directional contraction of the latent representation. By studying diffusion models on MNIST and ImageNet, as well as CycleGAN and an audio feedback experiment, we map how local and global manifold geometry evolve, and we introduce an eight-pattern taxonomy of collapse behaviors. Neural resonance provides a unified explanation for long-term degenerate behavior in generative models and provides practical diagnostics for identifying, characterizing, and eventually mitigating collapse.

</details>


### [273] [Back to Blackwell: Closing the Loop on Intransitivity in Multi-Objective Preference Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19041)
*Jiahao Zhang,Lujing Zhang,Keltin Grimes,Zhuohao Yu,Gokul Swamy,Zhiwei Steven Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes MaxEntBW, a game-theoretic solution to multi-objective intransitive preferences in PFT, and PROSPER, a scalable algorithm handling multiple objectives without scalarization; demonstrated via fine-tuning LLMs with multi-objective feedback, outperforming baselines at 7B and 3B scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Intransitive preferences (cyclic rankings) break the notion of a single optimal policy in preference fine-tuning (PFT). This can originate from inconsistent rankings on a single objective or from scalarizing multiple objectives. A robust, scalable solution is needed that remains well-defined under such multi-objective intransitivities.

Method: Introduce Maximum Entropy Blackwell Winner (MaxEntBW) as a game-theoretic solution concept for multi-objective intransitive preferences. Develop PROSPER, a provably efficient PFT algorithm that handles multiple objectives without scalarization and avoids reliance on self-play. Apply PROSPER to fine-tune large language models using multi-objective LLM-as-a-Judge feedback (rubric-based judges).

Result: PROSPER outperforms all baselines on instruction-following and general-chat benchmarks. Trained model checkpoints are released at 7B and 3B parameter scales, demonstrating scalability and effectiveness of the approach.

Conclusion: MaxEntBW provides a well-defined solution for intransitive, multi-objective preferences in PFT. PROSPER enables scalable, scalarization-free multi-objective fine-tuning with strong empirical performance on LLM alignment tasks.

Abstract: A recurring challenge in preference fine-tuning (PFT) is handling $\textit{intransitive}$ (i.e., cyclic) preferences. Intransitive preferences often stem from either $\textit{(i)}$ inconsistent rankings along a single objective or $\textit{(ii)}$ scalarizing multiple objectives into a single metric. Regardless of their source, the downstream implication of intransitive preferences is the same: there is no well-defined optimal policy, breaking a core assumption of the standard PFT pipeline. In response, we propose a novel, game-theoretic solution concept -- the $\textit{Maximum Entropy Blackwell Winner}$ ($\textit{MaxEntBW}$) -- that is well-defined under multi-objective intransitive preferences. To enable computing MaxEntBWs at scale, we derive $\texttt{PROSPER}$: a provably efficient PFT algorithm. Unlike prior self-play techniques, $\texttt{PROSPER}$ directly handles multiple objectives without requiring scalarization. We then apply $\texttt{PROSPER}$ to the problem of fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) from multi-objective LLM-as-a-Judge feedback (e.g., rubric-based judges), a setting where both sources of intransitivity arise. We find that $\texttt{PROSPER}$ outperforms all baselines considered across both instruction following and general chat benchmarks, releasing trained model checkpoints at the 7B and 3B parameter scales.

</details>


### [274] [IDLM: Inverse-distilled Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19066)
*David Li,Nikita Gushchin,Dmitry Abulkhanov,Eric Moulines,Ivan Oseledets,Maxim Panov,Alexander Korotin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Inverse-distilled Diffusion Language Models (IDLM) extend inverse distillation to discrete diffusion models to accelerate sampling (4x-64x) while preserving entropy and perplexity. A unique-solution theoretical guarantee and gradient-stable relaxations enable stable training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce slow multi-step sampling in Diffusion Language Models without degrading generation quality.

Method: The authors prove a unique solution exists for the inverse formulation in the discrete setting and introduce gradient-stable relaxations to enable effective backpropagation. They adapt inverse distillation to discrete diffusion language models (IDLM).

Result: Empirical results show 4x–64x reduction in inference steps across multiple DLMs, with preserved teacher entropy and perplexity.

Conclusion: IDLM makes inverse distillation viable for discrete diffusion models by ensuring solution uniqueness and stable training, achieving substantial speedups without sacrificing generation fidelity.

Abstract: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) have recently achieved strong results in text generation. However, their multi-step sampling leads to slow inference, limiting practical use. To address this, we extend Inverse Distillation, a technique originally developed to accelerate continuous diffusion models, to the discrete setting. Nonetheless, this extension introduces both theoretical and practical challenges. From a theoretical perspective, the inverse distillation objective lacks uniqueness guarantees, which may lead to suboptimal solutions. From a practical standpoint, backpropagation in the discrete space is non-trivial and often unstable. To overcome these challenges, we first provide a theoretical result demonstrating that our inverse formulation admits a unique solution, thereby ensuring valid optimization. We then introduce gradient-stable relaxations to support effective training. As a result, experiments on multiple DLMs show that our method, Inverse-distilled Diffusion Language Models (IDLM), reduces the number of inference steps by 4x-64x, while preserving the teacher model's entropy and generative perplexity.

</details>


### [275] [TimeRadar: A Domain-Rotatable Foundation Model for Time Series Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19068)
*Hui He,Hezhe Qiao,Yutong Chen,Kun Yi,Guansong Pang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes TimeRadar, a time series foundation model operating in a data-adaptive fractional time-frequency domain to improve unsupervised time series anomaly detection (TSAD) across unseen datasets, via Fractionally Modulated Time-Frequency Reconstruction (FTFRecon) with a learnable fractional order and a Contextual Deviation Learning (CDL) module.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TSFMs excel at supervised tasks but struggle with unsupervised TSAD because abnormal patterns resemble normal ones in standard time/frequency representations; need a dataset-agnostic representation to differentiate anomalies.

Method: Introduce TimeRadar that uses a fractional time-frequency representation; FTFRecon learns a fractional order to rotate the signal to an optimal time-frequency angle for reconstruction; CDL models local deviations from contextual data in the rotated domain.

Result: Claimed improvement in differentiating anomalies across diverse and unseen datasets due to adaptive reconstruction; ability to generalize beyond training data.

Conclusion: TimeRadar provides a generalist TSAD solution by adapting the time-frequency representation per input, with local deviation modeling; potential impact on unsupervised anomaly detection across varied domains.

Abstract: Current time series foundation models (TSFMs) primarily focus on learning prevalent and regular patterns within a predefined time or frequency domain to enable supervised downstream tasks (e.g., forecasting). Consequently, they are often ineffective for inherently unsupervised downstream tasks-such as time series anomaly detection (TSAD), which aims to identify rare, irregular patterns. This limitation arises because such abnormal patterns can closely resemble the regular patterns when presented in the same time/frequency domain. To address this issue, we introduce TimeRadar, an innovative TSFM built in a fractional time-frequency domain to support generalist TSAD across diverse unseen datasets. Our key insight is that rotating a time series into a data-dependent fractional time-frequency representation can adaptively differentiate the normal and abnormal signals across different datasets. To this end, a novel component, namely Fractionally modulated Time-Frequency Reconstruction (FTFRecon), is proposed in TimeRadar to leverage a learnable fractional order to rotate the time series to the most pronounced angle between a continuous time and frequency domain for accurate data reconstruction. This provides adaptive data reconstruction in an optimal time-frequency domain for each data input, enabling effective differentiation of the unbounded abnormal patterns from the regular ones across datasets, including unseen datasets. To allow TimeRadar to model local abnormality that is not captured by the global data reconstruction, we further introduce a Contextual Deviation Learning (CDL) component to model the local deviation of the input relative to its contextual time series data in the rotatable domain.

</details>


### [276] [RKHS Representation of Algebraic Convolutional Filters with Integral Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19094)
*Alejandro Parada-Mayorga,Alejandro Ribeiro,Juan Bazerque*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops a theory linking integral operators to reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHS) within algebraic signal processing, via a box-product construction of operator symbols that induces RKHS convolutional models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unify integral-operator analysis with RKHS within the algebraic signal processing framework to enable pointwise RKHS representations of filters, spectral localization, and learnable kernels in graphon-based systems.

Method: Define the range of integral operators as RKHS; construct reproducing kernels from a box product of operator symbols; analyze algebraic and spectral properties; show polynomial filtering corresponds to iterated box products, forming a unital kernel algebra; derive pointwise RKHS representations of filters; extend the framework to graphons and directed graphons; investigate spatial–spectral localization and finite-dimensional RKHS representations for regularized learning.

Result: Characterization of the induced RKHS as a convolutional model with kernels given by box-product operator symbols; polynomial filters map to iterated box products; establishment of a unital kernel algebra; explicit RKHS representations of filters via reproducing properties; links between eigendecompositions and RKHS representations in graphon signal processing; extension to directed graphons; localization results; finite-dimensional RKHS representations for optimal filters in regularized learning problems.

Conclusion: The framework provides a principled bridge between spectral decompositions and RKHS representations for integral-operator-based signal processing, enabling new localization results and learnable kernel-based architectures; offers foundations for practical learnable filters in graphon-based and neural architectures using integral operators.

Abstract: Integral operators play a central role in signal processing, underpinning classical convolution, and filtering on continuous network models such as graphons. While these operators are traditionally analyzed through spectral decompositions, their connection to reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHS) has not been systematically explored within the algebraic signal processing framework. In this paper, we develop a comprehensive theory showing that the range of integral operators naturally induces RKHS convolutional signal models whose reproducing kernels are determined by a box product of the operator symbols. We characterize the algebraic and spectral properties of these induced RKHS and show that polynomial filtering with integral operators corresponds to iterated box products, giving rise to a unital kernel algebra. This perspective yields pointwise RKHS representations of filters via the reproducing property, providing an alternative to operator-based implementations. Our results establish precise connections between eigendecompositions and RKHS representations in graphon signal processing, extend naturally to directed graphons, and enable novel spatial--spectral localization results. Furthermore, we show that when the spectral domain is a subset of the original domain of the signals, optimal filters for regularized learning problems admit finite-dimensional RKHS representations, providing a principled foundation for learnable filters in integral-operator-based neural architectures.

</details>


### [277] [Learning from Complexity: Exploring Dynamic Sample Pruning of Spatio-Temporal Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19113)
*Wei Chen,Junle Chen,Yuqian Wu,Yuxuan Liang,Xiaofang Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dynamic sample pruning (ST-Prune) speeds up spatio-temporal forecasting training by pruning easy/redundant samples based on the model's current learning state, achieving faster convergence with maintained or improved accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training on large, redundant spatio-temporal datasets is computationally expensive; data-centric inefficiency is under-addressed relative to model/optimizer improvements.

Method: Propose ST-Prune: a dynamic sampling-pruning mechanism that adaptively selects informative samples during training, removing low-utility samples in real time to accelerate convergence; validated on real-world ST datasets; claims scalability and universality.

Result: Significant reductions in training time while maintaining or improving predictive performance; demonstrated across multiple real-world datasets and model setups.

Conclusion: Training efficiency gains in spatio-temporal forecasting can be achieved via data-centric pruning; ST-Prune provides a practical, scalable, and universal approach that complements model/optimizer-focused methods.

Abstract: Spatio-temporal forecasting is fundamental to intelligent systems in transportation, climate science, and urban planning. However, training deep learning models on the massive, often redundant, datasets from these domains presents a significant computational bottleneck. Existing solutions typically focus on optimizing model architectures or optimizers, while overlooking the inherent inefficiency of the training data itself. This conventional approach of iterating over the entire static dataset each epoch wastes considerable resources on easy-to-learn or repetitive samples. In this paper, we explore a novel training-efficiency techniques, namely learning from complexity with dynamic sample pruning, ST-Prune, for spatio-temporal forecasting. Through dynamic sample pruning, we aim to intelligently identify the most informative samples based on the model's real-time learning state, thereby accelerating convergence and improving training efficiency. Extensive experiments conducted on real-world spatio-temporal datasets show that ST-Prune significantly accelerates the training speed while maintaining or even improving the model performance, and it also has scalability and universality.

</details>


### [278] [Uncertainty-Aware Rank-One MIMO Q Network Framework for Accelerated Offline Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19917)
*Thanh Nguyen,Tung Luu,Tri Ton,Sungwoong Kim,Chang D. Yoo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An uncertainty-aware Rank-One MIMO Q-network for offline RL that leverages OOD data via uncertainty-informed losses to maximize a lower confidence bound, with a Rank-One MIMO architecture achieving ensemble-like uncertainty at near-single-network cost; achieves state-of-the-art on D4RL with improved efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline RL suffers from extrapolation error due to out-of-distribution data; existing methods are overly conservative or costly. There's a need to exploit OOD data more effectively while maintaining computational efficiency.

Method: Quantify data uncertainty and incorporate it into training losses to maximize the lower confidence bound of the Q-function. Use a Rank-One MIMO architecture to model the uncertainty-aware Q-function, attaining ensemble-like uncertainty with cost close to a single network.

Result: Empirical evaluation on D4RL shows state-of-the-art performance and higher computational efficiency (speed/memory) than comparator methods.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-aware Rank-One MIMO Q nets effectively balance precision, speed, and memory, mitigating extrapolation error and improving offline RL performance; the approach provides a scalable path to leveraging OOD data.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) has garnered significant interest due to its safe and easily scalable paradigm. However, training under this paradigm presents its own challenge: the extrapolation error stemming from out-of-distribution (OOD) data. Existing methodologies have endeavored to address this issue through means like penalizing OOD Q-values or imposing similarity constraints on the learned policy and the behavior policy. Nonetheless, these approaches are often beset by limitations such as being overly conservative in utilizing OOD data, imprecise OOD data characterization, and significant computational overhead. To address these challenges, this paper introduces an Uncertainty-Aware Rank-One Multi-Input Multi-Output (MIMO) Q Network framework. The framework aims to enhance Offline Reinforcement Learning by fully leveraging the potential of OOD data while still ensuring efficiency in the learning process. Specifically, the framework quantifies data uncertainty and harnesses it in the training losses, aiming to train a policy that maximizes the lower confidence bound of the corresponding Q-function. Furthermore, a Rank-One MIMO architecture is introduced to model the uncertainty-aware Q-function, \TP{offering the same ability for uncertainty quantification as an ensemble of networks but with a cost nearly equivalent to that of a single network}. Consequently, this framework strikes a harmonious balance between precision, speed, and memory efficiency, culminating in improved overall performance. Extensive experimentation on the D4RL benchmark demonstrates that the framework attains state-of-the-art performance while remaining computationally efficient. By incorporating the concept of uncertainty quantification, our framework offers a promising avenue to alleviate extrapolation errors and enhance the efficiency of offline RL.

</details>


### [279] [Robust Predictive Uncertainty and Double Descent in Contaminated Bayesian Random Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19126)
*Michele Caprio,Katerina Papagiannouli,Siu Lun Chau,Sayan Mukherjee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A robust Bayesian random-feature regression framework using ε- and η-contaminated credal sets with pessimistic updating, yielding tractable bounds and an IHDR for robust predictive uncertainty, while preserving double-descent behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Misspecification in Bayesian RFs (prior and likelihood) can lead to overconfident or brittle predictions. Introducing contaminate-aware credal sets and pessimistic updating provides worst-case guarantees and robust uncertainty quantification.

Method: Replace Gaussian priors/likelihoods with ε- and η-contaminated credal sets. Perform inference via pessimistic generalized Bayesian updating. Derive lower/upper posterior predictive densities; define an Imprecise Highest Density Region (IHDR) and provide an efficient outer approximation via an adjusted Gaussian credible interval. Obtain predictive variance bounds (with mild truncation for the upper bound) and show leading-order RF growth is preserved.

Result: Explicit, tractable bounds for lower and upper posterior predictive densities under contamination; IHDR offers robust predictive-uncertainty quantification with an efficient Gaussian-interval approximation; variance bounds that respect RF asymptotics; robustness theory for Bayesian RF with worst-case guarantees under bounded misspecification.

Conclusion: Robustness of Bayesian RF is established: predictive uncertainty remains computationally tractable, retains the double-descent structure, and improves with explicit worst-case guarantees under prior/likelihood misspecification.

Abstract: We propose a robust Bayesian formulation of random feature (RF) regression that accounts explicitly for prior and likelihood misspecification via Huber-style contamination sets. Starting from the classical equivalence between ridge-regularized RF training and Bayesian inference with Gaussian priors and likelihoods, we replace the single prior and likelihood with $ε$- and $η$-contaminated credal sets, respectively, and perform inference using pessimistic generalized Bayesian updating. We derive explicit and tractable bounds for the resulting lower and upper posterior predictive densities. These bounds show that, when contamination is moderate, prior and likelihood ambiguity effectively acts as a direct contamination of the posterior predictive distribution, yielding uncertainty envelopes around the classical Gaussian predictive. We introduce an Imprecise Highest Density Region (IHDR) for robust predictive uncertainty quantification and show that it admits an efficient outer approximation via an adjusted Gaussian credible interval. We further obtain predictive variance bounds (under a mild truncation approximation for the upper bound) and prove that they preserve the leading-order proportional-growth asymptotics known for RF models. Together, these results establish a robustness theory for Bayesian random features: predictive uncertainty remains computationally tractable, inherits the classical double-descent phase structure, and is improved by explicit worst-case guarantees under bounded prior and likelihood misspecification.

</details>


### [280] [Detecting labeling bias using influence functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19130)
*Frida Jørgensen,Nina Weng,Siavash Bigdeli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Influence functions are explored as a tool to detect labeling bias by identifying mislabeled samples; demonstrated on MNIST with 20% flipped labels and strong detection (~90%), and on CheXpert where mislabeled samples show higher influence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Labeling bias arises from resource constraints and unconscious bias, causing unequal label error rates; standard fairness constraints assume labels reflect true distribution, making them insufficient under labeling bias; a method to detect and diagnose label errors is needed.

Method: Compute the influence of each training sample on model predictions via loss gradients and Hessians; employ a diagonal Hessian approximation for efficiency; develop a sample valuation pipeline; introduce controlled label noise by flipping 20% of labels for one class; evaluate on MNIST and CheXpert.

Result: On MNIST, roughly 90% of mislabeled samples are detected; on CheXpert, mislabeled samples consistently exhibit higher influence scores, supporting the usefulness of influence functions for identifying label errors.

Conclusion: Influence functions show promise as a tool for identifying labeling bias and debugging datasets; potential for aiding robust model training, with future work needed on scalability, noise-robustness, and validation across more domains.

Abstract: Labeling bias arises during data collection due to resource limitations or unconscious bias, leading to unequal label error rates across subgroups or misrepresentation of subgroup prevalence. Most fairness constraints assume training labels reflect the true distribution, rendering them ineffective when labeling bias is present; leaving a challenging question, that \textit{how can we detect such labeling bias?} In this work, we investigate whether influence functions can be used to detect labeling bias. Influence functions estimate how much each training sample affects a model's predictions by leveraging the gradient and Hessian of the loss function -- when labeling errors occur, influence functions can identify wrongly labeled samples in the training set, revealing the underlying failure mode. We develop a sample valuation pipeline and test it first on the MNIST dataset, then scaled to the more complex CheXpert medical imaging dataset. To examine label noise, we introduced controlled errors by flipping 20\% of the labels for one class in the dataset. Using a diagonal Hessian approximation, we demonstrated promising results, successfully detecting nearly 90\% of mislabeled samples in MNIST. On CheXpert, mislabeled samples consistently exhibit higher influence scores. These results highlight the potential of influence functions for identifying label errors.

</details>


### [281] [Test-Time Learning of Causal Structure from Interventional Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19131)
*Wei Chen,Rui Ding,Bojun Huang,Yang Zhang,Qiang Fu,Yuxuan Liang,Han Shi,Dongmei Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TICL links test-time training with joint causal inference to enable robust causal discovery under unseen interventions, using self-augmentation and a PC-inspired two-phase learning scheme; validated on bnlearn benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address generalization gaps in supervised causal learning when intervention targets are unknown, where distribution shifts impair performance across interventional settings.

Method: Introduce TICL: a joint Test-Time Training and Joint Causal Inference framework. Employ a self-augmentation strategy to generate instance-specific training data at test time, mitigating distribution shifts. Use a PC-inspired two-phase supervised learning procedure that leverages self-augmented data while maintaining theoretical identifiability.

Result: Empirical evaluation on bnlearn benchmarks shows TICL achieves superiority across causal discovery tasks and intervention target detection.

Conclusion: TICL improves generalization and identifiability in supervised causal learning under unknown interventions, demonstrating the effectiveness of test-time self-augmentation combined with joint causal inference.

Abstract: Supervised causal learning has shown promise in causal discovery, yet it often struggles with generalization across diverse interventional settings, particularly when intervention targets are unknown. To address this, we propose TICL (Test-time Interventional Causal Learning), a novel method that synergizes Test-Time Training with Joint Causal Inference. Specifically, we design a self-augmentation strategy to generate instance-specific training data at test time, effectively avoiding distribution shifts. Furthermore, by integrating joint causal inference, we developed a PC-inspired two-phase supervised learning scheme, which effectively leverages self-augmented training data while ensuring theoretical identifiability. Extensive experiments on bnlearn benchmarks demonstrate TICL's superiority in multiple aspects of causal discovery and intervention target detection.

</details>


### [282] [Celo2: Towards Learned Optimization Free Lunch](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19142)
*Abhinav Moudgil,Boris Knyazev,Eugene Belilovsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A normalized learned optimizer, with augmented meta-training, can be meta-trained efficiently and generalize to very large-scale tasks, enabling practical learned optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome poor meta-generalization and high meta-training costs of prior learned optimizers (e.g., VeLO) to create a general-purpose learned update rule that scales to large pretraining tasks.

Method: Use a simple normalized optimizer architecture with augmentation in meta-training; ensure compatibility with modern optimization harness (orthogonalization, separate update rules for input-output vs hidden weights, decoupled weight decay). Meta-train on small-scale tasks with limited compute (4.5 GPU hours).

Result: Meta-training consumes 4.5 GPU hours; scales stably to a billion-scale pretraining task (GPT-3 XL 1.3B), six orders of magnitude larger than meta-training distribution; strong performance on out-of-distribution tasks; practical with modern optimizers.

Conclusion: This approach makes learned optimizers practically applicable, enabling richer meta-training and data curation strategies to further improve performance.

Abstract: Learned optimizers are powerful alternatives to hand-designed update rules like Adam, yet they have seen limited practical adoption since they often fail to meta-generalize beyond their training distribution and incur high meta-training cost. For instance, prior work, VeLO, scaled meta-training to 4,000 TPU months ($\sim$10$\times$ GPT-3 compute) to meta-train a general-purpose optimizer but it failed to generalize beyond 600M parameters tasks. In this work, we present a surprising finding: by crafting a simple normalized optimizer architecture and augmenting meta-training, it becomes feasible to meta-train a performant general-purpose learned update rule on a tiny fraction of VeLO compute, 4.5 GPU hours to be precise. Our learned update rule scales stably to a billion-scale pretraining task (GPT-3 XL 1.3B) which is six orders of magnitude larger than its meta-training distribution. Furthermore, it shows strong performance across diverse out-of-distribution tasks and is compatible with modern optimization harness that includes orthogonalization, distinct update rules for input-output and hidden weights, and decoupled weight decay. In all, this work paves the way for practically applicable learnable optimization algorithms, unlocking exploration of richer meta-training and data curation recipes to further improve performance.

</details>


### [283] [Incremental Learning of Sparse Attention Patterns in Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19143)
*Oğuz Kaan Yüksel,Rodrigo Alvarez Lucendo,Nicolas Flammarion*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: High-order Markov chain task to study how transformers integrate information from multiple past positions; shows staged, incremental learning with sparse attention; competitive-to-cooperative shift among heads; differential equation models and stage-wise convergence; evidence for learning via a ladder of progressively simpler hypothesis classes; early stopping as implicit regularizer; implications for NLP generalization and algorithmic reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how transformers capture long-range, variably significant information and why learning proceeds in distinct stages, including dynamics with potentially misspecified hypotheses and implicit regularization.

Method: Propose a high-order Markov chain task; analyze transformers using sparse attention patterns; derive simplified differential equations to model training dynamics; prove stage-wise convergence; study early stopping effects.

Result: Transformers learn the task incrementally with stages defined by information acquisition; heads shift from competitive to cooperative over time; dynamics captured by ODEs; learning progression traverses simpler hypothesis classes before the full model class; early stopping biases the model toward simpler classes; provides a theoretical basis for staged learning and generalization insights.

Conclusion: Offers a framework to explain staged learning and emergent complex transformer behaviors; informs generalization in NLP and algorithmic reasoning; highlights the role of early stopping and hierarchical development in training.

Abstract: This paper introduces a high-order Markov chain task to investigate how transformers learn to integrate information from multiple past positions with varying statistical significance. We demonstrate that transformers learn this task incrementally: each stage is defined by the acquisition of specific information through sparse attention patterns. Notably, we identify a shift in learning dynamics from competitive, where heads converge on the most statistically dominant pattern, to cooperative, where heads specialize in distinct patterns. We model these dynamics using simplified differential equations that characterize the trajectory and prove stage-wise convergence results. Our analysis reveals that transformers ascend a complexity ladder by passing through simpler, misspecified hypothesis classes before reaching the full model class. We further show that early stopping acts as an implicit regularizer, biasing the model toward these simpler classes. These results provide a theoretical foundation for the emergence of staged learning and complex behaviors in transformers, offering insights into generalization for natural language processing and algorithmic reasoning.

</details>


### [284] [Virtual Parameter Sharpening: Dynamic Low-Rank Perturbations for Inference-Time Reasoning Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19169)
*Saba Kublashvili*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Virtual Parameter Sharpening (VPS) enables test-time, activation-conditioned low-rank perturbations of frozen transformer layers to achieve on-the-fly adaptation without parameter updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the rigidity of frozen models and the limitations of static adapters like LoRA by enabling dynamic, inference-time adaptation through activation-aware perturbations, with potential improvements in reasoning capabilities.

Method: Delta W = gamma * W^T V U^T W, where U and V are sparse activation-guided selectors or Sylvester-coupled regressors. Perturbation magnitude is modulated by an adaptive policy based on activation energy and token-level entropy. Multi-objective verification with iterative refinement is proposed for tasks with supervision. A complete algorithmic framework and mathematical analysis of spectral properties are provided.

Result: The work delivers a theoretical analysis of the perturbation’s spectral properties, an adaptive policy for perturbation magnitude, and a full algorithmic framework. It does not detail empirical results in the abstract, but provides implementation and experiments code.

Conclusion: VPS introduces a novel, test-time, activation-conditioned perturbation mechanism that augments frozen transformer reasoning without updating parameters, with potential to improve inference-time adaptability and reasoning in large language models.

Abstract: I introduce Virtual Parameter Sharpening (VPS), an inference-time technique that augments frozen transformer linear layers with dynamic, activation-conditioned low-rank perturbations. Unlike parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods such as LoRA, which learn static low-rank adapters, VPS constructs its perturbation factors on the fly from batch activation statistics and optional gradient signals, enabling test-time adaptation without persistent parameter updates. The perturbation takes the form Delta W = gamma * W^T V U^T W, where selector matrices U and V are constructed via sparse activation-guided selection or Sylvester-coupled regression. We provide a theoretical analysis of the perturbation's spectral properties and describe an adaptive policy system that modulates perturbation magnitude based on activation energy and token-level entropy. This system incorporates multi-objective verification with iterative refinement for tasks with ground-truth supervision. We present the complete algorithmic framework, analyze its mathematical foundations, and discuss the mechanisms by which activation-conditioned computation may enhance reasoning capabilities in large language models. Implementation and experimental code are available at https://github.com/Saba-Kublashvili/vps-virtual-parameter-synthesis .

</details>


### [285] [Online Realizable Regression and Applications for ReLU Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19172)
*Ilan Doron-Arad,Idan Mehalel,Elchanan Mossel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Entropy-based potential bounds realizable online regression with approximate pseudo-metric losses via a Dudley-type integral; yields horizon-free cumulative loss bounds and a q-vs-d dichotomy, plus regression-versus-classification separations for ReLU networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address differences between online regression and classification under realizability; provide computable, sharp bounds on minimax cumulative loss using covering numbers under a sup metric, enabling practical understanding of the effective dimension.

Method: Define entropy potential Φ(H) = ∫_0^{diam(H)} log N(H, ε) dε using ε-covering numbers under the induced sup metric. Prove D_onl(H) ≤ O(c) Φ(H) for c-approximate pseudo-metric losses. Apply to polynomial metric entropy classes to obtain finite horizon-free bounds; analyze two families: (i) q-vs-d finite total loss for L-Lipschitz regression iff q>d; (ii) bounded-norm k-ReLU networks show finite regression loss (≈~k^2) vs infinite for classification, with precise separations (one ReLU yields O(1) in regression).

Result: A generic entropy-potential method upper-bounds the minimax realizable cumulative loss by a Dudley-type integral that depends on covering numbers. For polynomial metric entropy, the potential is finite, giving horizon-free bounds and explicit dependence on effective dimension. The method yields: (i) a sharp q-vs-d dichotomy for Lipschitz regression (finite loss iff q>d); (ii) a separation between regression and classification for bounded-norm ReLU networks, with finite loss for regression (roughly k^2) and invariance to height for one ReLU, while classification remains impossible in some cases.

Conclusion: The entropy-potential framework provides a concrete, computable route to bound minimax cumulative loss in realizable online regression under approximate pseudo-metric losses, linking it to covering numbers and effective dimension. It demonstrates a clear dichotomy and separation results for practical models (Lipschitz regression, ReLU networks) and explains horizon-free behavior without margin or stochastic assumptions.

Abstract: Realizable online regression can behave very differently from online classification. Even without any margin or stochastic assumptions, realizability may enforce horizon-free (finite) cumulative loss under metric-like losses, even when the analogous classification problem has an infinite mistake bound. We study realizable online regression in the adversarial model under losses that satisfy an approximate triangle inequality (approximate pseudo-metrics). Recent work of Attias et al. shows that the minimax realizable cumulative loss is characterized by the scaled Littlestone/online dimension $\mathbb{D}_{\mathrm{onl}}$, but this quantity can be difficult to analyze.
  Our main contribution is a generic potential method that upper bounds $\mathbb{D}_{\mathrm{onl}}$ by a concrete Dudley-type entropy integral that depends only on covering numbers of the hypothesis class under the induced sup pseudo-metric. We define an \emph{entropy potential} $Φ(\mathcal{H})=\int_{0}^{diam(\mathcal{H})} \log N(\mathcal{H},\varepsilon)\,d\varepsilon$, where $N(\mathcal{H},\varepsilon)$ is the $\varepsilon$-covering number of $\mathcal{H}$, and show that for every $c$-approximate pseudo-metric loss, $\mathbb{D}_{\mathrm{onl}}(\mathcal{H})\le O(c)\,Φ(\mathcal{H})$. In particular, polynomial metric entropy implies $Φ(\mathcal{H})<\infty$ and hence a horizon-free realizable cumulative-loss bound with transparent dependence on effective dimension.
  We illustrate the method on two families. We prove a sharp $q$-vs.-$d$ dichotomy for realizable online learning (finite and efficiently achievable $Θ_{d,q}(L^d)$ total loss for $L$-Lipschitz regression iff $q>d$, otherwise infinite), and for bounded-norm $k$-ReLU networks separate regression (finite loss, even $\widetilde O(k^2)$, and $O(1)$ for one ReLU) from classification (impossible already for $k=2,d=1$).

</details>


### [286] [HybridFL: A Federated Learning Approach for Financial Crime Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19207)
*Afsana Khan,Marijn ten Thij,Guangzhi Tang,Anna Wilbik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid Federated Learning (HybridFL): a framework merging horizontal FL and vertical feature fusion to handle mixed data partitions in privacy-preserving fashion; shows strong gains over local transaction models and competitive with centralized training in financial crime detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world data are often bifurcated across parties (horizontal and vertical) and cannot be jointly learned under standard FL; need a method that preserves data locality while enabling joint learning.

Method: Proposes HybridFL architecture that integrates horizontal aggregation across distributed users and vertical fusion of private feature sets, leveraging privacy-preserving data exchange and secure aggregation (conceptual).

Result: Empirical evaluation on AMLSim and SWIFT datasets shows HybridFL outperforms the transaction-only local model and achieves performance on par with a centralized benchmark.

Conclusion: HybridFL enables effective privacy-preserving joint learning for hybrid data distributions, with practical potential for financial crime detection and similar domains.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) is a privacy-preserving machine learning paradigm that enables multiple parties to collaboratively train models on privately owned data without sharing raw information. While standard FL typically addresses either horizontal or vertical data partitions, many real-world scenarios exhibit a complex hybrid distribution. This paper proposes Hybrid Federated Learning (HybridFL) to address data split both horizontally across disjoint users and vertically across complementary feature sets. We evaluate HybridFL in a financial crime detection context, where a transaction party holds transaction-level attributes and multiple banks maintain private account-level features. By integrating horizontal aggregation and vertical feature fusion, the proposed architecture enables joint learning while strictly preserving data locality. Experiments on AMLSim and SWIFT datasets demonstrate that HybridFL significantly outperforms the transaction-only local model and achieves performance comparable to a centralized benchmark.

</details>


### [287] [How to Allocate, How to Learn? Dynamic Rollout Allocation and Advantage Modulation for Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19208)
*Yangyi Fang,Jiaye Lin,Xiaoliang Fu,Cong Qin,Haolin Shi,Chaowen Hu,Lu Pan,Ke Zeng,Xunliang Cai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DynaMO introduces a dual optimization framework for RL-based verifiable rewards in LLM reasoning, addressing (i) variance-aware, sequence-level rollout allocation, and (ii) gradient-aware token-level advantage modulation to prevent gradient attenuation of confident correct actions while stabilizing updates; uses Bernoulli variance as a proxy and gradient-magnitude bounds with entropy-based signals; demonstrates improvements on mathematical reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uniform rollout allocation ignores gradient variance across problems; softmax policy causes gradient attenuation for high-confidence correct actions and excessive updates can destabilize training.

Method: 1) Derive a variance-minimizing, sequence-level allocation by modeling gradient informativeness with Bernoulli variance; 2) Develop gradient-aware advantage modulation at the token level using theoretical gradient magnitude bounds and entropy-change signals to balance updates and counter attenuation.

Result: Extensive experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show consistent improvements over strong RLVR baselines.

Conclusion: DynaMO provides a theoretically grounded, dual-pronged optimization framework that improves RL-based verifiable rewards for LLM reasoning and offers a publicly available implementation.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has proven effective for Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning, yet current methods face key challenges in resource allocation and policy optimization dynamics: (i) uniform rollout allocation ignores gradient variance heterogeneity across problems, and (ii) the softmax policy structure causes gradient attenuation for high-confidence correct actions, while excessive gradient updates may destabilize training. Therefore, we propose DynaMO, a theoretically-grounded dual-pronged optimization framework. At the sequence level, we prove that uniform allocation is suboptimal and derive variance-minimizing allocation from the first principle, establishing Bernoulli variance as a computable proxy for gradient informativeness. At the token level, we develop gradient-aware advantage modulation grounded in theoretical analysis of gradient magnitude bounds. Our framework compensates for gradient attenuation of high-confidence correct actions while utilizing entropy changes as computable indicators to stabilize excessive update magnitudes. Extensive experiments conducted on a diverse range of mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over strong RLVR baselines. Our implementation is available at: \href{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/dynamo-680E/README.md}{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/dynamo}.

</details>


### [288] [Understanding Empirical Unlearning with Combinatorial Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19215)
*Shingo Kodama,Niv Cohen,Micah Adler,Nir Shavit*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-layer network analysis of unlearning shows erased knowledge can persist and resurface; uses combinatorial interpretability to inspect weight-encoded knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand persistence of knowledge and limitations of unlearning in pretrained models; leverage combinatorial interpretability to directly inspect knowledge encoded in weights.

Method: Reproduce baseline unlearning methods within the combinatorial interpretability framework for two-layer neural networks; assess whether knowledge is truly removed or only suppressed, and evaluate recoverability via fine-tuning; analyze how knowledge persists.

Result: Interpretability-based analysis reveals that knowledge can persist after unlearning and may resurface under fine-tuning; the approach clarifies conditions under which removal is incomplete.

Conclusion: The analysis exposes gaps in unlearning approaches, showing that true forgetting is harder than apparent suppression; motivates extending the framework to more complex models and developing robust evaluation metrics.

Abstract: While many recent methods aim to unlearn or remove knowledge from pretrained models, seemingly erased knowledge often persists and can be recovered in various ways. Because large foundation models are far from interpretable, understanding whether and how such knowledge persists remains a significant challenge. To address this, we turn to the recently developed framework of combinatorial interpretability. This framework, designed for two-layer neural networks, enables direct inspection of the knowledge encoded in the model weights. We reproduce baseline unlearning methods within the combinatorial interpretability setting and examine their behavior along two dimensions: (i) whether they truly remove knowledge of a target concept (the concept we wish to remove) or merely inhibit its expression while retaining the underlying information, and (ii) how easily the supposedly erased knowledge can be recovered through various fine-tuning operations. Our results shed light within a fully interpretable setting on how knowledge can persist despite unlearning and when it might resurface.

</details>


### [289] [Evaluating SAP RPT-1 for Enterprise Business Process Prediction: In-Context Learning vs. Traditional Machine Learning on Structured SAP Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19237)
*Amit Lal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RPT-1, SAP's tabular foundation model, approaches tuned GBDTs with 91-96% accuracy on 5-fold CV across three business tasks; AUC-ROC gaps 3.6-4.1 pts, R^2 gaps 8.9-11.1 pts; notable crossover at 75-100 context rows; recommends a hybrid workflow (screen with RPT-1, then selectively train GBDTs); reproducible via Hugging Face Spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate practical viability of SAP's RPT-1 tabular foundation model from a practitioner perspective by benchmarking against strong baselines on real enterprise tasks.

Method: Benchmark RPT-1 against tuned XGBoost/LightGBM/CatBoost on three SAP business scenarios (demand forecasting, data integrity, financial risk). Use five-fold cross-validation on datasets with 2.5k–3.2k rows. Compare accuracy, AUC-ROC, and R-squared. Analyze context-window effects and provide a deployable workflow."

Result: RPT-1 attains 91–96% of tuned GBDT accuracy without training examples. AUC-ROC gap ~3.6–4.1 points; R-squared gap ~8.9–11.1 points (regression tasks). A crossover occurs at ~75–100 context rows where RPT-1 outperforms XGBoost under limited data. All experiments are reproducible via publicly available Hugging Face Spaces.

Conclusion: Propose a practical hybrid workflow: use RPT-1 for rapid screening and then selectively train GBDT models where higher accuracy justifies effort. The study demonstrates competitive performance in enterprise contexts and provides reproducible results.

Abstract: Tabular foundation models aim to make machine learning accessible for enterprise data without task-specific training. This paper presents the first independent evaluation of SAP's Retrieval Pretrained Transformer (RPT-1) from a practitioner perspective. RPT-1 is a compact 64.6 MB model pretrained on 1.34 TB of structured data across 3.1 million tables. We benchmark it against tuned gradient-boosted decision trees (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) on three SAP business scenarios: demand forecasting across SD/MM/PP modules, predictive data integrity in BC/MM/QM, and financial risk classification in FI/CO/AR. Across five-fold cross-validation on datasets ranging from 2,500 to 3,200 rows, RPT-1 reaches 91-96% of tuned GBDT accuracy without any training examples. The classification gap is modest at 3.6-4.1 percentage points on AUC-ROC, though regression tasks show wider gaps of 8.9-11.1 percentage points on R-squared. An interesting finding is a crossover at roughly 75-100 context rows where RPT-1 actually outperforms XGBoost under limited data. Based on these results, we propose a practical hybrid workflow: use RPT-1 for rapid screening, then train GBDT selectively where prediction accuracy justifies the effort.
