All experiments are reproducible through publicly available Hugging Face Spaces.

</details>


### [290] [Alternating Bi-Objective Optimization for Explainable Neuro-Fuzzy Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19253)
*Qusai Khaled,Uzay Kaymak,Laura Genga*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: X-ANFIS introduces an alternating bi-objective gradient-based optimization for explainable adaptive neuro-fuzzy inference systems, achieving explainability without sacrificing accuracy and even recovering non-convex Pareto optima beyond the convex hull.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the accuracy–explainability trade-off in fuzzy rule-based systems; conventional MOO is costly and gradient scalarization misses non-convex Pareto regions.

Method: Bi-objective optimization with alternating gradient passes; uses Cauchy membership functions for stable training and semantically controlled initializations; introduces a differentiable explainability objective decoupled from the performance objective.

Result: Validated in ~5,000 experiments across nine UCI regression datasets; X-ANFIS consistently attains target distinguishability with competitive predictive accuracy and recovers Pareto-optimal solutions outside the MOO convex hull.

Conclusion: Proposes a scalable, effective framework for explainable neuro-fuzzy systems that balances accuracy and explainability and accesses non-convex Pareto regions that gradient-based scalarization cannot.

Abstract: Fuzzy systems show strong potential in explainable AI due to their rule-based architecture and linguistic variables. Existing approaches navigate the accuracy-explainability trade-off either through evolutionary multi-objective optimization (MOO), which is computationally expensive, or gradient-based scalarization, which cannot recover non-convex Pareto regions. We propose X-ANFIS, an alternating bi-objective gradient-based optimization scheme for explainable adaptive neuro-fuzzy inference systems. Cauchy membership functions are used for stable training under semantically controlled initializations, and a differentiable explainability objective is introduced and decoupled from the performance objective through alternating gradient passes. Validated in approximately 5,000 experiments on nine UCI regression datasets, X-ANFIS consistently achieves target distinguishability while maintaining competitive predictive accuracy, recovering solutions beyond the convex hull of the MOO Pareto front.

</details>


### [291] [DGPO: RL-Steered Graph Diffusion for Neural Architecture Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19261)
*Aleksei Liuliakov,Luca Hermes,Barbara Hammer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DGPO extends discrete graph diffusion to directed graphs (DAGs) using topological ordering and positional encoding, enabling RL fine-tuning for NAS; it matches optima on NAS-Bench-201 and demonstrates transferable priors and reward-driven control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph diffusion methods are designed for undirected graphs and fail to capture semantic directionality in DAGs like neural architectures; there is a need to steer directed combinatorial structures efficiently while preserving functional semantics (data flow).

Method: Directed Graph Policy Optimization (DGPO): augment discrete graph diffusion with topological ordering and positional encoding; apply reinforcement learning fine-tuning to DAGs; evaluate on NAS-Bench-101/201; pretrain on ~7% of search space and fine-tune to steer generation.

Result: DGPO matches the benchmark optimum on all three NAS-Bench-201 tasks: 91.61%, 73.49%, 46.77%. It demonstrates transferable priors: pretrained on 7% of the space, it generates near-oracle architectures after fine-tuning, within 0.32 percentage points of the full-data model and extrapolates 7.3 points beyond training ceiling. Bidirectional control experiments show genuine reward-driven steering; inverse optimization approaches random-chance accuracy (~9.5%).

Conclusion: Extending RL-steered discrete diffusion to handle directionality yields a controllable generative framework for directed combinatorial structures; DGPO demonstrates sample efficiency and transferable priors for DAG-based neural architectures.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning fine-tuning has proven effective for steering generative diffusion models toward desired properties in image and molecular domains. Graph diffusion models have similarly been applied to combinatorial structure generation, including neural architecture search (NAS). However, neural architectures are directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) where edge direction encodes functional semantics such as data flow-information that existing graph diffusion methods, designed for undirected structures, discard. We propose Directed Graph Policy Optimization (DGPO), which extends reinforcement learning fine-tuning of discrete graph diffusion models to DAGs via topological node ordering and positional encoding. Validated on NAS-Bench-101 and NAS-Bench-201, DGPO matches the benchmark optimum on all three NAS-Bench-201 tasks (91.61%, 73.49%, 46.77%). The central finding is that the model learns transferable structural priors: pretrained on only 7% of the search space, it generates near-oracle architectures after fine-tuning, within 0.32 percentage points of the full-data model and extrapolating 7.3 percentage points beyond its training ceiling. Bidirectional control experiments confirm genuine reward-driven steering, with inverse optimization reaching near random-chance accuracy (9.5%). These results demonstrate that reinforcement learning-steered discrete diffusion, once extended to handle directionality, provides a controllable generative framework for directed combinatorial structures.

</details>


### [292] [Spectral bias in physics-informed and operator learning: Analysis and mitigation guidelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19265)
*Siavash Khodakarami,Vivek Oommen,Nazanin Ahmadi Daryakenari,Maxim Beekenkamp,George Em Karniadakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spectral bias in PINNs, KANs, and neural operators is a dynamical phenomenon driven by optimization and loss design, not only representation; second-order optimization can accelerate high-frequency learning; spectral-aware losses mitigate bias for neural operators without extra inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To dissect how architecture, activations, loss formulations, and optimization strategies interact to produce spectral bias across PDE solvers, and to unify their analysis across elliptic, hyperbolic, and dispersive equations.

Method: Systematic empirical study using frequency-resolved error metrics, Barron-norm diagnostics, and higher-order moments. Benchmarks span elliptic, hyperbolic, and dispersive PDEs (e.g., KdV, wave, diffusion–reaction), turbulent flow reconstruction, and earthquake dynamics. Comparisons across architectures (PINNs, KANs, neural operators), activation functions, and optimizers (including second-order methods) with and without spectral-aware losses.

Result: Spectral bias is primarily dynamical rather than purely representational; second-order optimization changes the spectral learning order and improves early high-frequency recovery across PDE types. In neural operators, bias depends on architecture and can be mitigated via spectral-aware losses without increasing inference cost.

Conclusion: Mitigating spectral bias requires considering optimization dynamics and loss design in addition to architecture. Spectral-aware losses offer a practical route for neural operators, and second-order optimization can notably enhance high-frequency learning across PDE categories. The study provides a unified, metrics-driven framework to analyze and compare spectral bias across PDE solvers.

Abstract: Solving partial differential equations (PDEs) by neural networks as well as Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs), including physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), physics-informed KANs (PIKANs), and neural operators, are known to exhibit spectral bias, whereby low-frequency components of the solution are learned significantly faster than high-frequency modes. While spectral bias is often treated as an intrinsic representational limitation of neural architectures, its interaction with optimization dynamics and physics-based loss formulations remains poorly understood. In this work, we provide a systematic investigation of spectral bias in physics-informed and operator learning frameworks, with emphasis on the coupled roles of network architecture, activation functions, loss design, and optimization strategy. We quantify spectral bias through frequency-resolved error metrics, Barron-norm diagnostics, and higher-order statistical moments, enabling a unified analysis across elliptic, hyperbolic, and dispersive PDEs. Through diverse benchmark problems, including the Korteweg-de Vries, wave and steady-state diffusion-reaction equations, turbulent flow reconstruction, and earthquake dynamics, we demonstrate that spectral bias is not simply representational but fundamentally dynamical. In particular, second-order optimization methods substantially alter the spectral learning order, enabling earlier and more accurate recovery of high-frequency modes for all PDE types. For neural operators, we further show that spectral bias is dependent on the neural operator architecture and can also be effectively mitigated through spectral-aware loss formulations without increasing the inference cost.

</details>


### [293] [Taming Preconditioner Drift: Unlocking the Potential of Second-Order Optimizers for Federated Learning on Non-IID Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19271)
*Junkang Liu,Fanhua Shang,Hongying Liu,Jin Liu,Weixin An,Yuanyuan Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedPAC aligns and corrects preconditioners in federated second-order optimization to mitigate preconditioner drift, improving stability, convergence (linear speedup with partial participation), and accuracy (up to 5.8 percentage points on CIFAR-100 with ViTs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Naive federated second-order methods suffer from preconditioner drift due to heterogeneous curvature across clients and incompatible server metrics, causing instability or divergence on non-IID data.

Method: FedPAC decouples aggregation from geometry synchronization via Alignment (aggregate local preconditioners into a global reference and warm-start clients with the global preconditioner) and Correction (steer local updates using the global preconditioned direction to suppress long-term drift), with drift-coupled non-convex convergence guarantees and linear speedup under partial participation.

Result: The framework yields improved stability and accuracy across vision and language tasks; empirical gains include up to 5.8 percentage points absolute accuracy on CIFAR-100 with ViTs; theoretical guarantees with linear speedup.

Conclusion: Preconditioner alignment and correction effectively address geometry mismatch in federated second-order optimization, enabling reliable training with non-IID data and providing both theory and empirical evidence; code is released.

Abstract: Second-order optimizers can significantly accelerate large-scale training, yet their naive federated variants are often unstable or even diverge on non-IID data.
  We show that a key culprit is \emph{preconditioner drift}: client-side second-order training induces heterogeneous \emph{curvature-defined geometries} (i.e., preconditioner coordinate systems), and server-side model averaging updates computed under incompatible metrics, corrupting the global descent direction.
  To address this geometric mismatch, we propose \texttt{FedPAC}, a \emph{preconditioner alignment and correction} framework for reliable federated second-order optimization.
  \texttt{FedPAC} explicitly decouples parameter aggregation from geometry synchronization by:
  (i) \textbf{Alignment} (i.e.,aggregating local preconditioners into a global reference and warm-starting clients via global preconditioner); and
  (ii) \textbf{Correction} (i.e., steering local preconditioned updates using a global preconditioned direction to suppress long-term drift).
  We provide drift-coupled non-convex convergence guarantees with linear speedup under partial participation.
  Empirically, \texttt{FedPAC} consistently improves stability and accuracy across vision and language tasks, achieving up to $5.8\%$ absolute accuracy gain on CIFAR-100 with ViTs.
  Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/FedPAC-8B24.

</details>


### [294] [AdsorbFlow: energy-conditioned flow matching enables fast and realistic adsorbate placement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19289)
*Jiangjie Qiu,Wentao Li,Honghao Chen,Leyi Zhao,Xiaonan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AdsorbFlow is a deterministic, energy-conditioned diffusion-free model for locating low-energy adsorbate geometries on catalytic surfaces, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with far fewer sampling steps than diffusion baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Finding low-energy adsorption geometries is limited by the cost of DFT calculations and the challenge of proposing initial placements that relax into correct energy basins.

Method: AdsorbFlow trains a deterministic, energy-conditioned vector field over the rigid-body translation/rotation space of the adsorbate using conditional flow matching. Energy conditioning is provided via classifier-free guidance (not energy-gradient guidance), and sampling reduces to integrating an ordinary differential equation in as few as 5 steps.

Result: On OC20-Dense with full DFT verification, AdsorbFlow (EquiformerV2 backbone) achieves 61.4% SR@10 and 34.1% SR@1, outperforming AdsorbDiff (31.8% SR@1, 41.0% SR@10) and AdsorbML (47.7% SR@10). It uses ~20x fewer generative steps and has the lowest anomaly rate among baselines (6.8%). On 50 out-of-distribution systems, it retains 58.0% SR@10 with a MLFF-to-DFT gap of ~4 percentage points.

Conclusion: Deterministic transport via energy-conditioned flow matching is faster and more accurate than stochastic denoising for adsorbate placement, establishing a new effective paradigm for initialization in computational catalysis.

Abstract: Identifying low-energy adsorption geometries on catalytic surfaces is a practical bottleneck for computational heterogeneous catalysis: the difficulty lies not only in the cost of density functional theory (DFT) but in proposing initial placements that relax into the correct energy basins. Conditional denoising diffusion has improved success rates, yet requires $\sim$100 iterative steps per sample.
  Here we introduce AdsorbFlow, a deterministic generative model that learns an energy-conditioned vector field on the rigid-body configuration space of adsorbate translation and rotation via conditional flow matching. Energy information enters through classifier-free guidance conditioning -- not energy-gradient guidance -- and sampling reduces to integrating an ODE in as few as 5 steps.
  On OC20-Dense with full DFT single-point verification, AdsorbFlow with an EquiformerV2 backbone achieves 61.4% SR@10 and 34.1% SR@1 -- surpassing AdsorbDiff (31.8% SR@1, 41.0% SR@10) at every evaluation level and AdsorbML (47.7% SR@10) -- while using 20 times fewer generative steps and achieving the lowest anomaly rate among generative methods (6.8%). On 50 out-of-distribution systems, AdsorbFlow retains 58.0% SR@10 with a MLFF-to-DFT gap of only 4~percentage points. These results establish that deterministic transport is both faster and more accurate than stochastic denoising for adsorbate placement.

</details>


### [295] [Soft Sequence Policy Optimization: Bridging GMPO and SAPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19327)
*Svetlana Glazyrina,Maksim Kryzhanovskiy,Roman Ischenko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Soft Sequence Policy Optimization (SSPO), an off-policy GRPO objective that applies soft gates to token-level probability ratios within sequence-level importance weights to improve exploration and stability in LLM alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of GRPO-based policy optimization: misalignment between sequence rewards and token-level signals, loss of training signal due to clipping, and entropy collapse; aim for better exploration with stable training.

Method: Define Soft Sequence Policy Optimization: incorporate soft gating functions over token-level ratios inside sequence-level importance weights; off-policy objective; builds on SAPO and GMPO.

Result: The abstract outlines the proposed objective without empirical results; claims potential for enhanced policy exploration and training stability.

Conclusion: SSPO promises a principled balance between exploration and stability in GRPO-based LLM alignment, enabling adaptive token-level influence within sequence-level objectives; suitable for future empirical validation.

Abstract: A significant portion of recent research on Large Language Model (LLM) alignment focuses on developing new policy optimization methods based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Two prominent directions have emerged: (i) a shift toward sequence-level importance sampling weights that better align with the sequence-level rewards used in many tasks, and (ii) alternatives to PPO-style clipping that aim to avoid the associated loss of training signal and entropy collapse. Recent work, such as Soft Adaptive Policy Optimization (SAPO), reformulates the Scopic objective within the GRPO framework and achieves both sequence coherence and token adaptivity. Geometric-Mean Policy Optimization (GMPO) leverages token-wise ratio clipping within sequence importance sampling weights. Building on these ideas, this work proposes a new objective that promotes effective policy exploration while maintaining training stability. Specifically, we introduce Soft Sequence Policy Optimization, an off-policy reinforcement learning objective that incorporates soft gating functions over token-level probability ratios within sequence-level importance weights.

</details>


### [296] [CTS-Bench: Benchmarking Graph Coarsening Trade-offs for GNNs in Clock Tree Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19330)
*Barsat Khadka,Kawsher Roxy,Md Rubel Ahmed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CTS-Bench examines the trade-offs between graph coarsening and GNN-based CTS analysis; coarse graphs save memory/time but often degrade clock skew prediction, highlighting the need for CTS-aware coarsening.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address scalability barriers in CTS learning with GNNs due to large gate-level nets; evaluate how coarsening affects CTS-critical objectives.

Method: Construct CTS-Bench with 4,860 converged designs across five architectures; provide paired raw gate-level and clustered graphs derived from post-placement; use clock skew prediction as CTS task; quantify memory reduction and speedups; assess zero-shot R^2; analyze impact of generic graph clustering.

Result: Graph coarsening reduces GPU memory by up to 17.2x and training time by up to 3x, but often yields negative R^2 in zero-shot, indicating loss of CTS-relevant structure; generic clustering can harm CTS objectives despite unchanged global metrics.

Conclusion: CTS-Bench enables principled evaluation of CTS-aware coarsening strategies, supports benchmarking of GNNs and accelerators under physical-design constraints, and lays groundwork for learning-assisted CTS analysis and optimization.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are increasingly explored for physical design analysis in Electronic Design Automation, particularly for modeling Clock Tree Synthesis behavior such as clock skew and buffering complexity. However, practical deployment remains limited due to the prohibitive memory and runtime cost of operating on raw gate-level netlists. Graph coarsening is commonly used to improve scalability, yet its impact on CTS-critical learning objectives is not well characterized. This paper introduces CTS-Bench, a benchmark suite for systematically evaluating the trade-offs between graph coarsening, prediction accuracy, and computational efficiency in GNN-based CTS analysis. CTS-Bench consists of 4,860 converged physical design solutions spanning five architectures and provides paired raw gate-level and clustered graph representations derived from post-placement designs. Using clock skew prediction as a representative CTS task, we demonstrate a clear accuracy-efficiency trade-off. While graph coarsening reduces GPU memory usage by up to 17.2x and accelerates training by up to 3x, it also removes structural information essential for modeling clock distribution, frequently resulting in negative $R^2$ scores under zero-shot evaluation. Our findings indicate that generic graph clustering techniques can fundamentally compromise CTS learning objectives, even when global physical metrics remain unchanged. CTS-Bench enables principled evaluation of CTS-aware graph coarsening strategies, supports benchmarking of GNN architectures and accelerators under realistic physical design constraints, and provides a foundation for developing learning-assisted CTS analysis and optimization techniques.

</details>


### [297] [Partial Soft-Matching Distance for Neural Representational Comparison with Partial Unit Correspondence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19331)
*Chaitanya Kapoor,Alex H. Williams,Meenakshi Khosla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Partial soft-matching distance via partial optimal transport enables partial, rotation-sensitive correspondences in representational similarity, improving robustness to noise/outliers and enabling efficient, interpretable neuron/voxel rankings vs. full one-to-one matching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional representational similarity metrics require complete one-to-one matches, making them vulnerable to noise, outliers, and low-quality units in neural representations; a partial correspondence approach is needed to robustly compare representations while preserving interpretability.

Method: Extend the soft-matching distance to a partial optimal transport framework that allows some units (neurons/voxels) to remain unmatched, relaxing mass conservation while maintaining interpretable transport costs. This yields rotation-sensitive but robust correspondences and enables efficient ranking of units for cross-network alignment without costly iterative recomputation.

Result: Simulations show robustness to outliers and reliable model identification in noisy scenarios. fMRI data analyses automatically exclude low-reliability voxels and produce alignment-quality rankings that agree with brute-force methods. Alignment precision across homologous brain areas exceeds standard soft-matching. In deep networks, highly matched units correlate with similar maximally exciting images, while unmatched units diverge, enabling analysis of alignment quality subpopulations.

Conclusion: Partial soft-matching provides a principled and practical method for representational comparison under partial correspondence, combining theoretical robustness with scalable, interpretable analyses across brain data and deep networks.

Abstract: Representational similarity metrics typically force all units to be matched, making them susceptible to noise and outliers common in neural representations. We extend the soft-matching distance to a partial optimal transport setting that allows some neurons to remain unmatched, yielding rotation-sensitive but robust correspondences. This partial soft-matching distance provides theoretical advantages -- relaxing strict mass conservation while maintaining interpretable transport costs -- and practical benefits through efficient neuron ranking in terms of cross-network alignment without costly iterative recomputation. In simulations, it preserves correct matches under outliers and reliably selects the correct model in noise-corrupted identification tasks. On fMRI data, it automatically excludes low-reliability voxels and produces voxel rankings by alignment quality that closely match computationally expensive brute-force approaches. It achieves higher alignment precision across homologous brain areas than standard soft-matching, which is forced to match all units regardless of quality. In deep networks, highly matched units exhibit similar maximally exciting images, while unmatched units show divergent patterns. This ability to partition by match quality enables focused analyses, e.g., testing whether networks have privileged axes even within their most aligned subpopulations. Overall, partial soft-matching provides a principled and practical method for representational comparison under partial correspondence.

</details>


### [298] [Training-Free Cross-Architecture Merging for Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19332)
*Rishabh Bhattacharya,Vikaskumar Kalsariya,Naresh Manwani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Training-free heterogeneous GNN merging via operator-space alignment: H-GRAMA and UMPM enable cross-architecture merging (e.g., GCN to GAT) with moderate depth, yielding 1.2–1.9x speedups over ensembles while preserving specialist accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the limitation of parameter-space merging for GNNs caused by topology-sensitive message passing and architectural heterogeneity; enable cross-architecture merging without retraining.

Method: Propose H-GRAMA framework; formalize Universal Message Passing Mixture (UMPM) as a shared operator family; express heterogeneous GNN layers in a common functional language; perform merging at the operator level without retraining.

Result: Maintains high specialist accuracy in most compatible depth settings; achieves inference speedups of 1.2x to 1.9x over ensembles.

Conclusion: Operator-space grounding enables effective cross-architecture GNN merging without retraining, expanding the applicability of model merging to heterogeneous GNN designs.

Abstract: Model merging has emerged as a powerful paradigm for combining the capabilities of distinct expert models without the high computational cost of retraining, yet current methods are fundamentally constrained to homogeneous architectures. For GNNs, however, message passing is topology-dependent and sensitive to misalignment, making direct parameter-space merging unreliable. To bridge this gap, we introduce H-GRAMA (Heterogeneous Graph Routing and Message Alignment), a training-free framework that lifts merging from parameter space to operator space. We formalize Universal Message Passing Mixture (UMPM), a shared operator family that expresses heterogeneous GNN layers in a common functional language. H-GRAMA enables cross-architecture GNN merging (e.g., GCN to GAT) without retraining, retaining high specialist accuracy in most cases in compatible depth settings and achieving inference speedups of 1.2x to 1.9x over ensembles.

</details>


### [299] [Smooth Gate Functions for Soft Advantage Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19345)
*Egor Denisov,Svetlana Glazyrina,Maksim Kryzhanovskiy,Roman Ischenko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Explores how different smooth gate functions affect stability and performance in GRPO-based LLM training, formalizes admissible gate properties, and provides empirical guidance from math-reasoning experiments on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hard clipping in Group Relative Policy Optimization can cause instability; a smooth gate (as in SAPO) promises more stable updates and robust learning. The paper extends this to evaluate multiple gate families.

Method: Define admissible gate properties, identify several gate-function families, implement them in GRPO/SAPO framework, run experiments training Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct on mathematical reasoning tasks, compare stability and performance.

Result: Gate design choices influence stability and final performance; some gate families yield smoother updates with improved or comparable reasoning performance; provides practical design guidelines.

Conclusion: Adopt smoother, well-characterized gate functions in policy optimization to achieve robust, stable LLM training; mapping of gate properties to performance; guidance for practitioners designing policy optimization objectives.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has significantly advanced the training of large language models and enhanced their reasoning capabilities, while it remains susceptible to instability due to the use of hard clipping. Soft Adaptive Policy Optimization (SAPO) addresses this limitation by replacing clipping with a smooth sigmoid-based gate function, which leads to more stable updates. We have decided to push this theory further and investigate the impact of different gate functions on both training stability and final model performance. We formalize the key properties that admissible gates should satisfy and identify several families of such functions for empirical evaluation. This paper presents an analysis of our findings based on experiments conducted with the Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model on mathematical reasoning tasks. These results provide practical guidance for designing smoother and more robust policy optimization objectives for large language model training.

</details>


### [300] [Active perception and disentangled representations allow continual, episodic zero and few-shot learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19355)
*David Rawlinson,Gideon Kowadlo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A two-system Complementary Learning System (CLS) with a fast, non-generalizing learner and a slow, generalizing learner enables robust continual zero-shot and few-shot learning via context-driven bias that compels the slow system to encode novel stimuli using familiar, generalized representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalization is essential for ML, but fast adaptation tasks (continual, few-shot) suffer from interference when representations generalize overly. The authors seek a fast learner that avoids generalization to prevent destructive interference and to support rapid learning.

Method: Propose a parallel CLS architecture: a fast, disentangled learner provides contextual bias and operates as a parallel reasoning system; a conventional slow, statistical learner handles generalization. The fast system, by biasing perception, steers the slow system to encode novel inputs in familiar terms, enabling zero-shot and few-shot learning; both systems interact within an active perception framework.

Result: The architecture shows that fast, context-driven reasoning can coexist with slow, structured generalization, enabling robust continual learning and zero-shot/few-shot capabilities not easily achieved by traditional CLS or end-to-end generalization strategies.

Conclusion: A fast, non-generalizing module can complement a slow generalizer, yielding a practical pathway for robust continual learning through contextual bias and parallel reasoning.

Abstract: Generalization is often regarded as an essential property of machine learning systems. However, perhaps not every component of a system needs to generalize. Training models for generalization typically produces entangled representations at the boundaries of entities or classes, which can lead to destructive interference when rapid, high-magnitude updates are required for continual or few-shot learning. Techniques for fast learning with non-interfering representations exist, but they generally fail to generalize. Here, we describe a Complementary Learning System (CLS) in which the fast learner entirely foregoes generalization in exchange for continual zero-shot and few-shot learning. Unlike most CLS approaches, which use episodic memory primarily for replay and consolidation, our fast, disentangled learner operates as a parallel reasoning system. The fast learner can overcome observation variability and uncertainty by leveraging a conventional slow, statistical learner within an active perception system: A contextual bias provided by the fast learner induces the slow learner to encode novel stimuli in familiar, generalized terms, enabling zero-shot and few-shot learning. This architecture demonstrates that fast, context-driven reasoning can coexist with slow, structured generalization, providing a pathway for robust continual learning.

</details>


### [301] [LLMs Can Learn to Reason Via Off-Policy RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19362)
*Daniel Ritter,Owen Oertell,Bradley Guo,Jonathan Chang,Kianté Brantley,Wen Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An off-policy RL method for LLMs, OAPL, tolerates large training-inference policy lag without modifying the inference engine or using importance sampling, achieving competitive or superior performance with substantial training efficiency gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address policy lag inherent in distributed RL for LLMs, where training and inference policies diverge; avoid extra engineering to align policies or rely on importance sampling.

Method: Propose Optimal Advantage-based Policy Optimization with Lagged Inference policy (OAPL), an off-policy RL algorithm that explicitly accounts for lag between training and inference policies and does not require IS or inference-engine modifications.

Result: OAPL outperforms GRPO with importance sampling on competition math benchmarks, matches DeepCoder on LiveCodeBench while using 3x fewer generations, and exhibits improved test-time Pass@k scaling; demonstrates robustness to lags over 400 gradient steps and up to 100x more off-policy data than prior methods.

Conclusion: OAPL provides an efficient, effective off-policy RL framework for LLMs that tolerates significant training-inference lag and enables strong post-training improvements without changing the inference setup.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) approaches for Large Language Models (LLMs) frequently use on-policy algorithms, such as PPO or GRPO. However, policy lag from distributed training architectures and differences between the training and inference policies break this assumption, making the data off-policy by design. To rectify this, prior work has focused on making this off-policy data appear more on-policy, either via importance sampling (IS), or by more closely aligning the training and inference policies by explicitly modifying the inference engine. In this work, we embrace off-policyness and propose a novel off-policy RL algorithm that does not require these modifications: Optimal Advantage-based Policy Optimization with Lagged Inference policy (OAPL). We show that OAPL outperforms GRPO with importance sampling on competition math benchmarks, and can match the performance of a publicly available coding model, DeepCoder, on LiveCodeBench, while using 3x fewer generations during training. We further empirically demonstrate that models trained via OAPL have improved test time scaling under the Pass@k metric. OAPL allows for efficient, effective post-training even with lags of more than 400 gradient steps between the training and inference policies, 100x more off-policy than prior approaches.

</details>


### [302] [Stable Deep Reinforcement Learning via Isotropic Gaussian Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19373)
*Ali Saheb,Johan Obando-Ceron,Aaron Courville,Pouya Bashivan,Pablo Samuel Castro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Isotropic Gaussian embeddings are provably advantageous under non-stationary targets, enabling stable target tracking, maximal entropy under a variance budget, and balanced use of representation dimensions. A practical regularizer—Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization (SIGR)—shapes representations toward isotropic Gaussian during training, improving performance under non-stationarity and reducing collapse, dormancy, and instability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-stationarity in RL causes unstable training due to evolving objectives and data distributions. The work seeks representation schemes that provide stability, adaptability, and efficient use of representation capacity.

Method: Theoretical result showing isotropic Gaussian embeddings are advantageous for linear readouts under time-varying targets; introduction of Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization to bias representations toward an isotropic Gaussian distribution during training.

Result: Empirical demonstrations across multiple domains show improved performance under non-stationarity, with reduced representation collapse, neuron dormancy, and training instability.

Conclusion: Isotropic Gaussian representations confer stability and adaptability in non-stationary RL settings; SIGR offers a simple, computationally cheap mechanism to regularize representations toward isotropy, enhancing robustness and performance.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning systems often suffer from unstable training dynamics due to non-stationarity, where learning objectives and data distributions evolve over time. We show that under non-stationary targets, isotropic Gaussian embeddings are provably advantageous. In particular, they induce stable tracking of time-varying targets for linear readouts, achieve maximal entropy under a fixed variance budget, and encourage a balanced use of all representational dimensions--all of which enable agents to be more adaptive and stable. Building on this insight, we propose the use of Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization for shaping representations toward an isotropic Gaussian distribution during training. We demonstrate empirically, over a variety of domains, that this simple and computationally inexpensive method improves performance under non-stationarity while reducing representation collapse, neuron dormancy, and training instability.

</details>


### [303] [Spiking Graph Predictive Coding for Reliable OOD Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19392)
*Jing Ren,Jiapeng Du,Bowen Li,Ziqi Xu,Xin Zheng,Hong Jia,Suyu Ma,Xiwei Xu,Feng Xia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SIGHT is a plug-in, uncertainty-aware graph-learning module that uses iterative error-driven updates over spiking graph states to enable reliable OOD generalization in GNNs, improving accuracy, uncertainty estimation, and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Web4.0 and dynamic Web data cause OOD shifts in feature distributions and labeling; current uncertainty methods are post-hoc, insensitive to shifts, and hard to explain, undermining trustworthy deployment.

Method: An iterative, predictive-coding-like module that operates on spiking graph states, exposing mismatch signals via error-driven corrections; designed as a plug-in to existing GNNs to enable uncertainty-aware predictions and interpretability.

Result: Empirical evaluation across multiple graph benchmarks and diverse OOD scenarios shows consistent gains in predictive accuracy, uncertainty calibration/estimation, and interpretability when integrated with GNNs.

Conclusion: SIGHT advances reliable OOD generalization for graph learning by coupling principled uncertainty estimation with interpretable internal signals, via a lightweight, plug-in module.

Abstract: Graphs provide a powerful basis for modeling Web-based relational data, with expressive GNNs to support the effective learning in dynamic web environments. However, real-world deployment is hindered by pervasive out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts, where evolving user activity and changing content semantics alter feature distributions and labeling criteria. These shifts often lead to unstable or overconfident predictions, undermining the trustworthiness required for Web4Good applications. Achieving reliable OOD generalization demands principled and interpretable uncertainty estimation; however, existing methods are largely post-hoc, insensitive to distribution shifts, and unable to explain where uncertainty arises especially in high-stakes settings. To address these limitations, we introduce SpIking GrapH predicTive coding (SIGHT), an uncertainty-aware plug-in graph learning module for reliable OOD Generalization. SIGHT performs iterative, error-driven correction over spiking graph states, enabling models to expose internal mismatch signals that reveal where predictions become unreliable. Across multiple graph benchmarks and diverse OOD scenarios, SIGHT consistently enhances predictive accuracy, uncertainty estimation, and interpretability when integrated with GNNs.

</details>


### [304] [In Defense of Cosine Similarity: Normalization Eliminates the Gauge Freedom](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19393)
*Taha Bouhsine*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: On unit-sphere embeddings, diagonal gauge ambiguity vanishes and cosine distance equals half the squared Euclidean distance, making cosine-based and Euclidean-based rankings equivalent; normalization resolves the previously claimed pathologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify the alleged arbitrariness of cosine similarity in learned embeddings due to gauge freedom and distinguish objective-compatible geometry from objective-pathology; show how normalization restores a faithful metric correspondence.

Method: Theoretical analysis under unit-norm constraint: show that for any diagonal D, x and y on S^{d-1} satisfy D x and x have same norm; prove that cosine similarity becomes invariant to D; derive that d_cos(x,y) = (1/2) ||x - y||^2 when ||x||=||y||=1; deduce monotone relationship for neighbor rankings.

Result: D-ambiguity identically vanishes on the unit sphere; cosine-based distance is proportional to squared Euclidean distance; neighbor rankings under cosine and Euclidean metrics coincide for normalized embeddings; the supposed problem is not cosine similarity per se but lack of normalization.

Conclusion: Enforce normalization to ensure equivalence; the 'pathology' highlighted by Steck et al. stems from incompatible training objectives that ignore scale, not intrinsic issues with cosine similarity.

Abstract: Steck, Ekanadham, and Kallus [arXiv:2403.05440] demonstrate that cosine similarity of learned embeddings from matrix factorization models can be rendered arbitrary by a diagonal ``gauge'' matrix $D$. Their result is correct and important for practitioners who compute cosine similarity on embeddings trained with dot-product objectives. However, we argue that their conclusion, cautioning against cosine similarity in general, conflates the pathology of an incompatible training objective with the geometric validity of cosine distance on the unit sphere. We prove that when embeddings are constrained to the unit sphere $\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$ (either during or after training with an appropriate objective), the $D$-matrix ambiguity vanishes identically, and cosine distance reduces to exactly half the squared Euclidean distance. This monotonic equivalence implies that cosine-based and Euclidean-based neighbor rankings are identical on normalized embeddings. The ``problem'' with cosine similarity is not cosine similarity, it is the failure to normalize.

</details>


### [305] [One Size Fits None: Modeling NYC Taxi Trips](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19404)
*Tomas Eglinskas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: App-based tipping is largely unpredictable; traditional taxis' tipping is highly predictable; a single universal model is ineffective due to Simpson's paradox; category-specific models are recommended.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether tipping can be predicted across taxi modalities and whether a single model suffices across categories, using a range of modeling methods.

Method: Analysis of 280 million trips from 2024; comparison of modeling approaches from linear regression to deep neural networks; evaluation via R^2 for traditional taxis vs app-based for-hire services.

Result: Traditional taxis showed high predictability (R^2 ≈ 0.72) likely due to in-car payment screen; app-based tipping was largely random (R^2 ≈ 0.17). A single universal model is inadequate; combined models can appear accurate on average due to Simpson's paradox but fail for individual taxi categories.

Conclusion: Avoid universal models across taxi modalities; develop specialized models for each category to reliably predict tips.

Abstract: The rise of app-based ride-sharing has fundamentally changed tipping culture in New York City. We analyzed 280 million trips from 2024 to see if we could predict tips for traditional taxis versus high-volume for-hire services. By testing methods from linear regression to deep neural networks, we found two very different outcomes. Traditional taxis are highly predictable ($R^2 \approx 0.72$) due to the in-car payment screen. In contrast, app-based tipping is random and hard to model ($R^2 \approx 0.17$). In conclusion, we show that building one universal model is a mistake and, due to Simpson's paradox, a combined model looks accurate on average but fails to predict tips for individual taxi categories requiring specialized models.

</details>


### [306] [LEVDA: Latent Ensemble Variational Data Assimilation via Differentiable Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19406)
*Phillip Si,Peng Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LEVDA is an adjoint-free, latent-space 4DEnVar data assimilation method that uses a differentiable neural dynamics surrogate to perform ensemble-variational smoothing in latent space, achieving accurate, uncertainty-quantified assimilation with irregular sampling and improved efficiency over full-state 4DEnVar and latent baselines under sparse observations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global geophysical forecasts are fundamentally limited by chaotic dynamics and numerical errors. Classical variational smoothers require computationally expensive tangent-linear and adjoint models. Latent filtering approaches often enforce weak trajectory constraints and assume fixed observation grids. A latent-space, adjoint-free method that handles irregular spatiotemporal sampling and unknown parameters is desirable.

Method: LEVDA operates in the low-dimensional latent space of a pretrained differentiable neural dynamics surrogate. It performs four-dimensional ensemble-variational (4DEnVar) optimization within an ensemble subspace, jointly assimilating states and unknown parameters without adjoint code or observation-to-latent encoders. The surrogate is fully differentiable and continuous in time and space, naturally accommodating irregular sampling.

Result: Across three challenging geophysical benchmarks, LEVDA matches or outperforms state-of-the-art latent filtering baselines under severe observational sparsity, with more reliable uncertainty quantification. It also achieves substantially improved assimilation accuracy and computational efficiency compared to full-state 4DEnVar.

Conclusion: LEVDA bridges variational and latent-filtering approaches by enabling adjoint-free, efficient, and accurate assimilation in latent space, with robust uncertainty quantification and support for irregular sampling; it scales better than full-state methods while retaining data-assimilation fidelity.

Abstract: Long-range geophysical forecasts are fundamentally limited by chaotic dynamics and numerical errors. While data assimilation can mitigate these issues, classical variational smoothers require computationally expensive tangent-linear and adjoint models. Conversely, recent efficient latent filtering methods often enforce weak trajectory-level constraints and assume fixed observation grids. To bridge this gap, we propose Latent Ensemble Variational Data Assimilation (LEVDA), an ensemble-space variational smoother that operates in the low-dimensional latent space of a pretrained differentiable neural dynamics surrogate. By performing four-dimensional ensemble-variational (4DEnVar) optimization within an ensemble subspace, LEVDA jointly assimilates states and unknown parameters without the need for adjoint code or auxiliary observation-to-latent encoders. Leveraging the fully differentiable, continuous-in-time-and-space nature of the surrogate, LEVDA naturally accommodates highly irregular sampling at arbitrary spatiotemporal locations. Across three challenging geophysical benchmarks, LEVDA matches or outperforms state-of-the-art latent filtering baselines under severe observational sparsity while providing more reliable uncertainty quantification. Simultaneously, it achieves substantially improved assimilation accuracy and computational efficiency compared to full-state 4DEnVar.

</details>


### [307] [Federated Causal Representation Learning in State-Space Systems for Decentralized Counterfactual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19414)
*Nazal Mohamed,Ayush Mohanty,Nagi Gebraeel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A federated, privacy-preserving causal representation learning framework for interdependent state-space systems that enables decentralized counterfactual inference by exchanging latent states, with convergence guarantees to a centralized oracle, validated on synthetic and real datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Answering how a client’s output changes when other clients change their operation is hampered by high-dimensional, private data and proprietary local models; centralization is infeasible. The work aims to enable cross-client causal reasoning without data sharing.

Method: Each client learns a low-dimensional latent state representation that disentangles intrinsic dynamics from control-driven effects. A central server estimates the global state-transition and control structure. Clients perform decentralized counterfactuals by simulating alternative control inputs for others using exchanged latent states. The approach includes convergence proofs to a centralized oracle and privacy guarantees.

Result: Experiments show scalability and accurate cross-client counterfactual inference on both synthetic data and real-world industrial control system datasets.

Conclusion: The framework provides privacy-preserving, scalable causal inference for federated state-space systems, with convergence guarantees and practical effectiveness demonstrated on diverse datasets.

Abstract: Networks of interdependent industrial assets (clients) are tightly coupled through physical processes and control inputs, raising a key question: how would the output of one client change if another client were operated differently? This is difficult to answer because client-specific data are high-dimensional and private, making centralization of raw data infeasible. Each client also maintains proprietary local models that cannot be modified. We propose a federated framework for causal representation learning in state-space systems that captures interdependencies among clients under these constraints. Each client maps high-dimensional observations into low-dimensional latent states that disentangle intrinsic dynamics from control-driven influences. A central server estimates the global state-transition and control structure. This enables decentralized counterfactual reasoning where clients predict how outputs would change under alternative control inputs at others while only exchanging compact latent states. We prove convergence to a centralized oracle and provide privacy guarantees. Our experiments demonstrate scalability, and accurate cross-client counterfactual inference on synthetic and real-world industrial control system datasets.

</details>


### [308] [RAmmStein: Regime Adaptation in Mean-reverting Markets with Stein Thresholds -- Optimal Impulse Control in Concentrated AMMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19419)
*Pranay Anchuri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes an optimal-control framework for concentrated liquidity with a RL-based agent RAmmStein; solves HJB-QVI; uses mean-reversion OU features; shows regime-aware laziness improves ROI and reduces rebalances on high-frequency Coinbase data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LPs must balance fee revenue from tight price ranges against rebalancing costs such as gas and slippage; heuristic methods miss market dynamics; a principled approach can adapt to regimes.

Method: Formulate liquidity management as an impulse-control problem; derive HJB-QVI; propose an approximate solution RAmmStein (deep RL) that inputs OU mean-reversion speed (theta) and other features; learn action/inaction regions; evaluate on 1 Hz Coinbase data (≈6.8 million trades).

Result: Net ROI of 0.72% achieved; rebalancing frequency reduced by 67% relative to greedy strategy; 88% active time; regime-aware laziness preserves returns and improves capital efficiency.

Conclusion: Integrating optimal-control with deep RL yields regime-aware liquidity management with improved capital efficiency; method can adapt to market regimes and reduce operational costs, though potential limitations include model risk, data requirements, and generalization to different markets.

Abstract: Concentrated liquidity provision in decentralized exchanges presents a fundamental Impulse Control problem. Liquidity Providers (LPs) face a non-trivial trade-off between maximizing fee accrual through tight price-range concentration and minimizing the friction costs of rebalancing, including gas fees and swap slippage. Existing methods typically employ heuristic or threshold strategies that fail to account for market dynamics. This paper formulates liquidity management as an optimal control problem and derives the corresponding Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman quasi-variational inequality (HJB-QVI). We present an approximate solution RAmmStein, a Deep Reinforcement Learning method that incorporates the mean-reversion speed (theta) of an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process among other features as input to the model. We demonstrate that the agent learns to separate the state space into regions of action and inaction. We evaluate the framework using high-frequency 1Hz Coinbase trade data comprising over 6.8M trades. Experimental results show that RAmmStein achieves a superior net ROI of 0.72% compared to both passive and aggressive strategies. Notably, the agent reduces rebalancing frequency by 67% compared to a greedy rebalancing strategy while maintaining 88% active time. Our results demonstrate that regime-aware laziness can significantly improve capital efficiency by preserving the returns that would otherwise be eroded by the operational costs.

</details>


### [309] [PIS: A Physics-Informed System for Accurate State Partitioning of $Aβ_{42}$ Protein Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19444)
*Qianfeng Yu,Ningkang Peng,Yanhui Gu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A physics-informed deep learning framework (PIS) for metastable state partitioning in Aβ42 trajectories, integrating physical priors into topological feature extraction to improve state transition detection and provide interpretable, interactive analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end DL models struggle to capture subtle conformational state transitions in protein trajectories without explicit physical constraints; need physically grounded interpretability for β-amyloid aggregation research.

Method: PIS integrates pre-computed physical priors (e.g., radius of gyration, solvent-accessible surface area) into the extraction of topological features to perform robust metastable state partitioning; includes an interactive platform for dynamic monitoring of physical characteristics and multi-dimensional result validation.

Result: Achieves superior performance on the Aβ42 dataset by leveraging physics-informed priors in topology-based features; provides an interactive interface and validation framework; demonstration video available.

Conclusion: Physics-informed, topology-based metastable state partitioning improves robustness and interpretability of conformational analysis in β-amyloid trajectories and offers researchers a practical tool for physically grounded analysis.

Abstract: Understanding the conformational evolution of $β$-amyloid ($Aβ$), particularly the $Aβ_{42}$ isoform, is fundamental to elucidating the pathogenic mechanisms underlying Alzheimer's disease. However, existing end-to-end deep learning models often struggle to capture subtle state transitions in protein trajectories due to a lack of explicit physical constraints. In this work, we introduce PIS, a Physics-Informed System designed for robust metastable state partitioning. By integrating pre-computed physical priors, such as the radius of gyration and solvent-accessible surface area, into the extraction of topological features, our model achieves superior performance on the $Aβ_{42}$ dataset. Furthermore, PIS provides an interactive platform that features dynamic monitoring of physical characteristics and multi-dimensional result validation. This system offers biological researchers a powerful set of analytical tools with physically grounded interpretability. A demonstration video of PIS is available on https://youtu.be/AJHGzUtRCg0.

</details>


### [310] [SenTSR-Bench: Thinking with Injected Knowledge for Time-Series Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19455)
*Zelin He,Boran Han,Xiyuan Zhang,Shuai Zhang,Haotian Lin,Qi Zhu,Haoyang Fang,Danielle C. Maddix,Abdul Fatir Ansari,Akash Chandrayan,Abhinav Pradhan,Bernie Wang,Matthew Reimherr*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hybrid framework fuses general reasoning LLMs with time-series domain knowledge by injecting TSLM-derived insights into GRLM reasoning traces, aided by RL-based unsupervised trace elicitation, plus a new in-domain benchmark SenTSR-Bench, achieving large performance gains over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRLMs excel at general reasoning but lack domain-specific time-series knowledge; TSLMs have in-domain time-series understanding but struggle with generalization to complex questions. A hybrid approach to fuse reasoning and knowledge can close this gap.

Method: Introduce a hybrid knowledge-injection framework that injects TSLM-generated insights into GRLM reasoning traces. Use a reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) to elicit knowledge-rich, supervision-free traces, then transfer these traces into GRLMs for efficient in-domain knowledge injection. Release SenTSR-Bench, a multivariate time-series diagnostic reasoning benchmark from real-world industrial operations.

Result: Across SenTSR-Bench and public datasets, the method surpasses TSLMs by 9.1%–26.1% and GRLMs by 7.9%–22.4%, delivering robust, context-aware time-series diagnostic insights.

Conclusion: The framework enables strong time-series diagnostic reasoning by infusing GRLMs with in-domain knowledge via injected reasoning traces, with data-efficient trace elicitation (RLVR) and a new benchmark to validate performance.

Abstract: Time-series diagnostic reasoning is essential for many applications, yet existing solutions face a persistent gap: general reasoning large language models (GRLMs) possess strong reasoning skills but lack the domain-specific knowledge to understand complex time-series patterns. Conversely, fine-tuned time-series LLMs (TSLMs) understand these patterns but lack the capacity to generalize reasoning for more complicated questions. To bridge this gap, we propose a hybrid knowledge-injection framework that injects TSLM-generated insights directly into GRLM's reasoning trace, thereby achieving strong time-series reasoning with in-domain knowledge. As collecting data for knowledge injection fine-tuning is costly, we further leverage a reinforcement learning-based approach with verifiable rewards (RLVR) to elicit knowledge-rich traces without human supervision, then transfer such an in-domain thinking trace into GRLM for efficient knowledge injection. We further release SenTSR-Bench, a multivariate time-series-based diagnostic reasoning benchmark collected from real-world industrial operations. Across SenTSR-Bench and other public datasets, our method consistently surpasses TSLMs by 9.1%-26.1% and GRLMs by 7.9%-22.4%, delivering robust, context-aware time-series diagnostic insights.

</details>


### [311] [Making Conformal Predictors Robust in Healthcare Settings: a Case Study on EEG Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19483)
*Arjun Chatterjee,Sayeed Sajjad Razin,John Wu,Siddhartha Laghuvarapu,Jathurshan Pradeepkumar,Jimeng Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conformal prediction under distribution shift improves EEG seizure uncertainty with personalized calibration; >20pp gain in coverage with similar set sizes; open-source PyHealth implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty quantification in high-stakes clinical tasks is fragile under non-i.i.d. distribution shifts. Standard conformal methods risk poor coverage in healthcare settings.

Method: Systematic evaluation of multiple conformal prediction approaches on EEG seizure classification under distribution shift; introduces personalized calibration strategies; compares coverage and set sizes; provides an open-source PyHealth implementation.

Result: Personalized calibration achieves over 20 percentage point improvement in coverage while maintaining comparable prediction set sizes, across conformal prediction approaches evaluated.

Conclusion: Personalized calibration is an effective, practical strategy to bolster conformal prediction robustness to distribution shifts in healthcare predictions; implementation available in PyHealth for broader use.

Abstract: Quantifying uncertainty in clinical predictions is critical for high-stakes diagnosis tasks. Conformal prediction offers a principled approach by providing prediction sets with theoretical coverage guarantees. However, in practice, patient distribution shifts violate the i.i.d. assumptions underlying standard conformal methods, leading to poor coverage in healthcare settings. In this work, we evaluate several conformal prediction approaches on EEG seizure classification, a task with known distribution shift challenges and label uncertainty. We demonstrate that personalized calibration strategies can improve coverage by over 20 percentage points while maintaining comparable prediction set sizes. Our implementation is available via PyHealth, an open-source healthcare AI framework: https://github.com/sunlabuiuc/PyHealth.

</details>


### [312] [Federated Learning Playground](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19489)
*Bryan Guanrong Shan,Alysa Ziying Tan,Han Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Browser-based interactive platform extending TensorFlow Playground to teach Federated Learning concepts through user-controlled heterogeneity, hyperparameters, and aggregation methods with real-time visualizations; aims to lower entry barriers and support rapid FL prototyping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Educate newcomers on Federated Learning challenges (non-IID data, local overfitting, scalability) and provide a sandbox for exploring and comparing FL methods without coding or setup.

Method: An in-browser, TensorFlow Playground-inspired platform where users manipulate client data distributions, model hyperparameters, and aggregation algorithms; real-time visualizations show effects on client and global models; no coding required.

Result: Introduces an educational FL playground that enables intuitive learning, experimentation, and comparison of FL techniques in a low-friction environment; supports rapid prototyping and broader exploration of FL.

Conclusion: Democratizes FL exploration, lowering barriers to entry and promoting understanding and adoption of Federated Learning; serves as a versatile sandbox for education and method comparison.

Abstract: We present Federated Learning Playground, an interactive browser-based platform inspired by and extends TensorFlow Playground that teaches core Federated Learning (FL) concepts. Users can experiment with heterogeneous client data distributions, model hyperparameters, and aggregation algorithms directly in the browser without coding or system setup, and observe their effects on client and global models through real-time visualizations, gaining intuition for challenges such as non-IID data, local overfitting, and scalability. The playground serves as an easy to use educational tool, lowering the entry barrier for newcomers to distributed AI while also offering a sandbox for rapidly prototyping and comparing FL methods. By democratizing exploration of FL, it promotes broader understanding and adoption of this important paradigm.

</details>


### [313] [Softmax is not Enough (for Adaptive Conformal Classification)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19498)
*Navid Akhavan Attar,Hesam Asadollahzadeh,Ling Luo,Uwe Aickelin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Energy-based reweighting of nonconformity scores in conformal prediction using Helmholtz Free Energy from the pre-softmax logits improves adaptiveness and efficiency without adding post-hoc complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conformal prediction (CP) aims for distribution-free, efficient, and adaptive prediction sets. However, CP using softmax-derived nonconformity scores can be unreliable, limiting adaptiveness. The work seeks to enhance CP by leveraging pre-softmax logits via energy-based uncertainty, addressing miscalibration and over/under-confidence.

Method: Compute Helmholtz Free Energy from the logit space as an energy score for each sample. Reweight the CP nonconformity scores with a monotonic transformation of this energy. Evaluate four state-of-the-art score functions across multiple datasets and deep architectures to assess improvements in adaptiveness and efficiency.

Result: The energy-based enhancement improves the adaptiveness and efficiency of the conformal prediction sets across tested score functions, datasets, and architectures, without introducing post-hoc computational complexity.

Conclusion: Energy-based reweighting in the logit (pre-softmax) space is an effective, simple augmentation for conformal prediction, yielding more adaptive and efficient prediction sets without extra post-hoc steps.

Abstract: The merit of Conformal Prediction (CP), as a distribution-free framework for uncertainty quantification, depends on generating prediction sets that are efficient, reflected in small average set sizes, while adaptive, meaning they signal uncertainty by varying in size according to input difficulty. A central limitation for deep conformal classifiers is that the nonconformity scores are derived from softmax outputs, which can be unreliable indicators of how certain the model truly is about a given input, sometimes leading to overconfident misclassifications or undue hesitation. In this work, we argue that this unreliability can be inherited by the prediction sets generated by CP, limiting their capacity for adaptiveness. We propose a new approach that leverages information from the pre-softmax logit space, using the Helmholtz Free Energy as a measure of model uncertainty and sample difficulty. By reweighting nonconformity scores with a monotonic transformation of the energy score of each sample, we improve their sensitivity to input difficulty. Our experiments with four state-of-the-art score functions on multiple datasets and deep architectures show that this energy-based enhancement improves the adaptiveness of the prediction sets, leading to a notable increase in both efficiency and adaptiveness compared to baseline nonconformity scores, without introducing any post-hoc complexity.

</details>


### [314] [Less is More: Convergence Benefits of Fewer Data Weight Updates over Longer Horizon](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19510)
*Rudrajit Das,Neel Patel,Meisam Razaviyayn,Vahab Mirrokni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Finite inner optimization steps in bilevel data-mixing can critically affect convergence; the common T=1 greedy approach can fail even in simple cases. Optimal T grows with budget: T = Θ(log N) for full gradients and T = Θ((N log N)^{1/2}) for stochastic gradients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the theoretical implications of using a finite, typically small number of inner steps in bilevel data-mixing optimization and to characterize how the inner-step count T should scale with the optimization budget N.

Method: Rigorous convergence analysis of bilevel data mixing with a fixed inner-step count T. Proves that T=1 can fail (via a simple quadratic example). Under a fixed update budget N and strong convexity of per-domain losses, derives the optimal T scaling as Θ(log N) for full gradients and Θ((N log N)^{1/2}) for stochastic gradients. Supports findings with proof-of-concept experiments.

Result: Demonstrates that the greedy T=1 approach can be suboptimal or fail; provides explicit scaling laws for the optimal inner-iteration count under budget constraints; confirms theoretical predictions with experiments.

Conclusion: Practitioners should not default to T=1; the inner-step count should grow with the available budget, following the shown scaling laws. The results offer guidance on choosing T in bilevel data-mixing setups under both deterministic and stochastic-gradient regimes, with experimental validation.

Abstract: Data mixing--the strategic reweighting of training domains--is a critical component in training robust machine learning models. This problem is naturally formulated as a bilevel optimization task, where the outer loop optimizes domain weights to minimize validation loss, and the inner loop optimizes model parameters to minimize the weighted training loss. Classical bilevel optimization relies on hypergradients, which theoretically require the inner optimization to reach convergence. However, due to computational constraints, state-of-the-art methods use a finite, often small, number of inner update steps before updating the weights. The theoretical implications of this approximation are not well understood. In this work, we rigorously analyze the convergence behavior of data mixing with a finite number of inner steps $T$. We prove that the "greedy" practical approach of using $T=1$ can fail even in a simple quadratic example. Under a fixed parameter update budget $N$ and assuming the per-domain losses are strongly convex, we show that the optimal $T$ scales as $Θ(\log N)$ (resp., $Θ({(N \log N)}^{1/2})$) for the data mixing problem with access to full (resp., stochastic) gradients. We complement our theoretical results with proof-of-concept experiments.

</details>


### [315] [Variational Trajectory Optimization of Anisotropic Diffusion Schedules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19512)
*Pengxi Liu,Zeyu Michael Li,Xiang Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Variational diffusion with learnable anisotropic noise schedules (matrix-valued M_t(θ)); joint score and schedule training; derivative estimator; anisotropic Heun reverse-ODE; improves EDM across multiple datasets; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of isotropic or fixed noise schedules by allocating noise across subspaces, enabling more expressive diffusion processes and improved sample quality/efficiency.

Method: Introduce a trajectory-level objective that jointly trains the score network and a matrix-valued noise schedule M_t(θ). Parameterize M_t as a path in subspace-aware noise, derive an estimator for ∂score/∂θ, and implement an anisotropic generalization of a second-order Heun reverse-ODE for inference. Train on standard benchmarks and compare to EDM.

Result: Across CIFAR-10, AFHQv2, FFHQ, and ImageNet-64, the method consistently outperforms the baseline EDM model across all NFEs.

Conclusion: Trainable anisotropic noise schedules combined with an efficient anisotropic reverse-ODE solver yield better diffusion performance and practical inference, with code released.

Abstract: We introduce a variational framework for diffusion models with anisotropic noise schedules parameterized by a matrix-valued path $M_t(θ)$ that allocates noise across subspaces. Central to our framework is a trajectory-level objective that jointly trains the score network and learns $M_t(θ)$, which encompasses general parameterization classes of matrix-valued noise schedules. We further derive an estimator for the derivative with respect to $θ$ of the score that enables efficient optimization of the $M_t(θ)$ schedule. For inference, we develop an efficiently-implementable reverse-ODE solver that is an anisotropic generalization of the second-order Heun discretization algorithm. Across CIFAR-10, AFHQv2, FFHQ, and ImageNet-64, our method consistently improves upon the baseline EDM model in all NFE regimes. Code is available at https://github.com/lizeyu090312/anisotropic-diffusion-paper.

</details>


### [316] [Beyond Accuracy: A Unified Random Matrix Theory Diagnostic Framework for Crash Classification Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19528)
*Ibne Farabi Shihab,Sanjeda Akter,Anuj Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spectral diagnostics using Random Matrix Theory and HTSR quantify model quality beyond accuracy; the alpha exponent of spectral distributions tracks regularization level and aligns with expert judgment, enabling alpha-based early stopping and scalable model selection across diverse model families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current performance metrics fail to reveal silent overfitting. A universal spectral signal, through RMT/HTSR, can diagnose regularization quality across architectures and tasks.

Method: Compute model-specific spectral descriptors across nine model families (weights for transformer models; out-of-fold increments for tree ensembles; Hessians for logistic regression; affinity matrices for decision trees; graph Laplacians for KNN). Evaluate on two Iowa DOT crash classification tasks with large datasets. Estimate power-law exponent alpha; analyze its relation to overfitting, expert agreement, and cross-validated F1. Propose alpha-based stopping and selection; use Sparse Lanczos for scalability.

Result: Well-regularized models exhibit alpha in [2,4] (mean ~2.87, sd 0.34). Overfit variants show alpha<2 or spectral collapse. Alpha correlates with expert agreement (Spearman 0.89, p<0.001). Alpha-based stopping and model selection validated against F1 baselines. Framework scalable via Sparse Lanczos.

Conclusion: Spectral quality via alpha provides a robust, model-agnostic signal for regularization health, enabling reliable early stopping and model selection across diverse ML families; scalable approach suitable for large-tabular and NLP models.

Abstract: Crash classification models in transportation safety are typically evaluated using accuracy, F1, or AUC, metrics that cannot reveal whether a model is silently overfitting. We introduce a spectral diagnostic framework grounded in Random Matrix Theory (RMT) and Heavy-Tailed Self-Regularization (HTSR) that spans the ML taxonomy: weight matrices for BERT/ALBERT/Qwen2.5, out-of-fold increment matrices for XGBoost/Random Forest, empirical Hessians for Logistic Regression, induced affinity matrices for Decision Trees, and Graph Laplacians for KNN. Evaluating nine model families on two Iowa DOT crash classification tasks (173,512 and 371,062 records respectively), we find that the power-law exponent $α$ provides a structural quality signal: well-regularized models consistently yield $α$ within $[2, 4]$ (mean $2.87 \pm 0.34$), while overfit variants show $α< 2$ or spectral collapse. We observe a strong rank correlation between $α$ and expert agreement (Spearman $ρ= 0.89$, $p < 0.001$), suggesting spectral quality captures model behaviors aligned with expert reasoning. We propose an $α$-based early stopping criterion and a spectral model selection protocol, and validate both against cross-validated F1 baselines. Sparse Lanczos approximations make the framework scalable to large datasets.

</details>


### [317] [A Statistical Approach for Modeling Irregular Multivariate Time Series with Missing Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19531)
*Dingyi Nie,Yixing Wu,C. -C. Jay Kuo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A simple, time-agnostic representation for irregular multivariate time series uses per-variable statistics (mean, std of observed values; mean and variability of changes) as fixed-dimensional features, fed into standard classifiers, achieving state-of-the-art results on biomedical datasets while reducing complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Irregular time series with missing values pose challenges for traditional sequence models. The work questions whether temporal modeling is necessary for certain predictive tasks and proposes a time-agnostic approach that is efficient, interpretable, and robust to irregular sampling.

Method: For each variable, compute: (1) mean of observed values, (2) standard deviation of observed values, (3) mean of consecutive value changes, and (4) variability (e.g., std) of those changes. This yields a fixed-dimensional feature vector independent of time. Train standard classifiers (logistic regression, XGBoost) on these features. Evaluated on PhysioNet Challenge 2012, 2019, PAMAP2, and MIMIC-III datasets.

Result: The method achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing recent transformer and graph-based models by ~0.5–1.7% in AUROC/AUPRC and ~1.1–1.7% in accuracy/F1. It reduces computational complexity and ablations show feature extraction, not classifier choice, drives gains. Summary statistics outperform raw/imputed inputs in most benchmarks; missing patterns themselves can be predictive (e.g., PhysioNet 2019 sepsis prediction achieving 94.2% AUROC using only missing indicators with XGBoost, within 1.6% of using raw data).

Conclusion: Time-agnostic fixed-dimensional summaries can rival or exceed complex temporal models for irregular time series classification, offering an efficient, interpretable alternative when the task allows disregard of precise temporal ordering.

Abstract: Irregular multivariate time series with missing values present significant challenges for predictive modeling in domains such as healthcare. While deep learning approaches often focus on temporal interpolation or complex architectures to handle irregularities, we propose a simpler yet effective alternative: extracting time-agnostic summary statistics to eliminate the temporal axis. Our method computes four key features per variable-mean and standard deviation of observed values, as well as the mean and variability of changes between consecutive observations to create a fixed-dimensional representation. These features are then utilized with standard classifiers, such as logistic regression and XGBoost. Evaluated on four biomedical datasets (PhysioNet Challenge 2012, 2019, PAMAP2, and MIMIC-III), our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing recent transformer and graph-based models by 0.5-1.7% in AUROC/AUPRC and 1.1-1.7% in accuracy/F1-score, while reducing computational complexity. Ablation studies demonstrate that feature extraction-not classifier choice-drives performance gains, and our summary statistics outperform raw/imputed input in most benchmarks. In particular, we identify scenarios where missing patterns themselves encode predictive signals, as in sepsis prediction (PhysioNet, 2019), where missing indicators alone can achieve 94.2% AUROC with XGBoost, only 1.6% lower than using original raw data as input.
