Our results challenge the necessity of complex temporal modeling when task objectives permit time-agnostic representations, providing an efficient and interpretable solution for irregular time series classification.

</details>


### [318] [Grokking Finite-Dimensional Algebra](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19533)
*Pascal Jr Tikeng Notsawo,Guillaume Dumas,Guillaume Rabusseau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends grokking from groups to finite-dimensional algebras (FDA), linking learning multiplication to the structure tensor; analyzes how algebraic properties and tensor structure affect grokking and generalization, with real and finite-field settings and a unified framework for structured representation learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Grokking shows a delayed generalization gap during training. Extending the analysis to FDA broadens understanding of how neural nets internalize algebraic structure, revealing the influence of non-associative, non-commutative, and non-unital operations on generalization dynamics.

Method: Theoretical framing of FDA multiplication as a bilinear map defined by a structure tensor. For real algebras, relate learning to matrix factorization with implicit low-rank bias; for finite fields, highlight the need to learn discrete representations. Empirical investigations vary commutativity, associativity, unitality, and tensor sparsity/rank, examining effects on grokking time, generalization, and latent embeddings.

Result: Demonstrates that learning group operations is a special case of FDA learning. Shows grokking emerges across FDA settings; algebraic properties influence the timing of grokking; tensor sparsity and rank affect generalization; evidence of latent embeddings aligning with the algebra’s representation.

Conclusion: Provides a unified framework for grokking across algebraic structures, offering insights into how mathematical structure governs neural network generalization dynamics and suggesting directions for structured representation learning in neural networks.

Abstract: This paper investigates the grokking phenomenon, which refers to the sudden transition from a long memorization to generalization observed during neural networks training, in the context of learning multiplication in finite-dimensional algebras (FDA). While prior work on grokking has focused mainly on group operations, we extend the analysis to more general algebraic structures, including non-associative, non-commutative, and non-unital algebras. We show that learning group operations is a special case of learning FDA, and that learning multiplication in FDA amounts to learning a bilinear product specified by the algebra's structure tensor. For algebras over the reals, we connect the learning problem to matrix factorization with an implicit low-rank bias, and for algebras over finite fields, we show that grokking emerges naturally as models must learn discrete representations of algebraic elements. This leads us to experimentally investigate the following core questions: (i) how do algebraic properties such as commutativity, associativity, and unitality influence both the emergence and timing of grokking, (ii) how structural properties of the structure tensor of the FDA, such as sparsity and rank, influence generalization, and (iii) to what extent generalization correlates with the model learning latent embeddings aligned with the algebra's representation. Our work provides a unified framework for grokking across algebraic structures and new insights into how mathematical structure governs neural network generalization dynamics.

</details>


### [319] [The Sample Complexity of Replicable Realizable PAC Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19552)
*Kasper Green Larsen,Markus Engelund Mathiasen,Chirag Pabbaraju,Clement Svendsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Lower bound of roughly (log|H|)^{3/2} on sample complexity for replicable realizable PAC learning, shown via a hard instance built from a Cayley graph on H and spectral analysis of the adjacency matrix; almost matching upper bound on the same instance, suggesting tightness up to instance choice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify fundamental limits of replicable realizable PAC learning as a function of hypothesis class size; develop techniques to derive nontrivial lower bounds.

Method: Construct a hard learning problem using a Cayley graph over the hypothesis class H; analyze a random walk on this graph; study spectral properties of the adjacency matrix to bound sample complexity.

Result: Proves a lower bound with near (log|H|)^{3/2} dependence on |H| for the problem; provides an almost matching upper bound for the same lower-bound instance, indicating the bound is tight up to the choice of instance.

Conclusion: The work introduces novel techniques (Cayley-graph based construction and spectral analysis of random walks) to derive hard instances for replicable realizable PAC learning; tightening the lower bound likely requires a different problem instance.

Abstract: In this paper, we consider the problem of replicable realizable PAC learning. We construct a particularly hard learning problem and show a sample complexity lower bound with a close to $(\log|H|)^{3/2}$ dependence on the size of the hypothesis class $H$. Our proof uses several novel techniques and works by defining a particular Cayley graph associated with $H$ and analyzing a suitable random walk on this graph by examining the spectral properties of its adjacency matrix.
  Furthermore, we show an almost matching upper bound for the lower bound instance, meaning if a stronger lower bound exists, one would have to consider a different instance of the problem.

</details>


### [320] [Leap+Verify: Regime-Adaptive Speculative Weight Prediction for Accelerating Neural Network Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19580)
*Jeremy McEntire*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Leap+Verify uses speculative execution to speed up NN training by predicting future weights with analytic predictors within three dynamically detected regimes (chaotic, transition, stable) using activation-space cosine similarity as a Lyapunov proxy; momentum extrapolation fails, finite-difference predictors succeed with moderate acceptance rates, regime distribution depends on model scale, and phase boundaries are robust across seeds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To accelerate neural network training by predicting weights ahead of time and validating predictions to avoid degradation, inspired by speculative decoding and ASC, and to understand regime dynamics across model scales.

Method: Decompose training into three regimes detected in real time via activation-space cosine similarity; apply predictive schemes (momentum/linear/quadratic extrapolation) to forecast parameters K steps ahead within each regime; accept predictions only if validated against a held-out loss criterion; evaluate across GPT-2 124M and Qwen 2.5-1.5B on WikiText-103 with K in {5,10,25,50,75,100}; analyze acceptance rates, stability, and regime distribution across seeds.

Result: Momentum extrapolation catastrophically explodes loss; finite-difference predictors (linear, quadratic) achieve nonzero strict acceptance (e.g., 24% at K=5 in stable regime for 124M; 37% at K=5 in transition for 1.5B). Cross-seed results are highly consistent; regime boundaries are stable across seeds (within ±50 steps). Model scale affects regime distribution: 124M spends 34% stable; 1.5B spends 64% chaotic and reaches stability late (0–2 of 40 checkpoints). Larger models are more predictable when predictable but less often predictable; practical bottleneck is regime availability.

Conclusion: Speculative weight prediction can accelerate training under suitable regimes, but predictor choice and regime availability critically determine applicability; linear/quadratic extrapolation are viable in certain regimes while momentum extrapolation fails; regime dynamics are scale-dependent but robust across seeds.

Abstract: We introduce Leap+Verify, a framework that applies speculative execution -- predicting future model weights and validating predictions before acceptance -- to accelerate neural network training. Inspired by speculative decoding in language model inference and by the Automatically Scalable Computation (ASC) architecture for program execution, Leap+Verify decomposes training into three dynamically detected regimes (chaotic, transition, stable) using activation-space cosine similarity as a real-time Lyapunov proxy signal. Within each regime, analytic weight predictors (momentum, linear, quadratic extrapolation) attempt to forecast model parameters K training steps ahead; predictions are accepted only when validated against a held-out loss criterion. We evaluate Leap+Verify on GPT-2 124M and Qwen 2.5-1.5B trained on WikiText-103 across five random seeds, sweeping prediction depth K in {5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100}. Momentum-based prediction (Adam moment extrapolation) fails catastrophically at both scales, with predicted losses exceeding actuals by 100-10,000x -- a universal norm explosion in optimizer-state extrapolation. Finite-difference predictors (linear, quadratic) succeed where momentum fails: at 124M, they achieve 24% strict acceptance at K=5 in stable regimes; at 1.5B, they achieve 37% strict acceptance in transition regimes. The scale-dependent finding is in regime distribution: GPT-2 124M spends 34% of training in stable regime, while Qwen 1.5B spends 64% in chaotic regime and reaches stable in only 0-2 of 40 checkpoints. Larger models are more predictable when predictable, but less often predictable -- the practical bottleneck shifts from predictor accuracy to regime availability. Cross-seed results are highly consistent (less than 1% validation loss variance), and the three-regime framework produces identical phase boundaries (plus or minus 50 steps) across seeds.

</details>


### [321] [Advantage-based Temporal Attack in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19582)
*Shenghong He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AAT (Advantage-based Adversarial Transformer) introduces time-correlated adversarial perturbations for deep RL by using multi-scale causal self-attention to capture temporal dependencies, guided by a weighted advantage mechanism; it matches or surpasses baselines on Atari, DM Control Suite, and Google Football.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DRL models are vulnerable to adversarial inputs. Reward-based attacks that look ahead across time miss temporal dependencies between perturbations, leading to suboptimal attack effectiveness. Modeling time correlations can strengthen adversarial perturbations.

Method: Propose AAT with a multi-scale causal self-attention (MSCSA) module to capture dependencies between historical states and the current state, generating time-correlated perturbations. Introduce a weighted advantage mechanism to prioritize perturbations in high-advantage regions, guiding sampling toward effective perturbations across time steps. Evaluate on Atari, DeepMind Control Suite, and Google Football tasks.

Result: AAT achieves performance equal to or better than mainstream adversarial attack baselines across the listed benchmarks, demonstrating stronger temporal correlations and improved attack effectiveness.

Conclusion: Modeling temporal dependencies in perturbation generation via AAT enhances attack strength; the MSCSA and weighted advantage components effectively coordinate perturbations over time, offering a path to stronger sequential adversarial attacks and informing defense development.

Abstract: Extensive research demonstrates that Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) models are susceptible to adversarially constructed inputs (i.e., adversarial examples), which can mislead the agent to take suboptimal or unsafe actions. Recent methods improve attack effectiveness by leveraging future rewards to guide adversarial perturbation generation over sequential time steps (i.e., reward-based attacks). However, these methods are unable to capture dependencies between different time steps in the perturbation generation process, resulting in a weak temporal correlation between the current perturbation and previous perturbations.In this paper, we propose a novel method called Advantage-based Adversarial Transformer (AAT), which can generate adversarial examples with stronger temporal correlations (i.e., time-correlated adversarial examples) to improve the attack performance. AAT employs a multi-scale causal self-attention (MSCSA) mechanism to dynamically capture dependencies between historical information from different time periods and the current state, thus enhancing the correlation between the current perturbation and the previous perturbation. Moreover, AAT introduces a weighted advantage mechanism, which quantifies the effectiveness of a perturbation in a given state and guides the generation process toward high-performance adversarial examples by sampling high-advantage regions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the performance of AAT matches or surpasses mainstream adversarial attack baselines on Atari, DeepMind Control Suite and Google football tasks.

</details>


### [322] [Interpolation-Driven Machine Learning Approaches for Plume Shine Dose Estimation: A Comparison of XGBoost, Random Forest, and TabNet](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19584)
*Biswajit Sadhu,Kalpak Gupte,Trijit Sadhu,S. Anand*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Interpolation-assisted ML framework augments discrete plume dose data with shape-preserving interpolation to train tree-based models (RF, XGBoost) and TabNet for gamma-emitting radionuclides; XGBoost offers highest accuracy, with distinct feature-utilization patterns; a web GUI enables scenario evaluation and comparison with photon-transport references.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome safety-critical constraints, data scarcity, and architecture choice challenges in ML-based radiation dose assessment by integrating interpolation to create high-resolution training data and evaluating diverse models.

Method: Generate discrete dose datasets for 17 gamma-emitting radionuclides across varying downwind distances, release heights, and atmospheric stability using pyDOSEIA. Augment with shape-preserving interpolation to create dense training data. Compare Random Forest, XGBoost, and TabNet. Use permutation importance and attention-based attribution to interpret model behavior. Develop a web-based GUI for interactive scenario evaluation against photon-transport references.

Result: Interpolated high-resolution data improved predictive accuracy across all models; XGBoost achieved the best performance. Interpretability analyses showed tree-based models focus on geometry-dispersion features (release height, stability, distance) with radionuclide identity being secondary, while TabNet distributes attention more widely across features.

Conclusion: An interpolation-assisted ML framework can yield superior dose predictions in safety-critical plume scenarios, with XGBoost as the leading model and distinct interpretability profiles across model families. A user-friendly GUI facilitates transparent scenario evaluation and comparison with physics-based references.

Abstract: Despite the success of machine learning (ML) in surrogate modeling, its use in radiation dose assessment is limited by safety-critical constraints, scarce training-ready data, and challenges in selecting suitable architectures for physics-dominated systems. Within this context, rapid and accurate plume shine dose estimation serves as a practical test case, as it is critical for nuclear facility safety assessment and radiological emergency response, while conventional photon-transport-based calculations remain computationally expensive. In this work, an interpolation-assisted ML framework was developed using discrete dose datasets generated with the pyDOSEIA suite for 17 gamma-emitting radionuclides across varying downwind distances, release heights, and atmospheric stability categories. The datasets were augmented using shape-preserving interpolation to construct dense, high-resolution training data. Two tree-based ML models (Random Forest and XGBoost) and one deep learning (DL) model (TabNet) were evaluated to examine predictive performance and sensitivity to dataset resolution. All models showed higher prediction accuracy with the interpolated high-resolution dataset than with the discrete data; however, XGBoost consistently achieved the highest accuracy. Interpretability analysis using permutation importance (tree-based models) and attention-based feature attribution (TabNet) revealed that performance differences stem from how the models utilize input features. Tree-based models focus mainly on dominant geometry-dispersion features (release height, stability category, and downwind distance), treating radionuclide identity as a secondary input, whereas TabNet distributes attention more broadly across multiple variables. For practical deployment, a web-based GUI was developed for interactive scenario evaluation and transparent comparison with photon-transport reference calculations.

</details>


### [323] [ISO-Bench: Can Coding Agents Optimize Real-World Inference Workloads?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19594)
*Ayush Nangia,Shikhar Mishra,Aman Gokrani,Paras Chopra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ISO-Bench is a benchmark for coding agents to optimize real-world inference tasks, drawn from vLLM and SGLang, with 54 tasks requiring optimization patches evaluated against expert solutions, using combined hard (execution) and soft (LLM) metrics; results show no single agent dominates, bottleneck identification often precedes valid solutions, and scaffolding significantly affects performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for robust, realistic evaluation of coding agents' ability to optimize inference-serving code, beyond runtime metrics that can be gamed; bridging bottleneck discovery with implementable fixes in real-world frameworks.

Method: Construct ISO-Bench from 54 merged PR tasks in vLLM and SGLang; each task provides a codebase and bottleneck description; agents generate optimization patches; patches are evaluated against expert human solutions using both execution-based metrics and LLM-based assessments; compare performance of closed-source and open-source coding agents across diverse codebases; analyze impact of model and scaffolding.

Result: Hard (execution-based) and soft (LLM-based) metrics are both necessary for complete evaluation; no single agent dominates across codebases; agents often correctly identify bottlenecks but fail to produce working solutions; identical models with different scaffolding show substantial performance differences.

Conclusion: ISO-Bench highlights the importance of combining execution and reasoning metrics for realistic benchmarking of coding agents, and reveals that scaffolding plays a crucial role alongside the underlying model in agent performance; this has implications for benchmark design and agent development.

Abstract: We introduce ISO-Bench, a benchmark for coding agents to test their capabilities on real-world inference optimization tasks. These tasks were taken from vLLM and SGLang, two of the most popular LLM serving frameworks. Each task provides an agent with a codebase and bottleneck description, whereby the agent must produce an optimization patch evaluated against expert human solutions. We curated 54 tasks from merged pull requests with measurable performance improvements. While existing benchmarks heavily use runtime-based metrics, such approaches can be gamed to pass tests without capturing the actual intent of the code changes. Therefore, we combine both hard (execution-based) and soft (LLM-based) metrics to show that both are necessary for complete evaluation. While evaluating both closed and open-source coding agents, we find no single agent dominates across codebases. Surprisingly, agents often identify correct bottlenecks but fail to execute working solutions. We also show that agents with identical underlying models differ substantially, suggesting scaffolding is as important as the model.

</details>


### [324] [Variational Inference for Bayesian MIDAS Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19610)
*Luigi Simeone*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Coordinate Ascent Variational Inference (CAVI) method for Bayesian MIDAS regression with linear weight parameterizations, exploiting a normalization constraint to create a bilinear structure. Closed-form updates yield fast, near-Gibbs posterior means, with substantial speedups over Gibbs and ADVI, but mean-field underdispersion for coefficients. Empirical volatility forecasting shows practical speedups (monthly <10 ms) and good weight calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient Bayesian inference for MIDAS models with a bilinear coupling between impact coefficients and weighting function parameters, where generic HMC is unreliable and naive VI may be biased or slow. The goal is exact-structured variational updates leveraging conditional conjugacy to achieve accurate, fast inference.

Method: Develop a CAVI algorithm for Bayesian MIDAS with linear weight parameterizations and a normalization constraint separating coefficients from weight parameters. Exploit bilinear structure to retain conditional conjugacy; derive closed-form updates: Gaussian for regression coefficients and weight parameters, Inverse-Gamma for error variance; propagate uncertainty via second moments across blocks.

Result: CAVI achieves posterior means nearly identical to a block Gibbs sampler with speedups ranging from 107x to 1,772x across 21 data-generating configurations with up to 50 predictors. ADVI is biased (large) and slower. Weight parameters exhibit good calibration (>92% coverage); coefficient intervals show underdispersion (coverage declines from 89% to 55% as predictors increase). In an empirical real-world application to realized volatility forecasting on S&P 500, CAVI and Gibbs yield virtually identical point forecasts, with monthly estimation under 10 milliseconds.

Conclusion: Model-specific CAVI provides fast, accurate Bayesian inference for Bayesian MIDAS with linear weights, outperforming generic VI in calibration and speed. The mean-field nature introduces underdispersion for coefficients, suggesting structured VI or alternative priors to improve interval calibration; the approach scales well and is applicable to real-time forecasting.

Abstract: We develop a Coordinate Ascent Variational Inference (CAVI) algorithm for Bayesian Mixed Data Sampling (MIDAS) regression with linear weight parameterizations. The model separates impact coeffcients from weighting function parameters through a normalization constraint, creating a bilinear structure that renders generic Hamiltonian Monte Carlo samplers unreliable while preserving conditional conjugacy exploitable by CAVI. Each variational update admits a closed-form solution: Gaussian for regression coefficients and weight parameters, Inverse-Gamma for the error variance. The algorithm propagates uncertainty across blocks through second moments, distinguishing it from naive plug-in approximations. In a Monte Carlo study spanning 21 data-generating configurations with up to 50 predictors, CAVI produces posterior means nearly identical to a block Gibbs sampler benchmark while achieving speedups of 107x to 1,772x (Table 9). Generic automatic differentiation VI (ADVI), by contrast, produces bias 714 times larger while being orders of magnitude slower, confirming the value of model-specific derivations. Weight function parameters maintain excellent calibration (coverage above 92%) across all configurations. Impact coefficient credible intervals exhibit the underdispersion characteristic of mean-field approximations, with coverage declining from 89% to 55% as the number of predictors grows a documented trade-off between speed and interval calibration that structured variational methods can address. An empirical application to realized volatility forecasting on S&P 500 daily returns cofirms that CAVI and Gibbs sampling yield virtually identical point forecasts, with CAVI completing each monthly estimation in under 10 milliseconds.

</details>


### [325] [Is Your Diffusion Sampler Actually Correct? A Sampler-Centric Evaluation of Discrete Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19619)
*Luhan Tang,Longxuan Yu,Shaorong Zhang,Greg Ver Steeg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A sampler-centric oracle framework shows few-step discrete diffusion samplers are not distributionally correct even with perfect denoisers; standard metrics can mislead about sampling quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Disentangle sampler-induced error from denoiser approximation in discrete diffusion LMs to enable fair evaluation of generation quality.

Method: Replace learned denoisers with the exact Hidden Markov Model posterior derived from a ground-truth Markov chain; assess distributional correctness of samplers; analyze transition-level mismatches as a function of step count; compare common metrics (NLL, perplexity, MAUVE) to sampling fidelity.

Result: Discrepancies between the sampler and the target distribution persist for few steps under an oracle denoiser; transition-level mismatch decreases only as the number of steps grows toward the sequence length; improvements in standard metrics do not imply correct sampling.

Conclusion: Sampler-centric evaluation is needed for dLLMs; rely less on conventional metrics alone; longer or more accurate sampling procedures are required to achieve distributional correctness; code is provided for reproducibility.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion language models (dLLMs) provide a fast and flexible alternative to autoregressive models (ARMs) via iterative denoising with parallel updates. However, their evaluation is challenging: existing metrics conflate denoiser approximation error with sampler-induced error from the sampling dynamics, a problem that does not arise for ARMs whose autoregressive sampling exactly reflects the learned probability model. We introduce a sampler-centric oracle framework that replaces learned denoisers with an exact Hidden Markov Model posterior derived from a ground-truth Markov chain, isolating sampler-induced error in a controlled setting. We show that few-step discrete diffusion samplers are not distributionally correct even under an oracle denoiser, with transition-level mismatch that vanishes only as the number of steps approaches the sequence length. Moreover, improvements in negative log-likelihood, generative perplexity, or MAUVE do not imply correct sampling. Code is available at https://luhantang.github.io/dllm_sampler

</details>


### [326] [VecFormer: Towards Efficient and Generalizable Graph Transformer with Graph Token Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19622)
*Jingbo Zhou,Jun Xia,Siyuan Li,Yunfan Liu,Wenjun Wang,Yufei Huang,Changxi Chi,Mutian Hong,Zhuoli Ouyang,Shu Wang,Zhongqi Wang,Xingyu Wu,Chang Yu,Stan Z. Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VecFormer is a two-stage, vector-quantized graph transformer for efficient, generalizable node classification. It uses dual codebooks to reconstruct node features and structure, then performs attention at the Graph Token level on a cross codebook, reducing complexity and enhancing OOD performance; empirical results show improved speed and accuracy across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address exponential complexity of node-level attention in Graph Transformers and limited generalization in out-of-distribution (OOD) settings. Seek scalable, flexible representations via graph codes.

Method: Two-stage training: Stage 1 learns Graph Codes by reconstructing node features and graph structure using two codebooks. Stage 2 performs attention over Graph Tokens derived from cross codebook, enabling efficient global reasoning with reduced compute.

Result: Empirical experiments show VecFormer outperforms existing Graph Transformer in both performance and speed across datasets of various sizes.

Conclusion: VecFormer offers a scalable, generalizable framework for node classification that mitigates attention bottlenecks through vector-quantized graph codes and graph-token attention.

Abstract: Graph Transformer has demonstrated impressive capabilities in the field of graph representation learning. However, existing approaches face two critical challenges: (1) most models suffer from exponentially increasing computational complexity, making it difficult to scale to large graphs; (2) attention mechanisms based on node-level operations limit the flexibility of the model and result in poor generalization performance in out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios. To address these issues, we propose \textbf{VecFormer} (the \textbf{Vec}tor Quantized Graph Trans\textbf{former}), an efficient and highly generalizable model for node classification, particularly under OOD settings. VecFormer adopts a two-stage training paradigm. In the first stage, two codebooks are used to reconstruct the node features and the graph structure, aiming to learn the rich semantic \texttt{Graph Codes}. In the second stage, attention mechanisms are performed at the \texttt{Graph Token} level based on the transformed cross codebook, reducing computational complexity while enhancing the model's generalization capability. Extensive experiments on datasets of various sizes demonstrate that VecFormer outperforms the existing Graph Transformer in both performance and speed.

</details>


### [327] [Compositional Planning with Jumpy World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19634)
*Jesse Farebrother,Matteo Pirotta,Andrea Tirinzoni,Marc G. Bellemare,Alessandro Lazaric,Ahmed Touati*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes jumpy world models for multi-step dynamics to enable compositional planning with temporally extended policies, improving long-horizon performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Plan with temporal abstractions (pre-trained policies) to tackle complex tasks and reduce errors from long-horizon predictions; extend geometric policy composition via off-policy multi-step state occupancy models and alignment across timescales.

Method: Train jumpy world models to predict state occupancies induced by policies across timescales; introduce a consistency objective aligning multi-timescale predictions; use these predictions to evaluate arbitrary policy sequences.

Result: Zero-shot performance improved across many base policies on manipulation and navigation tasks; ~200% relative improvement over primitive-action planning on long-horizon tasks.

Conclusion: Jumpy world models with consistency objective enable effective compositional planning with temporal abstractions; improves long-horizon prediction quality and planning efficacy.

Abstract: The ability to plan with temporal abstractions is central to intelligent decision-making. Rather than reasoning over primitive actions, we study agents that compose pre-trained policies as temporally extended actions, enabling solutions to complex tasks that no constituent alone can solve. Such compositional planning remains elusive as compounding errors in long-horizon predictions make it challenging to estimate the visitation distribution induced by sequencing policies. Motivated by the geometric policy composition framework introduced in arXiv:2206.08736, we address these challenges by learning predictive models of multi-step dynamics -- so-called jumpy world models -- that capture state occupancies induced by pre-trained policies across multiple timescales in an off-policy manner. Building on Temporal Difference Flows (arXiv:2503.09817), we enhance these models with a novel consistency objective that aligns predictions across timescales, improving long-horizon predictive accuracy. We further demonstrate how to combine these generative predictions to estimate the value of executing arbitrary sequences of policies over varying timescales. Empirically, we find that compositional planning with jumpy world models significantly improves zero-shot performance across a wide range of base policies on challenging manipulation and navigation tasks, yielding, on average, a 200% relative improvement over planning with primitive actions on long-horizon tasks.

</details>


### [328] [Evaluating the Impact of Data Anonymization on Image Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19641)
*Marvin Chen,Manuel Eberhardinger,Johannes Maucher*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An evaluation framework to quantify how anonymization affects CBIR; finds a retrieval bias toward models trained on non-anonymized data; recommends privacy-conscious training strategies for CBIR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GDPR and privacy concerns drive anonymization of visual data; CBIR performance can degrade due to anonymization; gap in systematic evaluation; real-world use in DOKIQ project with police agency.

Method: Framework compares retrieval before/after anonymization; experiments across 3 anonymization methods, 4 degrees, 4 training strategies, using DINOv2 backbone; datasets include two public datasets and internal DOKIQ data.

Result: Models trained on original data yield retrievals most similar to non-anonymized baselines after anonymization; anonymization introduces bias and performance shifts; effects depend on method/degree/training strategy.

Conclusion: Provides actionable guidance for privacy-compliant CBIR; suggests choosing training strategies and anonymization settings to preserve retrieval quality; highlights need for privacy-aware design in CBIR systems.

Abstract: With the growing importance of privacy regulations such as the General Data Protection Regulation, anonymizing visual data is becoming increasingly relevant across institutions. However, anonymization can negatively affect the performance of Computer Vision systems that rely on visual features, such as Content-Based Image Retrieval (CBIR). Despite this, the impact of anonymization on CBIR has not been systematically studied. This work addresses this gap, motivated by the DOKIQ project, an artificial intelligence-based system for document verification actively used by the State Criminal Police Office Baden-Württemberg. We propose a simple evaluation framework: retrieval results after anonymization should match those obtained before anonymization as closely as possible. To this end, we systematically assess the impact of anonymization using two public datasets and the internal DOKIQ dataset. Our experiments span three anonymization methods, four anonymization degrees, and four training strategies, all based on the state of the art backbone Self-Distillation with No Labels (DINO)v2. Our results reveal a pronounced retrieval bias in favor of models trained on original data, which produce the most similar retrievals after anonymization. The findings of this paper offer practical insights for developing privacy-compliant CBIR systems while preserving performance.

</details>


### [329] [Spectral Phase Encoding for Quantum Kernel Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19644)
*Pablo Herrero Gómez,Antonio Jimeno Morenilla,David Muñoz-Hernández,Higinio Mora Mora*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Summary generation failed


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable

Method: Method extraction failed

Result: Result analysis unavailable

Conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

Abstract: Quantum kernel methods are promising for near-term quantum ma- chine learning, yet their behavior under data corruption remains insuf- ficiently understood. We analyze how quantum feature constructions degrade under controlled additive noise. We introduce Spectral Phase Encoding (SPE), a hybrid construc- tion combining a discrete Fourier transform (DFT) front-end with a diagonal phase-only embedding aligned with the geometry of diagonal quantum maps. Within a unified framework, we compare QK-DFT against alternative quantum variants (QK-PCA, QK-RP) and classi- cal SVM baselines under identical clean-data hyperparameter selection, quantifying robustness via dataset fixed-effects regression with wild cluster bootstrap inference across heterogeneous real-world datasets. Across the quantum family, DFT-based preprocessing yields the smallest degradation rate as noise increases, with statistically sup- ported slope differences relative to PCA and RP. Compared to classical baselines, QK-DFT shows degradation comparable to linear SVM and more stable than RBF SVM under matched tuning. Hardware exper- iments confirm that SPE remains executable and numerically stable for overlap estimation. These results indicate that robustness in quan- tum kernels depends critically on structure-aligned preprocessing and its interaction with diagonal embeddings, supporting a robustness-first perspective for NISQ-era quantum machine learning.

</details>


### [330] [NEXUS: A compact neural architecture for high-resolution spatiotemporal air quality forecasting in Delhi National Capital Region](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19654)
*Rampunit Kumar,Aditya Maheshwari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NEXUS delivers high-accuracy, efficient spatiotemporal forecasts of CO, NO, and SO2 for Delhi-NCR using 18k+ parameters, outperforming larger baselines and enabling real-time deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urban air pollution in megacities, especially Delhi-NCR, necessitates reliable and efficient forecasting to inform health protection and policy.

Method: NEXUS architecture with patch embedding, low-rank projections, adaptive fusion; 4-year atmospheric data across 16 grids; multi-pollutant forecasting; ablation analyses.

Result: R^2 >0.94 (CO), >0.91 (NO), >0.95 (SO2); parameter count 18,748 vs larger baselines (SCINet 35,552; Autoformer 68,704; FEDformer 298,080); high computational efficiency; insight into diurnal/seasonal patterns, meteorological thresholds, wind dispersion, spatial heterogeneity; component-wise ablations.

Conclusion: NEXUS provides accurate, compact spatiotemporal forecasts for urban air quality with potential for real-time deployment and extension to other regions and pollutants.

Abstract: Urban air pollution in megacities poses critical public health challenges, particularly in Delhi National Capital Region (NCR) where severe degradation affects millions. We present NEXUS (Neural Extraction and Unified Spatiotemporal) architecture for forecasting carbon monoxide, nitrogen oxide, and sulfur dioxide. Working with four years (2018--2021) of atmospheric data across sixteen spatial grids, NEXUS achieves R$^2$ exceeding 0.94 for CO, 0.91 for NO, and 0.95 for SO$_2$ using merely 18,748 parameters -- substantially fewer than SCINet (35,552), Autoformer (68,704), and FEDformer (298,080). The architecture integrates patch embedding, low-rank projections, and adaptive fusion mechanisms to decode complex atmospheric chemistry patterns. Our investigation uncovers distinct diurnal rhythms and pronounced seasonal variations, with winter months experiencing severe pollution episodes driven by temperature inversions and agricultural biomass burning. Analysis identifies critical meteorological thresholds, quantifies wind field impacts on pollutant dispersion, and maps spatial heterogeneity across the region. Extensive ablation experiments demonstrate each architectural component's role. NEXUS delivers superior predictive performance with remarkable computational efficiency, enabling real-time deployment for air quality monitoring systems.

</details>


### [331] [Representation Stability in a Minimal Continual Learning Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19655)
*Vishnu Subramanian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A minimal, persistent-state continual learner reveals emergent stability-plasticity dynamics in representation without heavy architectures or replay, transitioning from plastic to stable regimes under consistent input; perturbations induce bounded disruption with recovery, establishing a baseline for representational accumulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how internal representations evolve in continual learning independent of model architecture, optimization tricks, or replay mechanisms, by focusing on representational dynamics in a simple, persistent-state agent.

Method: A minimal agent maintains a persistent state vector updated incrementally with textual data; representational change measured by cosine similarity of successive normalized state vectors; defines a temporal stability metric; experiments across eight executions, plus a semantic perturbation to test robustness.

Result: Observed a plastic-to-stable transition under consistent input; perturbations cause bounded, reversible decreases in similarity; demonstrates that stable plasticity tradeoffs can emerge without regularization, replay, or complex architectures; provides a transparent empirical baseline for representational accumulation.

Conclusion: The study shows meaningful stability-plasticity tradeoffs can arise in a minimal continual learning setup, offering a baseline for future work on representational dynamics and guiding design choices that prioritize stable accumulation without heavy machinery.

Abstract: Continual learning systems are increasingly deployed in environments where retraining or reset is infeasible, yet many approaches emphasize task performance rather than the evolution of internal representations over time. In this work, we study a minimal continual learning agent designed to isolate representational dynamics from architectural complexity and optimization objectives. The agent maintains a persistent state vector across executions and incrementally updates it as new textual data is introduced. We quantify representational change using cosine similarity between successive normalized state vectors and define a stability metric over time intervals. Longitudinal experiments across eight executions reveal a transition from an initial plastic regime to a stable representational regime under consistent input. A deliberately introduced semantic perturbation produces a bounded decrease in similarity, followed by recovery and restabilization under subsequent coherent input. These results demonstrate that meaningful stability plasticity tradeoffs can emerge in a minimal, stateful learning system without explicit regularization, replay, or architectural complexity. The work establishes a transparent empirical baseline for studying representational accumulation and adaptation in continual learning systems.

</details>


### [332] [PaReGTA: An LLM-based EHR Data Encoding Approach to Capture Temporal Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19661)
*Kihyuk Yoon,Lingchao Mao,Catherine Chong,Todd J. Schwedt,Chia-Chun Chiang,Jing Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PaReGTA uses LLM-driven templated-text encoding of longitudinal EHR with explicit temporal cues, followed by lightweight contrastive fine-tuning of a sentence-embedding model and hybrid temporal pooling to produce fixed-dimensional patient representations; PaReGTA-RSS provides interpretability. In 39k migraine patients, it outperforms sparse baselines and handles unstable deep sequential models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Temporal information in structured EHRs is often lost in sparse representations; need data-efficient, model-agnostic methods that preserve temporal cues and provide interpretability.

Method: Convert EHR events to visit-level templated text with temporal cues; contrastive fine-tuning of a sentence-embedding model to learn domain-adapted visit embeddings; aggregate with hybrid temporal pooling capturing recency and informative visits; PaReGTA-RSS for interpretability by recomputing representations after removal of targeted factors and analyzing shifts via a ML model; uses pre-trained LLM, not from-scratch training.

Result: Improved migraine type classification compared to sparse baselines; deep sequential models unstable in the cohort; dataset: 39,088 migraine patients from All of Us.

Conclusion: PaReGTA offers a data-efficient, model-agnostic framework for encoding temporal information in EHRs, scalable to future specialized embeddings; PaReGTA-RSS provides factor-level interpretability.

Abstract: Temporal information in structured electronic health records (EHRs) is often lost in sparse one-hot or count-based representations, while sequence models can be costly and data-hungry. We propose PaReGTA, an LLM-based encoding framework that (i) converts longitudinal EHR events into visit-level templated text with explicit temporal cues, (ii) learns domain-adapted visit embeddings via lightweight contrastive fine-tuning of a sentence-embedding model, and (iii) aggregates visit embeddings into a fixed-dimensional patient representation using hybrid temporal pooling that captures both recency and globally informative visits. Because PaReGTA does not require training from scratch but instead utilizes a pre-trained LLM, it can perform well even in data-limited cohorts. Furthermore, PaReGTA is model-agnostic and can benefit from future EHR-specialized sentence-embedding models. For interpretability, we introduce PaReGTA-RSS (Representation Shift Score), which quantifies clinically defined factor importance by recomputing representations after targeted factor removal and projecting representation shifts through a machine learning model. On 39,088 migraine patients from the All of Us Research Program, PaReGTA outperforms sparse baselines for migraine type classification while deep sequential models were unstable in our cohort.

</details>


### [333] [PerturbDiff: Functional Diffusion for Single-Cell Perturbation Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19685)
*Xinyu Yuan,Xixian Liu,Ya Shi Zhang,Zuobai Zhang,Hongyu Guo,Jian Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PerturbDiff predicts perturbation responses by modeling distributions of cell states rather than individual cells, using Hilbert-space embeddings and a diffusion process over distributions to capture latent variability across unobserved factors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-cell perturbation experiments are destructive; there is a need to predict population-level responses accounting for latent factors (microenvironment, batch effects) that create a manifold of possible distributions for the same conditions.

Method: Embed cell-state distributions into a Hilbert space and perform a diffusion-based generative process directly over these distributions, enabling modeling of distribution shifts across latent factors and unobserved variability.

Result: PerturbDiff achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard single-cell response prediction benchmarks and shows substantially better generalization to unseen perturbations compared to distribution-matching baselines.

Conclusion: Shifting focus from per-cell predictions to distribution-level generative modeling enables robust approximation of perturbation responses under unobserved variability; the approach is scalable to multiple conditions and supports improved generalization to new perturbations.

Abstract: Building Virtual Cells that can accurately simulate cellular responses to perturbations is a long-standing goal in systems biology. A fundamental challenge is that high-throughput single-cell sequencing is destructive: the same cell cannot be observed both before and after a perturbation. Thus, perturbation prediction requires mapping unpaired control and perturbed populations. Existing models address this by learning maps between distributions, but typically assume a single fixed response distribution when conditioned on observed cellular context (e.g., cell type) and the perturbation type. In reality, responses vary systematically due to unobservable latent factors such as microenvironmental fluctuations and complex batch effects, forming a manifold of possible distributions for the same observed conditions. To account for this variability, we introduce PerturbDiff, which shifts modeling from individual cells to entire distributions. By embedding distributions as points in a Hilbert space, we define a diffusion-based generative process operating directly over probability distributions. This allows PerturbDiff to capture population-level response shifts across hidden factors. Benchmarks on established datasets show that PerturbDiff achieves state-of-the-art performance in single-cell response prediction and generalizes substantially better to unseen perturbations. See our project page (https://katarinayuan.github.io/PerturbDiff-ProjectPage/), where code and data will be made publicly available (https://github.com/DeepGraphLearning/PerturbDiff).

</details>


### [334] [Understanding the Curse of Unrolling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19733)
*Sheheryar Mehmood,Florian Knoll,Peter Ochs*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Non-asymptotic analysis reveals the origin of the curse of unrolling in differentiating through iterative algorithms; early truncation of derivative iterations mitigates the mismatch with the true Jacobian and reduces memory; warm-starting in bilevel optimization naturally induces implicit truncation, offering a practical remedy; supported by numerical experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why derivative iterates can diverge from the true Jacobian (the curse of unrolling) and provide practical strategies to obtain accurate Jacobians with lower memory, relevant to hyperparameter optimization and meta-learning.

Method: Develop non-asymptotic bounds and an analysis of the derivative update dynamics for unrolled solvers; identify algorithmic factors (e.g., number of unrolled steps, step sizes, initialization) that govern the deviation; propose early truncation of derivative computation and relate warm-starting to implicit truncation; validate with numerical experiments on representative examples.

Result: Key results include: (i) demonstration that truncating derivative iterations reduces the discrepancy between the computed and true Jacobians and lowers memory usage; (ii) warm-starting in bilevel optimization yields an implicit form of truncation that acts as a practical remedy; (iii) numerical experiments corroborate the theoretical insights across representative tasks.

Conclusion: Early truncation is an effective and practical strategy to mitigate the curse of unrolling and manage memory in unrolled differentiation; warm-starting provides a natural, implementable mechanism to induce implicit truncation; the work informs design choices in hyperparameter optimization and meta-learning pipelines.

Abstract: Algorithm unrolling is ubiquitous in machine learning, particularly in hyperparameter optimization and meta-learning, where Jacobians of solution mappings are computed by differentiating through iterative algorithms. Although unrolling is known to yield asymptotically correct Jacobians under suitable conditions, recent work has shown that the derivative iterates may initially diverge from the true Jacobian, a phenomenon known as the curse of unrolling. In this work, we provide a non-asymptotic analysis that explains the origin of this behavior and identifies the algorithmic factors that govern it. We show that truncating early iterations of the derivative computation mitigates the curse while simultaneously reducing memory requirements. Finally, we demonstrate that warm-starting in bilevel optimization naturally induces an implicit form of truncation, providing a practical remedy. Our theoretical findings are supported by numerical experiments on representative examples.

</details>


### [335] [The Confusion is Real: GRAPHIC - A Network Science Approach to Confusion Matrices in Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19770)
*Johanna S. Fröhlich,Bastian Heinlein,Jan U. Claar,Hans Rosenberger,Vasileios Belagiannis,Ralf R. Müller*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRAPHIC is an architecture-agnostic method that analyzes class-level confusion dynamics by constructing directed graphs from confusion matrices of intermediate-layer linear classifiers, enabling visualization and quantification of learning across epochs and layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of systematic, scalable visualization of inter-class confusion and its evolution during training, providing interpretable insights into class separability, dataset issues, and architectural behavior.

Method: Compute confusion matrices from intermediate-layer features using linear classifiers; treat these matrices as adjacency matrices of directed graphs; apply network-science metrics and visualizations to study learning dynamics across training epochs and layers.

Result: Demonstrates insights into linear class separability, reveals dataset issues and architectural behaviors; identifies semantic confusions such as similarities between 'flatfish' and 'man', and labeling ambiguities validated by human study; code released.

Conclusion: GRAPHIC offers a new perspective on how neural networks learn by exposing real confusions and learning dynamics, with an architecture-agnostic framework for class-level analysis.

Abstract: Explainable artificial intelligence has emerged as a promising field of research to address reliability concerns in artificial intelligence. Despite significant progress in explainable artificial intelligence, few methods provide a systematic way to visualize and understand how classes are confused and how their relationships evolve as training progresses. In this work, we present GRAPHIC, an architecture-agnostic approach that analyzes neural networks on a class level. It leverages confusion matrices derived from intermediate layers using linear classifiers. We interpret these as adjacency matrices of directed graphs, allowing tools from network science to visualize and quantify learning dynamics across training epochs and intermediate layers. GRAPHIC provides insights into linear class separability, dataset issues, and architectural behavior, revealing, for example, similarities between flatfish and man and labeling ambiguities validated in a human study. In summary, by uncovering real confusions, GRAPHIC offers new perspectives on how neural networks learn. The code is available at https://github.com/Johanna-S-Froehlich/GRAPHIC.

</details>


### [336] [Addressing Instrument-Outcome Confounding in Mendelian Randomization through Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19782)
*Shimeng Huang,Matthew Robinson,Francesco Locatello*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A cross-environment representation learning approach to identify latent exogenous genetic instruments for Mendelian Randomization with theoretical identifiability guarantees, validated by simulations and semi-synthetic experiments on All of Us data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unobserved confounding in MR arises from population stratification and assortative mating. With more multi-environment data, one can leverage cross-environment signals to recover exogenous instruments.

Method: Learn representations that are invariant across environments to extract latent exogenous instrument components. Provide theoretical identifiability guarantees under various mixing mechanisms, and validate via simulations and semi-synthetic experiments using All of Us data.

Result: Simulations and semi-synthetic experiments demonstrate effective recovery of latent instruments and robust MR estimates across environments.

Conclusion: Cross-environment invariance learning offers a viable route to robust MR by recovering latent exogenous instruments; the framework is supported by theory and empirical validation and can generalize to complex genetic architectures with multi-environment data.

Abstract: Mendelian Randomization (MR) is a prominent observational epidemiological research method designed to address unobserved confounding when estimating causal effects. However, core assumptions -- particularly the independence between instruments and unobserved confounders -- are often violated due to population stratification or assortative mating. Leveraging the increasing availability of multi-environment data, we propose a representation learning framework that exploits cross-environment invariance to recover latent exogenous components of genetic instruments. We provide theoretical guarantees for identifying these latent instruments under various mixing mechanisms and demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through simulations and semi-synthetic experiments using data from the All of Us Research Hub.

</details>


### [337] [Unsupervised Anomaly Detection in NSL-KDD Using $β$-VAE: A Latent Space and Reconstruction Error Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19785)
*Dylan Baptiste,Ramla Saddem,Alexandre Philippot,François Foyer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unsupervised anomaly detection for network traffic using beta-VAEs on NSL-KDD; latent-space distance to training projections can outperform reconstruction error for classification tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As OT integrates with IT, robust intrusion detection in unlabeled, evolving traffic becomes critical. The study investigates unsupervised anomaly scoring via β-VAE latent representations and reconstruction error on NSL-KDD.

Method: Train a β-VAE on network traffic data (likely unlabeled). Compare two anomaly scores: (1) distance in latent space from test samples to training projections, (2) reconstruction error. Evaluate on NSL-KDD, focusing on unsupervised classification tasks.

Result: Latent-space exploitation provides effective classification performance and can outperform reconstruction-based scoring in certain settings; reconstruction error remains a viable baseline, with trade-offs in sensitivity and thresholding.

Conclusion: Latent-space based anomaly scoring with β-VAE is a promising direction for unsupervised IDS, offering advantages in capturing data structure; future work should explore hybrid scoring, threshold selection, and generalization to varied datasets.

Abstract: As Operational Technology increasingly integrates with Information Technology, the need for Intrusion Detection Systems becomes more important. This paper explores an unsupervised approach to anomaly detection in network traffic using $β$-Variational Autoencoders on the NSL-KDD dataset. We investigate two methods: leveraging the latent space structure by measuring distances from test samples to the training data projections, and using the reconstruction error as a conventional anomaly detection metric. By comparing these approaches, we provide insights into their respective advantages and limitations in an unsupervised setting. Experimental results highlight the effectiveness of latent space exploitation for classification tasks.

</details>


### [338] [Bayesian Meta-Learning with Expert Feedback for Task-Shift Adaptation through Causal Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19788)
*Lotta Mäkinen,Jorge Loría,Samuel Kaski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A causally-aware Bayesian meta-learning framework that conditions task priors on latent causal task embeddings and expert-provided causal similarity judgments to reduce negative transfer in out-of-distribution transfer tasks; it provides theoretical bounds on prior mismatch and shows improved OOD adaptation in simulations and cross-disease clinical transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Meta-learning often fails when target tasks are out-of-distribution due to spurious correlations and distribution shift, leading to negative transfer. There is a need to leverage mechanistic (causal) similarity and operate with limited target-task data and noisy expert judgments in real deployments.

Method: Introduce a Bayesian meta-learning approach that conditions task-specific priors on precomputed latent causal embeddings. Use pairwise causal similarity judgments (potentially noisy) between source and target tasks to guide transfer. Provide theoretical analysis showing that conditioning on causal embeddings controls prior mismatch and mitigates negative transfer under task shift.

Result: Theoretically, prior-mismatch is controlled, reducing negative transfer under task shift. Empirically, the method achieves reduced negative transfer and better out-of-distribution adaptation in controlled simulations and a large-scale real-world clinical setting for cross-disease transfer, with causal embeddings aligning with underlying clinical mechanisms.

Conclusion: Causally-informed priors enable more robust meta-learning across task distributions by aligning transfer with mechanistic similarity. The framework remains effective under limited target data and noisy similarity judgments, with promising implications for real-world deployments; future work could address embedding quality, judgment noise, and broader domain validation.

Abstract: Meta-learning methods perform well on new within-distribution tasks but often fail when adapting to out-of-distribution target tasks, where transfer from source tasks can induce negative transfer. We propose a causally-aware Bayesian meta-learning method, by conditioning task-specific priors on precomputed latent causal task embeddings, enabling transfer based on mechanistic similarity rather than spurious correlations. Our approach explicitly considers realistic deployment settings where access to target-task data is limited, and adaptation relies on noisy (expert-provided) pairwise judgments of causal similarity between source and target tasks. We provide a theoretical analysis showing that conditioning on causal embeddings controls prior mismatch and mitigates negative transfer under task shift. Empirically, we demonstrate reductions in negative transfer and improved out-of-distribution adaptation in both controlled simulations and a large-scale real-world clinical prediction setting for cross-disease transfer, where causal embeddings align with underlying clinical mechanisms.

</details>


### [339] [Stop Preaching and Start Practising Data Frugality for Responsible Development of AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19789)
*Sophia N. Wilson,Guðrún Fjóla Guðmundsdóttir,Andrew Millard,Raghavendra Selvan,Sebastian Mair*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This position paper argues for data frugality in AI, noting that ever-larger datasets yield diminishing returns and higher energy use, and presents energy and carbon estimates for ImageNet-1K. It demonstrates that coreset-based subset selection can substantially reduce training energy with minimal accuracy loss and can mitigate dataset bias, followed by actionable recommendations to move from rhetoric to concrete practice for responsible AI development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address environmental and efficiency concerns of large-scale data in AI and close the gap between advocating data frugality and actually implementing it in practice.

Method: Provide indicative energy and carbon emissions estimates for the downstream use of ImageNet-1K; conduct empirical evaluation of coreset-based subset selection on training energy and accuracy; assess impact on dataset bias; articulate actionable recommendations.

Result: Evidence that data frugality is practical and beneficial: substantial reductions in training energy with minimal accuracy loss; potential mitigation of dataset bias; energy and emissions estimates for a widely used benchmark.

Conclusion: Advocates moving data frugality from rhetorical preaching to concrete, actionable practices in AI development to enable responsible and sustainable AI.

Abstract: This position paper argues that the machine learning community must move from preaching to practising data frugality for responsible artificial intelligence (AI) development. For long, progress has been equated with ever-larger datasets, driving remarkable advances but now yielding increasingly diminishing performance gains alongside rising energy use and carbon emissions. While awareness of data frugal approaches has grown, their adoption has remained rhetorical, and data scaling continues to dominate development practice. We argue that this gap between preach and practice must be closed, as continued data scaling entails substantial and under-accounted environmental impacts. To ground our position, we provide indicative estimates of the energy use and carbon emissions associated with the downstream use of ImageNet-1K. We then present empirical evidence that data frugality is both practical and beneficial, demonstrating that coreset-based subset selection can substantially reduce training energy consumption with little loss in accuracy, while also mitigating dataset bias. Finally, we outline actionable recommendations for moving data frugality from rhetorical preach to concrete practice for responsible development of AI.

</details>


### [340] [Drift Localization using Conformal Predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19790)
*Fabian Hinder,Valerie Vaquet,Johannes Brinkrolf,Barbara Hammer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A conformal-prediction-based approach to drift localization addresses high-dimensional, low-signal drift detection, outperforming local tests, with empirical validation on modern image datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Concept drift monitoring requires reliable drift localization; existing local testing methods struggle in high-dimensional, low-signal regimes; there is a need for robust, distribution-free techniques.

Method: Proposes a drift localization framework based on conformal predictions; discusses shortcomings of common local testing approaches; evaluates on state-of-the-art image datasets.

Result: Empirical demonstrations show the proposed conformal approach yields effective drift localization on contemporary image data, mitigating failures of local tests in challenging settings.

Conclusion: Conformal prediction-based drift localization offers a promising alternative to local tests for concept drift in high-dimensional settings, with demonstrated efficacy on image benchmarks.

Abstract: Concept drift -- the change of the distribution over time -- poses significant challenges for learning systems and is of central interest for monitoring. Understanding drift is thus paramount, and drift localization -- determining which samples are affected by the drift -- is essential. While several approaches exist, most rely on local testing schemes, which tend to fail in high-dimensional, low-signal settings. In this work, we consider a fundamentally different approach based on conformal predictions. We discuss and show the shortcomings of common approaches and demonstrate the performance of our approach on state-of-the-art image datasets.

</details>


### [341] [Decision MetaMamba: Enhancing Selective SSM in Offline RL with Heterogeneous Sequence Mixing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19805)
*Wall Kim,Chaeyoung Song,Hanul Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A dense-layer sequence mixer (Decision MetaMamba) replaces Mamba's token mixer, preserving local information and all-channel interactions, yielding state-of-the-art offline RL performance with a compact model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mamba's selective mechanism can skip key steps in RL sequences, causing information loss. There is a need to preserve local sequence information and perform comprehensive channel-wise mixing before selective scanning.

Method: Replace Mamba's token mixer with a dense layer-based sequence mixer and adjust positional encoding to retain local information. Perform sequence mixing across all channels before the Mamba stage to prevent information loss from selective scanning and residual gating.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows state-of-the-art performance across diverse offline RL tasks, achieved with a smaller parameter footprint.

Conclusion: Decision MetaMamba provides an effective, efficient architecture for offline RL, with strong potential for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Mamba-based models have drawn much attention in offline RL. However, their selective mechanism often detrimental when key steps in RL sequences are omitted. To address these issues, we propose a simple yet effective structure, called Decision MetaMamba (DMM), which replaces Mamba's token mixer with a dense layer-based sequence mixer and modifies positional structure to preserve local information. By performing sequence mixing that considers all channels simultaneously before Mamba, DMM prevents information loss due to selective scanning and residual gating. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our DMM delivers the state-of-the-art performance across diverse RL tasks. Furthermore, DMM achieves these results with a compact parameter footprint, demonstrating strong potential for real-world applications.

</details>


### [342] [I Dropped a Neural Net](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19845)
*Hyunwoo Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A puzzle: reordering shuffled residual network layers using stability cues and a two-stage search (pairings then block order).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand whether layer ordering information can be recovered from unlabelled layers and a training dataset, highlighting the interplay between training dynamics (dynamic isometry) and network structure.

Method: Decompose into two subproblems: (1) pair input/output projections of 48 residual blocks (48! possibilities for pairings); (2) order the 48 blocks (48! possibilities). Use a signal based on negative diagonal structure of W_out W_in under dynamic isometry to identify correct pairings via diagonal dominance. For ordering, start from a rough proxy (delta-norm or ||W_out||_F) and perform hill-climbing to minimize MSE with the target sequence.

Result: Theoretical/proposal framework showing how one might tackle an intractable combinatorial search by exploiting training stability invariants and informed seeding, reducing the search space from (48!)^2 to a tractable optimization guided by diagonal/dominance properties and heuristic initialization.

Conclusion: Dynamic-isometry-like constraints create detectable structure in paired projections, enabling accurate pairing; ordering can be achieved via seeded optimization. The work provides a conceptual pathway for reverse-engineering layer order, with practical success depending on the presence of the assumed stability properties and sufficient training data.

Abstract: A recent Dwarkesh Patel podcast with John Collison and Elon Musk featured an interesting puzzle from Jane Street: they trained a neural net, shuffled all 96 layers, and asked to put them back in order.
  Given unlabelled layers of a Residual Network and its training dataset, we recover the exact ordering of the layers. The problem decomposes into pairing each block's input and output projections ($48!$ possibilities) and ordering the reassembled blocks ($48!$ possibilities), for a combined search space of $(48!)^2 \approx 10^{122}$, which is more than the atoms in the observable universe. We show that stability conditions during training like dynamic isometry leave the product $W_{\text{out}} W_{\text{in}}$ for correctly paired layers with a negative diagonal structure, allowing us to use diagonal dominance ratio as a signal for pairing. For ordering, we seed-initialize with a rough proxy such as delta-norm or $\|W_{\text{out}}\|_F$ then hill-climb to zero mean squared error.

</details>


### [343] [Generalized Random Direction Newton Algorithms for Stochastic Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19893)
*Soumen Pachal,Prashanth L. A.,Shalabh Bhatnagar,Avinash Achar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A family of generalized Hessian estimators for objective optimization using random direction stochastic approximation (RDSA) with noisy function measurements. Bias can be tuned by the number of function evaluations; estimators are asymptotically unbiased; includes asymptotic and non-asymptotic convergence results for stochastic Newton methods and numerical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimate second-order information (Hessian) in stochastic optimization when only noisy function evaluations are available. Reducing estimation bias and improving convergence of stochastic Newton methods are key goals.

Method: Develop a family of generalized Hessian estimators via RDSA that use varying numbers of noisy function measurements. Analyze how bias order depends on measurement count, prove asymptotic unbiasedness, and provide both asymptotic and non-asymptotic convergence analyses for stochastic Newton methods that employ these estimators. Validate findings with numerical experiments.

Result: Demonstrates that more function measurements yield lower-order bias in the Hessian estimators; establishes asymptotic unbiasedness; provides convergence guarantees for stochastic Newton methods using the estimators in both asymptotic and finite-sample regimes; numerical experiments support the theory.

Conclusion: A tunable family of Hessian estimators in RDSA enables a bias-variance trade-off controlled by the number of function evaluations. This improves stochastic Newton methods under noise and its theory is supported by numerical validation.

Abstract: We present a family of generalized Hessian estimators of the objective using random direction stochastic approximation (RDSA) by utilizing only noisy function measurements. The form of each estimator and the order of the bias depend on the number of function measurements. In particular, we demonstrate that estimators with more function measurements exhibit lower-order estimation bias. We show the asymptotic unbiasedness of the estimators. We also perform asymptotic and non-asymptotic convergence analyses for stochastic Newton methods that incorporate our generalized Hessian estimators. Finally, we perform numerical experiments to validate our theoretical findings.

</details>


### [344] [DSDR: Dual-Scale Diversity Regularization for Exploration in LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19895)
*Zhongwei Wan,Yun Shen,Zhihao Dou,Donghao Zhou,Yu Zhang,Xin Wang,Hui Shen,Jing Xiong,Chaofan Tao,Zixuan Zhong,Peizhou Huang,Mi Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DSDR introduces a dual-scale diversity regularization framework for RL with verifiers in LLM reasoning, combining global diversity across correct reasoning trajectories with local, length-invariant token-level entropy regularization on correct trajectories, coupled via a global-to-local allocation mechanism. The approach aims to sustain exploratory signals and prevent entropy collapse, with theoretical guarantees and empirical gains on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limited exploration and premature convergence to a few reasoning patterns in RLVR, and to address the insufficiency of conventional entropy regularization for longer, path-level diversity.

Method: Decompose diversity into global and local components. Globally maximize diversity among correct reasoning trajectories to explore distinct solution modes; locally apply length-invariant token-level entropy regularization restricted to correct trajectories to prevent entropy collapse within each mode while preserving correctness. Couple scales via a global-to-local allocation that emphasizes local regularization for more distinctive correct trajectories. Provide theoretical guarantees that regularization is bounded while preserving optimal correctness, and that the coupling rule yields informative learning signals in group-based optimization.

Result: Theoretical contributions show bounded regularization preserves optimal correctness and maintains informative signals; experiments on multiple reasoning benchmarks report consistent improvements in accuracy and pass@k, with code available.

Conclusion: Dual-scale diversity regularization is effective for deep exploration in RLVR, enabling better exploration of reasoning modes and sustained learning signals; the approach is supported by theory and validated empirically.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiers (RLVR) is a central paradigm for improving large language model (LLM) reasoning, yet existing methods often suffer from limited exploration. Policies tend to collapse onto a few reasoning patterns and prematurely stop deep exploration, while conventional entropy regularization introduces only local stochasticity and fails to induce meaningful path-level diversity, leading to weak and unstable learning signals in group-based policy optimization. We propose DSDR, a Dual-Scale Diversity Regularization reinforcement learning framework that decomposes diversity in LLM reasoning into global and coupling components. Globally, DSDR promotes diversity among correct reasoning trajectories to explore distinct solution modes. Locally, it applies a length-invariant, token-level entropy regularization restricted to correct trajectories, preventing entropy collapse within each mode while preserving correctness. The two scales are coupled through a global-to-local allocation mechanism that emphasizes local regularization for more distinctive correct trajectories. We provide theoretical support showing that DSDR preserves optimal correctness under bounded regularization, sustains informative learning signals in group-based optimization, and yields a principled global-to-local coupling rule. Experiments on multiple reasoning benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in accuracy and pass@k, highlighting the importance of dual-scale diversity for deep exploration in RLVR. Code is available at https://github.com/SUSTechBruce/DSDR.

</details>


### [345] [De novo molecular structure elucidation from mass spectra via flow matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19912)
*Ghaith Mqawass,Tuan Le,Fabian Theis,Djork-Arné Clevert*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MSFlow is a two-stage encoder-decoder flow-matching model for translating mass spectra into molecular structures, achieving state-of-the-art performance for small molecules (up to 45% translation) with up to 14× improvement over prior work; code is released for non-commercial use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The inverse problem of translating mass spectra into full molecular structures is ill-posed and under-defined, hindering biological insight and metabolite discovery. A robust, accurate structure elucidation pipeline is needed to unlock MS-based discovery across chemistry and biology.

Method: A two-stage approach: (1) a formula-restricted transformer encodes mass spectra into a continuous, chemically informative embedding space; (2) a decoder flow-matching model reconstructs molecules from spectral embeddings. The method includes information-preserving molecular descriptors for encoding spectra and uses a discrete flow-based decoder; ablation studies validate these choices.

Result: MSFlow achieves state-of-the-art performance on structure elucidation for small molecules, translating up to 45% of mass spectra to their molecular representations, with up to 14× improvement over the previous best. A trained model is publicly available on GitHub for non-commercial users.
