Conclusion: The combination of spectrum encoding via a formula-restricted transformer and a discrete flow-based decoder enables substantially improved structure elucidation, demonstrating strong performance gains and sharing resources for non-commercial use to foster adoption and further research.

Abstract: Mass spectrometry is a powerful and widely used tool for identifying molecular structures due to its sensitivity and ability to profile complex samples. However, translating spectra into full molecular structures is a difficult, under-defined inverse problem. Overcoming this problem is crucial for enabling biological insight, discovering new metabolites, and advancing chemical research across multiple fields. To this end, we develop MSFlow, a two-stage encoder-decoder flow-matching generative model that achieves state-of-the-art performance on the structure elucidation task for small molecules. In the first stage, we adopt a formula-restricted transformer model for encoding mass spectra into a continuous and chemically informative embedding space, while in the second stage, we train a decoder flow matching model to reconstruct molecules from latent embeddings of mass spectra. We present ablation studies demonstrating the importance of using information-preserving molecular descriptors for encoding mass spectra and motivate the use of our discrete flow-based decoder. Our rigorous evaluation demonstrates that MSFlow can accurately translate up to 45 percent of molecular mass spectra into their corresponding molecular representations - an improvement of up to fourteen-fold over the current state-of-the-art. A trained version of MSFlow is made publicly available on GitHub for non-commercial users.

</details>


### [346] [Fully Convolutional Spatiotemporal Learning for Microstructure Evolution Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19915)
*Michael Trimboli,Mohammed Alsubaie,Sirani M. Perera,Ke-Gang Wang,Xianqi Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Self-supervised fully convolutional spatiotemporal model for microstructure evolution that predicts grain growth and spinodal decomposition with high fidelity and lower compute than RNNs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Phase-field simulations are accurate but computationally expensive; a fast, scalable, data-driven surrogate that preserves physical dynamics and statistics is needed.

Method: Train a fully convolutional spatiotemporal network in a self-supervised manner on sequential frames from phase-field simulations of microstructure processes (e.g., grain growth, spinodal decomposition); learn short-term dynamics and long-term statistics; compare against recurrent architectures and test generalization to unseen domains/parameters.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art predictive performance compared with recurrent models and substantially reduces training and inference costs; accurately captures both local evolution and long-term statistical properties; generalizes to unseen spatiotemporal domains and parameter variations.

Conclusion: Establishes a robust baseline for spatiotemporal learning in materials science and provides a scalable, data-driven alternative for fast, reliable microstructure simulations.

Abstract: Understanding and predicting microstructure evolution is fundamental to materials science, as it governs the resulting properties and performance of materials. Traditional simulation methods, such as phase-field models, offer high-fidelity results but are computationally expensive due to the need to solve complex partial differential equations at fine spatiotemporal resolutions. To address this challenge, we propose a deep learning-based framework that accelerates microstructure evolution predictions while maintaining high accuracy. Our approach utilizes a fully convolutional spatiotemporal model trained in a self-supervised manner using sequential images generated from simulations of microstructural processes, including grain growth and spinodal decomposition. The trained neural network effectively learns the underlying physical dynamics and can accurately capture both short-term local behaviors and long-term statistical properties of evolving microstructures, while also demonstrating generalization to unseen spatiotemporal domains and variations in configuration and material parameters. Compared to recurrent neural architectures, our model achieves state-of-the-art predictive performance with significantly reduced computational cost in both training and inference. This work establishes a robust baseline for spatiotemporal learning in materials science and offers a scalable, data-driven alternative for fast and reliable microstructure simulations.

</details>


### [347] [Rethinking LoRA for Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning in Large Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19926)
*Jin Liu,Yinbin Miao,Ning Xi,Junkang Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LA-LoRA decouples gradient interactions in DP-FL with LoRA adapters, improving robustness and convergence under strict DP noise; achieves SOTA results on LVMs/LLMs; notable gain of 16.83 percentage points over RoLoRA on Swin-B with ε=1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle the privacy-utility trade-off in differential-privacy federated learning (DPFL) for large vision and language models. The abstract identifies three underexplored challenges when applying LoRA in DPFL: gradient coupling from updating two asymmetric low-rank matrices, compounded DP noise amplification, and the sharpness of the global aggregated model.

Method: LA-LoRA (Local Alternating LoRA) decouples gradient interactions by locally alternating optimization of the two low-rank matrices and aligns update directions across clients to reduce gradient misalignment and variance under DP noise. The approach yields stronger convergence guarantees in noisy federated environments.

Result: Empirically, LA-LoRA achieves state-of-the-art performance on both vision (Swin Transformer) and language (RoBERTa) models, demonstrating robustness to DP noise and broad applicability to LVMs and LLMs. A highlighted result: Swin-B fine-tuned on Tiny-ImageNet with privacy budget ε=1 shows a 16.83 percentage-point improvement in test accuracy over RoLoRA. Code is released.

Conclusion: LA-LoRA effectively addresses the three identified challenges (gradient coupling, DP noise amplification, and sharpness) and enhances convergence and robustness in DPFL for large models, enabling broader PEFT deployment under stringent privacy constraints.

Abstract: Fine-tuning large vision models (LVMs) and large language models (LLMs) under differentially private federated learning (DPFL) is hindered by a fundamental privacy-utility trade-off. Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), a promising parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) method, reduces computational and communication costs by introducing two trainable low-rank matrices while freezing pre-trained weights. However, directly applying LoRA in DPFL settings leads to performance degradation, especially in LVMs. Our analysis reveals three previously underexplored challenges: (1) gradient coupling caused by the simultaneous update of two asymmetric low-rank matrices, (2) compounded noise amplification under differential privacy, and (3) sharpness of the global aggregated model in the parameter space. To address these issues, we propose LA-LoRA (\textbf{L}ocal \textbf{A}lternating \textbf{LoRA}), a novel approach that decouples gradient interactions and aligns update directions across clients to enhance robustness under stringent privacy constraints. Theoretically, LA-LoRA strengthens convergence guarantees in noisy federated environments. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LA-LoRA achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on Swin Transformer and RoBERTa models, showcasing robustness to DP noise and broad applicability across both LVMs and LLMs. For example, when fine-tuning the Swin-B model on the Tiny-ImageNet dataset under a strict privacy budget ($ε= 1$), LA-LoRA outperforms the best baseline, RoLoRA, by 16.83\% in test accuracy. Code is provided in \repolink.

</details>


### [348] [Expanding the Role of Diffusion Models for Robust Classifier Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19931)
*Pin-Han Huang,Shang-Tse Chen,Hsuan-Tien Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Using diffusion-model internal representations as an auxiliary signal in adversarial training improves robustness and disentanglement, leveraging both diffusion representations and synthetic data for robust learning across CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend diffusion models beyond synthetic data generation by extracting and leveraging their internal representations to enhance robust classifier training and representation quality.

Method: Incorporate diffusion model internal representations as an auxiliary learning signal within adversarial training. Systematically analyze representation diversity and partial robustness, compare with diffusion-generated synthetic data, and jointly utilize both signals. Evaluate on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet.

Result: Diffusion representations are diverse and partially robust; incorporating them as an auxiliary signal consistently improves adversarial robustness across datasets; representations become more disentangled; diffusion representations and synthetic data play complementary roles in shaping representations.

Conclusion: Jointly leveraging diffusion representations and synthetic data during adversarial training yields improved robustness and more disentangled representations, validated on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet.

Abstract: Incorporating diffusion-generated synthetic data into adversarial training (AT) has been shown to substantially improve the training of robust image classifiers. In this work, we extend the role of diffusion models beyond merely generating synthetic data, examining whether their internal representations, which encode meaningful features of the data, can provide additional benefits for robust classifier training. Through systematic experiments, we show that diffusion models offer representations that are both diverse and partially robust, and that explicitly incorporating diffusion representations as an auxiliary learning signal during AT consistently improves robustness across settings. Furthermore, our representation analysis indicates that incorporating diffusion models into AT encourages more disentangled features, while diffusion representations and diffusion-generated synthetic data play complementary roles in shaping representations. Experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet validate these findings, demonstrating the effectiveness of jointly leveraging diffusion representations and synthetic data within AT.

</details>


### [349] [DP-FedAdamW: An Efficient Optimizer for Differentially Private Federated Large Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19945)
*Jin Liu,Yinbin Miao,Ning Xi,Junkang Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DP-FedAdamW: an AdamW-based optimizer for Differentially Private Federated Learning (DPFL) that stabilizes second-moment variance, removes DP-induced bias, and aligns updates to curb client drift; establishes an unbiased second-moment estimator and linear convergence without heterogeneity assumptions; shows empirical gains on language/vision transformers and ResNet-18, with a 5.83% improvement on Tiny-ImageNet (Swin-Base, ε=1).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the instability of applying AdamW to DP-FL due to data heterogeneity and DP noise, which inflates the second-moment variance, biases the estimator, and exacerbates client drift.

Method: Introduce DP-FedAdamW to restore AdamW under DP by stabilizing second-moment variance, removing DP-induced bias, and aligning local updates with global descent to reduce drift.

Result: The approach yields an unbiased second-moment estimator and a linearly accelerated convergence rate without assuming data heterogeneity; provides tighter (ε,δ)-DP guarantees; empirically improves performance across language and vision models, including Tiny-ImageNet (5.83% gain for Swin-Base, ε=1).

Conclusion: DP-FedAdamW successfully adapts AdamW for DP-FL, delivering theoretical guarantees and practical improvements over existing DP-FL optimizers.

Abstract: Balancing convergence efficiency and robustness under Differential Privacy (DP) is a central challenge in Federated Learning (FL). While AdamW accelerates training and fine-tuning in large-scale models, we find that directly applying it to Differentially Private FL (DPFL) suffers from three major issues: (i) data heterogeneity and privacy noise jointly amplify the variance of second-moment estimator, (ii) DP perturbations bias the second-moment estimator, and (iii) DP amplify AdamW sensitivity to local overfitting, worsening client drift. We propose DP-FedAdamW, the first AdamW-based optimizer for DPFL. It restores AdamW under DP by stabilizing second-moment variance, removing DP-induced bias, and aligning local updates to the global descent to curb client drift. Theoretically, we establish an unbiased second-moment estimator and prove a linearly accelerated convergence rate without any heterogeneity assumption, while providing tighter $(\varepsilon,δ)$-DP guarantees. Our empirical results demonstrate the effectiveness of DP-FedAdamW across language and vision Transformers and ResNet-18. On Tiny-ImageNet (Swin-Base, $\varepsilon=1$), DP-FedAdamW outperforms the state-of-the-art (SOTA) by 5.83\%. The code is available in Appendix.

</details>


### [350] [Sparse Masked Attention Policies for Reliable Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19956)
*Caroline Horsch,Laurens Engwegen,Max Weltevrede,Matthijs T. J. Spaan,Wendelin Böhmer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A learnable masking method integrated with attention-based policies to remove information, improving generalization to unseen states in RL, outperforming PPO and standard masking on Procgen.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Abstraction methods in reinforcement learning may not generalize to unseen observations because the reduced-information representation and its extraction function can overfit or fail on new states.

Method: Introduce a learnable masking function that operates on the attention weights inside an attention-based policy network. The mask reduces information selectively, producing a reduced representation that is optimized jointly with the policy.

Result: The proposed method substantially improves policy generalization to unseen tasks on the Procgen benchmark compared with standard PPO and masking approaches.

Conclusion: Coupling an information-removal mechanism with attention dynamics addresses generalization weaknesses of prior abstractions, yielding better generalization to unseen RL tasks.

Abstract: In reinforcement learning, abstraction methods that remove unnecessary information from the observation are commonly used to learn policies which generalize better to unseen tasks. However, these methods often overlook a crucial weakness: the function which extracts the reduced-information representation has unknown generalization ability in unseen observations. In this paper, we address this problem by presenting an information removal method which more reliably generalizes to new states. We accomplish this by using a learned masking function which operates on, and is integrated with, the attention weights within an attention-based policy network. We demonstrate that our method significantly improves policy generalization to unseen tasks in the Procgen benchmark compared to standard PPO and masking approaches.

</details>


### [351] [On the Equivalence of Random Network Distillation, Deep Ensembles, and Bayesian Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19964)
*Moritz A. Zanger,Yijun Wu,Pascal R. Van der Vaart,Wendelin Böhmer,Matthijs T. J. Spaan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: In the infinite-width NTK limit, RND's self-predictive error matches deep ensemble predictive variance; with a tailored target, RND can reproduce Bayesian posterior predictive, enabling a Bayesian RND sampler.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide rigorous theoretical grounding for RND and connect it to Bayesian inference and ensemble-based uncertainty quantification.

Method: Neural tangent kernel analysis in the infinite-width limit; derive equivalences between RND uncertainty and ensemble/bayesian predictions; construct a specific RND target; develop a posterior sampling algorithm.

Result: 1) The squared self-predictive error of RND equals the predictive variance of a deep ensemble. 2) A crafted RND target makes the RND error distribution mirror the centered posterior predictive of Bayesian inference with wide networks; enables a posterior sampling algorithm to draw i.i.d. samples from the exact Bayesian posterior predictive.

Conclusion: Provides a unified theoretical perspective placing RND within deep ensembles and Bayesian inference, and offers a practically grounded uncertainty quantification approach via Bayesian RND.

Abstract: Uncertainty quantification is central to safe and efficient deployments of deep learning models, yet many computationally practical methods lack lacking rigorous theoretical motivation. Random network distillation (RND) is a lightweight technique that measures novelty via prediction errors against a fixed random target. While empirically effective, it has remained unclear what uncertainties RND measures and how its estimates relate to other approaches, e.g. Bayesian inference or deep ensembles. This paper establishes these missing theoretical connections by analyzing RND within the neural tangent kernel framework in the limit of infinite network width. Our analysis reveals two central findings in this limit: (1) The uncertainty signal from RND -- its squared self-predictive error -- is equivalent to the predictive variance of a deep ensemble. (2) By constructing a specific RND target function, we show that the RND error distribution can be made to mirror the centered posterior predictive distribution of Bayesian inference with wide neural networks. Based on this equivalence, we moreover devise a posterior sampling algorithm that generates i.i.d. samples from an exact Bayesian posterior predictive distribution using this modified \textit{Bayesian RND} model. Collectively, our findings provide a unified theoretical perspective that places RND within the principled frameworks of deep ensembles and Bayesian inference, and offer new avenues for efficient yet theoretically grounded uncertainty quantification methods.

</details>


### [352] [Unlearning Noise in PINNs: A Selective Pruning Framework for PDE Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19967)
*Yongsheng Chen,Yong Chen,Wei Guo,Xinghui Zhong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: P-PINN uses selective, layer-wise pruning to unlearn corrupted data in a pretrained PINN, via a joint residual-data fidelity indicator and a bias-based neuron importance measure, followed by fine-tuning on reliable data under PDE constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle the sensitivity of PDE inverse problems solved by PINNs to noisy observations by removing the influence of corrupted data, inspired by machine unlearning and structured pruning.

Method: Start from a PINN trained on full data. Compute a joint residual--data fidelity indicator (data misfit + PDE residuals) to split training data into reliable and corrupted subsets. Define a bias-based neuron importance that captures directional activation differences between subsets. Iteratively prune noise-sensitive neurons layer by layer. Fine-tune the resulting network on reliable data under original PDE constraints (lightweight post-processing, not full retraining).

Result: Experiments on extensive PDE inverse-problem benchmarks show substantial robustness gains, with up to 96.6% reduction in relative error compared with baseline PINNs, along with improved training stability and accuracy under noisy conditions.

Conclusion: Activation-level post hoc pruning is a promising mechanism to enhance the reliability of physics-informed learning in noise-contaminated settings.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) provide a promising framework for solving inverse problems governed by partial differential equations (PDEs) by integrating observational data and physical constraints in a unified optimization objective. However, the ill-posed nature of PDE inverse problems makes them highly sensitive to noise. Even a small fraction of corrupted observations can distort internal neural representations, severely impairing accuracy and destabilizing training. Motivated by recent advances in machine unlearning and structured network pruning, we propose P-PINN, a selective pruning framework designed to unlearn the influence of corrupted data in a pretrained PINN. Specifically, starting from a PINN trained on the full dataset, P-PINN evaluates a joint residual--data fidelity indicator, a weighted combination of data misfit and PDE residuals, to partition the training set into reliable and corrupted subsets. Next, we introduce a bias-based neuron importance measure that quantifies directional activation discrepancies between the two subsets, identifying neurons whose representations are predominantly driven by corrupted samples. Building on this, an iterative pruning strategy then removes noise-sensitive neurons layer by layer. The resulting pruned network is fine-tuned on the reliable data subject to the original PDE constraints, acting as a lightweight post-processing stage rather than a complete retraining. Numerical experiments on extensive PDE inverse-problem benchmarks demonstrate that P-PINN substantially improves robustness, accuracy, and training stability under noisy conditions, achieving up to a 96.6\% reduction in relative error compared with baseline PINNs. These results indicate that activation-level post hoc pruning is a promising mechanism for enhancing the reliability of physics-informed learning in noise-contaminated settings.

</details>


### [353] [Discrete Diffusion Models Exploit Asymmetry to Solve Lookahead Planning Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19980)
*Itamar Trainin,Shauli Ravfogel,Omri Abend,Amir Feder*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AR and NAR models can both achieve perfect lookahead task accuracy; NAR has data and architectural efficiency advantages due to reverse-decoding-based planning, leveraging forward-to-backward information flow, while AR requires more data and curriculum to converge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how autoregressive and non-autoregressive models differ in solving planning tasks requiring multi-step lookahead, and to identify mechanisms enabling NAR models to excel.

Method: Mechanistic analysis of training and inference dynamics for AR and NAR models (including discrete diffusion-based LLMs), focusing on planning requirements, forward vs reverse generation, and the use of future tokens in NAR to decode backwards.

Result: NAR models exploit reverse determinism to plan using future tokens, achieving perfect lookahead accuracy with exponentially fewer training examples and shallower architectures; AR models require more data and often curriculum adjustments to converge.

Conclusion: Planning tasks exhibit asymmetry: forward generation is complex due to branching; reverse generation is deterministic, favoring NAR. Both paradigms can solve lookahead tasks, but NAR offers efficiency and easier convergence, suggesting advantageous application of NAR for planning-centric tasks.

Abstract: While Autoregressive (AR) Transformer-based Generative Language Models are frequently employed for lookahead tasks, recent research suggests a potential discrepancy in their ability to perform planning tasks that require multi-step lookahead. In this work, we investigate the distinct emergent mechanisms that arise when training AR versus Non-Autoregressive (NAR) models, such as Discrete Diffusion Models (dLLMs), on lookahead tasks. By requiring the models to plan ahead to reach the correct conclusion, we analyze how these two paradigms fundamentally differ in their approach to the problem. We identify a critical asymmetry in planning problems: while forward generation requires complex lookahead at branching junctions, reverse generation is often deterministic. This asymmetry creates an opportunity for NAR models. Through mechanistic analysis of training and inference dynamics, we demonstrate that NAR models learn to solve planning tasks by utilizing future tokens to decode backwards, avoiding the need to learn complex traversal mechanisms entirely. Consequently, we report that both AR and NAR models are able to achieve perfect accuracy on the lookahead task. However, NAR models require exponentially fewer training examples and shallower architectures compared to AR models, which often fail to converge without specific curriculum adjustments.

</details>


### [354] [A Computationally Efficient Multidimensional Vision Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19982)
*Alaa El Ichi,Khalide Jbilou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tensor Cosine Product-based ViT (TCP-ViT) achieves 1/C parameter reduction with competitive accuracy on classification and segmentation by exploiting multilinear structure and cosine transform orthogonality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision Transformers are computationally and memory-intensive. A tensor-based framework leveraging multilinear image structure and cosine transform orthogonality aims to enable efficient attention and compact representations.

Method: Define and develop the tensor cosine product (Cproduct), study its algebraic properties, and integrate it into a Vision Transformer architecture (TCP-ViT) to create efficient attention and structured features.

Result: Experiments on standard classification and segmentation benchmarks show a uniform 1/C parameter reduction (across channels) with competitive accuracy.

Conclusion: TCP-ViT offers a compact, efficient ViT variant with maintained performance; promising for deployment, with potential for broader task applicability and need for deeper theoretical/empirical validations.

Abstract: Vision Transformers have achieved state-of-the-art performance in a wide range
  of computer vision tasks, but their practical deployment is limited by high
  computational and memory costs. In this paper, we introduce a novel tensor-based
  framework for Vision Transformers built upon the Tensor Cosine Product
  (Cproduct). By exploiting multilinear structures inherent in image data and the
  orthogonality of cosine transforms, the proposed approach enables efficient
  attention mechanisms and structured feature representations. We develop the
  theoretical foundations of the tensor cosine product, analyze its algebraic
  properties, and integrate it into a new Cproduct-based Vision Transformer
  architecture (TCP-ViT). Numerical experiments on standard classification and
  segmentation benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a uniform
  1/C parameter reduction (where C is the number of channels) while
  maintaining competitive accuracy.

</details>


### [355] [Counterfactual Understanding via Retrieval-aware Multimodal Modeling for Time-to-Event Survival Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19987)
*Ha-Anh Hoang Nguyen,Tri-Duc Phan Le,Duc-Hoang Pham,Huy-Son Nguyen,Cam-Van Thi Nguyen,Duc-Trong Le,Hoang-Quynh Le*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CURE is a multimodal, counterfactual survival model that uses cross-attention fusion and mixture-of-experts to integrate clinical, paraclinical, demographic, and multi-omics data, implicitly retrieves patient-specific latent subgroups, and improves time-to-event predictions with censoring. Evaluated on METABRIC and TCGA-LUAD, it outperforms baselines on Time-dependent C-index and Integrated Brier Score; code is public.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address time-to-event prediction under censoring with patient heterogeneity and rich multimodal data. Traditional methods struggle to leverage diverse data sources and uncover latent subgroups that influence survival and treatment effects.

Method: CURE framework: multimodal embedding with cross-attention to fuse clinical, paraclinical, demographic, and multi-omics signals; mixture-of-experts to refine omics contributions; implicit latent subgroup retrieval to capture baseline and treatment-dependent dynamics; counterfactual survival modeling.

Result: Consistent improvement over strong baselines on METABRIC and TCGA-LUAD in survival metrics (Time-dependent C-index and IBS). Demonstrates effective multimodal integration and latent subgroup discovery; public code available for reproducibility.

Conclusion: CURE advances multimodal counterfactual survival modeling by integrating diverse data, adaptively weighting omics signals, and uncovering patient-specific latent subgroups, offering a foundation for future treatment recommendation models and enhanced survival prediction.

Abstract: This paper tackles the problem of time-to-event counterfactual survival prediction, aiming to optimize individualized survival outcomes in the presence of heterogeneity and censored data. We propose CURE, a framework that advances counterfactual survival modeling via comprehensive multimodal embedding and latent subgroup retrieval. CURE integrates clinical, paraclinical, demographic, and multi-omics information, which are aligned and fused through cross-attention mechanisms. Complex multi-omics signals can be adaptively refined using a mixture-of-experts architecture, emphasizing the most informative omics components. Building upon this representation, CURE implicitly retrieves patient-specific latent subgroups that capture both baseline survival dynamics and treatment-dependent variations. Experimental results on METABRIC and TCGA-LUAD datasets demonstrate that proposed CURE model consistently outperforms strong baselines in survival analysis, evaluated using the Time-dependent Concordance Index ($C^{td}$) and Integrated Brier Score (IBS). These findings highlight the potential of CURE to enhance multimodal understanding and serve as a foundation for future treatment recommendation models. All code and related resources are publicly available to facilitate the reproducibility https://github.com/L2R-UET/CURE.

</details>


### [356] [A Secure and Private Distributed Bayesian Federated Learning Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20003)
*Nuocheng Yang,Sihua Wang,Zhaohui Yang,Mingzhe Chen,Changchuan Yin,Kaibin Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A distributed federated learning framework that integrates Byzantine robustness, privacy preservation, and convergence acceleration via Bayesian local models, selective neighbor exchange, and a GNN-RL driven topology optimization under partial network information; achieves robust, efficient learning with low overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address key DFL challenges: privacy leakage from honest-but-curious neighbors, slow convergence due to lack of central coordination, and vulnerability to Byzantine adversaries. The paper seeks to balance topology, security, privacy, and convergence under limited network knowledge.

Method: Each device trains a local Bayesian model and autonomously selects an optimal subset of neighbors for posterior exchange. The neighbor selection is formulated as an optimization problem under security and privacy constraints. The authors analytically characterize trade-offs among dynamic connectivity, Byzantine detection, privacy levels, and convergence speed. They then develop a fully distributed Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithm enabling devices to make autonomous connection decisions from local observations.

Result: Simulation results indicate superior robustness and efficiency with significantly lower overhead compared to traditional security and privacy schemes.

Conclusion: The framework provides a fully distributed solution for robust, privacy-aware, and fast-converging DFL by combining Bayesian local updates, selective neighbor exchange, and GNN-RL-driven topology decisions, achieving improved performance with lower overhead.

Abstract: Distributed Federated Learning (DFL) enables decentralized model training across large-scale systems without a central parameter server. However, DFL faces three critical challenges: privacy leakage from honest-but-curious neighbors, slow convergence due to the lack of central coordination, and vulnerability to Byzantine adversaries aiming to degrade model accuracy. To address these issues, we propose a novel DFL framework that integrates Byzantine robustness, privacy preservation, and convergence acceleration. Within this framework, each device trains a local model using a Bayesian approach and independently selects an optimal subset of neighbors for posterior exchange. We formulate this neighbor selection as an optimization problem to minimize the global loss function under security and privacy constraints. Solving this problem is challenging because devices only possess partial network information, and the complex coupling between topology, security, and convergence remains unclear. To bridge this gap, we first analytically characterize the trade-offs between dynamic connectivity, Byzantine detection, privacy levels, and convergence speed. Leveraging these insights, we develop a fully distributed Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithm. This approach enables devices to make autonomous connection decisions based on local observations. Simulation results demonstrate that our method achieves superior robustness and efficiency with significantly lower overhead compared to traditional security and privacy schemes.

</details>


### [357] [Learning Discriminative and Generalizable Anomaly Detector for Dynamic Graph with Limited Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20019)
*Yuxing Tian,Yiyan Qi,Fengran Mo,Weixu Zhang,Jian Guo,Jian-Yun Nie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A generalizable, model-agnostic DGAD framework that learns a discriminative boundary from normal/unlabeled data while leveraging limited labeled anomalies, via residual encoding, a dual-hypersphere restriction loss, and bi-boundary optimization with a normalizing-flow model of normal likelihood.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic graph anomaly detection lacks labeled anomalies; unsupervised approaches have ambiguous boundaries and semi-supervised methods overfit to scarce anomalies. There is a need to learn a robust boundary using normal/unlabeled data while effectively utilizing limited anomalous labels and generalizing to unseen anomalies.

Method: Three-component framework: (i) residual representation encoding to capture deviations between current interactions and historical context; (ii) restriction loss that confines normal representations within an interval bounded by two co-centered hyperspheres to ensure consistent scales and separability of anomalies; (iii) bi-boundary optimization that learns a discriminative, robust boundary by modeling the normal log-likelihood with a normalizing flow, enabling effective use of normal/unlabeled data with optional labeled anomalies.

Result: Extensive experiments show the proposed framework achieves superior performance across diverse evaluation settings, indicating strong generalization to unseen anomalies and effective use of limited labeled anomalies.

Conclusion: The framework offers a practical, generalizable, and model-agnostic solution for DGAD, enabling discriminative boundary learning from normal/unlabeled data while leveraging scarce anomaly labels to improve robustness and generalization.

Abstract: Dynamic graph anomaly detection (DGAD) is critical for many real-world applications but remains challenging due to the scarcity of labeled anomalies. Existing methods are either unsupervised or semi-supervised: unsupervised methods avoid the need for labeled anomalies but often produce ambiguous boundary, whereas semi-supervised methods can overfit to the limited labeled anomalies and generalize poorly to unseen anomalies. To address this gap, we consider a largely underexplored problem in DGAD: learning a discriminative boundary from normal/unlabeled data, while leveraging limited labeled anomalies \textbf{when available} without sacrificing generalization to unseen anomalies. To this end, we propose an effective, generalizable, and model-agnostic framework with three main components: (i) residual representation encoding that capture deviations between current interactions and their historical context, providing anomaly-relevant signals; (ii) a restriction loss that constrain the normal representations within an interval bounded by two co-centered hyperspheres, ensuring consistent scales while keeping anomalies separable; (iii) a bi-boundary optimization strategy that learns a discriminative and robust boundary using the normal log-likelihood distribution modeled by a normalizing flow. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of our framework across diverse evaluation settings.

</details>


### [358] [A Theory of How Pretraining Shapes Inductive Bias in Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20062)
*Nicolas Anguita,Francesco Locatello,Andrew M. Saxe,Marco Mondelli,Flavia Mancini,Samuel Lippl,Clementine Domine*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analytical theory for pretraining–fine-tuning in diagonal linear networks; identifies four fine-tuning regimes controlled by initialization scale; smaller early-layer scales enable feature reuse and refinement, improving generalization on tasks leveraging pretrained features; empirical evidence in nonlinear nets (CIFAR-100) suggests the effect transfers beyond the simplified model; highlights per-layer initialization as a key design knob.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve end-to-end theoretical understanding of how initialization choices influence the ability to reuse and refine pretrained features during fine-tuning, a gap in transfer learning theory.

Method: Derive exact generalization-error expressions for diagonal linear networks as a function of initialization parameters and task statistics; classify fine-tuning behavior into four regimes based on initialization; validate qualitatively with nonlinear-net experiments on CIFAR-100.

Result: Four distinct fine-tuning regimes emerge; small initialization scale in earlier layers fosters both feature reuse and refinement, yielding better generalization on tasks that rely on a subset of pretrained features; empirical alignment in nonlinear networks on CIFAR-100.

Conclusion: Per-layer initialization scale critically shapes the ability to maintain and adapt pretrained features during fine-tuning, offering theoretical insight into transfer learning generalization and practical guidance for initialization design across layers; results motivate further study in nonlinear and real-world tasks.

Abstract: Pretraining and fine-tuning are central stages in modern machine learning systems. In practice, feature learning plays an important role across both stages: deep neural networks learn a broad range of useful features during pretraining and further refine those features during fine-tuning. However, an end-to-end theoretical understanding of how choices of initialization impact the ability to reuse and refine features during fine-tuning has remained elusive. Here we develop an analytical theory of the pretraining-fine-tuning pipeline in diagonal linear networks, deriving exact expressions for the generalization error as a function of initialization parameters and task statistics. We find that different initialization choices place the network into four distinct fine-tuning regimes that are distinguished by their ability to support feature learning and reuse, and therefore by the task statistics for which they are beneficial. In particular, a smaller initialization scale in earlier layers enables the network to both reuse and refine its features, leading to superior generalization on fine-tuning tasks that rely on a subset of pretraining features. We demonstrate empirically that the same initialization parameters impact generalization in nonlinear networks trained on CIFAR-100. Overall, our results demonstrate analytically how data and network initialization interact to shape fine-tuning generalization, highlighting an important role for the relative scale of initialization across different layers in enabling continued feature learning during fine-tuning.

</details>


### [359] [Training-Free Generative Modeling via Kernelized Stochastic Interpolants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20070)
*Florentin Coeurdoux,Etienne Lempereur,Nathanaël Cuvelle-Magar,Thomas Eboli,Stéphane Mallat,Anastasia Borovykh,Eric Vanden-Eijnden*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Kernel-based generative modeling in the stochastic interpolant framework using a data-derived linear system for the drift, yielding training-free generation with flexibility to incorporate diverse feature maps; demonstrated on finance, turbulence, and images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on neural-network training in generative modeling by embedding the drift in a kernel/linear-system framework within stochastic interpolants. Achieve a data-dimension-insensitive parameterization (P independent of d), enable feature-map versatility, and maintain robustness to inexact estimates via a capable integrator for the diffusion term.

Method: Drift is b_t(x)=∇φ(x)ᵀ η_t with η_t solving a P×P linear system computed from data, where P does not depend on data dimension d. The diffusion coefficient D_t is optimized via Girsanov and diverges at t=0; an integrator is developed to handle this singularity. The framework accepts various feature maps (e.g., scattering transforms, pretrained generative models) for training-free generation and model combination.

Result: Demonstrates the approach on financial time series, turbulence, and image generation, showing training-free generation and the ability to combine models through the kernel-based drift.

Conclusion: Offers a scalable, training-free generative modeling framework within the stochastic interpolant setting by replacing neural-network training with a linear-system solution for the drift, while supporting flexible feature maps and robust integration to handle singular diffusion terms.

Abstract: We develop a kernel method for generative modeling within the stochastic interpolant framework, replacing neural network training with linear systems. The drift of the generative SDE is $\hat b_t(x) = \nablaφ(x)^\topη_t$, where $η_t\in\R^P$ solves a $P\times P$ system computable from data, with $P$ independent of the data dimension $d$. Since estimates are inexact, the diffusion coefficient $D_t$ affects sample quality; the optimal $D_t^*$ from Girsanov diverges at $t=0$, but this poses no difficulty and we develop an integrator that handles it seamlessly. The framework accommodates diverse feature maps -- scattering transforms, pretrained generative models etc. -- enabling training-free generation and model combination. We demonstrate the approach on financial time series, turbulence, and image generation.

</details>


### [360] [BarrierSteer: LLM Safety via Learning Barrier Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20102)
*Thanh Q. Tran,Arun Verma,Kiwan Wong,Bryan Kian Hsiang Low,Daniela Rus,Wei Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BarrierSteer introduces Control Barrier Functions to enforce safety constraints in the latent space of LLMs during inference, steering responses away from unsafe trajectories without retraining. It claims theoretical guarantees and strong empirical safety improvements across models/datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs remain vulnerable to adversarial prompts and unsafe content. There is a need for safety mechanisms that are both practically effective and well-founded in theory, without compromising model capability through retraining or parameter modifications.

Method: Learned non-linear safety constraints embedded in the model’s latent representation. A steering mechanism using Control Barrier Functions detects and prevents unsafe response trajectories during inference. Multiple safety constraints are merged efficiently. No modification to LLM parameters; safety is enforced in latent space.

Result: The approach substantially reduces adversarial success rates and unsafe generations and outperforms existing methods across multiple models and datasets.

Conclusion: BarierSteer provides a principled, computationally efficient safety mechanism that preserves the base model’s capabilities, offering theoretical guarantees and practical safety improvements for LLM deployment.

Abstract: Despite the state-of-the-art performance of large language models (LLMs) across diverse tasks, their susceptibility to adversarial attacks and unsafe content generation remains a major obstacle to deployment, particularly in high-stakes settings. Addressing this challenge requires safety mechanisms that are both practically effective and supported by rigorous theory. We introduce BarrierSteer, a novel framework that formalizes response safety by embedding learned non-linear safety constraints directly into the model's latent representation space. BarrierSteer employs a steering mechanism based on Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) to efficiently detect and prevent unsafe response trajectories during inference with high precision. By enforcing multiple safety constraints through efficient constraint merging, without modifying the underlying LLM parameters, BarrierSteer preserves the model's original capabilities and performance. We provide theoretical results establishing that applying CBFs in latent space offers a principled and computationally efficient approach to enforcing safety. Our experiments across multiple models and datasets show that BarrierSteer substantially reduces adversarial success rates, decreases unsafe generations, and outperforms existing methods.

</details>


### [361] [Reliable Abstention under Adversarial Injections: Tight Lower Bounds and New Upper Bounds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20111)
*Ezra Edelman,Surbhi Goel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Establishes a sharp separation in adversarially perturbed online learning with clean labels and abstention: a matching Ω(√T) lower bound for VC dimension 1, and a robust witnesses framework yielding distribution-agnostic upper bounds via two dimensions—inference and certificate. Applies to 2D halfspaces to obtain a first tilde O(T^{2/3}) bound, contrasting prior non-robust results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand the fundamental limits of online learning when data is a mix of i.i.d. and adversarial examples under a clean-label constraint and abstentions, and close the gap between distribution-aware and distribution-agnostic regimes.

Method: Lower bound via adversarial construction for VC dimension 1; introduce a potential-based framework using robust witnesses. Define two dimensions: inference dimension (gives tilde O(T^{1-1/k}) for dimension k) and certificate dimension (new relaxation). Apply to halfspaces in R^2, showing certificate dimension 3 leads to tilde O(T^{2/3}).

Result: Ω(√T) lower bound for VC dimension 1; a general upper bound framework yielding tilde O(T^{1-1/k}) for inference-dimension-k classes; certificate-dimension-based results with 2D halfspaces achieving tilde O(T^{2/3}); first distribution-agnostic bound for this class; highlights a separation between information regimes.

Conclusion: Introduces robust-witness-based, potential-driven methods that capture the trade-offs between abstention and robustness; defines inference and certificate dimensions to characterize learnability under adversarial corruption; opens avenues for tighter bounds and extensions to broader concept classes.

Abstract: We study online learning in the adversarial injection model introduced by [Goel et al. 2017], where a stream of labeled examples is predominantly drawn i.i.d.\ from an unknown distribution $\mathcal{D}$, but may be interspersed with adversarially chosen instances without the learner knowing which rounds are adversarial. Crucially, labels are always consistent with a fixed target concept (the clean-label setting). The learner is additionally allowed to abstain from predicting, and the total error counts the mistakes whenever the learner decides to predict and incorrect abstentions when it abstains on i.i.d.\ rounds. Perhaps surprisingly, prior work shows that oracle access to the underlying distribution yields $O(d^2 \log T)$ combined error for VC dimension $d$, while distribution-agnostic algorithms achieve only $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ for restricted classes, leaving open whether this gap is fundamental.
  We resolve this question by proving a matching $Ω(\sqrt{T})$ lower bound for VC dimension $1$, establishing a sharp separation between the two information regimes. On the algorithmic side, we introduce a potential-based framework driven by \emph{robust witnesses}, small subsets of labeled examples that certify predictions while remaining resilient to adversarial contamination. We instantiate this framework using two combinatorial dimensions: (1) \emph{inference dimension}, yielding combined error $\tilde{O}(T^{1-1/k})$ for classes of inference dimension $k$, and (2) \emph{certificate dimension}, a new relaxation we introduce. As an application, we show that halfspaces in $\mathbb{R}^2$ have certificate dimension $3$, obtaining the first distribution-agnostic bound of $\tilde{O}(T^{2/3})$ for this class. This is notable since [Blum et al. 2021] showed halfspaces are not robustly learnable under clean-label attacks without abstention.

</details>


### [362] [Adaptation to Intrinsic Dependence in Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20126)
*Yunxiao Zhao,Changxiao Cai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Randomized, distribution-agnostic unmasking schedule for diffusion language models yields KL convergence bounds scaling with total correlation; enables parallel sampling with K iterations and accelerates sampling for low-complexity distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unmasking schedule design theory for diffusion language models is limited; aim to adapt to unknown data dependencies without hyperparameters.

Method: Introduce a randomized unmasking schedule that specifies a random number of tokens revealed per iteration; analyze two parameter choices yielding KL divergence bounds ~ TC/K and ~ DTC/K.

Result: KL divergence bounds scale as ~ TC/K and ~ DTC/K, hold for K < L; show parallel-sampling convergence; improve upon prior theory; imply sampling acceleration for low-complexity distributions.

Conclusion: Shows adaptivity of DLMs to intrinsic data structures; randomized unmasking sizes benefit inference schedule design; provides theoretical guarantees for faster sampling under realistic regimes.

Abstract: Diffusion language models (DLMs) have recently emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive (AR) approaches, enabling parallel token generation beyond a rigid left-to-right order. Despite growing empirical success, the theoretical understanding of how unmasking schedules -- which specify the order and size of unmasked tokens during sampling -- affect generation quality remains limited. In this work, we introduce a distribution-agnostic unmasking schedule for DLMs that adapts to the (unknown) dependence structure of the target data distribution, without requiring any prior knowledge or hyperparameter tuning. In contrast to prior deterministic procedures that fix unmasking sizes, our method randomizes the number of tokens revealed at each iteration. We show that, for two specific parameter choices, the sampling convergence guarantees -- measured by Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence -- scale as $\widetilde O(\mathsf{TC}/K)$ and $\widetilde O(\mathsf{DTC}/K)$ respectively. Here, $K$ is the number of iterations, and $\mathsf{TC}$ and $\mathsf{DTC}$ are the total correlation and dual total correlation of the target distribution, capturing the intrinsic dependence structure underlying the data. Importantly, our guarantees hold in the practically relevant parallel-sampling regime $K<L$ where $L$ is the token sequence length. These results significantly improve upon prior convergence theories and yield substantial sampling acceleration for low-complexity distributions. Overall, our findings unveil the adaptivity of DLMs to intrinsic data structures and shed light on the benefit of randomized unmasking sizes in inference schedule design.

</details>


### [363] [LAD: Learning Advantage Distribution for Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20132)
*Wendi Li,Sharon Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A distribution-matching objective, Learning Advantage Distributions (LAD), replaces advantage maximization in RL for large-model reasoning to improve diversity and exploration without extra cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL objectives overfit to dominant reward signals in large-model reasoning, limiting exploration of valid reasoning trajectories and diversity.

Method: Introduce LAD: a distribution-matching framework that replaces advantage maximization with learning the distribution induced by advantages. Proves equivalence between optimal policy update and an advantage-based target distribution; minimizes f-divergence between policy-induced and advantage-induced distributions. Provides a gradient that boosts high-advantage actions while constraining probability growth to avoid collapse; requires no extra entropy regularization; matches GRPO cost and scales to LLM post-training.

Result: In controlled bandits, LAD recovers the multimodal advantage distribution. Empirically, on math and code reasoning tasks across multiple LLM backbones, LAD improves both accuracy and generative diversity without extra training cost.

Conclusion: LAD offers a principled, cost-free alternative to traditional advantage optimization by aligning the policy with an advantage-induced distribution, yielding better accuracy and diversity in reasoning tasks and scalable post-training deployment.

Abstract: Current reinforcement learning objectives for large-model reasoning primarily focus on maximizing expected rewards. This paradigm can lead to overfitting to dominant reward signals, while neglecting alternative yet valid reasoning trajectories, thereby limiting diversity and exploration. To address this issue, we introduce Learning Advantage Distributions (LAD), a distribution-matching framework that replaces advantage maximization with learning the advantage-induced distribution. By establishing the equivalence between the optimal policy update and an advantage-based target distribution, we derive a practical LAD objective formulated as minimizing an $f$-divergence between the policy-induced and advantage-induced distributions. This yields a gradient update that increases likelihood for high-advantage responses while suppressing over-confident probability growth, preventing collapse without requiring auxiliary entropy regularization. LAD incurs no extra training cost compared to GRPO and scales naturally to LLM post-training. In a controlled bandit setting, LAD faithfully recovers the multimodal advantage distribution, validating the theoretical formulation. Experiments on math and code reasoning tasks across several LLM backbones show that LAD reliably improves both accuracy and generative diversity.

</details>


### [364] [Behavior Learning (BL): Learning Hierarchical Optimization Structures from Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.20152)
*Zhenyao Ma,Yue Liang,Dongxu Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BL learns interpretable, identifiable, hierarchical optimization structures via a modular UMP-based framework, achieving universal approximation and identifiability (IBL), with strong empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivated by behavioral science to unify predictive performance, intrinsic interpretability, and identifiability in optimization-based modeling; addresses broad scientific domains where optimization is central.

Method: Parameterizes a compositional utility function built from modular blocks, each block representing a utility maximization problem (UMP). Supports single UMP or hierarchical compositions; introduces a smooth/monotone variant (IBL) to guarantee identifiability; provides universal approximation results and analyzes M-estimation properties.

Result: Theoretical contributions include a universal approximation property for BL and M-estimation properties for IBL. Empirically, BL demonstrates strong predictive performance, intrinsic interpretability, and scalability to high-dimensional data.

Conclusion: BL offers a unified framework that combines predictive power, interpretability, and identifiability for optimization-centric domains. It supports flexible architectures from single to hierarchical UMPs and provides practical code (GitHub) and installable package.

Abstract: Inspired by behavioral science, we propose Behavior Learning (BL), a novel general-purpose machine learning framework that learns interpretable and identifiable optimization structures from data, ranging from single optimization problems to hierarchical compositions. It unifies predictive performance, intrinsic interpretability, and identifiability, with broad applicability to scientific domains involving optimization. BL parameterizes a compositional utility function built from intrinsically interpretable modular blocks, which induces a data distribution for prediction and generation. Each block represents and can be written in symbolic form as a utility maximization problem (UMP), a foundational paradigm in behavioral science and a universal framework of optimization. BL supports architectures ranging from a single UMP to hierarchical compositions, the latter modeling hierarchical optimization structures. Its smooth and monotone variant (IBL) guarantees identifiability. Theoretically, we establish the universal approximation property of BL, and analyze the M-estimation properties of IBL. Empirically, BL demonstrates strong predictive performance, intrinsic interpretability and scalability to high-dimensional data. Code: https://github.com/MoonYLiang/Behavior-Learning ; install via pip install blnetwork.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [365] [Design and Biomechanical Evaluation of a Lightweight Low-Complexity Soft Bilateral Ankle Exoskeleton](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18569)
*Josée Mallah,Zakii Javed,Zafer Azak,Thomas Stone,Luigi G. Occhipinti*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A lightweight, soft, shoe-attached bilateral ankle exoskeleton for plantarflexion shows no gait obstruction in zero-torque mode, with a control system under development and ongoing testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide gait assistance for medical and nonmedical users by reducing the burden of exoskeleton mass/structure while maintaining comfort and natural gait.

Method: Design of a lightweight, soft bilateral ankle exoskeleton with a shoe attachment. Experimental testing comparing gait with the device in zero-torque mode to baseline, measuring kinematics/kinetics, and development of a control system; tests ongoing.

Result: Zero-torque operation caused no significant differences in lower-limb kinematics or kinetics compared to baseline; device is compliant and comfortable, indicating potential to provide effective assistance.

Conclusion: The device is feasible for non-obtrusive gait assistance; control system development is underway and further tests are planned to validate active assistance.

Abstract: Many people could benefit from exoskeleton assistance during gait, for either medical or nonmedical purposes. But exoskeletons bring added mass and structure, which in turn require compensating for. In this work, we present a lightweight, low-complexity, soft bilateral ankle exoskeleton for plantarflexion assistance, with a shoe attachment design that can be mounted on top of any pair of shoes. Experimental tests show no significant difference in lower limb kinematics and kinetics when wearing the exoskeleton in zero-torque mode relative to not wearing an exoskeleton, showing that our device does not obstruct healthy gait, and proving it as a compliant and comfortable device, promising to provide effective assistance. Hence, a control system was developed, and additional tests are underway.

</details>


### [366] [Enhancing Goal Inference via Correction Timing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18603)
*Anjiabei Wang,Shuangge Wang,Tesca Fitzgerald*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Timing of human corrections during robot control can serve as a signal to improve learning, enabling (1) identifying motion triggers for corrections, (2) faster inference of correction goals from initial motion, and (3) refining task objective constraints; timing improved learning for (1) and (2).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper investigates correction timing as a latent signal in human-robot feedback, addressing limitations of treating corrections solely as demonstrations or preferences and recognizing that the decision to intervene depends on task progress, alignment with expectations, dynamics, legibility, and optimality.

Method: Empirical analysis across three applications: (1) identifying motion features that prompt corrections, (2) inferring the final goal of a correction from its timing and initial direction, and (3) learning more precise task constraints; evaluates whether correction timing improves learning.

Result: Correction timing improves learning for applications (1) and (2), demonstrating that when the moment and initial trajectory of a correction are considered, the robot can better infer human intent and adjust features accordingly; the third application did not show the same level of improvement in this abstract.

Conclusion: Correction timing is a valuable signal for robot learning, providing new insights into human-robot collaboration by revealing what prompts corrections, enabling quicker goal inference, and aiding refinement of task constraints; suggests further exploration of timing-based learning signals.

Abstract: Corrections offer a natural modality for people to provide feedback to a robot, by (i) intervening in the robot's behavior when they believe the robot is failing (or will fail) the task objectives and (ii) modifying the robot's behavior to successfully fulfill the task. Each correction offers information on what the robot should and should not do, where the corrected behavior is more aligned with task objectives than the original behavior. Most prior work on learning from corrections involves interpreting a correction as a new demonstration (consisting of the modified robot behavior), or a preference (for the modified trajectory compared to the robot's original behavior). However, this overlooks one essential element of the correction feedback, which is the human's decision to intervene in the robot's behavior in the first place. This decision can be influenced by multiple factors including the robot's task progress, alignment with human expectations, dynamics, motion legibility, and optimality. In this work, we investigate whether the timing of this decision can offer a useful signal for inferring these task-relevant influences. In particular, we investigate three potential applications for this learning signal: (1) identifying features of a robot's motion that may prompt people to correct it, (2) quickly inferring the final goal of a human's correction based on the timing and initial direction of their correction motion, and (3) learning more precise constraints for task objectives. Our results indicate that correction timing results in improved learning for the first two of these applications. Overall, our work provides new insights on the value of correction timing as a signal for robot learning.

</details>


### [367] [OVerSeeC: Open-Vocabulary Costmap Generation from Satellite Images and Natural Language](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18606)
*Rwik Rana,Jesse Quattrociocchi,Dongmyeong Lee,Christian Ellis,Amanda Adkins,Adam Uccello,Garrett Warnell,Joydeep Biswas*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A zero-shot modular framework (OVerSeeC) that uses Interpret-Locate-Synthesize with LLMs and open-vocabulary segmentation to generate executable costmaps from satellite imagery guided by natural-language mission prompts for long-range planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To support flexible, mission-adaptive global planning by generating costmaps from satellite imagery that can incorporate unknown entities and compositional traversal rules expressed in natural language, overcoming fixed ontologies and static mappings.

Method: Decompose the problem into Interpret (LLM extracts entities and preferences), Locate (open-vocabulary segmentation identifies entities in high-res imagery), Synthesize (LLM converts preferences and masks into executable costmap code for planners).

Result: Empirically, the framework handles novel entities, respects ranked and compositional preferences, and produces routes aligned with human trajectories across diverse regions, demonstrating robustness to distribution shifts.

Conclusion: Modular composition of foundation models enables open-vocabulary, preference-aligned costmap generation for scalable, mission-adaptive global planning.

Abstract: Aerial imagery provides essential global context for autonomous navigation, enabling route planning at scales inaccessible to onboard sensing. We address the problem of generating global costmaps for long-range planning directly from satellite imagery when entities and mission-specific traversal rules are expressed in natural language at test time. This setting is challenging since mission requirements vary, terrain entities may be unknown at deployment, and user prompts often encode compositional traversal logic. Existing approaches relying on fixed ontologies and static cost mappings cannot accommodate such flexibility. While foundation models excel at language interpretation and open-vocabulary perception, no single model can simultaneously parse nuanced mission directives, locate arbitrary entities in large-scale imagery, and synthesize them into an executable cost function for planners. We therefore propose OVerSeeC, a zero-shot modular framework that decomposes the problem into Interpret-Locate-Synthesize: (i) an LLM extracts entities and ranked preferences, (ii) an open-vocabulary segmentation pipeline identifies these entities from high-resolution imagery, and (iii) the LLM uses the user's natural language preferences and masks to synthesize executable costmap code. Empirically, OVerSeeC handles novel entities, respects ranked and compositional preferences, and produces routes consistent with human-drawn trajectories across diverse regions, demonstrating robustness to distribution shifts. This shows that modular composition of foundation models enables open-vocabulary, preference-aligned costmap generation for scalable, mission-adaptive global planning.

</details>


### [368] [FORMICA: Decision-Focused Learning for Communication-Free Multi-Robot Task Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18622)
*Antonio Lopez,Jack Muirhead,Carlo Pinciroli*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A learning-based, communication-free multi-robot task allocation method (FORMICA) that predicts bid distributions via a mean-field approximation and end-to-end training to minimize Task Allocation Regret, achieving strong performance and scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In bandwidth-limited or adversarial environments, maintaining high-quality task allocations without inter-robot communication is essential. Traditional analytical mean-field approaches are sensitive to idealized assumptions and do not adapt to clustering or spatial heterogeneity.

Method: Robots predict the distribution of competing bids (mean-field) instead of individual bids and train end-to-end to minimize Task Allocation Regret, inspired by SPO. This reduces complexity from O(NT) to O(T). The framework is named FORMICA (Field-Oriented Regret-Minimizing Implicit Coordination Algorithm).

Result: Empirical results show substantial gains: in 16 robots and 64 tasks, system reward improves by 17% over a natural analytical baseline and approaches the MILP optimum; in larger scenarios with 256 robots and 4096 tasks, improvement is 7%. Training is fast (21 seconds on a laptop), indicating strong generalization to larger, unseen problem sizes.

Conclusion: FORMICA demonstrates robust, scalable, communication-free implicit coordination by predicting bid distributions and optimizing for regret, outperforming analytical baselines and matching MILP performance on moderate scales while generalizing to much larger swarms.

Abstract: Most multi-robot task allocation methods rely on communication to resolve conflicts and reach consistent assignments. In environments with limited bandwidth, degraded infrastructure, or adversarial interference, existing approaches degrade sharply. We introduce a learning-based framework that achieves high-quality task allocation without any robot-to-robot communication. The key idea is that robots coordinate implicitly by predicting teammates' bids: if each robot can anticipate competition for a task, it can adjust its choices accordingly. Our method predicts bid distributions to correct systematic errors in analytical mean-field approximations. While analytical predictions assume idealized conditions (uniform distributions, known bid functions), our learned approach adapts to task clustering and spatial heterogeneity. Inspired by Smart Predict-then-Optimize (SPO), we train predictors end-to-end to minimize Task Allocation Regret rather than prediction error. To scale to large swarms, we develop a mean-field approximation where each robot predicts the distribution of competing bids rather than individual bids, reducing complexity from $O(NT)$ to $O(T)$. We call our approach FORMICA: Field-Oriented Regret-Minimizing Implicit Coordination Algorithm. Experiments show FORMICA substantially outperforms a natural analytical baseline. In scenarios with 16 robots and 64 tasks, our approach improves system reward by 17% and approaches the optimal MILP solution. When deployed on larger scenarios (256 robots, 4096 tasks), the same model improves performance by 7%, demonstrating strong generalization. Training requires only 21 seconds on a laptop, enabling rapid adaptation to new environments.

</details>


### [369] [Soft Surfaced Vision-Based Tactile Sensing for Bipedal Robot Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18638)
*Jaeeun Kim,Junhee Lim,Yu She*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A vision-based tactile foot sensor enables deformable, skin-like sensing on a bipedal robot, turning foot-ground interactions into rich haptic signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Embodied sensing in legged locomotion enables perception to emerge from body-environment interactions; there is a need for foot-level tactile sensing to enhance stability, adaptability, and environmental awareness beyond proprioception.

Method: A soft-surfaced, vision-based tactile foot sensor captures contact deformations optically. From a contact image stream, the method estimates contact pose (position and orientation), visualizes shear, computes center of pressure (CoP), classifies terrain, and detects geometric features of the contact patch.

Result: Validation on tilting platforms and under visually obscured conditions shows that foot-borne tactile feedback improves balance control and terrain awareness beyond proprioception alone.

Conclusion: Integrating tactile perception into legged robot feet can improve stability, adaptability, and environmental awareness, advancing toward more compliant and intelligent locomotion systems.

Abstract: Legged locomotion benefits from embodied sensing, where perception emerges from the physical interaction between body and environment. We present a soft-surfaced, vision-based tactile foot sensor that endows a bipedal robot with a skin-like deformable layer that captures contact deformations optically, turning foot-ground interactions into rich haptic signals. From a contact image stream, our method estimates contact pose (position and orientation), visualizes shear, computes center of pressure (CoP), classifies terrain, and detects geometric features of the contact patch. We validate these capabilities on a tilting platform and in visually obscured conditions, showing that foot-borne tactile feedback improves balance control and terrain awareness beyond proprioception alone. These findings suggest that integrating tactile perception into legged robot feet improves stability, adaptability, and environmental awareness, offering a promising direction toward more compliant and intelligent locomotion systems. For the supplementary video, please visit: https://youtu.be/ceJiy9q_2Aw

</details>


### [370] [Infinite-Dimensional Closed-Loop Inverse Kinematics for Soft Robots via Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18655)
*Carina Veil,Moritz Flaschel,Ellen Kuhl,Cosimo Della Santina*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Extends closed-loop inverse kinematics to infinite-dimensional soft-robot shape spaces by composing actuation-to-shape and shape-to-task mappings, using an infinite-dimensional chain rule to obtain a Jacobian-based CLIK, with neural operators to learn actuation-to-shape mappings; demonstrated on constant-curvature theory and a three-fiber morphoelastic soft arm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inverse kinematics for underactuated, infinite-DOF soft robots is challenging; existing CLIK assumes finite-dimensional space. The work aims to enable end-to-end differentiation over the full robot shape by modeling actuation-to-shape and shape-to-task mappings in continuous, infinite dimensions.

Method: Formulate an actuation-to-shape map followed by a shape-to-task map, derive the infinite-dimensional differential end-to-end kinematics via a chain rule, and develop a Jacobian-based CLIK. Use neural operator networks to learn the actuation-to-shape map from simulation data (differentiable). Validate analytically on a constant-curvature segment and numerically on a three-fiber soft robotic arm using morphoelasticity and active filament theory.

Result: Derived an infinite-dimensional Jacobian-based CLIK framework and demonstrated learning of the actuation-to-shape map with neural operators; validated analytically for a constant-curvature segment and applied to a three-fiber soft arm model, enabling differentiable control using full-body shape information.

Conclusion: The framework enables differentiable, end-to-end control of soft robots in infinite-dimensional shape spaces by leveraging neural operators to learn actuation-to-shape mappings and composing them with shape-to-task mappings, expanding CLIK applicability to soft robotics.

Abstract: While kinematic inversion is a purely geometric problem for fully actuated rigid robots, it becomes extremely challenging for underactuated soft robots with infinitely many degrees of freedom. Closed-loop inverse kinematics (CLIK) schemes address this by introducing end-to-end mappings from actuation to task space for the controller to operate on, but typically assume finite dimensions of the underlying virtual configuration space. In this work, we extend CLIK to the infinite-dimensional domain to reason about the entire soft robot shape while solving tasks. We do this by composing an actuation-to-shape map with a shape-to-task map, deriving the differential end-to-end kinematics via an infinite-dimensional chain rule, and thereby obtaining a Jacobian-based CLIK algorithm. Since the actuation-to-shape mapping is rarely available in closed form, we propose to learn it from simulation data using neural operator networks, which are differentiable. We first present an analytical study on a constant-curvature segment, and then apply the neural version of the algorithm to a three-fiber soft robotic arm whose underlying model relies on morphoelasticity and active filament theory. This opens new possibilities for differentiable control of soft robots by exploiting full-body shape information in a continuous, infinite-dimensional framework.

</details>


### [371] [Robotic Fruits with Tunable Stiffness and Sensing: Towards a Methodology for Developing Realistic Physical Twins of Fruits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18661)
*Saitarun Nadipineni,Keshav Pandiyan,Kaspar Althoefer,Shinichi Hirai,Thilina Dulantha Lalitharatne*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces tunable soft physical twins to mimic kiwi fruit stiffness across ripeness for benchmarking robotic grippers; demonstrates high tunability accuracy, actionable sensor feedback, and durability over cycles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address inefficiency, cost, and waste of using real fruit to benchmark soft grippers due to variability in mechanical properties; need controllable, data-driven benchmarking platforms.

Method: Developed a fiber-reinforced pneumatic physical twin of kiwi; tuned stiffness to reflect ripeness; validated via stiffness measurements across trials; used a commercial gripper to test gripping and collect sensor feedback; performed 50-cycle stress test.

Result: Stiffness tunability accuracy between 97.35% and 99.43% across trials; sensor feedback correlates with applied gripping forces; 50-cycle test shows 0.56%-1.10% stiffness error maintenance.

Conclusion: Physical twins offer sustainable, controllable benchmarks and training platforms for robotic grippers; tunable stiffness enables realistic, repeatable testing and reduces reliance on real fruit.

Abstract: The global agri-food sector faces increasing challenges from labour shortages, high consumer demand, and supply-chain disruptions, resulting in substantial losses of unharvested produce. Robotic harvesting has emerged as a promising alternative; however, evaluating and training soft grippers for delicate fruits remains difficult due to the highly variable mechanical properties of natural produce. This makes it difficult to establish reliable benchmarks or data-driven control strategies. Existing testing practices rely on large quantities of real fruit to capture this variability, leading to inefficiency, higher costs, and waste. The methodology presented in this work aims to address these limitations by developing tunable soft physical twins that emulate the stiffness characteristics of real fruits at different ripeness levels. A fiber-reinforced pneumatic physical twin of a kiwi fruit was designed and fabricated to replicate the stiffness at different ripeness levels. Experimental results show that the stiffness of the physical twin can be tuned accurately over multiple trials (97.35 - 99.43% accuracy). Gripping tasks with a commercial robotic gripper showed that sensor feedback from the physical twin can reflect the applied gripping forces. Finally, a stress test was performed over 50 cycles showed reliable maintenance of desired stiffness (0.56 - 1.10% error).
