This work shows promise that robotic physical twins could adjust their stiffness to resemble that of real fruits. This can provide a sustainable, controllable platform for benchmarking and training robotic grippers.

</details>


### [372] [Toward AI Autonomous Navigation for Mechanical Thrombectomy using Hierarchical Modular Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning (HM-MARL)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18663)
*Harry Robertshaw,Nikola Fischer,Lennart Karstensen,Benjamin Jackson,Xingyu Chen,S. M. Hadi Sadati,Christos Bergeles,Alejandro Granados,Thomas C Booth*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hierarchical Modular Multi-Agent RL (HM-MARL) enables autonomous navigation of two endovascular devices for mechanical thrombectomy; shows strong generalization in silico and promising in vitro results, but sim-to-real transfer and left-side anatomy challenges remain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce access barriers and operator dependence in mechanical thrombectomy by enabling autonomous, generalizable navigation through long vascular paths.

Method: A hierarchical modular multi-agent RL framework decomposes the navigation from femoral artery to the ICA into specialized subtasks for two devices (guide catheter and guidewire). Each subtask is learned with Soft Actor-Critic. Validation in silico (single-vasculature and multi-vasculature models) and in vitro (physical vasculature phantoms) to assess generalization and feasibility.

Result: In silico: single-vasculature models yielded 92-100% success across anatomies; multi-vasculature models yielded 56-80% across anatomies. In vitro: 100% success from femoral artery to the right common carotid artery and 80% to the right ICA; failure on left-side vessel due to anatomy and catheter type. First in vitro autonomous navigation demonstration in MT vasculature.

Conclusion: HM-MARL demonstrates generalization across anatomical variations and enables autonomous MT navigation in vitro, but sim-to-real transfer remains a challenge. Future work should incorporate world models and validate on unseen in vitro data toward clinical translation.

Abstract: Mechanical thrombectomy (MT) is typically the optimal treatment for acute ischemic stroke involving large vessel occlusions, but access is limited due to geographic and logistical barriers. Reinforcement learning (RL) shows promise in autonomous endovascular navigation, but generalization across 'long' navigation tasks remains challenging. We propose a Hierarchical Modular Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (HM-MARL) framework for autonomous two-device navigation in vitro, enabling efficient and generalizable navigation. HM-MARL was developed to autonomously navigate a guide catheter and guidewire from the femoral artery to the internal carotid artery (ICA). A modular multi-agent approach was used to decompose the complex navigation task into specialized subtasks, each trained using Soft Actor-Critic RL. The framework was validated in both in silico and in vitro testbeds to assess generalization and real-world feasibility. In silico, a single-vasculature model achieved 92-100% success rates on individual anatomies, while a multi-vasculature model achieved 56-80% across multiple patient anatomies. In vitro, both HM-MARL models successfully navigated 100% of trials from the femoral artery to the right common carotid artery and 80% to the right ICA but failed on the left-side vessel superhuman challenge due to the anatomy and catheter type used in navigation. This study presents the first demonstration of in vitro autonomous navigation in MT vasculature. While HM-MARL enables generalization across anatomies, the simulation-to-real transition introduces challenges. Future work will refine RL strategies using world models and validate performance on unseen in vitro data, advancing autonomous MT towards clinical translation.

</details>


### [373] [Scout-Rover cooperation: online terrain strength mapping and traversal risk estimation for planetary-analog explorations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18688)
*Shipeng Liu,J. Diego Caporale,Yifeng Zhang,Xingjue Liao,William Hoganson,Wilson Hu,Shivangi Misra,Neha Peddinti,Rachel Holladay,Ethan Fulcher,Akshay Ram Panyam,Andrik Puentes,Jordan M. Bretzfelder,Michael Zanetti,Uland Wong,Daniel E. Koditschek,Mark Yim,Douglas Jerolmack,Cynthia Sung,Feifei Qian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Scout-rover cooperation using a high-mobility legged scout to map regolith strength from leg-terrain interactions and feed rover locomotion models for traversal risk; validated in lunar simulant and dune analogs, demonstrating online strength mapping and risk-aware planning to extend reach in deformable terrains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable safe access to hazardous, deformable planetary terrains (e.g., Martian dunes, lunar craters) where loose regolith impedes rover mobility and risks damage.

Method: A hybrid team: legged scout measures regolith strength via proprioceptive interactions during locomotion and builds spatial terrain maps; these maps are integrated with rover mobility models to estimate traversal risk and guide path planning; validated with analogue missions at NASA Ames Lunar Simulant Testbed and White Sands Dune Field.

Result: Online terrain-strength maps derived from legged locomotion reliably capture spatial variability and predict mobility failure modes; rover-specific traversal-risk estimation enables safe navigation to scientific targets; risk-aware planning avoids hazardous regions and expands reachable science workspace.

Conclusion: Combining embodied terrain sensing with heterogeneous rover cooperation enhances operational robustness and expands the science reach in deformable planetary environments.

Abstract: Robot-aided exploration of planetary surfaces is essential for understanding geologic processes, yet many scientifically valuable regions, such as Martian dunes and lunar craters, remain hazardous due to loose, deformable regolith. We present a scout-rover cooperation framework that expands safe access to such terrain using a hybrid team of legged and wheeled robots. In our approach, a high-mobility legged robot serves as a mobile scout, using proprioceptive leg-terrain interactions to estimate regolith strength during locomotion and construct spatially resolved terrain maps. These maps are integrated with rover locomotion models to estimate traversal risk and inform path planning.
  We validate the framework through analogue missions at the NASA Ames Lunar Simulant Testbed and the White Sands Dune Field. Experiments demonstrate (1) online terrain strength mapping from legged locomotion and (2) rover-specific traversal-risk estimation enabling safe navigation to scientific targets. Results show that scout-generated terrain maps reliably capture spatial variability and predict mobility failure modes, allowing risk-aware path planning that avoids hazardous regions. By combining embodied terrain sensing with heterogeneous rover cooperation, this framework enhances operational robustness and expands the reachable science workspace in deformable planetary environments.

</details>


### [374] [CLASH: Collision Learning via Augmented Sim-to-real Hybridization to Bridge the Reality Gap](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18707)
*Haotian He,Ning Guo,Siqi Shi,Qipeng Liu,Wenzhao Lian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A data-efficient method (CLASH) learns a surrogate collision model to create a high-fidelity hybrid simulator from a weak base simulator, using very few real-world samples, which improves policy transfer and reduces collision compute time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the sim-to-real gap in contact-rich dynamics where traditional physics engines balance speed and accuracy poorly, causing transfer failures of policies trained in simulation.

Method: Distill a base model from an imperfect simulator (MuJoCo) to encode general physical priors, then fine-tune this model with a small set of real-world interactions (as few as 10 samples) to correct inertial/collision inaccuracies. Integrate the learned surrogate into a hybrid simulator that accelerates collision computation and improves predictive accuracy for policy training.

Result: Hybrid simulator achieves higher predictive accuracy and ~50% faster collision computations. Policies trained with CLASH transfer more robustly to the real world: RL-based sequential pushing success doubles; model-based control shows notable performance gains.

Conclusion: A minimal data real-world fine-tuning of a base physics model yields a high-fidelity, efficient hybrid simulator that enhances sim-to-real transfer for collision-rich tasks, suggesting broad applicability and data-efficient pathways for accurate contact dynamics in robotics.

Abstract: The sim-to-real gap, particularly in the inaccurate modeling of contact-rich dynamics like collisions, remains a primary obstacle to deploying robot policies trained in simulation. Conventional physics engines often trade accuracy for computational speed, leading to discrepancies that prevent direct policy transfer. To address this, we introduce Collision Learning via Augmented Sim-to-real Hybridization (CLASH), a data-efficient framework that creates a high-fidelity hybrid simulator by learning a surrogate collision model from a minimal set of real-world data. In CLASH, a base model is first distilled from an imperfect simulator (MuJoCo) to capture general physical priors; this model is then fine-tuned with a remarkably small number of real-world interactions (as few as 10 samples) to correct for the simulator's inherent inaccuracies. The resulting hybrid simulator not only achieves higher predictive accuracy but also reduces collision computation time by nearly 50\%. We demonstrate that policies obtained with our hybrid simulator transfer more robustly to the real world, doubling the success rate in sequential pushing tasks with reinforecement learning and significantly increase the task performance with model-based control.

</details>


### [375] [Temporal Action Representation Learning for Tactical Resource Control and Subsequent Maneuver Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18716)
*Hoseong Jung,Sungil Son,Daesol Cho,Jonghae Park,Changhyun Choi,H. Jin Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TART introduces a Temporal Action Representation framework for tactical resource control and subsequent maneuvers under hybrid action space constraints, using mutual-information-based contrastive learning to capture temporal dependencies and discretizing representations into a codebook to enable multi-modal, context-aware policies. Evaluated on maze navigation with discrete action budgets and an F-16 air combat simulator, it outperforms hybrid-action baselines by leveraging limited resources and generating coherent maneuvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in hybrid-action methods that fail to capture causal temporal dependencies between resource usage and maneuver decisions, and to represent multi-modal tactical choices in fast-evolving scenarios with limited sensing and energy budgets.

Method: Temporal Action Representation learning (TART) using a mutual-information-based contrastive objective to enforce temporal dependencies between resources and maneuvers. Learned representations are quantized via a discrete codebook to condition the policy, enabling multi-modal action planning and temporally coherent behavior. Evaluations conducted in two domains: maze navigation with limited discrete actions and a high-fidelity F-16 air combat simulator.

Result: TART consistently outperforms hybrid-action baselines in both domains, demonstrating improved resource efficiency and context-aware subsequent maneuvers through learned, discrete-conditioned representations.

Conclusion: The framework provides a principled approach to capture temporal causal structure and multi-modality in resource-constrained control, with discrete codebook conditioning enabling robust, context-aware decisions under limited sensing and energy budgets.

Abstract: Autonomous robotic systems should reason about resource control and its impact on subsequent maneuvers, especially when operating with limited energy budgets or restricted sensing. Learning-based control is effective in handling complex dynamics and represents the problem as a hybrid action space unifying discrete resource usage and continuous maneuvers. However, prior works on hybrid action space have not sufficiently captured the causal dependencies between resource usage and maneuvers. They have also overlooked the multi-modal nature of tactical decisions, both of which are critical in fast-evolving scenarios. In this paper, we propose TART, a Temporal Action Representation learning framework for Tactical resource control and subsequent maneuver generation. TART leverages contrastive learning based on a mutual information objective, designed to capture inherent temporal dependencies in resource-maneuver interactions. These learned representations are quantized into discrete codebook entries that condition the policy, capturing recurring tactical patterns and enabling multi-modal and temporally coherent behaviors. We evaluate TART in two domains where resource deployment is critical: (i) a maze navigation task where a limited budget of discrete actions provides enhanced mobility, and (ii) a high-fidelity air combat simulator in which an F-16 agent operates weapons and defensive systems in coordination with flight maneuvers. Across both domains, TART consistently outperforms hybrid-action baselines, demonstrating its effectiveness in leveraging limited resources and producing context-aware subsequent maneuvers.

</details>


### [376] [RoboCurate: Harnessing Diversity with Action-Verified Neural Trajectory for Robot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18742)
*Seungku Kim,Suhyeok Jang,Byungjun Yoon,Dongyoung Kim,John Won,Jinwoo Shin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RoboCurate uses simulator replay to filter synthetic robot actions and validate motion consistency, boosting data quality for learning; shows sizable performance gains across tabletop, DexMimicGen, and real-world dexterous tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap where synthetic data from video generation has inconsistent action quality and vision-language models cannot reliably validate physical actions; enable scalable, high-quality synthetic data for robot learning through action-consistent validation and diverse appearance augmentation.

Method: Replay predicted actions in a physics simulator to assess action quality by comparing motion consistency between simulator rollout and the generated video; augment observation diversity with image-to-image editing and action-preserving video-to-video transfer to expand appearance without altering actions.

Result: Substantial relative improvements in success rates: +70.1% on GR-1 Tabletop (300 demos), +16.1% on DexMimicGen in pre-training, +179.9% in ALLEX humanoid dexterous manipulation.

Conclusion: RoboCurate demonstrates that action-consistent simulation validation plus appearance augmentation can significantly improve learning from synthetic data, offering a scalable pathway for higher-quality robotic datasets, while noting dependencies on simulator fidelity and computational cost.

Abstract: Synthetic data generated by video generative models has shown promise for robot learning as a scalable pipeline, but it often suffers from inconsistent action quality due to imperfectly generated videos. Recently, vision-language models (VLMs) have been leveraged to validate video quality, but they have limitations in distinguishing physically accurate videos and, even then, cannot directly evaluate the generated actions themselves. To tackle this issue, we introduce RoboCurate, a novel synthetic robot data generation framework that evaluates and filters the quality of annotated actions by comparing them with simulation replay. Specifically, RoboCurate replays the predicted actions in a simulator and assesses action quality by measuring the consistency of motion between the simulator rollout and the generated video. In addition, we unlock observation diversity beyond the available dataset via image-to-image editing and apply action-preserving video-to-video transfer to further augment appearance. We observe RoboCurate's generated data yield substantial relative improvements in success rates compared to using real data only, achieving +70.1% on GR-1 Tabletop (300 demos), +16.1% on DexMimicGen in the pre-training setup, and +179.9% in the challenging real-world ALLEX humanoid dexterous manipulation setting.

</details>


### [377] [Learning to Localize Reference Trajectories in Image-Space for Visual Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18803)
*Finn Lukas Busch,Matti Vahs,Quantao Yang,Jesús Gerardo Ortega Peimbert,Yixi Cai,Jana Tumova,Olov Andersson*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LoTIS introduces robot-agnostic visual navigation by localizing a reference RGB trajectory in the current view to guide navigation without camera calibration or robot-specific training; demonstrates strong cross-embodiment generalization with high success rates across sim/real and challenging tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for robot-agnostic, calibration-free visual navigation that generalizes across different robots and viewpoints by decoupling perception from action and avoiding imitation of robot-specific behavioral priors.

Method: Predict image-space coordinates of reference trajectory points as they would appear in the robot's current view, effectively localizing the trajectory in the image plane. Train with cross-trajectory variation to improve robustness to viewpoint and camera changes. Use robot-agnostic, image-space guidance that integrates with local planners, enabling zero-shot transfer across embodiments without camera calibration or robot-specific training.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 20–50 percentage points in success rate on conventional forward navigation, achieving 94–98% success across diverse simulated and real environments. Shows over 5x improvements on challenging tasks (e.g., backward traversal) where baselines fail. Demonstrates that even a video from a phone camera can enable different robots to navigate to any point on the trajectory; code, demo, and videos available.

Conclusion: Decoupling perception from action and localizing trajectory points in image space yields robust, robot-agnostic guidance that transfers zero-shot across embodiments and aligns well with local planning. The approach reduces reliance on camera calibration and robot-specific training while delivering strong realistic performance.

Abstract: We present LoTIS, a model for visual navigation that provides robot-agnostic image-space guidance by localizing a reference RGB trajectory in the robot's current view, without requiring camera calibration, poses, or robot-specific training. Instead of predicting actions tied to specific robots, we predict the image-space coordinates of the reference trajectory as they would appear in the robot's current view. This creates robot-agnostic visual guidance that easily integrates with local planning. Consequently, our model's predictions provide guidance zero-shot across diverse embodiments. By decoupling perception from action and learning to localize trajectory points rather than imitate behavioral priors, we enable a cross-trajectory training strategy for robustness to viewpoint and camera changes. We outperform state-of-the-art methods by 20-50 percentage points in success rate on conventional forward navigation, achieving 94-98% success rate across diverse sim and real environments. Furthermore, we achieve over 5x improvements on challenging tasks where baselines fail, such as backward traversal. The system is straightforward to use: we show how even a video from a phone camera directly enables different robots to navigate to any point on the trajectory. Videos, demo, and code are available at https://finnbusch.com/lotis.

</details>


### [378] [Habilis-$β$: A Fast-Motion and Long-Lasting On-Device Vision-Language-Action Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18813)
*Tommoro Robotics,:,Jesoon Kang,Taegeon Park,Jisu An,Soo Min Kimm,Jaejoon Kim,Jinu Pahk,Byungju Kim,Junseok Lee,Namheon Baek,Sungwan Ha,Hojun Baek,Eduardo Ayerve Cruz,Wontae Kim,Junghyeon Choi,Yousuk Lee,Joonmo Han,Sunghyun Cho,Sunghyun Kwon,Soyoung Lee,Jun Ki Lee,Seung-Joon Yi,Byoung-Tak Zhang,Theo Taeyeong Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Habilis-β is a fast, long-lasting on-device Vision-Language-Action model evaluated with a Productivity-Reliability Plane (Tasks per Hour and Mean Time Between Intervention). It outperforms a π0.5 baseline in both simulation and real-world scenarios and achieves leading RoboTwin 2.0 results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between single-trial success metrics and real-world continuous operation by introducing metrics that capture speed and durability in long-running deployment.

Method: Pre-train language-free on large-scale play data to learn interaction priors; fine-tune with cyclic task demonstrations to model state drift. Use ESPADA for phase-adaptive motion shaping, rectified-flow distillation for high-frequency control on edge devices, and classifier-free guidance as a deployment-time knob to balance instruction adherence with priors.

Result: In 1-hour continuous-run evaluations, Habilis-β achieves PRP metrics. Simulation: 572.6 TPH and 39.2 s MTBI (vs. 120.5 TPH and 30.5 s for π0.5). Real-world humanoid logistics: 124 TPH and 137.4 s MTBI (vs. 19 TPH and 46.1 s for π0.5). Achieves highest reported performance on RoboTwin 2.0 leaderboard across representative tasks.

Conclusion: A cohesive design—robust priors via play data, state-drift-aware demonstrations, motion shaping, efficient distillation, and deployment-time CFG—yields a fast, robust on-device VLA system suitable for continuous real-world operation and competitive benchmarking.

Abstract: We introduce Habilis-$β$, a fast-motion and long-lasting on-device vision-language-action (VLA) model designed for real-world deployment. Current VLA evaluation remains largely confined to single-trial success rates under curated resets, which fails to capture the fast-motion and long-lasting capabilities essential for practical operation. To address this, we introduce the Productivity-Reliability Plane (PRP), which evaluates performance through Tasks per Hour (TPH) and Mean Time Between Intervention (MTBI) under a continuous-run protocol that demands both high-speed execution and sustained robustness. Habilis-$β$ achieves high performance by integrating language-free pre-training on large-scale play data for robust interaction priors with post-training on cyclic task demonstrations that capture state drift across consecutive task iterations. The system further employs ESPADA for phase-adaptive motion shaping to accelerate free-space transit, utilizes rectified-flow distillation to enable high-frequency control on edge devices, and incorporates classifier-free guidance (CFG) as a deployment-time knob to dynamically balance instruction adherence and learned interaction priors. In 1-hour continuous-run evaluations, Habilis-$β$ achieves strong performance under the PRP metrics, compared to $π_{0.5}$ in both simulation and real-world environments. In simulation, Habilis-$β$ achieves 572.6 TPH and 39.2 s MTBI (vs. 120.5 TPH and 30.5 s for $π_{0.5}$), while in a real-world humanoid logistics workflow it achieves 124 TPH and 137.4 s MTBI (vs. 19 TPH and 46.1 s for $π_{0.5}$). Finally, Habilis-$β$ achieves the highest reported performance on the standard RoboTwin 2.0 leaderboard across representative tasks, validating its effectiveness in complex manipulation scenarios.

</details>


### [379] [RotorSuite: A MATLAB/Simulink Toolbox for Tilt Multi-Rotor UAV Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18814)
*Nicola Cigarini,Giulia Michieletto,Angelo Cenedese*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A MATLAB/Simulink toolbox (RotorSuite) for modeling and simulating a wide class of multi-rotor platforms (including tilted/tilting configurations) using analytical and physics-based approaches, with docs and examples to speed up accurate dynamics modeling for research, education, and development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce time, user dependency, and errors in building accurate dynamic models of advanced rotorcraft platforms as they evolve beyond standard coplanar/collinear quadrotors.

Method: Develop a dual-approach toolbox in MATLAB/Simulink providing analytical and physics-based dynamic models; supports various geometries, inputs, and high-level interfaces; includes documentation and example use cases.

Result: RotorSuite toolbox with comprehensive docs and examples enabling didactic, research, and industrial use for modeling and simulating multi-rotor dynamics.

Conclusion: The toolbox fills a gap between theory and practice, enabling rapid, accurate modeling, validation, and development for next-generation aerial platforms.

Abstract: In recent years, aerial platforms have evolved from passive flying sensors into versatile, contact-aware robotic systems, leading to rapid advances in platform design. Standard coplanar and collinear quadrotors have been complemented by modern tilted and tilting multi-rotor platforms with enhanced maneuverability. To properly analyze, control, and validate the performance of these emerging platforms, an accurate modeling step is required; however, this can be time-consuming, user-dependent and error-prone. To address this issue, we propose a MATLAB/Simulink toolbox for modeling and simulating the dynamics of a broad class of multi-rotor platforms through both an analytical and physics-based approaches. The toolbox, named RotorSuite, is provided with comprehensive documentation and example use cases, representing a valuable tool for didactic, research, and industrial development purposes.

</details>


### [380] [GRAB: A Systematic Real-World Grasping Benchmark for Robotic Food Waste Sorting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18835)
*Moniesha Thilakarathna,Xing Wang,Min Wang,David Hinwood,Shuangzhe Liu,Damith Herath*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GRAB provides a real-world, large-scale benchmark for robotic grasping in food-contaminant sorting, integrating pre-grasp conditions and deformable objects, with 1,750 attempts across four high-fidelity scenes; finds object quality dominates performance in clutter, with vision quality and clutter level also affecting grasping; advocates multimodal grippers for cross-category robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks rely on limited simulations and simple metrics, neglecting pre-grasp conditions and environmental variability; need a comprehensive, real-world dataset to evaluate graspability under challenging, real-world food waste sorting scenarios.

Method: Develop GRAB framework with diverse deformable objects, advanced grasp-pose estimation, and explicit pre-grasp condition metrics; conduct large-scale experimental evaluation—1,750 contaminant grasp attempts across four high-fidelity scenes—comparing multiple industrial grasping modalities; measure graspability under heterogeneous conditions.

Result: Reveals distinct gripper strengths and limitations; object quality is the dominant factor in cluttered settings; vision quality and clutter levels have moderate impact; performance varies by object and modality; practical implication: mandates multimodal gripper development for cross-category robustness.

Conclusion: To enable effective robotic food waste sorting, benchmarks must incorporate pre-grasp conditions, diverse object qualities, and real-world scene clutter; GRAB provides a framework that can guide design choices and evaluation of multimodal grippers and perception pipelines.

Abstract: Food waste management is critical for sustainability, yet inorganic contaminants hinder recycling potential. Robotic automation presents a compelling approach to this challenge by accelerating the sorting process through automated contaminant removal. Still, the diverse and unpredictable nature of contaminants creates major challenges for robotic grasping. Benchmarking frameworks are critical for evaluating challenges from various perspectives. However, existing protocols rely on limited simulation datasets, prioritise simple metrics such as success rate, and overlook key object and environment-related pre-grasp conditions. This paper introduces GRAB, a comprehensive Grasping Real-World Article Benchmarking framework that addresses this gap by integrating diverse deformable objects, advanced grasp-pose-estimation vision, and, importantly, pre-grasp conditions, establishing a set of critical graspability metrics. It systematically compares industrial grasping modalities through an in-depth experimental evaluation involving 1,750 food contaminant grasp attempts across four high-fidelity scenes. This large-scale evaluation provides an extensive assessment of grasp performance for food waste sorting, offering a level of depth that has rarely been explored in previous studies. The results reveal distinct gripper strengths and limitations, with object quality emerging as the dominant performance factor in cluttered environments, while vision quality and clutter levels play moderate roles. These findings highlight essential design considerations and reinforce the necessity of developing multimodal gripper technologies capable of robust cross-category performance for effective robotic food waste sorting.

</details>


### [381] [When the Inference Meets the Explicitness or Why Multimodality Can Make Us Forget About the Perfect Predictor](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18850)
*J. E. Domínguez-Vidal,Alberto Sanfeliu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hybrid human intention inference and explicit communication improves human-robot collaborative object transportation; combined system preferred; natural interaction matters; performance thresholds affect user perception.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address uncertainty in predicting human intentions and explore explicit communication to elicit intent; evaluate how different systems affect collaboration in a dynamic task with obstacles.

Method: Four systems: two intention predictors (force-based, velocity-based) and two explicit interfaces (button, voice). Integration into IVO robot with force sensor and LiDAR; 75 volunteers, 255 runs, three rounds (inference, communication, combined). Task: transport object 5-7 m with obstacles; measure user preferences and performance.

Result: Results indicate that after achieving sufficient performance, users don't notice improvements and view them positively; users prefer more natural systems even with higher failure rates; the best option is the combination of both strategies.

Conclusion: A hybrid approach combining prediction-based and explicit communication provides the best human-robot collaboration; design should optimize for naturalness and robustness.

Abstract: Although in the literature it is common to find predictors and inference systems that try to predict human intentions, the uncertainty of these models due to the randomness of human behavior has led some authors to start advocating the use of communication systems that explicitly elicit human intention. In this work, it is analyzed the use of four different communication systems with a human-robot collaborative object transportation task as experimental testbed: two intention predictors (one based on force prediction and another with an enhanced velocity prediction algorithm) and two explicit communication methods (a button interface and a voice-command recognition system). These systems were integrated into IVO, a custom mobile social robot equipped with force sensor to detect the force exchange between both agents and LiDAR to detect the environment. The collaborative task required transporting an object over a 5-7 meter distance with obstacles in the middle, demanding rapid decisions and precise physical coordination. 75 volunteers perform a total of 255 executions divided into three groups, testing inference systems in the first round, communication systems in the second, and the combined strategies in the third. The results show that, 1) once sufficient performance is achieved, the human no longer notices and positively assesses technical improvements; 2) the human prefers systems that are more natural to them even though they have higher failure rates; and 3) the preferred option is the right combination of both systems.

</details>


### [382] [Gait Asymmetry from Unilateral Weakness and Improvement With Ankle Assistance: a Reinforcement Learning based Simulation Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18862)
*Yifei Yuan,Ghaith Androwis,Xianlian Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RL-based musculoskeletal simulation assesses unilateral weakness effects on gait symmetry and evaluates ankle exoskeleton assistance, showing increasing asymmetry with greater impairment and partial symmetry improvement with 50% strength.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unilateral muscle weakness commonly causes asymmetric gait and disrupts interlimb coordination. A simulation- and learning-based workflow is needed to quantify impairment effects and preliminarily assess assistive strategies before human experiments.

Method: A reinforcement learning–driven musculoskeletal model induces asymmetry by reducing right-leg muscle strength to 75%, 50%, and 25% of baseline. Gait asymmetry is quantified via toe-off timing, peak contact forces, and joint-level symmetry metrics; ankle ROM symmetry is summarized with a symmetry index (SI) and correlation (r). Ankle exoskeleton assistance is evaluated at 50% strength.

Result: Progressive weakness yielded larger temporal and kinematic asymmetry, most pronounced at the ankle. Ankle ROM symmetry degraded from near-symmetric at 100% strength (SI = +6.4%; r = 0.974) to severe asymmetry at 25% strength (SI = -47.1%; r = 0.889) with a load shift toward the unimpaired limb. At 50% strength, ankle exoskeleton support improved kinematic symmetry vs. unassisted defeat, reducing ankle SI from 25.8% to 18.5% and increasing ankle r from 0.948 to 0.966, though peak loading remained biased toward the unimpaired side.

Conclusion: The framework enables controlled evaluation of impairment severity and assistive strategies, providing a basis for future validation in human experiments and aiding early controller development.

Abstract: Unilateral muscle weakness often leads to asymmetric gait, disrupting interlimb coordination and stance timing. This study presents a reinforcement learning (RL) based musculoskeletal simulation framework to (1) quantify how progressive unilateral muscle weakness affects gait symmetry and (2) evaluate whether ankle exoskeleton assistance can improve gait symmetry under impaired conditions. The overarching goal is to establish a simulation- and learning-based workflow that supports early controller development prior to patient experiments. Asymmetric gait was induced by reducing right-leg muscle strength to 75%, 50%, and 25% of baseline. Gait asymmetry was quantified using toe-off timing, peak contact forces, and joint-level symmetry metrics. Increasing weakness produced progressively larger temporal and kinematic asymmetry, most pronounced at the ankle. Ankle range of motion symmetry degraded from near-symmetric behavior at 100% strength (symmetry index, SI = +6.4%; correlation r=0.974) to severe asymmetry at 25% strength (SI = -47.1%, r=0.889), accompanied by a load shift toward the unimpaired limb. At 50% strength, ankle exoskeleton assistance improved kinematic symmetry relative to the unassisted impaired condition, reducing the magnitude of ankle SI from 25.8% to 18.5% and increasing ankle correlation from r=0.948 to 0.966, although peak loading remained biased toward the unimpaired side. Overall, this framework supports controlled evaluation of impairment severity and assistive strategies, and provides a basis for future validation in human experiments.

</details>


### [383] [Equivalence and Divergence of Bayesian Log-Odds and Dempster's Combination Rule for 2D Occupancy Grids](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18872)
*Tatiana Berlenko,Kirill Krinkin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BetP-matched pignistic framework shows Bayesian log-odds fusion generally outperforms Dempster's rule in occupancy grids, but results depend on the chosen matching criterion; reusable for future comparisons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To fairly compare fusion rules (Bayesian log-odds vs Dempster's rule) independent of sensor parameterization using pignistic transformation in occupancy-grid mapping.

Method: Develop a pignistic-transform-based methodology to align per-observation decision probabilities (BetP matching and normalized plausibility matching) and evaluate Bayesian vs Dempster fusion across simulations, real LiDAR datasets, and downstream planning.

Result: Under BetP matching: 15/15 directional consistency, p = 3.1e-5; small absolute differences (0.001-0.022) favor Bayesian fusion. Under normalized plausibility matching: direction reverses, indicating the result depends on the matching criterion.

Conclusion: The BetP-based methodology effectively isolates the fusion-rule effect; conclusions are criterion-dependent; framework is reusable for future Bayesian/belief-function comparisons.

Abstract: We introduce a pignistic-transform-based methodology for fair comparison of Bayesian log-odds and Dempster's combination rule in occupancy grid mapping, matching per-observation decision probabilities to isolate the fusion rule from sensor parameterization. Under BetP matching across simulation, two real lidar datasets, and downstream path planning, Bayesian fusion is consistently favored (15/15 directional consistency, p = 3.1e-5) with small absolute differences (0.001-0.022). Under normalized plausibility matching, the direction reverses, confirming the result is matching-criterion-specific. The methodology is reusable for any future Bayesian/belief function comparison.

</details>


### [384] [Temporal-Logic-Aware Frontier-Based Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18951)
*Azizollah Taheri,Derya Aksaray*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces commit states in an automaton for scLTL motion planning under unknown label locations, enabling sound and complete frontier-based exploration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unknown label locations hinder reliable satisfaction of scLTL specifications; need to preserve all potential satisfying paths while the robot makes progress.

Method: Define commit states that capture irreversible action consequences, integrate them into a frontier-based exploration framework to maintain all feasible satisfying paths while advancing toward the task.

Result: The algorithm is sound and complete; validated through simulations demonstrating effective progression toward task satisfaction under uncertainty.

Conclusion: Commit-state framework enhances temporal logic motion planning in unknown environments, with potential extensions to real-world tests and scalability analyses.

Abstract: This paper addresses the problem of temporal logic motion planning for an autonomous robot operating in an unknown environment. The objective is to enable the robot to satisfy a syntactically co-safe Linear Temporal Logic (scLTL) specification when the exact locations of the desired labels are not known a priori. We introduce a new type of automaton state, referred to as commit states. These states capture intermediate task progress resulting from actions whose consequences are irreversible. In other words, certain future paths to satisfaction become not feasible after taking those actions that lead to the commit states. By leveraging commit states, we propose a sound and complete frontier-based exploration algorithm that strategically guides the robot to make progress toward the task while preserving all possible ways of satisfying it. The efficacy of the proposed method is validated through simulations.

</details>


### [385] [TactEx: An Explainable Multimodal Robotic Interaction Framework for Human-Like Touch and Hardness Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18967)
*Felix Verstraete,Lan Wei,Wen Fan,Dandan Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TactEx unifies vision, touch, and language to estimate object hardness and guide manipulation, demonstrated on fruit-ripeness assessment with explainable, multimodal interfaces and robust segmentation; achieves 90% task success and generalizes to new tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hardness perception is critical for safe, dexterous manipulation in contact-rich robotics, and there is a need for explainable, multimodal perception that integrates tactile sensing with vision and language grounding.

Method: Multimodal architecture fusing GelSight-Mini tactile streams with RGB, using a ResNet50+LSTM to estimate hardness from sequential tactile data; a cross-modal alignment module integrates visual cues with guidance from a large language model; Grounded-SAM (GSAM) preferred over YOLO for fine-grained contact-site segmentation; a lightweight LLM produces grounded natural-language explanations linked to tactile outputs; end-to-end evaluation on fruit ripeness with statistical significance.

Result: The method achieves statistically significant class separation (p < 0.01 for all fruit pairs) in hardness estimation; end-to-end tasks reach 90% success on simple user queries; GSAM offers superior robustness for contact-site segmentation; the approach generalizes to novel tasks without large-scale tuning.

Conclusion: Combining pretrained visual and tactile models with language grounding can realize explainable, human-like touch perception and decision-making in robotics, enabling robust hardness estimation and interactive guidance across tasks.

Abstract: Accurate perception of object hardness is essential for safe and dexterous contact-rich robotic manipulation. Here, we present TactEx, an explainable multimodal robotic interaction framework that unifies vision, touch, and language for human-like hardness estimation and interactive guidance. We evaluate TactEx on fruit-ripeness assessment, a representative task that requires both tactile sensing and contextual understanding. The system fuses GelSight-Mini tactile streams with RGB observations and language prompts. A ResNet50+LSTM model estimates hardness from sequential tactile data, while a cross-modal alignment module combines visual cues with guidance from a large language model (LLM). This explainable multimodal interface allows users to distinguish ripeness levels with statistically significant class separation (p < 0.01 for all fruit pairs). For touch placement, we compare YOLO with Grounded-SAM (GSAM) and find GSAM to be more robust for fine-grained segmentation and contact-site selection. A lightweight LLM parses user instructions and produces grounded natural-language explanations linked to the tactile outputs. In end-to-end evaluations, TactEx attains 90% task success on simple user queries and generalises to novel tasks without large-scale tuning. These results highlight the promise of combining pretrained visual and tactile models with language grounding to advance explainable, human-like touch perception and decision-making in robotics.

</details>


### [386] [Bumper Drone: Elastic Morphology Design for Aerial Physical Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18976)
*Pongporn Supa,Alex Dunnett,Feng Xiao,Rui Wu,Mirko Kovac,Basaran Bahadir Kocer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A drone with elastic horns enables touch-and-go contact with walls, leveraging passive dynamics to stabilize near obstacles and reduce pitch oscillations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address uncertain contact forces in aerial physical interactions by exploiting environmental contact as embodied control, enabling near-obstacle navigation without active obstacle avoidance.

Method: Equip a quadrotor with elastic horns creating a mass-spring-damper-like contact dynamic; observe self-regulated bumping motion and passive stabilization; compare elastic vs rigid horns and horn geometries; use standard attitude PID for sustained contact.

Result: Elastic horns absorb impact energy and maintain stability, reducing pitch oscillations; 38% reduction with the lower horn configuration yields ~54% improvement for the lower arrangement; platform also achieves stable sustained contact with static objects using a conventional attitude controller.

Conclusion: Environmental interaction via elastic horn design can serve as embodied control to simplify near-obstacle navigation and contact, with elastic dampening improving stability and energy absorption, reducing the need for active sensing and control.

Abstract: Aerial robots are evolving from avoiding obstacles to exploiting the environmental contact interactions for navigation, exploration and manipulation. A key challenge in such aerial physical interactions lies in handling uncertain contact forces on unknown targets, which typically demand accurate sensing and active control. We present a drone platform with elastic horns that enables touch-and-go manoeuvres - a self-regulated, consecutive bumping motion that allows the drone to maintain proximity to a wall without relying on active obstacle avoidance. It leverages environmental interaction as a form of embodied control, where low-level stabilisation and near-obstacle navigation emerge from the passive dynamic responses of the drone-obstacle system that resembles a mass-spring-damper system. Experiments show that the elastic horn can absorb impact energy while maintaining vehicle stability, reducing pitch oscillations by 38% compared to the rigid horn configuration. The lower horn arrangement was found to reduce pitch oscillations by approximately 54%. In addition to intermittent contact, the platform equipped with elastic horns also demonstrates stable, sustained contact with static objects, relying on a standard attitude PID controller.

</details>


### [387] [FruitTouch: A Perceptive Gripper for Gentle and Scalable Fruit Harvesting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18991)
*Ruohan Zhang,Mohammad Amin Mirzaee,Wenzhen Yuan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FruitTouch is a compact sensorized gripper with embedded vision-based tactile sensing, enabling robust, size-agnostic fruit grasping with real-time force, slip, and softness feedback; validated in real-world harvesting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rising labor shortages in fruit harvesting create demand for reliable, compact grippers that can handle diverse fruits while providing rich tactile feedback to prevent damage and improve picking efficiency.

Method: Design of a compact gripper integrating high-resolution vision-based tactile sensing via an optimized optical path and embedded camera, enabling real-time force estimation, slip detection, and softness prediction; aims for wide size range, low cost, and mechanical simplicity.

Result: Real-world fruit-harvesting experiments show robust grasp stability and effective damage prevention, validating the approach.

Conclusion: FruitTouch delivers a compact, cost-effective solution that leverages tactile sensing to enable stable, safe grasping across diverse fruits and assists real-time decision making in automated harvesting.

Abstract: The automation of fruit harvesting has gained increasing significance in response to rising labor shortages. A sensorized gripper is a key component of this process, which must be compact enough for confined spaces, able to stably grasp diverse fruits, and provide reliable feedback on fruit conditions for efficient harvesting. To address this need, we propose FruitTouch, a compact gripper that integrates high-resolution, vision-based tactile sensing through an optimized optical design. This configuration accommodates a wide range of fruit sizes while maintaining low cost and mechanical simplicity. Tactile images captured by an embedded camera provide rich information for real-time force estimation, slip detection, and softness prediction. We validate the gripper in real-world fruit harvesting experiments, demonstrating robust grasp stability and effective damage prevention.

</details>


### [388] [A Checklist for Deploying Robots in Public: Articulating Tacit Knowledge in the HRI Community](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19038)
*Claire Liang,Franziska Babel,Hannah Pelikan,Sydney Thompson,Xiang Zhi Tan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A guideline-based, open-source checklist for preparing public robot deployments in HRI, organized as modular flip cards within a hierarchical deployment-phase framework, refined by community input and demonstrated in real studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uncover and codify tacit knowledge in public HRI deployments, reducing guardrail omissions and the barrier to entry for researchers and practitioners.

Method: Derives essential topics from researchers’ experiences; conducts interviews with six interdisciplinary public HRI experts; arranges topics as modular flip cards in a hierarchical table aligned with deployment phases and key domains; validates via application to real public studies; releases as an interactive, customizable web resource.

Result: Produces a reusable, evolvable checklist/resource (cards, list, and web tool) that captures joint expertise and can be updated by the community; demonstrated in real deployments.

Conclusion: The checklist is a practical, adaptable resource that consolidates tacit knowledge for public HRI, enabling better preparation, transparency, and continual evolution of best practices.

Abstract: Many of the challenges encountered in in-the-wild public deployments of robots remain undocumented despite sharing many common pitfalls. This creates a high barrier of entry and results in repetition of avoidable mistakes. To articulate the tacit knowledge in the HRI community, this paper presents a guideline in the form of a checklist to support researchers in preparing for robot deployments in public. Drawing on their own experience with public robot deployments, the research team collected essential topics to consider in public HRI research. These topics are represented as modular flip cards in a hierarchical table, structured into deployment phases and important domains. We interviewed six interdisciplinary researchers with expertise in public HRI and show how including community input refines the checklist. We further show the checklist in action in context of real public studies. Finally, we contribute the checklist as an open-source, customizable community resource that both collects joint expertise for continual evolution and is usable as a list, set of cards, and an interactive web tool.

</details>


### [389] [Path planning for unmanned surface vehicle based on predictive artificial potential field. International Journal of Advanced Robotic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19062)
*Jia Song,Ce Hao,Jiangcheng Su*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A time-aware predictive artificial potential field (PAPF) for high-speed USVs improves path smoothness and efficiency by addressing local minima and turning-angle limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional artificial potential field (APF) methods often suffer from local minima, abrupt turns, and suboptimal global planning for high-speed unmanned surface vehicles, leading to longer travel times and higher energy use.

Method: Develop a predictive APF that incorporates time information and predictive potential; introduce three modifications—angle limit, velocity adjustment, and predictive potential—to enhance path feasibility and flatness; compare with traditional APF and validate via simulations, focusing on reachability and local minima handling.

Result: PAPF constrains the maximum turning angle, shortens sailing time, and improves obstacle avoidance compared to traditional APF; it mitigates concave local minima, improves reachability in challenging scenarios, and yields more energy-efficient paths for USVs.

Conclusion: The time-aware predictive APF provides smoother, faster, and more energy-efficient trajectories for high-speed USVs, addressing key weaknesses of traditional APF; further validation and real-world testing are warranted to confirm practical effectiveness.

Abstract: Path planning for high-speed unmanned surface vehicles requires more complex solutions to reduce sailing time and save energy. This article proposes a new predictive artificial potential field that incorporates time information and predictive potential to plan smoother paths. It explores the principles of the artificial potential field, considering vehicle dynamics and local minimum reachability. The study first analyzes the most advanced traditional artificial potential field and its drawbacks in global and local path planning. It then introduces three modifications to the predictive artificial potential field-angle limit, velocity adjustment, and predictive potential to enhance the feasibility and flatness of the generated path. A comparison between the traditional and predictive artificial potential fields demonstrates that the latter successfully restricts the maximum turning angle, shortens sailing time, and intelligently avoids obstacles. Simulation results further verify that the predictive artificial potential field addresses the concave local minimum problem and improves reachability in special scenarios, ultimately generating a more efficient path that reduces sailing time and conserves energy for unmanned surface vehicles.

</details>


### [390] [Design, Locomotion, and Control of Amphibious Robots: Recent Advances](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19077)
*Yi Jin,Chang Liu,Roger D. Quinn,Robert J. Wood,C. Chase Cao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A concise synthesis of advances in amphibious robots focusing on locomotion, actuation, and sensor-control integration with emphasis on autonomy and adaptability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge land-water capabilities to enable conservation, disaster response, and defense, requiring robust, versatile, and efficient amphibious robots.

Method: Literature review of recent progress in movement strategies, material-based actuators, and control systems; synthesis of challenges and opportunities.

Result: Identifies key trends in locomotion strategies, actuators, and autonomy/control architectures; highlights interdisciplinary needs and open problems.

Conclusion: Future work should pursue more efficient, resilient, and multifunctional amphibious robots, with integrated sensing, control, and adaptive materials to handle diverse environments.

Abstract: Amphibious robots, operating seamlessly across land and water, are advancing applications in conservation, disaster response, and defense. Their performance depends on locomotion mechanisms, actuation technologies, and sensor-control integration. This review highlights recent progress in these areas, examining movement strategies, material-based actuators, and control systems for autonomy and adaptability. Challenges and opportunities are outlined to guide future research toward more efficient, resilient, and multifunctional amphibious robots.

</details>


### [391] [A User-driven Design Framework for Robotaxi](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19107)
*Yue Deng,Changyang He*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Qualitative study of real-world robotaxi experiences via 18 semi-structured interviews and autoethnography; reveals motivations, benefits, and challenges, and proposes an end-to-end user-driven design framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill the gap in understanding passenger experience beyond technical driving performance by moving from simulations to real-world robotaxi use.

Method: Qualitative study using 18 semi-structured interviews and autoethnographic ride experiences; thematic analysis of user experiences and perceptions.

Result: Findings: motivations (low cost, social recommendation, curiosity); benefits (sense of agency, consistent driving/ride experience); challenges (limited flexibility, opaque access, management difficulty, robustness in edge cases, emergency handling); privacy, safety, ethics, and trust shape experiences; privacy often indeﬁerent but sensitive to leakage; safety perceptions polarized; ethical concerns include accountability and absence of human-like social norms; proposed a user-driven design framework across end-to-end journey: pre-ride configuration, context-aware pickup facilitation, in-ride explainability, and post-ride feedback.

Conclusion: Advocates a user-centered design framework to guide robotaxi interaction and service design across the entire trip lifecycle.

Abstract: Robotaxis are emerging as a promising form of urban mobility, yet research has largely emphasized technical driving performance while leaving open how passengers experience and evaluate rides without a human driver. To address the limitations of prior work that often relies on simulated or hypothetical settings, we investigate real-world robotaxi use through 18 semi-structured interviews and autoethnographic ride experiences. We found that users were drawn to robotaxis by low cost, social recommendation, and curiosity. They valued a distinctive set of benefits, such as an increased sense of agency, and consistent driving behavioral consistency and standardized ride experiences. However, they encountered persistent challenges around limited flexibility, insufficient transparency, management difficulty, robustness concerns in edge cases, and emergency handling concerns. Robotaxi experiences were shaped by privacy, safety, ethics, and trust. Users were often privacy-indifferent yet sensitive to opaque access and leakage risks; safety perceptions were polarized; and ethical considerations surfaced round issues such as accountability, feedback responsibility and absence of human-like social norms. Based on these findings, we propose a user-driven design framework spanning the end-to-end journey, such as pre-ride configuration (hailing), context-aware pickup facilitation (pick-up) in-ride explainability (traveling), and accountable post-ride feedback (drop-off) to guide robotaxi interaction and service design.

</details>


### [392] [Understanding Fire Through Thermal Radiation Fields for Mobile Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19108)
*Anton R. Wagner,Madhan Balaji Rao,Xuesu Xiao,Sören Pirk*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a real-time 3D thermal radiation field for fire-aware navigation by fusing depth and thermal data, estimating radiation in empty spaces via Stefan-Boltzmann law, and embedding this field into a cost map to plan thermally safe paths; validated on a Spot robot in controlled experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous robots in fire-affected environments must understand heat hazards to navigate safely. Real-time, spatially continuous thermal information enables safer trajectories and expands capabilities for search-and-rescue and firefighting.

Method: 1) Register depth and thermal images to create a 3D point cloud annotated with temperature. 2) Detect fires from temperature data. 3) Use Stefan-Boltzmann law to approximate radiative heat in unobserved/empty spaces. 4) Build a continuous thermal radiation field across the environment. 5) Incorporate this field into the navigation cost map to compute collision-free, thermally safe paths. 6) Validate on a Boston Dynamics Spot in controlled experiments.

Result: The approach enables avoidance of hazardous thermal regions while achieving navigation goals. Demonstrated feasibility of constructing and utilizing a real-time thermal field for planning in fire-relevant environments.

Conclusion: This work advances autonomous deployment in fire-affected environments by providing an end-to-end framework to perceive, estimate, and reason about thermal hazards for safe navigation; applicable to search-and-rescue, firefighting, and hazardous material response.

Abstract: Safely moving through environments affected by fire is a critical capability for autonomous mobile robots deployed in disaster response. In this work, we present a novel approach for mobile robots to understand fire through building real-time thermal radiation fields. We register depth and thermal images to obtain a 3D point cloud annotated with temperature values. From these data, we identify fires and use the Stefan-Boltzmann law to approximate the thermal radiation in empty spaces. This enables the construction of a continuous thermal radiation field over the environment. We show that this representation can be used for robot navigation, where we embed thermal constraints into the cost map to compute collision-free and thermally safe paths. We validate our approach on a Boston Dynamics Spot robot in controlled experimental settings. Our experiments demonstrate the robot's ability to avoid hazardous regions while still reaching navigation goals. Our approach paves the way toward mobile robots that can be autonomously deployed in fire-affected environments, with potential applications in search-and-rescue, firefighting, and hazardous material response.

</details>


### [393] [Distributed and Consistent Multi-Robot Visual-Inertial-Ranging Odometry on Lie Groups](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19173)
*Ziwei Kang,Yizhi Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Distributed collaborative VIO with UWB (DC-VIRO) improves multi-robot localization by jointly estimating robot states and anchor positions, leveraging inter-robot observations and a right-invariant Lie-group formulation to preserve observability and enable anchor self-calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In GPS-denied multi-robot scenarios, VIO suffers from drift and UWB-based methods often assume a single robot with pre-calibrated anchors. A distributed approach that jointly utilizes VIO, UWB, and shared anchor observations can enhance robustness and autonomy.

Method: Tightly fuse visual-inertial odometry with UWB ranging across multiple robots. Explicitly include anchor positions in the system state to handle calibration uncertainty. Use inter-robot communication to share anchor observations, providing additional geometric constraints. Employ a right-invariant error formulation on Lie groups to preserve VIO observability and estimator consistency in a distributed setting.

Result: Simulation studies with multiple robots show notable improvements in localization accuracy and robustness. The framework also enables anchor self-calibration within distributed operation.

Conclusion: DC-VIRO effectively addresses anchor calibration uncertainty and drift in GPS-denied multi-robot localization by fusing VIO and UWB in a distributed, observability-preserving framework, suitable for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Reliable localization is a fundamental requirement for multi-robot systems operating in GPS-denied environments. Visual-inertial odometry (VIO) provides lightweight and accurate motion estimation but suffers from cumulative drift in the absence of global references. Ultra-wideband (UWB) ranging offers complementary global observations, yet most existing UWB-aided VIO methods are designed for single-robot scenarios and rely on pre-calibrated anchors, which limits their robustness in practice. This paper proposes a distributed collaborative visual-inertial-ranging odometry (DC-VIRO) framework that tightly fuses VIO and UWB measurements across multiple robots. Anchor positions are explicitly included in the system state to address calibration uncertainty, while shared anchor observations are exploited through inter-robot communication to provide additional geometric constraints. By leveraging a right-invariant error formulation on Lie groups, the proposed approach preserves the observability properties of standard VIO, ensuring estimator consistency. Simulation results with multiple robots demonstrate that DC-VIRO significantly improves localization accuracy and robustness, while simultaneously enabling anchor self-calibration in distributed settings.

</details>


### [394] [Distributional Stability of Tangent-Linearized Gaussian Inference on Smooth Manifolds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19179)
*Junghoon Seo,Hakjin Lee,Jaehoon Sim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces tangent-linearized Gaussian inference on smooth manifolds and derives non-asymptotic W2 stability bounds for projection marginalization and surface-measure conditioning, separating local geometric distortion from tail leakage. Provides closed-form Gaussian diagnostics based on (μ,Σ) and curvature/reach surrogates, validated by circle and planar-pushing experiments with a calibration threshold near sqrt(||Σ||_op)/R ≈ 1/6; highlights normal-direction uncertainty as the main failure mode when locality breaks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gaussian inference on manifolds is essential in robotics, but exact marginalization/conditioning is generally non-Gaussian and geometry-dependent; there is a need for rigorous, quantitative stability diagnostics and practical switching criteria for inference strategies.

Method: Develops tangent-linearized Gaussian inference and derives explicit non-asymptotic Wasserstein-2 (W2) stability bounds for projection marginalization and surface-measure conditioning. Decomposes distortion into local second-order geometric distortion and nonlocal tail leakage; for Gaussian inputs provides closed-form diagnostics using (μ,Σ) and curvature/reach surrogates.

Result: Provides non-asymptotic W2 stability bounds that quantify how projection and conditioning distort Gaussian beliefs on manifolds; yields practical, closed-form diagnostics to detect when linearization is reliable. Calibration threshold observed in experiments (~1/6 in terms of sqrt(||Σ||_op)/R) and identification of normal-direction uncertainty as the dominant failure mode when locality degrades.

Conclusion: The diagnostics enable practitioners to decide when to switch from single-chart linearization to multi-chart or sample-based manifold inference, offering a robust, geometry-aware framework for Gaussian inference on manifolds.

Abstract: Gaussian inference on smooth manifolds is central to robotics, but exact marginalization and conditioning are generally non-Gaussian and geometry-dependent. We study tangent-linearized Gaussian inference and derive explicit non-asymptotic $W_2$ stability bounds for projection marginalization and surface-measure conditioning. The bounds separate local second-order geometric distortion from nonlocal tail leakage and, for Gaussian inputs, yield closed-form diagnostics from $(μ,Σ)$ and curvature/reach surrogates. Circle and planar-pushing experiments validate the predicted calibration transition near $\sqrt{\|Σ\|_{\mathrm{op}}}/R\approx 1/6$ and indicate that normal-direction uncertainty is the dominant failure mode when locality breaks. These diagnostics provide practical triggers for switching from single-chart linearization to multi-chart or sample-based manifold inference.

</details>


### [395] [Visual Prompt Guided Unified Pushing Policy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19193)
*Hieu Bui,Ziyan Gao,Yuya Hosoda,Joo-Ho Lee*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A unified pushing policy with lightweight prompts integrated into a flow-matching policy improves non-prehensile pushing and generalizes across planning problems; it also serves as a low-level primitive in VLM-guided planning for table-cleaning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limited scope and efficiency of multi-step push plans built from predefined primitives, enabling a reactive, generalizable pushing policy guided by high-level prompting.

Method: Develop a unified pushing policy that incorporates a lightweight prompting mechanism into a flow-matching policy to generate reactive, multimodal pushing actions; use a visual prompt provided by a high-level planner to enable reuse across planning problems; evaluate as a low-level primitive within a VLM-guided planning framework for table-cleaning tasks.

Result: The approach outperforms existing baselines in pushing tasks and effectively functions as a practical low-level primitive within a VLM-guided planning framework, solving table-cleaning tasks efficiently.

Conclusion: A lightweight prompting-enhanced flow-matching pushing policy provides a generalizable, reactive solution for non-prehensile manipulation and can be integrated into higher-level planners to improve planning efficiency and versatility.

Abstract: As one of the simplest non-prehensile manipulation skills, pushing has been widely studied as an effective means to rearrange objects. Existing approaches, however, typically rely on multi-step push plans composed of pre-defined pushing primitives with limited application scopes, which restrict their efficiency and versatility across different scenarios. In this work, we propose a unified pushing policy that incorporates a lightweight prompting mechanism into a flow matching policy to guide the generation of reactive, multimodal pushing actions. The visual prompt can be specified by a high-level planner, enabling the reuse of the pushing policy across a wide range of planning problems. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed unified pushing policy not only outperforms existing baselines but also effectively serves as a low-level primitive within a VLM-guided planning framework to solve table-cleaning tasks efficiently.

</details>


### [396] [The Price Is Not Right: Neuro-Symbolic Methods Outperform VLAs on Structured Long-Horizon Manipulation Tasks with Significantly Lower Energy Consumption](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19260)
*Timothy Duggan,Pierrick Lorang,Hong Lu,Matthias Scheutz*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Neuro-symbolic planning with PDDL outperforms a fine-tuned open-weight Vision-Language-Action model on structured, long-horizon towers of Hanoi tasks, with higher success, generalization to unseen block counts, and far lower energy use; end-to-end VLA shows poor data/energy efficiency for such tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess practicality of VLA models for long-horizon robotic manipulation and compare to a structured, symbolic planning approach.

Method: Head-to-head evaluation in simulation on Towers of Hanoi variants (3-block and 4-block). Compare fine-tuned VLA π0 vs neuro-symbolic PDDL planning + learned low-level control. Measure task success and energy consumption during training and execution.

Result: 3-block: neuro-symbolic 95% vs VLA 34%. 4-block: neuro-symbolic generalizes to 78%; VLAs fail. Training energy: VLA ~100x higher than neuro-symbolic. Performance/energy efficiency advantages favor symbolic approach for long-horizon tasks.

Conclusion: Explicit symbolic structure improves reliability, data efficiency, and energy efficiency for long-horizon manipulation; suggests trade-offs between end-to-end foundation-model approaches and structured reasoning architectures. Code/models available at provided URL.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have recently been proposed as a pathway toward generalist robotic policies capable of interpreting natural language and visual inputs to generate manipulation actions. However, their effectiveness and efficiency on structured, long-horizon manipulation tasks remain unclear. In this work, we present a head-to-head empirical comparison between a fine-tuned open-weight VLA model π0 and a neuro-symbolic architecture that combines PDDL-based symbolic planning with learned low-level control. We evaluate both approaches on structured variants of the Towers of Hanoi manipulation task in simulation while measuring both task performance and energy consumption during training and execution. On the 3-block task, the neuro-symbolic model achieves 95% success compared to 34% for the best-performing VLA. The neuro-symbolic model also generalizes to an unseen 4-block variant (78% success), whereas both VLAs fail to complete the task. During training, VLA fine-tuning consumes nearly two orders of magnitude more energy than the neuro-symbolic approach. These results highlight important trade-offs between end-to-end foundation-model approaches and structured reasoning architectures for long-horizon robotic manipulation, emphasizing the role of explicit symbolic structure in improving reliability, data efficiency, and energy efficiency. Code and models are available at https://price-is-not-right.github.io

</details>


### [397] [3D Shape Control of Extensible Multi-Section Soft Continuum Robots via Visual Servoing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19273)
*Abhinav Gandhi,Shou-Shan Chiang,Cagdas D. Onal,Berk Calli*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A vision-based control approach for regulating the whole-body shape of extensible multisection soft continuum manipulators via 2.5D shape visual servoing, enabling global convergence, redundancy exploitation, and proprioception-free operation using image-based state estimation and an IK-generated reference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations in existing visual servoing for continuum robots (end-effector pose control, local convergence, and reliance on proprioception). Exploit kinematic redundancy by regulating full-body shape and using an external camera to observe the entire robot.

Method: Develops a model-based 2.5D shape visual servoing controller that uses an external camera to estimate the robot's full-body state, requires no proprioceptive data, and uses an inverse kinematics solver to generate reference features for the desired configuration. The image stream closes the shape control loop. Experimental validation on a multisection continuum manipulator demonstrates full-body shape regulation while achieving precise end-effector positioning, with a smooth transient response and 1 mm steady-state error. Proof-of-concept object manipulation (stacking, pouring, pulling) shows applicability.

Result: The controller achieves global asymptotic convergence in 3D space, accurate regulation of the robot’s whole-body shape, and end-effector positioning with 1 mm steady-state error; demonstrates practical manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed vision-based whole-body shape control for soft continuum robots eliminates dependency on proprioceptive sensing, leverages redundancy, and uses a 2.5D visual servoing approach to achieve globally stable operation, with promising manipulation capabilities.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel vision-based control algorithm for regulating the whole body shape of extensible multisection soft continuum manipulators. Contrary to existing vision-based control algorithms in the literature that regulate the robot's end effector pose, our proposed control algorithm regulates the robot's whole body configuration, enabling us to leverage its kinematic redundancy. Additionally, our model-based 2.5D shape visual servoing provides globally stable asymptotic convergence in the robot's 3D workspace compared to the closest works in the literature that report local minima. Unlike existing visual servoing algorithms in the literature, our approach does not require information from proprioceptive sensors, making it suitable for continuum manipulators without such capabilities. Instead, robot state is estimated from images acquired by an external camera that observes the robot's whole body shape and is also utilized to close the shape control loop. Traditionally, visual servoing schemes require an image of the robot at its reference pose to generate the reference features. In this work, we utilize an inverse kinematics solver to generate reference features for the desired robot configuration and do not require images of the robot at the reference. Experiments are performed on a multisection continuum manipulator demonstrating the controller's capability to regulate the robot's whole body shape while precisely positioning the robot's end effector. Results validate our controller's ability to regulate the shape of continuum robots while demonstrating a smooth transient response and a steady-state error within 1 mm. Proof-of-concept object manipulation experiments including stacking, pouring, and pulling tasks are performed to demonstrate our controller's applicability.

</details>


### [398] [Safe and Interpretable Multimodal Path Planning for Multi-Agent Cooperation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19304)
*Haojun Shi,Suyu Ye,Katherine M. Guerrerio,Jianzhi Shen,Yifan Yin,Daniel Khashabi,Chien-Ming Huang,Tianmin Shu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CaPE (Code as Path Editor) uses a vision-language model to synthesize a path-editing program that updates a robot's path plans based on the environment and language from other agents, with verification by a model-based planner, enabling safe, interpretable, plug-and-play multimodal coordination across diverse robotic scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decentralized agents must quickly adapt to others' behavior, and when intentions are uncertain, language communication can enhance safety. A practical approach requires interpretable, verifiable planning updates that can operate across heterogeneous agents and tasks.

Method: CaPE integrates a vision-language model to generate a code-like path editing program, which is then grounded and verified by a model-based planner. The program edits the agent's path in response to environmental changes and language cues from other agents, ensuring safety and interpretability. The approach is validated in simulated and real-world settings with multi-robot and human-robot cooperation across autonomous driving, household, and joint-carry tasks; it is designed as a plug-and-play module for existing robotic systems.

Result: CaPE enables safe, interpretable path-plan updates aligned with language cues, demonstrating effective open-ended cooperation across diverse scenarios and enabling easy integration as a modular component in existing robotic systems.

Conclusion: Combining VLM-based path-editing program synthesis with model-based planning verification yields a safe, interpretable, and adaptable multimodal path-planning framework (CaPE) that supports robust open-ended cooperation among heterogeneous agents.

Abstract: Successful cooperation among decentralized agents requires each agent to quickly adapt its plan to the behavior of other agents. In scenarios where agents cannot confidently predict one another's intentions and plans, language communication can be crucial for ensuring safety. In this work, we focus on path-level cooperation in which agents must adapt their paths to one another in order to avoid collisions or perform physical collaboration such as joint carrying. In particular, we propose a safe and interpretable multimodal path planning method, CaPE (Code as Path Editor), which generates and updates path plans for an agent based on the environment and language communication from other agents.
